name: FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING

topics.title: Basic Elements of Real-Time Signal Processing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Elements of Real-Time Signal Processing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time Digital Signal Processing (RT-DSP)** refers to the processing of digitized signals at a rate that is commensurate with the rate at which the signal samples arrive at the system. The output is produced *within a strictly bounded time interval* (deadline) such that the result is available before the next sample (or next block of samples) is input to the system.

A **Real-Time Signal Processing System** is therefore a hardware–software engineered assembly whose purpose is to acquire, transform, and re-emit continuous-time (analog) signals using discrete-time numerical techniques, while **guaranteeing deterministic throughput** with bounded latency.

The **Basic Elements** of any such system form a canonical processing chain. They are:

1. **Analog Input Signal** — the continuous-time, continuous-amplitude physical signal (e.g., speech, vibration, biomedical ECG).
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — a continuous-time low-pass filter (typically a Butterworth or Bessel type) used to band-limit the analog input prior to sampling.
3. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** — captures and holds the instantaneous amplitude of the filtered signal constant during the conversion interval of the ADC.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts the held analog sample into a binary word of $B$ bits.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — the programmable arithmetic engine that executes the chosen algorithm (FIR, IIR, FFT, etc.).
6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — converts the processed digital words back into a continuous-amplitude discrete-time staircase waveform.
7. **Reconstruction (Smoothing) Filter** — a continuous-time low-pass filter that removes the spectral images and produces a smooth analog output.
8. **Analog Output Signal** — the processed continuous-time result delivered to the actuator/speaker/load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Coffee Vending Machine at a Railway Station**
> Imagine a passenger inserts a coin, makes a selection, and the machine *must* dispense the coffee before the train departs (a hard deadline). The system must work *in real-time*, not store the request for hours.
> 
> | Stage in Coffee Machine | Equivalent in RT-DSP |
> |---|---|
> | Customer's spoken order | Analog input signal $x(t)$ |
> | Microphone cleaning noise (low-pass hiss filter) | Anti-aliasing filter |
> | Microphone digitizing the voice | Sample-and-Hold + ADC |
> | CPU mixing the coffee | Digital Signal Processor |
> | Pumping the liquid | DAC |
> | Final strainer removing coffee grits | Reconstruction filter |
> | Cup handed to the customer | Analog output $y(t)$ |

**Why are these elements needed?** The physical world is **analog**, but our processors are **digital**. The chain above is the *bridge* that lets a silicon chip "see," "think about," and "speak back to" the real world without losing the speed (real-time) or the quality (bandwidth) of the original information.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Nyquist Rate** — Minimum sampling rate, equal to $2 f_m$ Hz, where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component in the input.
- **Standard Audio Sampling Rate** — **44.1 kHz** (CD) or **48 kHz** (professional audio).
- **Typical ADC Bit-Depths** — 8, 12, 16, 24 bits.
- **Voice-band Limit** — **4 kHz** (telephony), requiring an 8 kHz sampling rate.
- **Speed of Light / Sound** — Not directly used here, but imply physical propagation delays that *bound* real-time deadlines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-by-Block Operational Logic

### 🔹 Stage 1 — Analog Input $x(t)$
- Continuous in both **time** and **amplitude**.
- Bandwidth $B_x$ is assumed finite (the system depends on this; without it, perfect reconstruction is impossible).

### 🔹 Stage 2 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)
**Why?** Sampling creates periodic spectral replicas at multiples of $f_s$. If the original signal contains any frequency above $f_s/2$, those replicas *overlap* (alias) and corrupt the baseband.
**How?** A continuous-time low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \le f_s/2$ removes these offending components *before* they can alias.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** A very sharp AAF (high order) minimizes aliasing but introduces severe group-delay distortion, which is unacceptable in real-time control loops. Hence, **Bessel filters** are often preferred in RT systems because of their linear-phase (constant group-delay) response.

### 🔹 Stage 3 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H)
**Why?** An ADC requires a *finite* aperture time $T_{ap}$ to perform its conversion. If the input changes during this interval, the result is ambiguous.
**How?** A switch (typically a MOSFET) closes for an instant to charge a hold capacitor $C_h$ to $x(nT)$, then opens; the capacitor holds the voltage steady for the ADC.

**Two modes:**
- **Flat-top sample** (real S/H) — value is held constant for the entire conversion → introduces a $(\sin \pi f T_{ap})/(\pi f T_{ap})$ aperture distortion, often equalized digitally.
- **Natural sample** (impulse sampler) — ideal mathematical model; instantaneous.

### 🔹 Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
Two sub-processes:
1. **Quantization** — continuous amplitude $\rightarrow$ discrete amplitude (out of $2^B$ levels).
2. **Coding** — discrete amplitude $\rightarrow$ binary word.

**Quantization Step Size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{X_{max} - X_{min}}{2^B - 1}$$

**Quantization Error (Q-noise):** Bounded, statistically modeled as uniform:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q(n) \le +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

### 🔹 Stage 5 — Digital Signal Processor
This is the **heart** of the system. The processor must:
- Compute the output $y(n)$ from input $x(n)$ within the sample period $T_s = 1/f_s$.
- Meet *hard real-time* constraints — failure to finish computation causes data loss or system instability.
- Example algorithms: FIR filter, IIR filter, FFT, adaptive filters, modulators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Deadline Equation:** The total processing time per sample $T_{proc}$ must satisfy $T_{proc} < T_s$. The slack time $T_{slack} = T_s - T_{proc}$ is the *schedulability margin* used by RTOS design.

### 🔹 Stage 6 — Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)
- Produces a **staircase waveform** $y_h(t)$ that holds each sample value for a full sample period $T_s$.
- Acts as a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**.

### 🔹 Stage 7 — Reconstruction (Smoothing) Filter
- A continuous-time low-pass filter that removes the high-frequency spectral images of the staircase.
- Cutoff at $f_s/2$.
- Often the *dual* of the AAF (e.g., if AAF is 5th-order Butterworth, the reconstruction filter is also 5th-order Butterworth).

### 🔹 Stage 8 — Analog Output $y(t)$
A clean, continuous-time, continuous-amplitude representation of the processed information.

---

## 2.2 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear repeatedly in KTU Module 1, Module 2, and Module 3 questions. Memorize them with units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ | Hz | Hard bound; $f_s$ is sampling rate, $f_m$ is max signal frequency. |
| 2 | Sample Period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive samples. |
| 3 | Quantization Step | $\Delta = \dfrac{X_{max} - X_{min}}{2^B}$ | Volts (or signal unit) | $B$ is ADC bit-width. |
| 4 | Quantization Error Bound | $e_q \in \left[-\dfrac{\Delta}{2},\, +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right]$ | Volts | Uniform distribution assumed. |
| 5 | Q-Noise Power (uniform) | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | V² | Variance of quantization error. |
| 6 | SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio) | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 B + 1.76$ | dB | For full-scale sine-wave input. |
| 7 | SQNR in Linear Units | $\text{SQNR} \approx 1.5 \cdot 2^{2B}$ | (ratio) | $1.5 = 3/2$ from sine statistics. |
| 8 | Anti-Alias Filter Cutoff | $f_c \le f_s/2$ | Hz | Practically chosen at $0.4 f_s$ for safety margin. |
| 9 | Reconstruction Filter Cutoff | $f_c \le f_s/2$ | Hz | Removes spectral images at $k f_s$. |
| 10 | Real-Time Deadline | $T_{proc} < T_s$ | seconds | Schedulability constraint. |
| 11 | Effective Bits (ENOB) | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | Indicates real ADC quality. |
| 12 | Aperture Distortion | $H_{ap}(f) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi f T_{ap})}{\pi f T_{ap}}$ | (unitless) | Need digital equalization if $T_{ap} \not\ll T_s$. |
| 13 | Spectral Image Spacing | $\Delta f_{image} = f_s$ | Hz | Replicas appear at $f_c \pm k f_s$. |
| 14 | Processing Latency | $T_{lat} = T_{s/h} + T_{ADC} + T_{proc} + T_{DAC}$ | seconds | Total deterministic delay. |
| 15 | Oversampling Ratio (OSR) | $\text{OSR} = f_s / (2 f_m)$ | (ratio) | $> 1$ gives filter design margin. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Remember the absolute value trick:** When a derivation requires $\vert H(f) \vert$, write `\vert H(f) \vert` in $\LaTeX$. Never write `|H(f)|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the table syntax.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telephony (4 kHz voice, 8 kHz sampling)** — uses exactly this chain; an 8-bit $\mu$-law codec per ITU-T G.711.
- **Digital Hearing Aids** — latency must be $< 10$ ms to avoid comb-filter effect and to prevent auditory feedback oscillation.
- **MP3 Players / Smartphones** — DAC + reconstruction filter chain with $f_s = 44.1$ kHz.
- **Radar / Sonar** — wideband real-time FFT engines for pulse compression.
- **Biomedical ECG Monitors** — 12-bit ADC at 500 Hz, with the chain detecting arrhythmias in real time.
- **Industrial Motor Control (Field-Oriented Control)** — closed-loop DSP executing IIR/PI controllers at $f_s = 10$ kHz–20 kHz.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive**. Each algebraic transition is shown explicitly — no steps skipped, no "it can be shown that" shortcuts. This is exactly how a KTU Board Examiner expects the work to appear in the answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Sampling in the Time and Frequency Domains

**Goal:** Show mathematically how sampling multiplies a signal by an impulse train and produces spectral replicas in the frequency domain.

**Step 1 — Define the continuous-time signal.**
Let the analog input be a band-limited signal $x(t)$ whose Fourier Transform $X(f)$ is non-zero only for $\vert f \vert \le f_m$.

**Step 2 — Define the impulse train sampler.**
A periodic train of unit impulses spaced by $T_s$ is given by:

$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the signal by the impulse train (this IS the sampling operation).**
The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_s(t) &= x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) \\
       &= x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - n T_s) \\
       &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(n T_s)\, \delta(t - n T_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** The product of a continuous function with an impulse is non-zero only at the instants of the impulses, picking out the value $x(nT_s)$ at each.

**Step 4 — Apply the Fourier Transform using the modulation property.**
The Fourier Transform of a product in time becomes a *periodic convolution* in frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_s(f) &= \mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t)\} \\
       &= X(f) * \mathcal{F}\{\delta_T(t)\} \\
       &= X(f) * \left( f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** The Fourier series of an impulse train is itself an impulse train in the frequency domain, with weight $1/T_s$ per impulse; we write this as $f_s \sum_k \delta(f - kf_s)$ because $f_s = 1/T_s$.

**Step 5 — Perform the convolution.**
Convolution with an impulse train replicates and shifts the spectrum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_s(f) &= f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[ X(f) * \delta(f - k f_s) \right] \\
       &= f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** The baseband spectrum $X(f)$ is replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$, scaled by $f_s$.

**Step 6 — Apply the Nyquist condition for no overlap.**
The replicas do not overlap if the highest frequency $f_m$ satisfies:

$$
f_m < \frac{f_s}{2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f_s > 2 f_m
$$

This is the **Shannon-Nyquist Sampling Theorem**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization Error Power and SQNR

**Goal:** Compute the noise power of a uniform mid-tread quantizer and derive the famous $6.02 B + 1.76$ dB formula.

**Step 1 — Express the quantization error.**
The quantizer output $x_Q$ differs from the input $x$ by the error $e_q = x_Q - x$. For a uniform quantizer with step $\Delta$ and input range spanning $2^N \cdot \Delta$ levels:

$$
-\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le +\frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume a uniform probability distribution.**
Since the error is bounded and, for full-scale signals, well-approximated as uniformly distributed, the PDF is:

$$
p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for } -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le +\frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean (DC) of the error.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{e_q} &= \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q \\
          &= \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q \\
          &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^2}{2} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \\
          &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \left( \frac{\Delta^2}{8} - \frac{\Delta^2}{8} \right) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** The mean of a symmetric uniform distribution is zero — the quantizer has no DC bias on average.

**Step 4 — Compute the variance (noise power).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{e_q}^2 &= \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q \\
               &= \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q \\
               &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \\
               &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 (\Delta/2)^3}{3} \\
               &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta^3}{24} \\
               &= \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** The quantization noise variance is $\Delta^2/12$, a result every DSP student must derive in exams.

**Step 5 — Express the step size in terms of the number of bits $B$ and full-scale range $V_{FS}$.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^B}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the signal power for a full-scale sine wave.**
For $x(t) = A \sin(2 \pi f t)$ with $A = V_{FS}/2$:

$$
P_x = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}
$$

**Step 7 — Form the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= \frac{P_x}{\sigma_{e_q}^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}^2}{\Delta^2} \\
            &= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta} \right)^2 \\
            &= \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2B} = 1.5 \cdot 4^{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to decibels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left( 1.5 \cdot 4^{B} \right) \\
                 &= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 B \log_{10}(4) \\
                 &= 1.7609 + 10 B \cdot (0.6021) \\
                 &= 1.76 + 6.02\, B \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **6.02 dB per bit rule** that KTU loves to ask in module tests.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reconstruction of the Continuous Signal (Ideal Low-Pass)

**Goal:** Show that an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$ recovers the original signal $x(t)$ from the sampled spectrum $X_s(f)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the sampled spectrum.**

$$
X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the ideal reconstruction filter.**

$$
H_r(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \le f_s/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Textual explanation:** The gain $T_s$ exactly compensates the $f_s$ scaling that sampling introduced.

**Step 3 — Multiply in the frequency domain.**

$$
Y(f) = H_r(f) \cdot X_s(f) = T_s \cdot f_s \cdot X(f) = X(f)
$$

since $T_s f_s = 1$.

**Step 4 — Inverse Fourier Transform gives the recovered signal.**

$$
y(t) = x(t)
$$

**Conclusion:** Perfect reconstruction is achievable in theory, provided (i) $f_s \ge 2 f_m$, (ii) the AAF and reconstruction filter are ideal, and (iii) no quantization is performed. In practice, non-idealities cause reconstruction error, motivating *oversampling* and *sigma-delta* techniques covered in Module 4.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numeric Problem Skeleton

| Given Quantity | Symbol | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| Max input frequency | $f_m$ | 4 kHz (speech) or 20 kHz (audio) |
| ADC bit-width | $B$ | 8 / 12 / 16 |
| Full-scale voltage | $V_{FS}$ | 3.3 V (typical 3.3 V CMOS ADC) |
| Sampling rate | $f_s$ | 8 kHz / 44.1 kHz / 48 kHz |
| Required SNR | $\text{SNR}_{dB}$ | 60 dB / 90 dB / 120 dB |
| Compute | — | $\Delta$, $\sigma_q^2$, SQNR, ENOB, $T_s$ |

> [!TIP]
> A 1-mark KTU question almost always tests: *"How many bits are required for a 60 dB SNR?"* — invert the formula:
> 
> $$B = \left\lceil \frac{\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{60 - 1.76}{6.02} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 9.67 \right\rceil = 10 \text{ bits}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Real-Time DSP System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input x of t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter AAF]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold S or H]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    F --> G[Reconstruction Smoothing Filter]
    G --> H[Analog Output y of t]
    A -.feedback.-> E
```

**Textual Annotation:**
- The top row is the **forward path** of a unidirectional real-time chain.
- The dotted feedback arrow indicates that, in many systems (e.g., control loops), the DSP also receives a delayed copy of the output for closed-loop adaptation.
- Each block must complete its work in $\le T_s$ seconds, where $T_s = 1/f_s$.

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the ADC Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Filtered Analog Input] --> Q1[Quantizer Mid-Tread]
    Q1 --> C1[Binary Encoder B-bit]
    C1 --> O1[Digital Word x of n]
    Q1 -.error.-> E1[Quantization Error e sub q]
```

**Textual Annotation:**
- The quantizer is the *only* source of non-recoverable information loss in the chain.
- For $B = 8$ bits, there are exactly $256$ distinct output codes; the spacing is $\Delta = V_{FS}/256$.

## 4.3 Internal Architecture of the DAC Subsystem (Zero-Order Hold)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Digital Word y of n] --> Z[Zero-Order Hold ZOH]
    Z --> SC[Staircase Waveform y sub h of t]
    SC --> R1[Smoothing LPF]
    R1 --> OUT[Reconstructed Analog y of t]
```

**Textual Annotation:**
- The ZOH holds each digital value constant for the full sample interval $T_s$, producing a *staircase* output.
- The smoothing LPF averages the staircase to recover the smooth analog waveform.
- The spectrum of the staircase contains the desired baseband plus images at $k f_s$ (for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$) — the LPF suppresses these.

## 4.4 Real-Time Scheduling / Deadline Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Sample Period T sub s] --> T2[ADC Conversion T sub ADC]
    T1 --> T3[DSP Computation T sub proc]
    T1 --> T4[DAC Conversion T sub DAC]
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T5[Slack T sub slack] --> T6[Idle or Next Sample Wait]
    T4 --> T5
```

**Textual Annotation:**
- The total budget $T_s$ is partitioned into deterministic sub-tasks.
- $T_{slack} = T_s - (T_{ADC} + T_{proc} + T_{DAC})$.
- If $T_{slack} < 0$, the system is **unschedulable** — samples will be lost.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Real-Time Signal Processing system. List the basic elements of a typical real-time DSP system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Real-Time Signal Processing system is one in which the processed output is produced within a strictly bounded time interval (deadline) such that the result is available before the arrival of the next sample, ensuring deterministic throughput.
- **List of Basic Elements (1.5 marks):** The basic elements are:
  1. Analog input signal $x(t)$
  2. Anti-aliasing filter (AAF)
  3. Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit
  4. Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
  5. Digital Signal Processor (DSP)
  6. Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)
  7. Reconstruction (smoothing) filter
  8. Analog output signal $y(t)$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
**What is the need for an anti-aliasing filter in a real-time DSP system? State the Nyquist sampling theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Need for AAF (2 marks):** When a signal is sampled, its spectrum is replicated at integer multiples of the sampling frequency $f_s$. If the original signal contains frequency components above $f_s/2$, these replicas **overlap** and cause **aliasing** — an irreversible distortion. The anti-aliasing filter is a continuous-time low-pass filter that band-limits the input to $\le f_s/2$ before sampling, thus preventing aliasing.
- **Nyquist Theorem (1 mark):** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling rate satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_m$, where $2 f_m$ is called the Nyquist rate.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) for Module 1 typically provide an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Both options are given below with full sub-parts and complete model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the basic elements of a real-time digital signal processing system. Describe the role of the anti-aliasing filter and the reconstruction filter in detail.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw and label the block diagram (2 marks):**

The chain is: **Analog Input → Anti-Aliasing Filter → Sample-and-Hold → ADC → DSP → DAC → Reconstruction Filter → Analog Output**.

**Step 2 — Explain the role of the Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF) (2.5 marks):**
- It is a continuous-time low-pass filter placed *before* the S/H and ADC.
- Its cutoff frequency $f_c$ is set at $f_s/2$ (Nyquist frequency), or practically at $0.4 f_s$ to provide a transition-band margin.
- It removes any input frequency components above $f_s/2$ so that after sampling, no spectral overlap (aliasing) occurs.
- In real-time systems, a **Bessel filter** is preferred over Butterworth or Chebyshev because of its linear phase (constant group delay), which preserves the *shape* of waveforms — critical for control and audio applications.
- Aliasing is **irreversible**; it cannot be corrected by DSP after the fact, so the AAF is a *mandatory* element.

**Step 3 — Explain the role of the Reconstruction Filter (2.5 marks):**
- The DAC output is a *staircase* waveform containing the desired baseband plus spectral images centered at $k f_s$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- The reconstruction filter is a continuous-time low-pass filter that passes the baseband and attenuates all images.
- Its cutoff is at $f_s/2$.
- It is essentially the **time-domain dual** of the anti-aliasing filter.
- The output is a smooth analog signal $y(t)$ that approximates the original processed waveform.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A music signal has a maximum frequency of 20 kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 48$ kHz and quantized using a 16-bit ADC. Compute the (i) sample period, (ii) quantization step size for a 3.3 V full-scale range, (iii) quantization noise power, and (iv) theoretical SQNR.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_m = 20$ kHz, $f_s = 48$ kHz, $B = 16$ bits, $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V.

**(i) Sample Period (1 mark):**

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{48 \times 10^3} = 20.833\ \mu s
$$

**(ii) Quantization Step Size (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^B} = \frac{3.3}{2^{16}} = \frac{3.3}{65536} = 5.035 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{V} = 50.35\ \mu\text{V}
$$

**(iii) Quantization Noise Power (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(5.035 \times 10^{-5})^2}{12} = \frac{2.535 \times 10^{-9}}{12} = 2.113 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{V}^2
$$

**(iv) Theoretical SQNR (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 B + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08\ \text{dB}
$$

**Verification using linear formula (extra credit):**

$$
\text{SQNR} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2B} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{32} = 6.44 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
10 \log_{10}(6.44 \times 10^9) = 98.09\ \text{dB} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the sampling process in detail. Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal and state the Nyquist sampling theorem.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define sampling (1 mark):**
Sampling is the process of converting a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x(nT_s)$ by recording the amplitude at regular intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$.

**Step 2 — Define the impulse train (1 mark):**

$$
\delta_T(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the sampled signal (1 mark):**

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(n T_s) \cdot \delta(t - n T_s)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Fourier Transform and use modulation property (2 marks):**

$$
X_s(f) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t)\} = X(f) * \mathcal{F}\{\delta_T(t)\} = X(f) * \left[ f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Convolve with the impulse train (1 mark):**

$$
X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 6 — State the theorem and condition (1 mark):**
> A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_m$. The replicas do not overlap; a low-pass reconstruction filter with cutoff $f_s/2$ isolates the baseband.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 12-bit ADC is used in a DSP system with a $\pm 5$ V input range. The sampling rate is 100 kHz. Determine the (i) quantization step, (ii) RMS quantization noise, (iii) SQNR, and (iv) the effective number of bits (ENOB) if the measured SNR is 70 dB.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $B = 12$, $V_{FS} = 10$ V (since $\pm 5$ V), $f_s = 100$ kHz, $\text{SNR}_{measured} = 70$ dB.

**(i) Quantization Step (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^B} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.441 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 2.441\ \text{mV}
$$

**(ii) RMS Quantization Noise (1.5 marks):**

$$
\sigma_q = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{2.441 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464} = 7.05 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = 0.705\ \text{mV}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical SQNR (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\ \text{dB}
$$

**(iv) Effective Number of Bits (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{68.24}{6.02} = 11.33 \approx 11\ \text{bits}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 12-bit ADC performs closer to an 11.3-bit ideal ADC, indicating some non-ideality (clock jitter, integral nonlinearity, etc.).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in Module 1 questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the role of the Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** — many students jump directly from "filter" to "ADC" without explaining *why* a hold capacitor is needed. Always mention aperture uncertainty.
> 2. **Confusing anti-aliasing filter with reconstruction filter** — both are low-pass, but AAF is *before* the sampler (analog) and reconstruction is *after* the DAC (analog). Examiners *love* to test this distinction.
> 3. **Writing $\vert H(f) \vert$ in a markdown table using pipe characters** — the table breaks. Always use `\vert H(f) \vert` in $\LaTeX$.
> 4. **Computing SQNR using $6B$ instead of $6.02B$** — the 0.02 is *exam-worthy*; using 6 gives wrong rounding in problems targeting 12-bit or 16-bit ADCs.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners *strictly deduct* 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical problems.
> 6. **Confusing sample rate with sample period** — writing "$f_s = 20.8\ \mu s$" instead of "$T_s = 20.8\ \mu s$" is a 1-mark penalty.
> 7. **Skipping the derivation of the quantization noise variance $\Delta^2/12$** — KTU 14-mark questions often ask the *derivation*; a "memorized" answer without integration steps loses 3–4 marks.
> 8. **Aliasing and causality confusion** — aliasing is a *frequency-domain* problem; the AAF is a *time-domain* solution placed *before* sampling. Writing "AAF is needed because the signal is not bandlimited" is incomplete — write "AAF band-limits the signal to $\le f_s/2$ *to* prevent aliasing."

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this block for last-minute revision the night before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ A real-time DSP system processes samples *as they arrive*, with output produced before the next sample (deterministic deadline $T_{proc} < T_s$).
- ✅ The 8 basic elements: **Input → AAF → S/H → ADC → DSP → DAC → Reconstruction → Output**.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** is a *continuous-time LPF* placed *before* the sampler; its cutoff is $f_s/2$ (or $0.4 f_s$ practically).
- ✅ **Sample-and-Hold** holds the analog value constant during ADC conversion to avoid aperture uncertainty.
- ✅ **ADC** does two things: *quantization* (continuous → discrete amplitude) and *coding* (decimal → binary).
- ✅ **Quantization step** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B$.
- ✅ **Quantization error** is uniform on $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with variance $\Delta^2/12$.
- ✅ **SQNR (dB)** $= 6.02 B + 1.76$ for full-scale sine input — the **6-dB-per-bit rule**.
- ✅ **Nyquist rate** $f_N = 2 f_m$ — minimum sampling rate for perfect reconstruction.
- ✅ Sampling multiplies the signal by an impulse train → spectrum becomes $f_s \sum_k X(f - k f_s)$ — periodic replicas.
- ✅ **Reconstruction filter** is a *continuous-time LPF* placed *after* the DAC; cutoff $f_s/2$; it removes spectral images.
- ✅ **DAC** acts as a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**, producing a staircase waveform.
- ✅ **Real-time deadline equation:** $T_{proc} < T_s = 1/f_s$.
- ✅ **Total system latency:** $T_{lat} = T_{S/H} + T_{ADC} + T_{proc} + T_{DAC}$.
- ✅ **Effective number of bits (ENOB)** is what a real ADC actually delivers, computed from measured SNR.
- ✅ **Oversampling** (OSR $> 1$) relaxes AAF design requirements and pushes quantization noise out of band.
- ✅ Always carry **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
- ✅ In $\LaTeX$ tables, write `\vert H(f) \vert`, never `|H(f)|` (the pipe breaks the table).
- ✅ For a 14-mark derivation, the *integration steps* (limits, integrand, antiderivative) MUST appear; memorize $\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^2 de_q = \Delta^3 / 12$.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Basic Elements of Real-Time Signal Processing Systems**
> *— KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 Aligned | 2024 Scheme Compliant —*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Interface - Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analog Interface - Sampling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Real-Time Digital Signal Processing (RT-DSP), the **Analog Interface** refers to the boundary electronics and signal-conditioning circuitry that bridges the continuous-time (analog) physical world and the discrete-time (digital) computational engine. The **Sampling** stage is the first and most critical operation within this interface, where a continuous-time analog signal $x_a(t)$ is converted into a discrete-time sequence $x[n] = x_a(nT)$ by measuring and recording its instantaneous amplitude at uniformly spaced time intervals $T$ (the **sampling period**), with a corresponding rate $f_s = 1/T$ known as the **sampling frequency**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sampling is the *first operation* in the Analog Interface block. It is governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, and in practice it is implemented as a cascade of three sub-systems: **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF) → Sample-and-Hold (S/H) → Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. The S/H and ADC together physically realize the *mathematical act* of sampling. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Camera" View Imagine you are trying to capture the graceful motion of a spinning ceiling fan using a **movie camera** that can only record **24 still frames per second**: - If the fan is spinning **slowly** (say 1 rotation per second), each frame captures a noticeably different blade position — you can later *reconstruct* the smooth motion. ✅ - If the fan is spinning **very fast** (say 30 rotations per second), two consecutive frames may catch the blade at almost identical positions, making the fan appear to spin **backward** at a slow speed — a perceptual illusion. ❌ This perceptual illusion is precisely the engineering phenomenon known as **aliasing**. To avoid it, the **number of frames per second (sampling rate)** must be at least **twice the maximum rotational speed (signal bandwidth)**. This is the essence of the Nyquist criterion. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of the Analog Interface > [!NOTE] > **Core Block Chain (Memorize this for KTU):** > > $$\text{Analog Input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Anti-Aliasing Filter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sample \& Hold} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ADC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DSP Core}$$ > > $$\text{DSP Core} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DAC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Reconstruction Filter (ZOH smoother)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Analog Output}$$ **Key physical constant / standard metric (bolded for KTU boards):** - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the analog signal. - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N/2 = f_{max}$ — the highest frequency that *can* be unambiguously represented. - **Standard audio CD sampling rate:** **44.1 kHz** (covers up to ~22.05 kHz, the human hearing limit). - **Standard voice (telephony) sampling rate:** **8 kHz** (covers up to 4 kHz). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Broken Down Step-by-Step ### Step 1: Band-Limiting the Input (Anti-Aliasing) - **Why:** Real-world analog signals are rarely strictly band-limited; they contain noise and high-frequency components that would *fold back* into the desired baseband and corrupt the digital representation. - **How:** A **low-pass analog filter** (typically a Butterworth or Chebyshev Type-I of order 4 to 8) with cutoff frequency $f_c \le f_s/2$ is placed *before* the sampler. - **Practical rule:** The cutoff is set slightly below $f_s/2$ to allow for the filter's non-ideal roll-off (transition band). ### Step 2: The Sampling Switch - **Why:** A pure mathematical impulse-train sampler cannot be physically built. - **How:** An **electronic switch** (typically a MOSFET, JFET, or a CMOS transmission gate) connects the input to a holding capacitor for a brief duration $\tau$ called the **aperture time**, repeating every $T$ seconds. ### Step 3: Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Stabilization - **Why:** The ADC requires a **constant input voltage** during the conversion time $T_{conv}$ (which can be microseconds to nanoseconds). Without S/H, the signal would drift during conversion, causing **aperture error**. - **How:** A **hold capacitor** $C_H$ stores the sampled voltage. The buffered output is fed to the ADC. ### Step 4: Analog-to-Digital Conversion - The held voltage is quantized into one of $2^B$ discrete levels, where $B$ is the ADC resolution in bits. - Output: a $B$-bit binary word $x[n]$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Samples per second (Hz) | | 2 | Sampling Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Seconds per sample | | 3 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing (Hz) | | 4 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_{Nyq} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Highest representable baseband frequency (Hz) | | 5 | Sampled Signal (Impulse Train) | $x_s(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_a(nT)\,\delta(t-nT)$ | Mathematical model | | 6 | Frequency-Domain Sampled Signal | $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_a\!\left(f - k f_s\right)$ | Periodic spectrum replication | | 7 | Reconstruction (Ideal Low-Pass) | $x_a(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t-nT}{T}\right)$ | Whittaker–Shannon interpolation | | 8 | Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$ | Volts, where $V_{FS}$ is full-scale range | | 9 | SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise) | $\mathrm{SQNR} \approx 6.02\,B + 1.76$ | dB, for full-scale sine input | | 10 | Hold Capacitor Droop | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ | Volts/sec — must be << ADC LSB during $T_{conv}$ | | 11 | Aperture Time | $\tau_a$ | Acquisition window in seconds | | 12 | Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter) | $\Delta V \approx 2\pi f_{in} \, V_{pk} \, t_j$ | Error voltage due to clock jitter $t_j$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** This Analog Interface is the literal "front door" of every digital audio player, mobile phone, MRI machine, software-defined radio, and industrial control system. Designing it correctly is the difference between a $1 smartphone mic working and a $10{,}000$ scientific instrument working. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility - **Biomedical:** ECG machines sample at 250–500 Hz to capture cardiac waveforms without aliasing motion artifacts. - **Telecommunications:** 4G LTE uses variable sampling rates tied to channel bandwidth (1.92 MHz to 30.72 MHz). - **Radar / Sonar:** RF signals at GHz are first *down-converted* to an IF, then sampled by high-speed ADCs (e.g., 250 MSPS). - **Industrial Control:** Sensors (temperature, pressure, vibration) are sampled at 2× to 10× the control-loop bandwidth, with $\ge 12$-bit ADCs for resolution. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mathematical Model of Uniform Sampling ### Step 1 — Define the Ideal Sampling Function We model the sampling operation as multiplication of the analog signal $x_a(t)$ by an **impulse train** $p(t)$. $$ p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT) $$ Each impulse $\delta(t-nT)$ "captures" the value of $x_a(t)$ at the exact instant $t = nT$. ### Step 2 — Express the Sampled Signal in Time Domain Multiplying $x_a(t)$ by the impulse train yields the mathematically idealized sampled signal $x_s(t)$: $$ x_s(t) = x_a(t) \cdot p(t) = x_a(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT) $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Sifting Property of the Impulse Using the sifting property $x_a(t)\,\delta(t-nT) = x_a(nT)\,\delta(t-nT)$, we obtain: $$ x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_a(nT)\,\delta(t - nT) $$ This is the **canonical representation** of a sampled signal. The discrete-time sequence is simply $x[n] = x_a(nT)$, with the impulse carrying only the *amplitude* information. ### Step 4 — Move to the Frequency Domain via the Fourier Transform The Fourier transform of an impulse train is also an impulse train (scaled by $1/T$): $$ \mathcal{F}\{p(t)\} = P(f) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - k f_s\right) $$ The sampled signal in the frequency domain is the **convolution of** $X_a(f)$ with $P(f)$, but convolution of a spectrum with an impulse train yields **periodic replication**: $$ X_s(f) = X_a(f) * P(f) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_a\!\left(f - k f_s\right) $$ ### Step 5 — Interpret the Result (The Heart of the Sampling Theorem) This equation reveals a profound result: $$ \begin{aligned} X_s(f) &= \frac{1}{T}\,X_a(f) && \text{(original spectrum, centered at } k=0\text{)} \\ &\quad + \frac{1}{T}\,X_a(f - f_s) && \text{(replica shifted by } +f_s\text{)} \\ &\quad + \frac{1}{T}\,X_a(f + f_s) && \text{(replica shifted by } -f_s\text{)} \\ &\quad + \cdots \end{aligned} $$ - If $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (i.e., $f_{max} \le f_s/2$), the replicas **do not overlap** → original signal is fully recoverable by a low-pass filter. ✅ - If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the replicas **overlap** → information is destroyed, and the overlapping region is called the **aliasing distortion** band. ❌ This mathematically proves the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Aliasing Frequency Formula Given a sinusoid $x_a(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$ sampled at $f_s$, the apparent (aliased) frequency observed in the digital domain is: $$ f_{alias} = \left| f_0 - k f_s \right| \quad \text{where } k = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\dfrac{f_0}{f_s}\right) $$ **Example (a classic KTU problem):** A 7 kHz tone is sampled at $f_s = 6$ kHz. $$ \begin{aligned} k &= \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{7000}{6000}\right) = \mathrm{round}(1.1667) = 1 \\ f_{alias} &= |7000 - 1 \cdot 6000| = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned} $$ So a 7 kHz signal *appears* as a 1 kHz tone after sampling — it has "folded" into the baseband. ## 3.3 Derivation: Reconstruction via Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) In practice, a **DAC** does not output ideal sinc-interpolated impulses. Instead, it holds each sample value constant for one period $T$, producing a **staircase waveform**. Mathematically, this is convolution of the impulse train with a rectangular pulse $h_{ZOH}(t)$ of width $T$ and height 1: $$ h_{ZOH}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The frequency response of the ZOH (the magnitude part) is a **sinc envelope** that attenuates higher frequencies — this naturally acts as a mild low-pass filter, but with a $\mathrm{sinc}$-shaped droop in the passband that must be corrected by an **equalization filter** $H_{eq}(f) = 1/\mathrm{sinc}(fT)$. $$ \begin{aligned} H_{ZOH}(f) &= \mathcal{F}\{h_{ZOH}(t)\} = T \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(fT)\,e^{-j\pi f T} \\ |H_{ZOH}(f)| &= T \left\vert \mathrm{sinc}(fT) \right\vert = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T)}{\pi f T} \end{aligned} $$ At $f = 0$: $|H_{ZOH}(0)| = T$ (unity DC gain after normalization). At $f = f_s/2$: $|H_{ZOH}(f_s/2)| = T \cdot 2/\pi \approx 0.637\,T$ (the famous $-4$ dB droop at Nyquist). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Sampling Rate | Application | Signal Bandwidth $f_{max}$ | Minimum $f_s$ (Nyquist) | Practical $f_s$ Used | Reason for Oversampling | |-------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------| | Telephone voice | 3.4 kHz | 6.8 kHz | 8 kHz | Filter relaxation | | Audio CD | 20 kHz | 40 kHz | 44.1 kHz | Guard band + anti-alias roll-off | | EEG | 100 Hz | 200 Hz | 250–500 Hz | Adequate transition band | | Vibration analysis | 5 kHz | 10 kHz | 25.6 kHz | Δ-Σ ADC oversampling ratio | | Software-defined radio (HF) | 30 MHz | 60 MHz | 65–125 MSPS | ADC technology limit | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Analog Interface (Transmit Path) ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Analog Source
Sensor or Signal] --> aaf[Anti-Aliasing Filter
Analog LPF Order 4 to 8] aaf --> sh[Sample and Hold
S and H Circuit] sh --> mux[Channel Multiplexer
Optional Multi-Channel] mux --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter
ADC Resolution B bits] adc --> buf[Output Buffer
Three State or FIFO] buf --> dsp[DSP Core
CPU or FPGA or ASIC] dsp --> dac[DAC Digital to Analog Converter] dac --> recf[Reconstruction Filter
Analog LPF Smoothing] recf --> out[Analog Output
Actuator or Speaker] classDef analog fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#0B3D91 classDef mixed fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F08000,color:#7A3D00 classDef digital fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1E8E3E,color:#0D5C26 class src,aaf,recf,out analog class sh,mux,dac mixed class adc,buf,dsp digital ``` ## 4.2 Sampling Operation — Time and Frequency Domain View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TD[Time Domain] A1[Continuous Signal xa of t] --> A2[Impulse Train Sampler
Multiplication by p of t] A2 --> A3[Sampled Signal xs of t
Weighted Impulses] end subgraph FD[Frequency Domain] B1[Spectrum Xa of f
Single Lobe Bandwidth fmax] --> B2[Replication by Convolution
with Impulse Train] B2 --> B3[Spectrum Xs of f
Periodic Replicas Every fs] end TD -.Fourier Pair.-> FD A1 -.Transform.-> B1 A3 -.Transform.-> B3 classDef timeclass fill:#FFF0F0,stroke:#C0392B,color:#641E16 classDef freqclass fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#1B2631 class A1,A2,A3 timeclass class B1,B2,B3 freqclass ``` ## 4.3 Sample-and-Hold Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR sw[CMOS Switch
MOSFET or JFET] --> chold[Hold Capacitor CH
Typically 10 to 100 pF] chold --> bufv[Buffer Amplifier
High Input Z Low Output Z] bufv --> outADC[To ADC Input] ctrl[Control Logic
Sampling Clock fs] -->|Sample Pulse| sw ctrl -->|Hold Pulse| chold classDef swclass fill:#FEF9E7,stroke:#B7950B,color:#7D6608 classDef capclass fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#C0392B,color:#641E16 classDef ctrlclass fill:#EAF2F8,stroke:#1F618D,color:#154360 class sw swclass class chold capclass class bufv,outADC ctrlclass ``` ## 4.4 Aliasing Visualization Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Input Signal at f0 equals 7 kHz] --> S2{Sampling Rate Decision} S2 -->|fs equals 20 kHz| S3[Spectrum Non Overlapping
Clean Reconstruction] S2 -->|fs equals 10 kHz| S4[Replicas Just Touching
Boundary Case] S2 -->|fs equals 6 kHz| S5[Replicas Overlap
Aliasing Detected at 1 kHz] S3 --> R1[Original Recovered
fa equals 7 kHz] S4 --> R2[Marginal Reconstruction
Possible with Sharp Filter] S5 --> R3[Aliased Output
fa appears as 1 kHz] classDef good fill:#E8F8F5,stroke:#117A65,color:#0E6251 classDef warn fill:#FEF5E7,stroke:#CA6F1E,color:#7E5109 classDef bad fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#C0392B,color:#78281F class S3,R1 good class S4,R2 warn class S5,R3 bad ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read these diagrams left-to-right / top-to-bottom.** The color coding follows the *physical signal nature*: blue = pure analog, orange = mixed/transition, green = pure digital. This matches the KTU examiner's preferred schematic convention. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is the Nyquist rate for a signal whose highest frequency component is 4.5 kHz? **Model Answer:** The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that *a band-limited continuous-time signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be uniquely and completely reconstructed from its samples, provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least twice the highest frequency component present in the signal*, i.e., $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. **Nyquist rate** for $f_{max} = 4.5$ kHz: $$ f_N = 2 \times 4.5 \text{ kHz} = 9 \text{ kHz} $$ Hence the signal must be sampled at $f_s \ge 9$ kHz to avoid aliasing. *(3 marks: 1 for theorem, 1 for formula, 1 for computation)* ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between Natural Sampling and Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold) Sampling. Why is the latter preferred in ADC-based systems? **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Natural Sampling | Flat-Top (S/H) Sampling | |-----------|------------------|--------------------------| | Sample shape | Follows the input waveform between samples (top of sample mirrors the curve) | Constant amplitude for entire sample duration $T$ | | Mathematical form | $x_s(t) = x_a(t)\cdot p(t)$ | $x_s(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n} x_a(nT)\,h(t-nT)$ where $h(t)$ is a rectangular pulse | | Frequency response | Scaled replicas of $X_a(f)$ with $\sin(\pi f \tau)/(\pi f \tau)$ envelope | Replicas weighted by $\mathrm{sinc}(fT)$ — introduces a known droop that can be equalized | | Implementation | Theoretically idealized switch with no hold capacitor | Practical: switch + hold capacitor + buffer | | ADC compatibility | Poor — signal changes during conversion | Excellent — stable voltage for full $T_{conv}$ | **Why preferred for ADC:** The ADC needs a *constant input voltage* during its conversion time $T_{conv}$ to avoid aperture error; flat-top sampling provides exactly this stable value. *(3 marks)* ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Analog Interface** in a real-time DSP system. Describe the role of the **anti-aliasing filter** and justify the choice of its cutoff frequency. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The Analog Interface in a real-time DSP system consists of three cascaded blocks preceding the DSP core: $$ \text{Analog Input} \rightarrow \text{Anti-Aliasing Filter} \rightarrow \text{Sample-and-Hold} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{DSP Core} $$ Optionally, a reconstruction chain follows the DSP: $$ \text{DSP Core} \rightarrow \text{DAC} \rightarrow \text{Reconstruction Filter} \rightarrow \text{Analog Output} $$ **Role of the Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF) (2 marks):** The AAF is a low-pass analog filter placed *before* the sampler. Its job is to **band-limit** the incoming analog signal so that *all* frequency components above $f_s/2$ are attenuated to a level below the ADC's quantization noise floor. Without the AAF, any out-of-band component (noise, harmonics, RF interference) would **fold back** into the baseband via aliasing and become *indistinguishable* from legitimate in-band content. **Choice of Cutoff Frequency (2 marks):** The cutoff frequency $f_c$ must satisfy: $$ f_c \le \frac{f_s}{2} $$ In practice, $f_c$ is chosen **slightly below** $f_s/2$ (e.g., at $0.45 f_s$ for a telephony codec sampling at 8 kHz, $f_c \approx 3.6$ kHz). This is because: 1. Real filters have a finite **transition band** — they don't drop from passband to stopband instantaneously. 2. The stopband attenuation must be **greater than the SQNR** of the ADC, otherwise aliased residual energy appears as quantization-level noise. 3. Common filter choices: **Butterworth** (maximally flat passband) or **Chebyshev Type-I** (steeper roll-off, small passband ripple). --- **(b)** A band-limited analog signal $x_a(t)$ has spectral support from DC to 5 kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 12$ kHz. **(i)** Sketch the spectrum of the sampled signal. **(ii)** Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. **(iii)** If the same signal is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz, what is the alias frequency of a 6.5 kHz tone present in it? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Spectrum sketch (3 marks):** The original spectrum $X_a(f)$ is a single lobe from $-5$ kHz to $+5$ kHz with peak amplitude, say, $A/T$. After sampling at $f_s = 12$ kHz, the spectrum is replicated every 12 kHz: - Replicas at $\pm 12$ kHz, $\pm 24$ kHz, $\pm 36$ kHz, ... - Since $f_s = 12$ kHz $> 2 \times 5$ kHz $= 10$ kHz, the replicas **do not overlap** (guard band = 2 kHz between adjacent replicas' edges). - An ideal low-pass filter of cutoff $f_c = 5$ kHz can recover $x_a(t)$ losslessly. **(ii) Minimum sampling rate (2 marks):** $$ f_{s,min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz} $$ **(iii) Alias frequency of 6.5 kHz tone at $f_s = 8$ kHz (2 marks):** First check: $f_s = 8$ kHz is *less* than the Nyquist rate ($2 \times 6.5 = 13$ kHz), so aliasing *will* occur. $$ \begin{aligned} k &= \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{6500}{8000}\right) = \mathrm{round}(0.8125) = 1 \\ f_{alias} &= \vert 6500 - 1 \cdot 8000 \vert = 1500 \text{ Hz} = 1.5 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned} $$ A 6.5 kHz tone will appear as a **1.5 kHz** tone after sampling at 8 kHz. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain the **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** with a circuit diagram. Discuss the role of the **hold capacitor** and the **aperture time**, and explain the significance of **aperture jitter**. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Circuit Description (3 marks):** A basic S/H circuit consists of: 1. An **analog switch** (a CMOS transmission gate or a JFET) controlled by a sampling clock. 2. A **hold capacitor** $C_H$ (typically 10 pF to 1 nF) connected from the switch output to ground. 3. A **high-input-impedance buffer amplifier** (often a unity-gain op-amp voltage follower) that presents $C_H$'s voltage to the ADC without loading it. 4. **Control logic** generating the sample/hold timing signal. **Operation Phases:** - **Sample phase:** The switch is closed; $C_H$ charges rapidly to the instantaneous input voltage through the switch's on-resistance $R_{on}$. The time constant is $\tau_s = R_{on} \cdot C_H$, and acquisition requires $\approx 7\tau_s$ to settle to 12-bit accuracy. - **Hold phase:** The switch opens; $C_H$ retains its voltage (ideally) while the ADC performs conversion. **Hold Capacitor Role (2 marks):** $C_H$ acts as a **voltage memory element**. Its key trade-off: - **Larger $C_H$** → less droop and less feedthrough, but longer acquisition time and lower slew rate. - **Smaller $C_H$** → faster acquisition, but more droop due to leakage current $I_{leak}$ and more $kT/C$ thermal noise. The droop rate is $\frac{dV}{dt} = I_{leak}/C_H$, which must produce a voltage change much smaller than 1 LSB ($\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B$) over the hold time $T_H$. **Aperture Time & Jitter (2 marks):** - **Aperture time** $\tau_a$ is the (small) time delay between the *command* to hold and the *actual* moment the switch opens. It is the time during which the signal is "frozen" for the ADC. - **Aperture jitter** $t_j$ is the *uncertainty* (random variation) in aperture time from sample to sample, caused by clock phase noise, switch driver slew variation, etc. - The resulting **voltage error** for an input sinusoid of frequency $f_{in}$ and peak amplitude $V_{pk}$ is: $$ \Delta V = 2\pi f_{in}\,V_{pk}\,t_j $$ - For high-frequency inputs, $t_j$ must be much smaller than $1/(2\pi f_{in})$ to maintain SNR. Example: at $f_{in} = 20$ kHz with $V_{pk} = 1$ V, keeping $\Delta V$ below 1 LSB of a 16-bit, 2 V$_{pp}$ ADC ($\Delta = 30.5\,\mu V$) requires $t_j < 0.24$ ns — a tough design constraint that drives the use of low-jitter crystal oscillators. --- **(b)** Describe the operation of a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** as used in the **DAC reconstruction** stage. Derive its frequency response and show that it introduces a **sinc-shaped amplitude droop** that must be corrected. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **ZOH Operation (2 marks):** A ZOH takes the discrete samples $x[n]$ from the DSP and holds each value constant for one sampling period $T$, producing a staircase approximation $\hat{x}(t)$ of the original analog signal. The DAC chip itself typically implements a ZOH internally. **Impulse Response (1 mark):** $$ h_{ZOH}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Frequency Response Derivation (3 marks):** Take the Fourier transform of the rectangular pulse: $$ \begin{aligned} H_{ZOH}(f) &= \int_{0}^{T} 1 \cdot e^{-j 2\pi f t}\,dt \\ &= \left[ \frac{e^{-j 2\pi f t}}{-j 2\pi f} \right]_{0}^{T} \\ &= \frac{1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T}}{j 2\pi f} \\ &= \frac{e^{-j\pi f T}\left(e^{j\pi f T} - e^{-j\pi f T}\right)}{j 2\pi f} \\ &= e^{-j\pi f T} \cdot \frac{2 j \sin(\pi f T)}{j 2\pi f} \\ &= T \cdot e^{-j\pi f T} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T)}{\pi f T} \\ &= T \cdot e^{-j\pi f T} \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(fT) \end{aligned} $$ **Magnitude Response (1 mark):** $$ \left\vert H_{ZOH}(f) \right\vert = T \cdot \left\vert \mathrm{sinc}(fT) \right\vert $$ **Analysis and Correction (1 mark — this is the KTU "explain" point):** - At $f = 0$: $\vert H_{ZOH}(0) \vert = T$ (unity DC gain after $1/T$ normalization). - At $f = f_s/2$: $\vert H_{ZOH}(f_s/2) \vert = T \cdot \sin(\pi/2)/(\pi/2) = 2T/\pi \approx 0.637\,T$ → a **-3.92 dB droop** at Nyquist. - The droop is a **sinc function** of frequency: $|H_{ZOH}(f)| = |\sin(\pi f T)/(\pi f T)|$. - To compensate, a **digital equalization filter** $H_{eq}(f)$ is placed *before* the DAC in the DSP, with response: $$ H_{eq}(f) = \frac{\pi f T}{\sin(\pi f T)} \quad \text{for } \vert f \vert < f_s/2 $$ This inverse-sinc filter restores flat passband response. The residual uncorrected errors are usually well below the system noise floor, and the sinc envelope *also* helps by attenuating the spectral images around $kf_s$, partially acting as a reconstruction filter (a separate analog LPF is still required to remove images above $f_s/2$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic (and how to avoid them):** > > 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate vs. Nyquist Frequency** — many students write $f_N = f_s/2$. The correct definition is: **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_{max}$ (a *required* sampling rate), **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s/2$ (a *bandwidth limit*). KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. (Lose 1 mark for swapping them.) > > 2. **Forgetting the $1/T$ scaling factor** in the frequency-domain expression $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T}\sum X_a(f - kf_s)$. The amplitude *decreases* as sampling rate *increases* — a counterintuitive but frequently asked point. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.) > > 3. **Aliasing formula sign error** — students compute $f_{alias} = f_0 + k f_s$ instead of the correct $f_{alias} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$. The absolute value is essential. (Lose 1 mark.) > > 4. **Omitting the "sinc" envelope** in the ZOH frequency response — many write only the magnitude and forget the linear phase $e^{-j\pi fT}$ (which represents a half-sample delay). Examiners often give a partial mark only. > > 5. **Drawing AAF *after* the S/H or ADC** — this is fundamentally wrong. The AAF must come *first* to prevent aliased components from corrupting subsequent stages. > > 6. **Saying "anti-aliasing filter cuts off at 22 kHz for CD audio"** without mentioning the transition band. Always state the filter is *analog* with a non-zero roll-off, and the cutoff is *slightly below* $f_s/2$. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion); $f_{max} \le f_s/2$ is the recoverable band. - **Ideal sampler:** Impulse-train modulation, modelled as $x_s(t) = \sum x_a(nT)\,\delta(t-nT)$. - **Spectrum replication:** $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_k X_a(f - kf_s)$ — periodic with period $f_s$. - **Anti-aliasing filter:** Analog LPF placed *first*; cutoff $\le f_s/2$; stopband attenuation must exceed ADC SQNR ($\approx 6.02B + 1.76$ dB). - **Sample-and-Hold:** Switch + hold capacitor + buffer; stabilizes voltage during ADC conversion. - **Aperture time $\tau_a$:** Delay from hold command to actual hold. **Aperture jitter $t_j$:** Random variation, causes error $\Delta V = 2\pi f_{in} V_{pk} t_j$. - **Hold capacitor droop:** $dV/dt = I_{leak}/C_H$; must be $\ll 1$ LSB over hold time. - **Quantization:** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^B$; SQNR $\approx 6.02B + 1.76$ dB. - **ZOH reconstruction:** Holds each sample for $T$ seconds; impulse response is a rectangle; frequency response is $T \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(fT) \cdot e^{-j\pi fT}$; -3.92 dB droop at $f_s/2$. - **ZOH compensation:** Digital inverse-sinc filter $H_{eq}(f) = \pi fT / \sin(\pi fT)$ flattens passband. - **Aliasing frequency formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f_0 - kf_s \vert$ where $k = \mathrm{round}(f_0/f_s)$. - **Oversampling** (using $f_s \gg 2 f_{max}$) relaxes analog filter design, improves SQNR (4× oversampling → +6 dB), and allows Δ-Σ converters. - **Block chain to memorize:** Sensor → AAF → S/H → ADC → DSP → DAC → Reconstruction Filter → Actuator. - **Standard rates to remember:** 8 kHz (voice), 44.1 kHz (CD audio), 48 kHz (pro audio), 22.05 kHz (half-CD), 96/192 kHz (high-res audio). - **Nyquist Rate ≠ Nyquist Frequency** — one is a rate requirement, the other is a frequency limit. KTU loves this distinction. - **Practical rule of thumb:** $f_s$ should be **2.5× to 4× $f_{max}$** in real systems, not exactly 2×, to allow filter transition bands.

topics.title: Quantization and Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quantization and Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Quantization?

In **Real-Time Digital Signal Processing (RT-DSP)**, a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal must be converted into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal before it can be processed by a Digital Signal Processor. The **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** performs this task in three fundamental stages:

1. **Sampling** – Discretization in time (governed by Nyquist Theorem).
2. **Quantization** – Discretization in amplitude (governed by the resolution of the ADC).
3. **Encoding** – Assignment of a unique binary codeword to each discrete amplitude level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Quantization** is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude (infinite-precision) sampled signal $x(nT_s)$ onto a finite set of discrete amplitude levels using a **Quantizer** $Q(\cdot)$. The output $x_q(nT_s)$ belongs to a finite alphabet of $L = 2^B$ levels, where $B$ is the number of bits per sample of the ADC.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase" and the "Ruler"

Imagine you are measuring the length of a table using a **ruler that has markings only every 1 cm**.
- The **true length** could be `124.378 cm` (continuous amplitude).
- The **ruler forces you to round** to the nearest marking: `124 cm` (quantized amplitude).

The difference between the actual length and the rounded length is the **Quantization Error** $e_q$. Now imagine instead of a 1 cm ruler you have a ruler with markings every 1 mm. You get a more accurate reading, but it takes more "symbols" (cm vs. mm) to record the answer.

**In DSP terms:**
- The **1 cm spacing** corresponds to a **large step size** $\Delta$ (low-resolution ADC, e.g., 4-bit).
- The **1 mm spacing** corresponds to a **small step size** $\Delta$ (high-resolution ADC, e.g., 16-bit).
- More bits $B$ → smaller step size → finer quantization → higher fidelity.

## 1.3 Why is Quantization Necessary?

Digital systems (memory, processors, communication channels) can only store and manipulate **finite-precision binary words**. Since an analog sample can take *infinitely many* values in a continuous range $[-X_{max}, +X_{max}]$, it must be approximated to one of a *finite* set of representable levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Sampling introduces aliasing (a *frequency-domain* problem), whereas Quantization introduces **Quantization Noise** (an *amplitude-domain* problem). Unlike sampling error, quantization error is **irreversible and non-recoverable** in practical real-time systems.

## 1.4 Quantization and Encoding — The Pair

Once the quantizer maps a sample to a discrete level, the **encoder** assigns a unique **binary codeword** (e.g., Natural Binary, Gray code, Two's Complement, Sign-Magnitude) to that level. The pair $(Q, E)$ forms the **ADC** in any DSP front-end.

The full block-flow is:

$$
\underbrace{x_a(t)}_{\text{Analog}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Sampling}}\; \underbrace{x(nT_s)}_{\text{Discrete-time}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Quantization } Q(\cdot)}\; \underbrace{x_q(nT_s)}_{\text{Discrete-amplitude}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Encoding}}\; \underbrace{b_0 b_1 \dots b_{B-1}}_{\text{Binary codeword}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Quantizers

### 2.1.1 Uniform (Linear) Quantizer
The quantization step size $\Delta$ is **constant** across the entire input range. Two subclasses exist:

- **Mid-Tread Quantizer**: Has a zero-output level (dead zone). Used when the signal frequently crosses zero.
- **Mid-Rise Quantizer**: No zero output; the transfer characteristic is symmetric but offset by $\Delta/2$.

For a **uniform mid-rise quantizer** with input range $[-X_{max}, +X_{max}]$ and $B$ bits:

$$
\text{Number of levels: } L = 2^B
$$

$$
\text{Step size: } \Delta = \frac{2 X_{max}}{L} = \frac{2 X_{max}}{2^B}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these two subtypes?**
> KTU frequently asks: *"Which quantizer avoids a dead zone near zero?"* — The answer is **Mid-Rise**, since it always outputs one of $L$ levels with no idle zero interval. Mid-tread is preferred for signals with high DC content (e.g., biomedical signals) because of its symmetric idle zone.

### 2.1.2 Non-Uniform Quantizer
The step size $\Delta$ **varies** with signal amplitude — typically smaller for low-amplitude signals and larger for high-amplitude signals. This is essential for compressing signals like **speech**, which exhibits a wide dynamic range.

Standard implementations:
- **$\mu$-law companding** (used in North American/Japanese telecom): $y = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$
- **A-law companding** (used in Europe/India): piecewise logarithmic.

## 2.2 Quantization Error Model

The quantizer $Q$ is modeled as adding a **noise signal** $e_q(n)$ to the original sample:

$$
x_q(n) = x(n) + e_q(n)
$$

The error is defined as:

$$
e_q(n) = x_q(n) - x(n)
$$

### Assumptions for the Statistical Model (KTU High-Yield):
1. The error $e_q(n)$ is **uniformly distributed** in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
2. The error is **uncorrelated** with the input signal $x(n)$ (assumption holds when the signal is "busy" — i.e., spans many levels).
3. Samples of $e_q(n)$ are **uncorrelated** with each other → modeled as **white noise**.

Under these assumptions, we can compute the mean and variance of the error.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of quantization levels | $L$ | $L = 2^B$ | dimensionless |
| Step size (mid-rise) | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = 2X_{max} / 2^B$ | Volts (V) |
| Peak-to-Peak input range | $X_{pp}$ | $X_{pp} = 2 X_{max}$ | Volts (V) |
| Quantization error range | $e_q$ | $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ | Volts (V) |
| Mean of quantization error | $\mu_{e_q}$ | $0$ | V (zero-mean) |
| Variance of quantization error (Quantization Noise Power) | $\sigma_{e_q}^2$ | $\Delta^2 / 12$ | V² (Watt load) |
| Quantization Noise Power (signal load) | $P_{q}$ | $\Delta^2 / 12$ | W (into 1 Ω) |
| Signal Power (full-scale sinusoid) | $P_{s}$ | $X_{max}^2 / 2$ | W |
| Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR}$ | $P_s / P_q$ | dimensionless (ratio) |
| SQNR in dB (full-scale sine input) | $\text{SQNR}_{dB}$ | $6.02\,B + 1.76$ | dB |
| SQNR improvement per added bit | — | $\approx 6.02$ dB | dB/bit |
| SNR threshold for telephony | — | $\geq 26$ dB (4-bit $\mu$-law) | dB |
| SNR threshold for CD-quality audio | — | $\geq 96$ dB (16-bit) | dB |
| Output bit-rate of ADC | $R_b$ | $R_b = B \cdot f_s$ | bits/second (bps) |
| $\mu$-law compression | $y$ | $\text{sgn}(x) \cdot \ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)/\ln(1+\mu)$ | $\mu = 255$ (US standard) |
| A-law compression | $y$ | Piecewise linear-log | $A = 87.6$ (European) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "6 dB per bit" rule is a board-exam favorite.** Every additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**. Memorize this.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio CD**: 16-bit linear quantizer at $f_s = 44.1$ kHz → SQNR $\approx 98$ dB → exceeds human ear dynamic range (~96 dB).
- **Telephony (PSTN)**: 8-bit $\mu$-law (255) companded quantizer at 8 kHz → ~64 kbps bitstream.
- **Instrumentation ADCs**: 24-bit $\Sigma$-Delta quantizers for seismic and biomedical signals.
- **Radar / SDR**: 12–14-bit high-speed quantizers for IF sampling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean of Quantization Error

We assume the error is uniformly distributed over a single quantization interval of width $\Delta$.

**Step 1:** State the probability density function of $e_q$:

$$
p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for } -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le +\frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the definition of the expected value (mean) of $e_q$:

$$
\mu_{e_q} = E\{e_q\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q) \, d e_q
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the uniform PDF:

$$
\mu_{e_q} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, d e_q
$$

**Step 4:** Pull the constant $1/\Delta$ out and integrate (the integrand $e_q$ is an odd function over a symmetric interval):

$$
\mu_{e_q} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^2}{2} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left( \frac{\Delta^2}{8} - \frac{\Delta^2}{8} \right) = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** The quantization error is **zero-mean** — the quantizer is an unbiased estimator of the sample value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance (Noise Power) of Quantization Error

**Step 1:** Write the variance definition:

$$
\sigma_{e_q}^2 = E\{(e_q - \mu_{e_q})^2\} = E\{e_q^2\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, d e_q
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the uniform PDF:

$$
\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, d e_q
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \left( \frac{\Delta}{2} \right)^3
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$
\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{8} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **quantization noise power** is exactly $\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}}$, independent of the signal statistics (under the uniform-error assumption).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoid

Consider a full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = X_{max} \sin(2\pi f t)$ with amplitude $X_{max}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the average signal power of the sinusoid across a load $R = 1\,\Omega$:

$$
P_s = \frac{X_{max}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Recall the quantization noise power:

$$
P_q = \sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{X_{max}^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{12 \cdot X_{max}^2}{2 \cdot \Delta^2} = \frac{6 \cdot X_{max}^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\Delta = 2X_{max} / 2^B$:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{6 \cdot X_{max}^2}{(2X_{max}/2^B)^2} = \frac{6 \cdot X_{max}^2 \cdot 2^{2B}}{4 \cdot X_{max}^2} = \frac{6}{4} \cdot 2^{2B} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2B}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to decibels using $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2B}) = 1.76 + 20B \log_{10}(2)
$$

**Step 6:** Since $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$:

$$
\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,B \;\; \text{dB}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The famous **"6 dB per bit"** rule. Each additional bit of resolution improves SNR by exactly **6.02 dB**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bit-Rate of the ADC

**Step 1:** Each sample produces $B$ bits.

**Step 2:** Samples are produced at rate $f_s = 1/T_s$:

$$
R_b = B \cdot f_s \quad [\text{bits/second}]
$$

**Step 3:** For a CD-quality stereo system: $B = 16$, $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, 2 channels:

$$
R_b = 16 \times 44100 \times 2 = 1{,}411{,}200 \text{ bps} \approx 1.41 \text{ Mbps}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 10-bit uniform mid-rise ADC has a full-scale range of $\pm 5$ V. Compute (a) step size $\Delta$, (b) quantization noise power $P_q$, and (c) SQNR for a full-scale sinusoid.

**Solution (a):**

$$
L = 2^{10} = 1024, \quad \Delta = \frac{2 X_{max}}{L} = \frac{2 \times 5}{1024} = \frac{10}{1024} \approx 9.77 \text{ mV}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
P_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(9.77 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = \frac{9.54 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \approx 7.95 \,\mu\text{W}
$$

**Solution (c):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 10 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

---

## 3.6 Encoding Schemes — Comparative Table

| **Encoding Scheme** | **$B = 4$ Code for $-8$ to $+7$** | **Advantage** | **Disadvantage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Binary (Unsigned)** | $0000$–$1111$ for $0$–$15$ | Simple, fast | Cannot represent negatives |
| **Sign-Magnitude** | 1 sign bit + 3 magnitude bits | Easy human reading | Two codes for zero |
| **Two's Complement** | $-8 = 1000$, $-1 = 1111$, $0 = 0000$ | Single zero, easy arithmetic | Asymmetric range |
| **Offset Binary** | $0$–$15$ mapped to $-8$ to $+7$ | Used in Flash ADCs | Awkward arithmetic |
| **Gray Code** | Adjacent levels differ in 1 bit | **Minimizes bit-error glitches** | Slower arithmetic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The encoder does NOT affect the SQNR mathematically. The choice of code affects **hardware complexity**, **error propagation** in transmission, and **glitch energy** in DAC reconstruction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Quantization as Additive Noise Model

The quantizer is statistically modeled as a noise-injection system. This is the canonical **"Quantization Noise Model"** used throughout DSP textbooks and KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x of n - Original Sampled Signal"] --> C["Adder Junction"]
    B["e of n - Quantization Noise Source\n Uniform in -Delta half to +Delta half\n Mean = 0\n Variance = Delta squared over 12"] --> C
    C --> D["x q of n - Quantized Signal"]
    D --> E["Encoder Block\n Maps Level to B bit Codeword"]
    E --> F["b0 b1 ... bB-1\n Binary Output Stream"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The original sample $x(n)$ and the noise $e_q(n)$ are summed at the junction to produce the quantized output $x_q(n)$, which is then mapped to a binary codeword by the encoder. This is a *signal-flow* abstraction, not a physical connection.

---

## 4.2 Mid-Rise vs. Mid-Tread Quantizer Transfer Characteristics

The transfer characteristic $x_q$ vs. $x$ of a uniform quantizer is a **staircase function**. Two common variants are used:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MIDRISE["MID-RISE QUANTIZER - No dead zone, levels at +- Delta half, +- 3 Delta half ..."]
        MR1["Input x range -Xmax to +Xmax"]
        MR2["Step size Delta constant"]
        MR3["Output levels at 0, +- Delta, +- 2 Delta ..."]
        MR4["No zero level exactly unless n is even"]
    end

    subgraph MIDTREAD["MID-TREAD QUANTIZER - Dead zone around zero, levels at 0, +- Delta, +- 2 Delta ..."]
        MT1["Input x range -Xmax to +Xmax"]
        MT2["Step size Delta constant"]
        MT3["Output levels include 0 exactly"]
        MT4["Idle zone of width Delta around origin"]
    end

    MR1 --> MR2 --> MR3 --> MR4
    MT1 --> MT2 --> MT3 --> MT4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Staircase key takeaways:**
> - **Mid-Rise** ⇒ origin lies in the middle of a *riser* (vertical jump) ⇒ no output is exactly zero.
> - **Mid-Tread** ⇒ origin lies in the middle of a *tread* (flat step) ⇒ output can be exactly zero, giving a dead zone of width $\Delta$.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Complete ADC Front-End

This is the standard real-time DSP front-end showing the role of quantization and encoding within a full system:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Analog Input\n xa of t"] --> A2["Anti Alias LPF\n -Cutoff at fs over 2-"]
    A2 --> A3["Sample and Hold\n S and H"]
    A3 --> A4["Uniform Quantizer\n Q of dot\n L = 2 power B levels"]
    A4 --> A5["Encoder\n Natural Binary\n or Gray Code"]
    A5 --> A6["Digital Output\n B bit word at rate fs"]
    A6 --> A7["DSP Processor\n PBCPT504 Core"]
    A7 --> A8["DAC\n Reconstruction"]
    A8 --> A9["Reconstructed Analog\n y of t"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-function note for KTU students:** The Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit must hold the analog voltage *constant* during the quantization interval, otherwise the quantizer sees a *time-varying* value and produces a *dynamic* error in addition to the static quantization error.

---

## 4.4 $\mu$-Law Companding Block Flow

The non-uniform quantizer is realized by combining a **compressor** (at the transmitter) with a uniform quantizer, then an **expander** (at the receiver). The pair is called a **Compander**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input x"] --> C1["Compressor\n mu law non linearity\n y = sgn x times log of 1 plus mu times mod x"]
    C1 --> UQ["Uniform Quantizer\n B = 8 bits typically\n Step Delta uniform"]
    UQ --> CH["Channel\n Transmission"]
    CH --> UDEQ["Uniform Dequantizer"]
    UDEQ --> E1["Expander\n Inverse mu law"]
    E1 --> Y["Output approx x"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define quantization. Distinguish between uniform and non-uniform quantization.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Quantization is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude sampled signal onto a finite set of $L = 2^B$ discrete amplitude levels using an ADC's quantizer block $Q(\cdot)$. The output $x_q(n)$ differs from the input $x(n)$ by the quantization error $e_q(n) = x_q(n) - x(n)$.

- **Uniform Quantization:** Step size $\Delta$ is constant across the entire input range. Simple to implement, used in ADCs with linear transfer functions. The quantization noise variance is constant: $\sigma^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$.
- **Non-Uniform Quantization:** Step size $\Delta$ varies with input amplitude — finer resolution for small signals, coarser for large signals. Achieved via companding ($\mu$-law, A-law). Used in speech telephony to maintain perceptual SQNR across the wide dynamic range of human voice.

---

### **Q2.** Why does increasing the number of bits $B$ by 1 improve the SQNR by approximately 6 dB?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
For a uniform $B$-bit quantizer with full-scale sinusoidal input, the SQNR in dB is:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,B
$$

When $B$ increases by 1 (say from $B$ to $B+1$):

- The number of levels doubles: $L \to 2L$.
- The step size halves: $\Delta \to \Delta/2$.
- The quantization noise power $P_q = \Delta^2 / 12$ is reduced by a factor of 4 (i.e., 6.02 dB).
- The signal power is unchanged.

Therefore, the ratio $P_s / P_q$ increases by a factor of 4, which corresponds to $10 \log_{10}(4) \approx 6.02$ dB improvement in SQNR. This is the basis of the **"6 dB per bit" rule**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
