> 1. **Forgetting to convert** between linear ratio and dB when writing the SQNR formula — students often write $\text{SQNR} = 6.02B$ instead of $1.76 + 6.02B$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Mixing up mid-rise and mid-tread** step-size formulas. Mid-rise uses $2X_{max}/2^B$; some students write $X_{max}/2^B$ — **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring the uniform-error assumption.** Always state it before computing $\sigma^2 = \Delta^2/12$. **Lose 1 mark** in derivations.
> 4. **Using linear power** instead of dB when comparing systems. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. For companding questions, **forgetting to mention that companding is a transmitter–receiver (compressor–expander) pair**, not just a single block.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Q. A (a) 7 marks + Q. A (b) 7 marks*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern Question]**

**Q. A (a) [7 marks]:** A 12-bit uniform mid-rise ADC quantizes a full-scale sinusoidal signal with peak amplitude $X_{max} = 4$ V. Compute (i) the step size, (ii) the quantization noise power, and (iii) the SQNR in dB. State clearly the assumptions made in computing the quantization noise power.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Step size:**

$$
L = 2^{12} = 4096 \quad \text{levels}
$$

$$
\Delta = \frac{2 X_{max}}{L} = \frac{2 \times 4}{4096} = \frac{8}{4096} \approx 1.953 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) Quantization noise power** (assuming uniform distribution of error in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, zero-mean, white):

$$
P_q = \sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(1.953 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = \frac{3.815 \times 10^{-6}}{12} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W}
$$

**(iii) SQNR for full-scale sinusoid:**

Signal power: $P_s = X_{max}^2 / 2 = 16 / 2 = 8$ W (into $R = 1\ \Omega$).

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{8}{3.18 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.516 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(2.516 \times 10^{7}) \approx 74.01 \text{ dB}
$$

**Cross-check using formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 12 = 1.76 + 72.24 = 74.00$ dB. ✓

**Assumptions stated:**
1. Quantization error is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
2. Error is zero-mean and uncorrelated with the input signal.
3. Successive error samples are uncorrelated (white noise).
4. Signal spans the full-scale range (no clipping).

---

**Q. A (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the $\mu$-law companding scheme used in digital telephony. Why is non-uniform quantization necessary for speech signals? Show the compression expression.

**Model Solution:**

**Necessity:** Human speech has a dynamic range of approximately 50–60 dB. A uniform quantizer would allocate equal step sizes to loud and soft passages, leading to **poor SQNR for low-amplitude consonants** (e.g., 's', 't', 'f'). Non-uniform quantization provides **finer resolution for low amplitudes** and coarser resolution for high amplitudes, giving a perceptually uniform SNR.

**$\mu$-Law Compression Expression:**

$$
y = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}
$$

where $x \in [-1, +1]$ is the normalized input, $y$ is the compressed output, and $\mu = 255$ is the standard value for North American/Japanese PCM telephony.

**Block flow (already shown in Section 4.4):** Compressor $\to$ Uniform Quantizer (8-bit, $f_s = 8$ kHz) $\to$ Channel $\to$ Uniform Dequantizer $\to$ Expander.

**Standard bit-rate:** $R_b = 8 \times 8000 = 64$ kbps per voice channel (the E1/T1 PCM standard).

**SQNR gain:** 8-bit $\mu$-law PCM achieves $\sim$38 dB SQNR, equivalent to a 13-bit linear system — a saving of 5 bits per sample.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern Question]**

**Q. B (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the expression for the quantization noise power of a uniform $B$-bit mid-rise quantizer, and show that the SQNR for a full-scale sinusoidal input is $1.76 + 6.02\,B$ dB.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mid-rise quantizer with $L = 2^B$ levels and full-scale range $\pm X_{max}$:

$$
\Delta = \frac{2 X_{max}}{2^B} = 2^{1-B} X_{max}
$$

**Step 2:** Error model: $x_q(n) = x(n) + e_q(n)$, with $e_q$ uniform in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, zero-mean, white.

**Step 3:** Quantization noise power:

$$
P_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, d e_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Step 4:** Signal power of full-scale sinusoid: $P_s = X_{max}^2 / 2$.

**Step 5:** SQNR ratio:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{X_{max}^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{6 X_{max}^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\Delta = 2 X_{max} / 2^B$:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{6 X_{max}^2 \cdot 2^{2B}}{4 X_{max}^2} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2B}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to dB:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20B \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\,B \;\; \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Proven. The "6 dB per bit" rule emerges naturally.

---

**Q. B (b) [7 marks]:** Compare uniform, non-uniform ($\mu$-law), and A-law quantization. List the values of $\mu$ and $A$ used in standards. State the applications where each is preferred.

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **Uniform** | **$\mu$-Law** | **A-Law** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression | None (linear) | Logarithmic, smooth | Piecewise linear-log |
| Compression parameter | — | $\mu = 255$ | $A = 87.6$ |
| Typical bits | 12–16 | 8 | 8 |
| Standard bit-rate | Variable | 64 kbps (8k×8) | 64 kbps (8k×8) |
| Dynamic range | Limited | High (~38 dB SQNR) | High (~38 dB SQNR) |
| Origin passing through zero? | Depends (mid-rise/mid-tread) | No (smooth, offset) | No (smooth, offset) |
| Application | CD audio, instrumentation, SDR | North American/Japanese PSTN | European/Indian PSTN |
| Complexity | Lowest | Medium | Medium |
| Equivalent linear bits | $B$ | $B + 5 \approx 13$ | $B + 5 \approx 13$ |

**Key Differences:**
- $\mu$-law has a **smooth logarithmic curve**; A-law uses **13 linear segments** approximating a logarithm.
- Both achieve perceptually uniform SQNR across the speech dynamic range.
- A-law has a defined linear segment for small inputs, avoiding infinite slope at origin (better for very low amplitudes).
- The $\mu$-law compressor is continuous and differentiable; the A-law is piecewise.

**Preference:**
- **Uniform** → high-fidelity audio (CD, DVD, Blu-ray), instrumentation, radar, software-defined radio.
- **$\mu$-law** → legacy North American/Japanese telecom (T1 carrier).
- **A-law** → European/Indian telecom (E1 carrier), ISDN, modern VoIP gateways.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this list and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Quantization** is discretization of *amplitude*; **Sampling** is discretization of *time*. Do not confuse them.
- [ ] Number of levels $L = 2^B$, where $B$ is the number of bits.
- [ ] **Step size (mid-rise):** $\Delta = 2X_{max} / 2^B = 2^{1-B} X_{max}$.
- [ ] **Mid-tread quantizer** has a zero-output level (dead zone); **mid-rise** does not.
- [ ] **Quantization error** $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ is **zero-mean**.
- [ ] **Quantization noise power** $P_q = \Delta^2 / 12$ (uniform-error assumption).
- [ ] **Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (full-scale sinusoid):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,B$.
- [ ] **"6 dB per bit" rule:** each extra bit improves SQNR by $\approx 6$ dB.
- [ ] **Signal power (sine):** $P_s = X_{max}^2 / 2$; **DC:** $P_s = A^2$.
- [ ] **Bit-rate of ADC:** $R_b = B \cdot f_s$ bits/second.
- [ ] **$\mu$-law companding** uses $\mu = 255$; smooth logarithmic compression.
- [ ] **A-law companding** uses $A = 87.6$; piecewise linear (13 segments).
- [ ] 8-bit $\mu$-law PCM ≈ 13-bit linear performance for speech.
- [ ] Standard PCM telephony: 8 bits/sample × 8 kHz = **64 kbps** per channel.
- [ ] **Quantization noise is irreversible** — unlike sampling, it cannot be removed by reconstruction.
- [ ] **Encoder** does NOT alter SQNR; it affects hardware complexity and transmission error sensitivity.
- [ ] **Gray code** is preferred for Flash ADCs because adjacent-level errors cause only 1-bit glitches.
- [ ] For **16-bit CD audio at 44.1 kHz**, SQNR $\approx 98$ dB ≈ human hearing limit.
- [ ] $\Sigma$-Delta (oversampled) quantizers trade bit-rate for resolution, using **noise-shaping** filters to push quantization noise out of the signal band.
- [ ] KTU expects derivation of $\sigma^2 = \Delta^2/12$ with **explicit integral steps** — do not skip.
- [ ] Always state the **3 statistical assumptions** (uniform, zero-mean, white) before any noise-power calculation.

---

> **END OF NOTES — QUANTIZATION AND ENCODING (MODULE 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoothing Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoothing Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoothing Filter:** A linear time-invariant (LTI) discrete-time system whose magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is monotonically non-increasing for $\omega \in [0, \pi]$, passing low-frequency signal components (the **smooth trend**) while attenuating high-frequency components (the **noise**). It is mathematically the discrete-time equivalent of an *integrator* or *averager*.

In the context of real-time DSP, a smoothing filter is any **low-pass digital filter** applied to a sampled data stream, sample-by-sample, with the explicit purpose of suppressing short-term fluctuations and revealing the underlying long-term trend of the signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PBCPT504 / Module 1):** Smoothing filters are introduced as a primitive class of *pre-processing* operators in real-time DSP. They are the gateway concept to FIR and IIR filter design, convolution, and difference-equation realization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching the **stock price of a company** on a screen that updates every second:

- The **raw price** flickers wildly every second — small jumps up and down due to micro-trades.
- The **smoothed price** (e.g., a 5-minute average) drifts slowly, hiding the noise.

That visual averaging is exactly what a smoothing filter does in software. Mathematically, it computes a *weighted local average* of recent samples. Physically, it acts like a **low-pass RC filter** in analog electronics — but operating on discrete numbers at each clock tick of the DSP processor.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following quantities are **boldly highlighted** because they are universally expected in any KTU 2024 valuation key:

- **Cutoff Frequency ($\omega_c$):** the angular frequency at which $\vert H(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of the DC gain (the **$-3\text{ dB}$ point**).
- **Passband Ripple ($\delta_p$):** maximum allowed deviation in the passband, typically in **dB**.
- **Stopband Attenuation ($\delta_s$):** minimum attenuation in the stopband, typically in **dB**.
- **Transition Width ($\Delta \omega$):** the bandwidth between passband edge and stopband edge.
- **Group Delay ($\tau_g$):** the time delay experienced by each frequency component, a critical metric for *real-time* systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-time consequence of group delay:** A symmetric FIR smoothing filter has a *constant* group delay equal to $(N-1)/2$ samples. This is the reason why DSP textbooks prefer symmetric (linear-phase) moving-average filters for real-time instrumentation — every frequency is delayed by exactly the same amount, preserving the *shape* of the waveform.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Smoothing Filters

Smoothing filters are classified along two orthogonal axes: **(a) impulse response length** (FIR vs IIR) and **(b) coefficient symmetry** (uniform vs weighted). The four most common forms encountered in KTU examinations are:

1. **Simple Moving Average (SMA) — Uniform FIR**
   - Also called the *boxcar* or *rectangular-window* filter.
   - Coefficients are all equal: $h[n] = \frac{1}{N}$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$.
   - $N$ is the **window length** (number of samples averaged).

2. **Weighted Moving Average (WMA) — Non-uniform FIR**
   - Coefficients are symmetric and triangular (or any prescribed shape).
   - $h[n] = w[n]$ with $\sum w[n] = 1$.

3. **Exponential Smoothing (ES) — First-order IIR**
   - $y[n] = \alpha \, x[n] + (1 - \alpha)\, y[n-1]$, with $0 < \alpha < 1$.
   - Equivalent to an *infinite* weighted average with exponentially decaying weights.

4. **Cascaded Smoothing — Higher-order IIR**
   - $H(z) = \left(\frac{1 - \alpha}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}}\right)^K$, where $K$ is the cascade order.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Why and How

- **Why smooth?** Sensor noise (thermal, shot, quantization) is broadband and high-frequency. The *signal of interest* is typically band-limited to a much lower frequency. Smoothing exploits this spectral separation.
- **How?** Averaging $N$ independent noisy samples reduces the noise standard deviation by $\sqrt{N}$ (a direct consequence of the Central Limit Theorem), while the *deterministic* DC/low-frequency component is preserved.
- **Cost?** Group delay, transient smearing, and — for IIR types — possible instability or limit cycles.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU 2024 numerical on smoothing filters. Memorize it.

| Filter Type | Difference Equation | Transfer Function $H(z)$ | Impulse Response $h[n]$ | DC Gain $H(e^{j0})$ | 3-dB Cutoff (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform MA (length $N$)** | $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]$ | $H(z) = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$ | $h[n] = \frac{1}{N}, \; 0 \le n \le N-1$ | $1$ | $\omega_c \approx \frac{2.18}{N}$ rad/sample |
| **Exponential Smoothing** | $y[n] = \alpha x[n] + (1-\alpha)y[n-1]$ | $H(z) = \frac{\alpha}{1 - (1-\alpha)z^{-1}}$ | $h[n] = \alpha(1-\alpha)^n u[n]$ | $1$ | $\omega_c \approx \alpha$ rad/sample (for small $\alpha$) |
| **Weighted MA (triangular)** | $y[n] = \frac{2}{N(N+1)}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}(N-k)x[n-k]$ | $H(z) = \frac{2}{N(N+1)}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}\cdot$ triangular window factor | Triangular | $1$ | $\omega_c \approx \frac{3.27}{N}$ rad/sample |
| **Cascaded First-Order ($K$ stages)** | $y[n] = \alpha x[n] + (1-\alpha)y[n-1]$ iterated $K$ times | $H(z) = \left(\frac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)z^{-1}}\right)^K$ | $\alpha^K \binom{n+K-1}{n}(1-\alpha)^n u[n]$ | $1$ | $\omega_c \approx \alpha \sqrt{2^{1/K}-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used in the table:**
> - $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes magnitude; written in LaTeX as `\vert` to avoid markdown table breakage.
> - $u[n]$ is the **unit step** function.
> - $\binom{n+K-1}{n}$ is the binomial coefficient.
> - All transfer functions are in terms of the **delay operator** $z^{-1}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smoothing filters are the workhorse of **real-time instrumentation and control**:

- **Biomedical DSP:** removing 50/60 Hz power-line interference and high-frequency EMG noise from ECG signals.
- **Audio DSP:** de-essing, de-clipping pre-processing, and DC-blocking.
- **Industrial control:** low-pass filtering of tachometer and accelerometer feedback before feeding the PID controller.
- **Telecommunications:** matched filtering of baseband pulses before symbol decision.
- **Embedded sensor nodes (IoT):** running average on temperature, pressure, or humidity readings to reduce radio transmission payload (only the smoothed value is sent).

The **single most decisive engineering trade-off** is between *smoothing strength* (large $N$ or small $\alpha$) and *responsiveness* (small group delay). The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes this trade-off in the design questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of the Uniform Moving Average Filter

**Statement:** Show that a length-$N$ uniform moving average filter has the transfer function

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

and the magnitude response

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We start from the convolution definition of the filter. By the definition of the moving average, the output at time $n$ is the arithmetic mean of the current and the previous $N-1$ input samples.

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$

This is the *convolution* of $x[n]$ with the rectangular pulse $h[n] = \frac{1}{N}$ supported on $0 \le n \le N-1$. To convert to the $z$-domain, we take the Z-transform of both sides and use the linearity and time-shift properties.

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}\, X(z) = \frac{X(z)}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}$$

The bracketed sum is a finite geometric series. We apply the closed-form identity $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} r^k = \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$ with $r = z^{-1}$.

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k} = \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

Substituting this back into the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$ yields the transfer function.

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

This is the **standard textbook form** of the boxcar filter. To find the magnitude response, we evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$ and use Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$.

$$\left\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega N} \right\vert = \left\vert e^{-j\omega N/2}\left(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2}\right) \right\vert = 2\left\vert \sin\left(\frac{\omega N}{2}\right) \right\vert$$

$$\left\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega} \right\vert = 2\left\vert \sin\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \right\vert$$

Taking the ratio and dividing by $N$ gives the **Dirichlet kernel** form of the magnitude response.

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\frac{\vert \sin(\omega N/2) \vert}{\vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC gain verification:** Setting $\omega = 0$, we have $\sin(0) = 0$ in both numerator and denominator, so L'Hôpital's rule (or the limit $\lim_{x\to 0} \sin(Nx)/\sin(x) = N$) gives $\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 1$. The filter preserves the DC component perfectly, as required for any sensible smoother.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Response of the First-Order Exponential Smoother

**Statement:** Show that the IIR smoother

$$y[n] = \alpha x[n] + (1 - \alpha) y[n-1]$$

has the steady-state DC gain of 1, the $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff at $\omega_c = \alpha$ rad/sample (small-$\alpha$ approximation), and a step response that rises monotonically without overshoot.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We Z-transform both sides of the difference equation. By linearity, and using the time-shift property $y[n-1] \leftrightarrow z^{-1}Y(z)$:

$$Y(z) = \alpha X(z) + (1 - \alpha) z^{-1} Y(z)$$

Rearranging to isolate $Y(z)$ on the left:

$$Y(z)\left[1 - (1 - \alpha) z^{-1}\right] = \alpha X(z)$$

Dividing, we obtain the transfer function.

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - (1 - \alpha) z^{-1}}$$

To study the frequency response, set $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\alpha}{1 - (1 - \alpha) e^{-j\omega}}$$

The magnitude squared (which is needed for power-spectrum analysis) is:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = \frac{\alpha^2}{1 + (1 - \alpha)^2 - 2(1 - \alpha)\cos\omega}$$

To find the $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff, we set $\vert H(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2}$ (since the DC gain is 1). This produces the implicit equation:

$$\frac{\alpha^2}{1 + (1-\alpha)^2 - 2(1-\alpha)\cos\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Solving for $\cos\omega_c$:

$$\cos\omega_c = \frac{2\alpha^2 - 1 - (1-\alpha)^2}{2(1-\alpha)} = \frac{2\alpha^2 - 1 - 1 + 2\alpha - \alpha^2}{2(1-\alpha)} = \frac{\alpha^2 + 2\alpha - 2}{2(1-\alpha)}$$

For small $\alpha$ (which is the regime where the smoother is effective), we expand $\cos\omega_c \approx 1 - \omega_c^2/2$ and $(1-\alpha) \approx 1$, leading to the well-known engineering approximation.

$$\omega_c \approx \alpha \quad \text{(rad/sample, for } \alpha \ll 1\text{)}$$

For the step response, we feed $x[n] = u[n]$. By the final-value theorem (assuming stability, i.e., $\vert 1 - \alpha \vert < 1$):

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} y[n] = \lim_{z \to 1}(1 - z^{-1}) H(z) \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = H(1) = \frac{\alpha}{1 - (1 - \alpha)} = 1$$

So the output rises from 0 to 1 in steady state. The transient is governed by the single real pole at $z = 1 - \alpha$, hence the step response is a **monotonically rising exponential** with no overshoot. This is exactly why exponential smoothing is preferred in control systems where overshoot is unacceptable.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Noise Reduction Factor of the Moving Average Filter

**Statement:** Prove that if $x[n] = s[n] + v[n]$, where $s[n]$ is the deterministic signal and $v[n]$ is zero-mean white noise with variance $\sigma_v^2$, then the variance of the noise at the output of an $N$-point moving average is $\sigma_v^2 / N$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

The filter output is given by the convolution sum.

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ s[n-k] + v[n-k] \right]$$

By linearity, the signal part and the noise part superpose. We are only interested in the noise component $y_v[n]$:

$$y_v[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} v[n-k]$$

The variance of a linear combination of *uncorrelated* zero-mean random variables is the sum of the squared coefficients times the individual variance. Since $v[n-k]$ are samples of white noise, they are mutually uncorrelated.

$$\sigma_{y_v}^2 = E\{y_v^2[n]\} = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} E\{v^2[n-k]\} = \frac{1}{N^2}\cdot N \cdot \sigma_v^2 = \frac{\sigma_v^2}{N}$$

The standard deviation reduces by $\sqrt{N}$:

$$\sigma_{y_v} = \frac{\sigma_v}{\sqrt{N}}$$

This is the **fundamental justification** for using a moving-average filter as a noise reducer. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) improves by a factor of $N$ in power (or $\sqrt{N}$ in amplitude).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Group Delay of a Symmetric FIR Smoother

**Statement:** Show that a length-$N$ linear-phase FIR filter with symmetric coefficients $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$ has a constant group delay of $(N-1)/2$ samples.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

For a linear-phase FIR filter, the frequency response can be written as $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} A(\omega)$, where $A(\omega)$ is a real-valued function of $\omega$ (the *zero-phase* or *amplitude* response). Substituting into the expression for group delay:

$$\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\left[-\frac{\omega(N-1)}{2} + \angle A(\omega)\right]$$

Since $A(\omega)$ is real (not complex), its argument $\angle A(\omega)$ is either $0$ or $\pi$ (a constant) almost everywhere except at the zeros of $A(\omega)$. Hence its derivative is zero almost everywhere.

$$\tau_g(\omega) = \frac{N-1}{2} \quad \text{samples}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical impact:** If you need the *current* (real-time) value of a smooth signal, you cannot use a symmetric FIR filter with $N > 1$ — there is an unavoidable $(N-1)/2$-sample delay. This is why *causal* IIR filters (like exponential smoothing) are used in feedback control, where the present output must react to the present input.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Direct-Form-I Realization of a Length-$N$ Moving Average Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xn["x[n] (input)"] --> d0["z^-1"]
    d0 --> d1["z^-1"]
    d1 --> d2["z^-1"]
    d2 --> d3["z^-1 (last)"]
    d3 --> dN["... delay line ..."]
    dN --> dotN["z^-1"]
    dotN --> sum["Sigma: Sum Node"]
    xn --> coef0["x (1/N)"]
    d0 --> coef1["x (1/N)"]
    d1 --> coef2["x (1/N)"]
    d2 --> coef3["x (1/N)"]
    d3 --> coef4["x (1/N)"]
    dN --> coefM["x (1/N)"]
    dotN --> coefK["x (1/N)"]
    coef0 --> sum
    coef1 --> sum
    coef2 --> sum
    coef3 --> sum
    coef4 --> sum
    coefM --> sum
    coefK --> sum
    sum --> yn["y[n] (output)"]
    style xn fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style yn fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style sum fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
```

**Figure explanation:** Each unit delay $z^{-1}$ shifts the input by one sample. The most recent $N$ samples are multiplied by the (uniform) coefficient $1/N$ and summed to produce the output. This is the **canonical tapped-delay-line** structure used universally in real-time DSP hardware (e.g., the SHARC and TMS320 families).

### 4.2 Direct-Form-II Realization of the First-Order Exponential Smoother

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xn["x[n]"] --> mulA["x alpha"]
    mulA --> sum["+ (add)"]
    yn1["y[n-1] (delayed feedback)"] --> mulB["x (1 - alpha)"]
    mulB --> sum
    sum --> yn["y[n] (output)"]
    yn --> delay["z^-1"]
    delay --> yn1
    style xn fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style yn fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style sum fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
    style delay fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

**Figure explanation:** The IIR smoother uses a *single* delay element and a *feedback* path. The recursion is: multiply current input by $\alpha$, multiply previous output by $(1-\alpha)$, add, and feed back. This is **transcendently more memory-efficient** than the FIR counterpart — only **one** state variable $y[n-1]$ is stored, regardless of the equivalent time constant.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Real-Time Smoothing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AnalogWorld["Analog Signal Source"]
        sensor["Physical Sensor (e.g., thermocouple)"]
        sensor --> bpf["Analog Anti-Alias Filter (BW < Fs/2)"]
    end
    bpf --> adc["ADC (Sampling at Fs)"]
    adc --> buffer["Input Buffer (FIFO)"]
    buffer --> smoother["Digital Smoothing Filter (the topic)"]
    smoother --> decim["Optional Decimator (down-sample)"]
    decim --> controller["Downstream Processor / Controller"]
    controller --> feedback["Feedback Path (control input)"]
    feedback --> actuator["Actuator / DAC / Display"]
    style sensor fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0
    style adc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style smoother fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:3px
    style controller fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

**Figure explanation:** This is the *complete* real-time DSP pipeline. The smoothing filter is the **central block** that interfaces the noisy, sampled data stream to the downstream decision logic. The thicker border on the smoother block emphasizes its role as the focus of this module.

### 4.4 Frequency-Response Comparison Chart (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SMA["Uniform MA, N=4"]
        a1["Dirichlet kernel pattern: |sin 2w / sin w/2| / 4"]
        a1 --> a2["Main lobe: narrow, deep nulls at w = 2pi k / N"]
        a2 --> a3["Stopband attenuation: only ~13 dB (poor)"]
    end
    subgraph EXP["Exponential, alpha=0.25"]
        b1["Smooth, monotonically decreasing curve"]
        b1 --> b2["No ripples, no nulls"]
        b2 --> b3["Stopband attenuation: gradual, -20 dB/dec"]
    end
    subgraph CASC["Cascaded 4th order IIR"]
        c1["Sharper transition than 1st order"]
        c1 --> c2["Steepness = 4 x 20 dB/dec = 80 dB/dec"]
        c2 --> c3["More group delay, more ringing"]
    end
    style SMA fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d
    style EXP fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style CASC fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

**Figure explanation:** This block-map summarizes the trade-offs the KTU examiner expects you to know. The **exponential smoother** has the *best* time-domain behavior (no ringing), the **uniform MA** has the *most predictable* frequency response (deep nulls at known frequencies), and the **cascaded IIR** has the *sharpest* roll-off at the cost of more delay.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define a smoothing filter. Why is it called a *low-pass* filter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A smoothing filter is a discrete-time linear system whose magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is largest at $\omega = 0$ (DC) and monotonically non-increasing as $\omega$ increases toward $\pi$. It is called a *low-pass* filter because it allows low-frequency components of the input signal (the slow-varying "smooth" part) to pass through to the output with little attenuation, while attenuating the high-frequency components (the rapid "noise" part). In real-time DSP, it is implemented as a weighted average of recent samples. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between an FIR and an IIR smoothing filter. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FIR Smoother | IIR Smoother |
|---|---|---|
| Impulse response | Finite length | Infinite length |
| Memory | Stores last $N$ input samples | Stores last output sample(s) |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditionally stable |
| Phase | Can be exactly linear | Nonlinear phase |
| Example | Uniform moving average $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]$ | Exponential smoother $y[n] = \alpha x[n] + (1-\alpha)y[n-1]$ |
| Group delay | Constant $(N-1)/2$ | Frequency-dependent |

**(3 marks — 2 for distinction, 1 for examples)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a) or Q1(b) style]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Derive the transfer function and magnitude response of a 5-point uniform moving average filter. Plot (or describe) the magnitude response. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A DC signal of amplitude 5 V is corrupted by zero-mean white noise of variance $\sigma_v^2 = 4 \text{ V}^2$. The composite signal $x[n] = 5 + v[n]$ is passed through an 8-point uniform moving average filter. Find the SNR at the input and the SNR at the output. Comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

For an $N$-point uniform moving average, the difference equation is:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$

Substituting $N = 5$:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{5}\left[x[n] + x[n-1] + x[n-2] + x[n-3] + x[n-4]\right]$$

Z-transforming both sides:

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{5}X(z)\left[1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + z^{-4}\right]$$

The bracketed sum is a finite geometric series with ratio $z^{-1}$ and 5 terms.

$$1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + z^{-4} = \frac{1 - z^{-5}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

Therefore the transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{5}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-5}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

The magnitude response, evaluated on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$, is:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{5}\left\vert \frac{\sin(5\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$$

**Key features (to be listed for full marks):**
- DC gain: $\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 1$ (since $\lim_{x\to 0}\sin(5x)/\sin(x) = 5$).
- Zeros on the unit circle at $\omega_k = 2\pi k / 5$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, 4$ (i.e., the five 5th roots of unity excluding $z=1$).
- Periodic in $\omega$ with period $2\pi$.
- First sidelobe peak near $\omega \approx 3\pi/5$ has magnitude $\approx 1/5 \cdot 1/\sin(3\pi/10) \approx 0.224$, giving a stopband attenuation of about $-13 \text{ dB}$.

**(7 marks — 2 for difference equation, 2 for transfer function, 2 for magnitude response, 1 for key features)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Input SNR:**
The signal is DC at amplitude $A = 5 \text{ V}$, so the signal *power* is $P_s = A^2 = 25 \text{ W}$ (assuming 1-ohm load).
The noise variance is $\sigma_v^2 = 4 \text{ V}^2$, so noise power is $P_{n,\text{in}} = 4 \text{ W}$.

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = \frac{P_s}{P_{n,\text{in}}} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{in,dB}} = 10\log_{10}(6.25) \approx 7.96 \text{ dB}$$

**Output SNR:**
For an $N = 8$ uniform moving average, the signal (being DC) is preserved exactly: $P_{s,\text{out}} = P_s = 25 \text{ W}$.
The noise variance is reduced by a factor of $N$ (derived in §3.3 above):

$$\sigma_{v,\text{out}}^2 = \frac{\sigma_v^2}{N} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5 \text{ V}^2$$

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{s,\text{out}}}{P_{n,\text{out}}} = \frac{25}{0.5} = 50 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out,dB}} = 10\log_{10}(50) \approx 16.99 \text{ dB}$$

**Improvement:**

$$\Delta \text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{50}{6.25}\right) = 10\log_{10}(8) \approx 9.03 \text{ dB}$$

Equivalently, the SNR has been multiplied by $N = 8$ (in power), confirming the theoretical $\sqrt{N}$ reduction in noise standard deviation.

**Comment (required for full marks):** The SNR improvement is a direct consequence of the incoherence of white noise across samples versus the coherence of the DC signal. The 5-point MA is a *linear* operator, so it cannot create new information; it only redistributes the existing energy, exploiting the spectral difference between signal and noise.

**(7 marks — 2 for input SNR, 2 for output SNR, 2 for improvement, 1 for the qualitative comment)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 alternative]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** A first-order exponential smoothing filter is described by the difference equation $y[n] = 0.2 x[n] + 0.8 y[n-1]$. Find its transfer function $H(z)$, the pole location, the DC gain, and the impulse response $h[n]$. Is the filter stable? Justify. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For the same filter, derive the magnitude-squared response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ and find the frequency $\omega_c$ at which $\vert H(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = 0.5$ (the $-3 \text{ dB}$ cutoff). Comment on the meaning of the cutoff for real-time instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Z-transforming the difference equation:

$$Y(z) = 0.2\, X(z) + 0.8\, z^{-1} Y(z)$$

Collecting $Y(z)$ terms:

$$Y(z)\left[1 - 0.8 z^{-1}\right] = 0.2 X(z)$$

Hence the transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.2}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

Equivalently, multiplying numerator and denominator by $z$:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.2 z}{z - 0.8}$$

**Pole location:** The denominator $z - 0.8 = 0$ gives a single real pole at $z_p = 0.8$.

**DC gain:** Evaluate at $z = 1$:

$$H(1) = \frac{0.2}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0.2}{0.2} = 1$$

So the DC gain is unity — the smoother passes the DC component without attenuation. **(This is a critical point the KTU examiner checks.)**

**Impulse response:** With $H(z) = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$ and recognizing the geometric-series form $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \leftrightarrow a^n u[n]$:

$$h[n] = 0.2 \cdot (0.8)^n u[n] = 0.2 \cdot 0.8^n, \quad n \ge 0$$

**Stability:** The single pole is at $z_p = 0.8$, which lies *inside* the unit circle ($\vert z_p \vert = 0.8 < 1$). Therefore, the system is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output). Additionally, the impulse response is absolutely summable:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = 0.2 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 0.8^n = 0.2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.8} = 1 < \infty$$

confirming stability.

**(7 marks — 1 for transfer function, 1 for pole, 1 for DC gain, 2 for impulse response, 2 for stability justification)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The frequency response is obtained by setting $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{0.2}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\omega}}$$

The magnitude squared is:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = H(e^{j\omega}) H^*(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{0.2}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\omega}} \cdot \frac{0.2}{1 - 0.8 e^{j\omega}} = \frac{0.04}{1 + 0.64 - 1.6\cos\omega} = \frac{0.04}{1.64 - 1.6\cos\omega}$$

**Setting the $-3 \text{ dB}$ condition** $\vert H(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = 0.5$:

$$\frac{0.04}{1.64 - 1.6\cos\omega_c} = 0.5$$

Solving:

$$1.64 - 1.6\cos\omega_c = \frac{0.04}{0.5} = 0.08$$

$$1.6\cos\omega_c = 1.64 - 0.08 = 1.56$$

$$\cos\omega_c = \frac{1.56}{1.6} = 0.975$$

$$\omega_c = \arccos(0.975) \approx 0.2233 \text{ rad/sample}$$

Converting to Hz (assuming a sampling rate $F_s$, expressed in samples/sec):

$$f_c = \frac{\omega_c F_s}{2\pi} \approx 0.0355\, F_s \text{ Hz}$$

**Comparison with the small-$\alpha$ approximation:** The approximation gives $\omega_c \approx \alpha = 0.2$ rad/sample. The exact value $0.2233$ rad/sample is reasonably close, validating the engineering rule of thumb. (For larger $\alpha$, the approximation degrades.)

**Comment for real-time instrumentation (required for full marks):** The cutoff frequency $\omega_c \approx 0.22$ rad/sample means that any spectral component of the input signal *above* this frequency is attenuated by more than 3 dB. In an instrumentation system sampling at, say, $F_s = 1 \text{ kHz}$, this corresponds to $f_c \approx 35.5$ Hz. Signal components above 35.5 Hz (e.g., 50 Hz mains interference) will be significantly suppressed, while the slow-varying sensor signal of interest (typically < 10 Hz) is preserved. The trade-off is a *transient response time* of roughly $1/f_c \approx 28$ ms — the smoother takes about 3 time constants ($3\tau = 3/\omega_c F_s$) to reach a new steady state.

**(7 marks — 2 for magnitude-squared derivation, 2 for cutoff equation, 1 for numerical answer, 2 for the instrumentation comment)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Smoothing Filters**
> 1. **The "DC gain is not 1" trap:** Many students write $H(1) = \alpha$ for the exponential smoother and lose 1 mark. The DC gain is **always** $H(1) = \alpha / [1 - (1 - \alpha)] = 1$ (for any $\alpha \in (0, 1]$). If the DC gain is not 1, the smoother is *biased* — it does not pass the constant component correctly.
> 2. **Forgetting the difference between $z$-plane and $\omega$-plane:** When asked for the "frequency response," do *not* substitute $z = 1$ — that gives the DC gain. Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$.
> 3. **Confusing group delay with phase delay:** For linear-phase FIRs they are equal; for IIRs (like exponential smoothing) they are *not*. KTU 2024 valuations explicitly check whether you compute $\tau_g = -d\phi/d\omega$ correctly.
> 4. **Missing the stability check:** For IIR smoothers, *always* verify that all poles lie inside the unit circle. A pole at $z = 1.2$ (which is what $\alpha < 0$ would give) is a **disastrous** mistake that costs 2 marks.
> 5. **The $\sin(Nx)/\sin(x)$ limit:** When verifying DC gain of the MA filter, do not blindly write $0/0 = 1$. Apply L'Hôpital's rule or use the known limit $\lim_{x \to 0} \sin(Nx)/\sin(x) = N$. The examiner awards the mark only if the *method* is shown.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smoothing Filters (Module 1)**

- [x] **Definition:** A *low-pass* discrete-time filter that suppresses high-frequency noise while preserving the low-frequency trend of a signal.
- [x] **Two families:** FIR (uniform/weighted moving average) and IIR (exponential, cascaded).
- [x] **MA filter key equation:** $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$.
- [x] **MA transfer function:** $H(z) = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$.
- [x] **MA magnitude response:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$.
- [x] **Exponential smoother equation:** $y[n] = \alpha x[n] + (1 - \alpha) y[n-1]$.
- [x] **Exponential smoother transfer function:** $H(z) = \frac{\alpha}{1 - (1 - \alpha) z^{-1}}$.
- [x] **Exponential smoother pole:** at $z_p = 1 - \alpha$; stable for $0 < \alpha < 2$.
- [x] **DC gain of any good smoother:** exactly 1 (verified by $H(1) = 1$).
- [x] **Noise reduction (MA):** variance reduced by factor $N$; SNR improved by $N$ in power.
- [x] **Noise reduction (exp.):** equivalent to a single-pole RC; the 3-dB bandwidth is approximately $\alpha$ rad/sample for small $\alpha$.
- [x] **Group delay of symmetric FIR:** constant at $(N-1)/2$ samples.
- [x] **Group delay of IIR:** frequency-dependent, larger near the cutoff.
- [x] **Cascading $K$ first-order IIRs:** sharpens the roll-off by a factor of $K$ (in dB/decade) at the cost of more delay and more transient ringing.
- [x] **Real-time implementation:** MA needs a *circular buffer* of $N$ samples; exponential smoother needs only **one** state variable.
- [x] **Stability criterion (IIR):** all poles must satisfy $\vert z_p \vert < 1$.
- [x] **Linear phase advantage:** symmetric FIR smoothers preserve the *shape* of the smoothed signal — vital in biomedical and audio DSP.
- [x] **Trade-off golden rule:** Stronger smoothing (larger $N$ or smaller $\alpha$) ↔ larger delay ↔ slower transient response. Choose based on the application's bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Data Converters?

**Data Converters** are the fundamental electronic interface circuits that bridge the continuous **analog (real-world) domain** and the discrete **digital (processor) domain**. They are the "translators" of any real-time digital signal processing (DSP) system, converting signals between human-perceivable analog waveforms and binary machine code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Data converters are mixed-signal integrated circuits responsible for transforming analog signals (continuous in time and amplitude) into digital codes (discrete in time and amplitude) via **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)**, or vice versa through **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The United Nations Interpreter

Imagine the United Nations assembly:
* **Member States (Speaker)** speak dozens of different languages (analog signals with varying amplitudes, frequencies, and noise).
* **The Interpreter (Data Converter)** listens carefully, takes rapid notes (sampling), and translates the message into one universal, structured language (binary 1s and 0s).
* **The UN Secretary (DSP Processor)** receives the universal language, processes it (filtering, FFT, modulation), and the interpreter translates the response back.

If the interpreter is slow, information is lost. If the interpreter is imprecise, the translation is corrupted. The speed (**sampling rate**) and accuracy (**resolution in bits**) of this interpreter define the quality of the entire global communication.

### 1.3 Core Classification of Data Converters

| Converter Family | Purpose | Common Architectures |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** | Analog $\rightarrow$ Digital | Flash, SAR, Pipeline, Sigma-Delta, Dual-Slope |
| **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)** | Digital $\rightarrow$ Analog | R-2R Ladder, Weighted Resistor, Sigma-Delta, Thermometer |

### 1.4 Fundamental Performance Metrics

* **Resolution (N):** The number of discrete binary levels an ADC can produce. Expressed in **bits**. A 12-bit ADC has $2^{12} = \mathbf{4096}$ quantization levels.
* **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** The rate (in Samples Per Second, SPS) at which the analog input is captured. Measured in **Hz, kSPS, MSPS**.
* **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The maximum peak-to-peak analog input voltage the converter can handle, typically denoted as $V_{FS}$ or $V_{REF}$.
* **Quantization Step (LSB):** The smallest voltage increment distinguishable, given by $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^N$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU examinations, data converters are evaluated based on three axes — **static performance** (INL, DNL, Offset, Gain Error), **dynamic performance** (SNR, SINAD, ENOB, THD), and **application suitability** (audio, instrumentation, communications).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Conversion Process

For an **ADC**, conversion occurs in two mathematically distinct stages:

### Stage 1: Sampling (Discretization in Time)
* The continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train.
* Governed strictly by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
* If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency content folds back into the baseband irreversibly.

### Stage 2: Quantization (Discretization in Amplitude)
* Each sampled amplitude is rounded to the nearest discrete voltage level $\Delta$ (the LSB).
* This rounding action introduces an irreducible **quantization error** $e_q = x_Q(n) - x(n)$, bounded within the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2)$.

### 2.2 The Two-Step Reconstruction Process (DAC)

For a **DAC**, the process reverses:
* **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):** Each digital code is held constant for one sampling period $T_s$. This produces a staircase waveform.
* **Reconstruction Filtering:** A low-pass filter (reconstruction filter) smooths the staircase back into a continuous waveform, removing spectral images centered at multiples of $f_s$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | Volts (V) | $V_{FS}$ is Full-Scale Voltage |
| Max Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$ | Volts (V) | Uniform distribution assumed |
| RMS Quantization Noise | $e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N \sqrt{12}}$ | Volts (V) | Derived from uniform PDF |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB | For ideal full-scale sine input |
| Effective Number of Bits | $ENOB = \dfrac{SINAD - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | From measured SINAD |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling frequency |
| Aperture Time | $t_a = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}_{max}}$ | seconds (s) | Tracking rate capability |
| Throughput (Flash) | $f_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{T_{comp}}$ | SPS | Limited by comparator delay |
| DNL (Differential Non-Linearity) | $DNL(i) = \dfrac{\Delta_{actual}(i) - \Delta}{\Delta}$ | LSB | Static linearity metric |
| INL (Integral Non-Linearity) | $INL(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} DNL(j)$ | LSB | Cumulative static error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Rule:** Every extra bit of resolution (N) theoretically improves the SNR by exactly **6.02 dB**. This is the most heavily tested relationship in KTU Module 1 derivations.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

* **Audio Systems (44.1 kSPS, 16-bit CD Quality):** Used in MP3 players, hearing aids, and mixing consoles.
* **Oscilloscopes & SDR (1 GSPS+):** High-speed Flash/Pipeline ADCs digitize RF signals for software-defined radio.
* **Industrial Sensors (24-bit Sigma-Delta):** Precision weighing scales, temperature acquisition, strain gauges.
* **Video/Displays:** HDMI DACs drive pixel voltages on monitors; video ADCs capture camera sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Quantization Noise Power

**Problem Statement:** Derive the RMS value of the quantization noise assuming the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed between $-\Delta/2$ and $+\Delta/2$.

**Step 1 — Define the Probability Density Function (PDF).**
Because the input signal is assumed to vary randomly across many quantization levels (an assumption valid for real-world signals, not DC), the error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed. The PDF is:

$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for} \quad e_q \in \left[-\frac{\Delta}{2}, \frac{\Delta}{2}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Write the Mean-Square Value Integral.**
The average power (mean-square value) of a random variable is the integral of the square weighted by the PDF:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the PDF and Integrate.**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Limits.**

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right] = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \left[ \frac{\Delta^3}{8} + \frac{\Delta^3}{8} \right] = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Take the Square Root to get RMS.**

$$e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta^2}{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

Since $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^N$, we substitute:

$$\boxed{e_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N \cdot 2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}\sqrt{3}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

**Step 1 — Compute Signal Power.**
For a full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ where the peak $A = V_{FS}/2$, the RMS signal power is:

$$P_{signal} = \left(\frac{V_{FS}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Use the Quantization Noise Power from §3.1.**

$$P_{noise} = \overline{e_q^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Signal-to-Noise Ratio.**

$$SNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^N)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^N}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^N = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Decibels.**

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2)$$

Since $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$:

$$\boxed{SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02 N + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

**Verification:** For N = 16 bits (CD quality audio), $SNR = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB — matching the published CD audio specification. 

## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)

**Step 1 — Inverse the SNR Formula.** Starting from $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$:

$$6.02N = SNR - 1.76$$

$$N = \frac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$$

**Step 2 — Replace SNR with SINAD.** Since real converters have distortion (THD) and spurious components, KTU uses SINAD (Signal-to-Noise-and-Distortion):

$$\boxed{ENOB = \frac{SINAD_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} \text{ bits}}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**
A 12-bit ADC is measured to have $SINAD = 70$ dB. What is the actual usable resolution?

$$ENOB = \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{68.24}{6.02} \approx 11.33 \text{ bits}$$

This means the practical resolution is degraded by roughly 0.67 bits due to real-world circuit imperfections.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic ADC Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Analog Input Signal x of t] --> saa[Sample and Hold S/H]
    saa --> qnt[Quantizer N-bit]
    saa --> clk1[Clock Generator f_s]
    clk1 --> saa
    qnt --> enc[Binary Encoder]
    enc --> out1[Digital Output Code N-bits]
    qnt --> err[Quantization Error e_q]
    style src1 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style out1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style err fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
```

**Reading the diagram:** The analog waveform enters the **Sample and Hold** stage, frozen momentarily by the clock at rate $f_s$. The held voltage is then compared to discrete levels in the **Quantizer**, and the **Binary Encoder** issues the corresponding N-bit digital word. The residual error is the unavoidable quantization noise.

## 4.2 SAR ADC Architecture (Successive Approximation Register)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vin[Analog Input V_in] --> comp[Comparator]
    ref[DAC V_REF] --> comp
    comp --> sar[SAR Logic Engine]
    sar --> reg[N-bit Register MSB to LSB]
    reg --> ref
    sar --> eoc[End of Conversion EOC Flag]
    sar --> dOut[Parallel Digital Output]
    style vin fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style dOut fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style sar fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9
```

**Operational Note:** The SAR sets the MSB to 1 and checks if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}/2$. If true, MSB remains 1; else, it resets to 0. The test then proceeds to the next bit, halving the comparison range. After **N cycles**, the input is resolved to N-bit accuracy. This makes the SAR ideal for **medium-speed, high-resolution** applications (e.g., 18-bit, 1 MSPS data acquisition).

## 4.3 Flash ADC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vIn[Analog Input V_in] --> rStr[String of 2^N Resistors]
    rStr --> t1[Threshold T_1 = 0.5 LSB]
    rStr --> t2[Threshold T_2 = 1.5 LSB]
    rStr --> t3[Threshold T_k = k minus 0.5 LSB]
    rStr --> tN[Threshold T_{2^N-1}]
    t1 --> p1[Comparator 1]
    t2 --> p2[Comparator 2]
    t3 --> p3[Comparator k]
    tN --> pN[Comparator N-1]
    p1 --> enc1[Priority Encoder]
    p2 --> enc1
    p3 --> enc1
    pN --> enc1
    enc1 --> dOut2[N-bit Thermometer to Binary Code]
    style vIn fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style dOut2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style enc1 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
```

**Key Insight:** Flash ADCs use $2^N - 1$ comparators in parallel, making them the **fastest** architecture (gigahertz sampling). The trade-off is exponential hardware — a 10-bit Flash ADC requires **1023 comparators**, limiting resolution to ~8 bits in practice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "quantization" as applied to ADCs. Explain quantization error and quantization noise. Mention the range of quantization error.**

**Model Answer:**
Quantization is the process of converting a continuous-amplitude sampled signal into a discrete-amplitude digital code by assigning each sample to the nearest of $2^N$ predefined voltage levels. The difference between the actual sampled analog value and its assigned quantized value is called **quantization error** $e_q$. When the input signal varies sufficiently, the error behaves like a random variable uniformly distributed in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2)$, where $\Delta$ is the LSB voltage. Its RMS value $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$ is referred to as **quantization noise**, an irreducible white-noise-like distortion inherent to all finite-resolution converters. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is aperture time in an ADC and why is it significant for high-frequency signal acquisition?**

**Model Answer:**
Aperture time $t_a$ is the small but finite time window during which the sample-and-hold circuit acquires the input voltage before freezing it for quantization. Its significance lies in the fact that the input must not change by more than $\pm \text{LSB}/2$ during this window, otherwise aperture error occurs. For a full-scale sinusoid $V_{FS} \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum slew rate is $2\pi f V_{FS}$, giving the constraint:

$$t_a \le \frac{1}{2^N \cdot 2\pi f_{max}}$$

Smaller $t_a$ (achieved by fast S/H switches) is essential for digitizing high-frequency signals accurately. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** of an ideal N-bit ADC. What is the SQNR in dB for a 14-bit converter? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **Flash**, **SAR**, and **Sigma-Delta** ADC architectures in terms of speed, resolution, hardware complexity, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**
Refer to **§3.2** above. The derivation yields $SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
For N = 14:
$$SNR_{14} = 6.02(14) + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}$$
The RMS quantization noise is $V_{FS}/(2^{14+1}\sqrt{3}) = V_{FS}/(56,894)$. **(7 marks — full derivation in §3.2 earns full credit.)**

**Model Solution (b):**

| Architecture | Speed | Resolution | Hardware | Applications |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flash | Fastest (GSPS) | Low (≤8 bits) | $2^N - 1$ comparators | Oscilloscopes, Radar, SDR |
| SAR | Medium (≤5 MSPS) | Medium-High (12–18 bits) | 1 comparator, 1 DAC | Data acquisition, Instrumentation |
| Sigma-Delta | Slow (oversampled) | Very High (16–24 bits) | Modulator + Digital filter | Audio, Weighing scales, Sensors |

SAR offers the best trade-off; Sigma-Delta trades speed for extreme resolution via **noise shaping** and **oversampling**. **(7 marks)** *(7 marks)*

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** An 8-bit ADC has a full-scale input range of $0$ to $5$ V. Calculate: (i) the quantization step (LSB), (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the RMS quantization noise voltage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the operation of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** with a neat block diagram. State one advantage and one disadvantage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**
Given: $N = 8$, $V_{FS} = 5$ V.

**(i) LSB / Quantization Step:**
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**
$$e_{q,max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 9.766 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) RMS Quantization Noise:**
$$e_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.01953}{3.464} = 5.638 \text{ mV}$$

*(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)*

**Model Solution (b):**
Refer to **§4.2** for the block diagram. **Operation:** The SAR ADC performs a **binary search** through the input range $0$ to $V_{REF}$. It begins by setting the MSB to 1 (mid-scale), the internal DAC produces $V_{REF}/2$, and a comparator checks whether $V_{in}$ is greater or less than this value. The bit is retained or cleared, and the process iterates for the next bit. After N clock cycles, the digital approximation matches the input within $\pm \text{LSB}/2$.

**Advantage:** Excellent trade-off — moderate speed (up to 5 MSPS) with high resolution (up to 18 bits) using minimal hardware (single comparator + DAC).
**Disadvantage:** Conversion time is proportional to resolution ($N$ clock cycles per sample), making it unsuitable for high-frequency, gigahertz-class signals. *(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Errors in (a):** Students forget to convert $\Delta$ from V to mV when reporting RMS noise. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing LSB and MSB in SAR diagrams:** The MSB is tested first, not the LSB. Drawing the iteration arrow in the wrong direction costs 1 mark.
> 3. **In SNR derivations:** KTU deducts 2 marks if the +1.76 dB term is forgotten — it arises from the $10\log_{10}(1.5)$ term, NOT a fudge factor.
> 4. **In Sigma-Delta explanations:** Do NOT call it "an averaging ADC" — it is an **oversampling + noise-shaping** converter. This distinction is the single most common differentiator between an 8-mark and a 12-mark answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Data converters** are the analog-digital bridge: ADCs convert real-world signals to bits; DACs reconstruct analog waveforms from bits.
* The **two-step ADC process** is **sample-and-hold** (discretize time) followed by **quantization** (discretize amplitude).
* **Quantization step (LSB):** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ — the voltage "granularity" of the converter.
* **RMS quantization noise:** $V_{FS}/(2^{N+1}\sqrt{3})$ — the irreducible noise floor of an ideal ADC.
* **The Golden SNR Formula:** $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB — memorize verbatim; every extra bit gives **6 dB** of dynamic range.
* **ENOB** is the *effective* (real-world) resolution derived from measured SINAD, not the datasheet N.
* **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — violation causes **aliasing**, an irreversible corruption.
* **Flash ADC:** Fastest ($2^N - 1$ comparators in parallel), but exponential hardware.
* **SAR ADC:** Best general-purpose trade-off; N clock cycles per conversion.
* **Sigma-Delta ADC:** Uses **oversampling** and **noise shaping** to push quantization noise out of the band of interest, achieving 24-bit resolution.
* **Aperture time** must be small enough so the input does not change by more than 1 LSB during sampling.
* **Static errors (DNL, INL)** describe DC linearity; **dynamic errors (SNR, SINAD, ENOB)** describe AC performance — KTU loves testing the distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DSP Hardware and DSP Hardware Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DSP Hardware and DSP Hardware Options

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is DSP Hardware?

**DSP Hardware** refers to the specialized physical electronic components—processors, memory units, data converters, and supporting logic—that are architected to perform Digital Signal Processing operations in real time, at speeds dictated by the input signal's sample rate.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, DSP hardware is the *engine* that transforms the abstract mathematical operations of DSP (filtering, FFT, convolution) into measurable, time-bound physical computations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *DSP Hardware is a class of digital microelectronics optimized for numerically intensive signal processing tasks, characterized by features such as fast Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) units, dedicated address generators, multiple memory buses, and deterministic instruction cycle timing.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Specialist Doctor vs. The General Physician

Imagine you are sick. You have two choices:
- **The General Physician (General Purpose Microprocessor - GPP):** Can diagnose *anything* from a headache to a heart condition, but takes longer for specialized tests, may need to call in specialists, and works sequentially through a checklist.
- **The Cardiac Specialist (DSP Processor):** Cannot treat a cold, but performs heart surgery rapidly, with dedicated tools (stents, ECG machines) right at the workstation, executing pre-planned steps in a fixed, optimized sequence.

A **DSP chip** is that cardiac specialist. It sacrifices flexibility for **raw numerical speed, deterministic timing, and sustained data throughput**—exactly what real-time signal processing demands.

### 1.3 Why Dedicated DSP Hardware?

Three engineering pressures force designers to use specialized hardware rather than a general-purpose CPU:

1. **Real-Time Constraint (Hard Deadline):** If a sample arrives every $T_s$ seconds, the processing of sample $n$ must finish before sample $n+1$ arrives, or data is lost.
2. **Computational Density:** Algorithms like FIR filtering or FFT demand millions of MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operations per second.
3. **Power & Cost Efficiency:** Mobile and embedded DSP applications (hearing aids, smartphones, radar) require low power per operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metric (KTU Highlight):** Throughput in DSP is measured in **MMACS** (Million Multiply-Accumulates per Second) or **GFLOPS** (Giga Floating-Point Operations Per Second). The clock cycle budget per sample is $T_s / N_{ops}$ and **must never be violated** for hard real-time systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Architectural Dichotomy: Von Neumann vs. Harvard

The single most important architectural decision in any DSP hardware is the memory-processor connection.

### A. Von Neumann Architecture (GPP Default)

A **single bus** carries both instructions and data. Fetch and execute cannot happen simultaneously; this is the *Von Neumann Bottleneck*.

- **Pros:** Simpler hardware, flexible programming.
- **Cons:** At any given clock, only one memory transaction occurs. Terrible for DSP's tight loops.

### B. Harvard Architecture (DSP Default)

**Separate program memory bus and data memory bus.** The CPU can fetch the *next instruction* while simultaneously reading the *current data*—enabling true single-cycle MAC operations.

- **Modified Harvard:** Common in modern DSPs. Allows limited data access from program memory (used heavily for coefficient tables in FIR filters).
- **Super Harvard / VLIW extensions:** Multiple data memory banks (X, Y) accessed in parallel (e.g., SHARC, TigerSHARC).

## 2.2 Core Hardware Features of a DSP Processor

| # | Hardware Feature | Functional Purpose | Real-Time Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fast MAC Unit** | Single-cycle Multiply + Accumulate | Enables $N$-tap FIR filter in $N$ cycles |
| 2 | **Multiple Memory Buses** | Parallel fetch of operands & coefficients | Removes memory bottleneck |
| 3 | **Dedicated Address Generators (DAGs)** | Hardware circular buffer pointer arithmetic | Zero-overhead looping, modulo addressing |
| 4 | **Hardware Loop Buffer / Zero-Overhead Loop** | Repeats a block of code without branch penalty | Critical for tight inner loops |
| 5 | **Pipelined Execution Units** | Overlapped fetch-decode-execute | Higher clock-rate effective throughput |
| 6 | **Saturation Arithmetic** | Clips overflow to max/min value | Avoids wrap-around distortion in audio |
| 7 | **Barrel Shifter** | Single-cycle bit shifts | Normalization in floating point |
| 8 | **On-Chip Cache / SRAM** | Low latency coefficient access | Predictable, fast memory access |
| 9 | **DMA Controller** | Background I/O without CPU intervention | CPU-free data movement |
| 10 | **Multiprocessor / Link Ports** | Inter-chip communication (e.g., SHARC links) | Scalable parallel processing |

## 2.3 The DSP Hardware Options Taxonomy

KTU requires students to be able to *compare and contrast* the available hardware choices. There are five main options.

### Option 1: General Purpose Microprocessor (GPP) / Microcontroller
- Example: Intel x86, ARM Cortex-A series.
- **Use case:** When DSP functions are minor and software flexibility matters (e.g., host PC running MATLAB, Raspberry Pi audio).
- **Limitation:** Not optimized for sustained real-time MAC throughput.

### Option 2: Dedicated DSP Processor (DSP μP)
- Example: Texas Instruments TMS320C6000 series, Analog Devices SHARC ADSP-214xx.
- **Use case:** High-volume, deterministic real-time DSP (audio codecs, motor control, baseband).
- **Strength:** Lowest development time for classic DSP algorithms.

### Option 3: Microcontroller with DSP Extensions
- Example: ARM Cortex-M4/M7 (with SIMD + MAC), TI MSP430 with MAC.
- **Use case:** Embedded control loops needing modest DSP (sensors, IoT edge nodes).
- **Strength:** Single-chip system (DSP + peripherals + low power).

### Option 4: ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)
- Example: Custom audio codec chip, dedicated MP3 decoder.
- **Use case:** Ultra-high volume consumer products (billions of units).
- **Strength:** Lowest power, lowest unit cost at volume. Zero programmability.

### Option 5: FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Array)
- Example: Xilinx Zynq, Intel (Altera) Cyclone, Lattice ECP5.
- **Use case:** High-throughput, parallel DSP (5G baseband, radar, video processing, prototyping ASICs).
- **Strength:** Massive parallelism (hundreds of MAC units), hardware reconfigurable, no silicon fabrication needed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical quantitative core** for Module 1. Memorize all relations.

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Sample Period | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | $f_s$ in Hz, $T_s$ in seconds |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest input freq |
| Processing Deadline | $T_{proc} \leq T_s$ | Hard real-time condition |
| Real-Time MAC Requirement | $\text{MACS} \geq f_s \cdot N_{taps}$ | For FIR of length $N_{taps}$ |
| FFT Real-Time Cost (radix-2) | $N \log_2 N$ complex MACs per FFT | $N$ = FFT length |
| Instruction Rate | $\text{IPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{\text{Cycles per MAC}}$ | $f_{clk}$ in MHz |
| SNR (Fixed-Point, $B$ bits) | $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02 B + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | $B$ = bit width of mantissa |
| Dynamic Range (Floating) | $\text{DR} \approx 6.02 \cdot B_{exp} \text{ dB}$ | $B_{exp}$ = exponent bits |
| Arithmetic Intensity | $\text{AI} = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{ \text{Memory Accesses (Bytes)}}$ | Determines GPP vs. FPGA suitability |
| Roofline Bound (HW) | $\text{Perf} \leq \min(\text{Peak FLOPs},\ \text{AI} \cdot \text{BW})$ | Bandwidth-limited vs. compute-limited |

> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** In all written answers, write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert G(\omega) \vert$ — never with the keyboard pipe `|`, as KTU's LaTeX-rendered answer sheets can break.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **5G/4G Base Stations:** FPGAs for massive MIMO front-end.
- **Hearing Aids:** Ultra-low-power DSP ASICs (e.g., ON Semi Ezairo).
- **Automotive Radar:** DSP processors for FFT-based range-Doppler processing.
- **Smart Speakers:** Multicore DSP + ARM SoC.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** GPU + FPGA for back-projection reconstruction.

## 2.6 Selection Decision Logic (KTU Favorite)

A flowchart for hardware choice is shown in Section 4. The key trade-offs:

- **Volume > 1 M units/year?** → Consider **ASIC**.
- **Need parallelism, prototype fast?** → **FPGA**.
- **Mid-volume, algorithm evolving?** → **DSP Processor** or **Cortex-M DSP MCU**.
- **Host/control dominant, DSP light?** → **GPP / ARM Linux board.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This topic is more architectural than purely algebraic. KTU examiners therefore test the *quantitative reasoning behind the architectural choice*. Below are the canonical derivations every KTU topper must be able to reproduce.

## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Time MAC Budget for an FIR Filter

**Problem Statement:** An FIR filter has $N = 64$ taps. Input sample rate is $f_s = 48\,\text{kHz}$ (audio CD). Determine the minimum DSP speed in MMACS required to process this filter in real time.

**Step 1 — Identify the per-sample operation count.**
A direct-form FIR filter computes:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

Each output sample requires exactly $N$ multiply–accumulate operations. There are no shortcuts at the algorithmic level (without symmetry, which we ignore here).

**Step 2 — Compute samples per second.**
$$f_s = 48{,}000 \text{ samples/second}$$

**Step 3 — Total MAC operations per second.**
$$\text{MACS} = N \cdot f_s$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**
$$\text{MACS} = 64 \cdot 48{,}000$$

$$\text{MACS} = 3{,}072{,}000 \text{ MACs/second}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MMACS (Million MACs per second).**
$$\text{MACS} = 3.072 \text{ MMACS}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the real-time feasibility margin.**
A KTU-acceptable design adds a 25% margin for interrupt overhead and I/O:

$$\text{MACS}_{\text{design}} = 1.25 \cdot 3.072$$

$$\text{MACS}_{\text{design}} \approx 3.84 \text{ MMACS}$$

> **Interpretation:** A DSP rated at **4 MMACS or higher** is sufficient. This is trivially met by any modern DSP, illustrating why FIR filters are computationally *easy* for hardware but critical for algorithm design.

## 3.2 Derivation: FFT Real-Time Feasibility

**Problem Statement:** A 1024-point radix-2 complex FFT must be performed every $10\,\text{ms}$ on a streaming baseband signal. Determine the required MMACS, and check if a 200 MIPS DSP suffices.

**Step 1 — MACs per FFT.**
For a radix-2 FFT of length $N$:
$$\text{MAC}_{\text{FFT}} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot \log_2 N$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N = 1024$.**
$$\log_2 1024 = 10$$

$$\text{MAC}_{\text{FFT}} = \frac{1024}{2} \cdot 10 = 5120 \text{ MACs per FFT}$$

**Step 3 — FFTs per second (from the time window).**
$$f_{\text{FFT}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{FFT}}} = \frac{1}{10\,\text{ms}} = 100 \text{ FFTs/second}$$

**Step 4 — Sustained MAC rate.**
$$\text{MACS} = 5120 \cdot 100 = 512{,}000 \text{ MACs/sec} = 0.512 \text{ MMACS}$$

**Step 5 — MIPS equivalence.**
On a DSP with 1 cycle per MAC (single-cycle MAC), this equals **0.512 MIPS** of MAC work. But the full FFT also has address generation and bit-reversal, multiplying by an empirical factor of ~2 to 3:

$$\text{Total MIPS} \approx 2.5 \cdot 0.512 \approx 1.28 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
A 200 MIPS DSP is **vastly over-specified** for this single FFT. The 200 MIPS figure is *not* the bottleneck.

> **Engineering Insight:** The MIPS count is rarely the constraint for FFTs of moderate size. Memory bandwidth, especially coefficient loading, becomes the real limit — which is why high-end DSPs use on-chip cache + DMA.

## 3.3 Derivation: Fixed-Point vs Floating-Point SNR Trade-off

**Problem Statement:** A DSP uses 16-bit fixed-point arithmetic. Compare the dynamic range against a 32-bit floating-point DSP (8-bit exponent, 24-bit mantissa).

**Step 1 — Fixed-point SNR formula.**
$$\text{SNR}_{fp} \approx 6.02 \cdot B + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $B = 16$.**
$$\text{SNR}_{fp} = 6.02 \cdot 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Floating-point dynamic range (exponent bits only).**
$$\text{DR}_{fl} \approx 6.02 \cdot B_{exp} \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $B_{exp} = 8$.**
$$\text{DR}_{fl} = 6.02 \cdot 8 = 48.16 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Wait — this seems contradictory.**

This is a *common student trap*. Floating-point does not have lower dynamic range. The exponent controls the **range of representable magnitudes**, while the mantissa controls the **precision within that range**.

> **Correct interpretation:**
> - **Dynamic range (magnitude span) of 32-bit float ≈ $48 \cdot \log_{10}(2) \cdot 8 \approx 1.46 \times 10^{38}$ — far exceeding 16-bit fixed.**
> - **SNR per sample (precision) of 32-bit float ≈ 6.02 × 24 + 1.76 ≈ 146 dB** (limited by mantissa, not exponent).

**Step 6 — Final comparative statement.**
| Format | Range | SNR (precision) |
|---|---|---|
| 16-bit Fixed | $2^{15}$ levels | ~98 dB |
| 32-bit Float (E8,M24) | $\approx 10^{\pm 38}$ | ~146 dB |

**Conclusion:** Floating-point is chosen when signals have **wide dynamic range** (e.g., audio with quiet + loud passages) and developers want to *ignore* scaling — at the cost of silicon area and power.

## 3.4 Analytical Trade-off Matrix: Choosing DSP Hardware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is *frequently* asked as a 7-mark question in KTU Module 1.

| Criterion | GPP | DSP μP | DSP-Enabled MCU | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak MAC throughput | Low–Med | High | Medium | Highest (per area) | Highest (parallel) |
| Power Efficiency | Poor | Good | Excellent | Best | Medium |
| Flexibility / Reprogrammability | High | High | High | None | High (reconfig) |
| Time-to-Market | Fastest | Fast | Fast | Slowest (months) | Fast |
| Unit Cost (high vol) | High | Medium | Low | Lowest | Medium |
| Development Cost (NRE) | Low | Low | Low | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Determinism / Real-Time | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Perfect | Good (with care) |
| Parallelism | Low (few cores) | Medium | Low | Custom (highest) | Massive (hundreds of MACs) |
| Typical Use | Host PC | Audio/Codec | IoT, Motor Ctrl | Phones, Hearing Aids | 5G, Radar, Prototyping |

## 3.5 Algorithmic Derivation: Why Harvard Architecture Wins for DSP

**Step 1 — Instruction fetch + data access per MAC cycle (FIR).**
Each MAC requires:
- 1 multiplication
- 1 addition
- 2 memory reads (one sample, one coefficient)
- 1 memory write (accumulator update)

**Step 2 — Single-bus (Von Neumann) cost.**
With one bus, each memory access takes a separate cycle. Minimum cycles per MAC = **4 cycles** (1 read coeff, 1 read data, 1 multiply, 1 add+write).

**Step 3 — Harvard cost.**
With three buses (P, X, Y for program, data, data), all three memory ops occur *in parallel* in a single cycle. Minimum cycles per MAC = **1 cycle**.

**Step 4 — Speedup ratio.**
$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{4}{1} = 4\times$$

**Step 5 — For an $N$-tap FIR.**
$$T_{\text{VN}} = 4N \cdot T_{clk}, \quad T_{\text{Harvard}} = N \cdot T_{clk}$$

This 4× advantage is exactly why *every* serious DSP chip from TI, ADI, and others is Harvard-based.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modified Harvard DSP Processor — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PMEM[Program Memory Bus]
    DMEMX[Data Memory X Bus]
    DMEMY[Data Memory Y Bus]
    AGX[Address Generator X]
    AGY[Address Generator Y]
    AGP[Address Generator P]
    MAC[Multiplier Accumulator Unit]
    SHFT[Barrel Shifter]
    REGS[Register File]
    CTRL[Program Sequencer and Control Unit]
    LOOPS[Hardware Loop Buffer]
    DMA[Direct Memory Access Controller]
    IO[Serial and Parallel I/O Ports]

    CTRL --> PMEM
    AGP --> PMEM
    AGX --> DMEMX
    AGY --> DMEMY
    PMEM --> REGS
    DMEMX --> MAC
    DMEMY --> MAC
    MAC --> SHFT
    SHFT --> REGS
    REGS --> MAC
    CTRL --- LOOPS
    LOOPS --- CTRL
    DMA --- DMEMX
    DMA --- DMEMY
    DMA --- IO
    REGS --> IO
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The three independent buses (Program, Data X, Data Y) allow simultaneous fetch + dual operand read in **one clock cycle**.
- The **Multiplier-Accumulator (MAC)** is the computational heart. It computes $a \cdot b + c$ in a single cycle.
- The **Barrel Shifter** performs any bit-shift in one cycle — used for fixed-point scaling.
- The **Address Generators** (DAGs) handle circular buffer pointer math *in hardware*, with zero software overhead.
- The **Hardware Loop Buffer** keeps tight inner loops in fast on-chip memory.
- The **DMA Controller** moves data between memory and I/O *without disturbing the CPU*, essential for streaming I/O.

## 4.2 DSP Hardware Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New DSP Project] --> B{Volume per Year?}
    B -->|Less than 10K| C{Algorithm Evolving?}
    B -->|10K to 1M| D{Parallelism Needed?}
    B -->|More than 1M| E[Choose ASIC if algorithm is frozen]
    C -->|Yes| F[Choose DSP Processor or ARM Cortex-M]
    C -->|No| G[Choose DSP Processor for stability]
    D -->|Yes Massive| H[Choose FPGA]
    D -->|Moderate| F
    E --> I[Consider FPGA Prototype then ASIC]
    F --> J{Real Time Strict?}
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|Hard Deadline| K[Use RTOS and Static Scheduling]
    J -->|Soft Real Time| L[Use Linux or Bare Metal]
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Throughput vs Power Across Hardware Options

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GPP[GPP x86 ARM Cortex A] ---|Low Power Low MAC| DSP1[Fixed Point DSP Processor]
    DSP1 ---|Medium Power High MAC| DSP2[Floating Point DSP Processor]
    DSP2 ---|High Throughput Reconfigurable| FPGA[FPGA]
    FPGA ---|Lowest Power Custom Silicon| ASIC[ASIC]
    MCU[ARM Cortex M4 M7] ---|Ultra Low Power Modest DSP| DSP1
    DSP1 ---|Multicore Option| MC[Multicore DSP e.g. TMS320C66x]
    MC ---|High Throughput| FPGA
```

**Interpretation:** Reading left-to-right corresponds to **increasing throughput and decreasing unit-cost at volume**, while power efficiency is a U-shaped curve — moderate for FPGAs (high leakage) and best for ASICs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. Why is Harvard preferred for DSP?  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory structure | Single shared memory for program & data | Separate program & data memories |
| Buses | One bus | Two or more independent buses |
| Fetch and Execute | Sequential (overlapped only via cache) | Truly simultaneous |
| Suitability for DSP | Poor (memory bottleneck) | Excellent (parallel MAC in 1 cycle) |
