**Problem Statement:** Compute the algorithmic load in MIPS for a 64-tap FIR filter running on a processor with $\tau = 4$ clock cycles per MAC, sampling at $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ (telephony rate).

### Step 1 — Identify Operations Per Sample

A 64-tap FIR requires 64 MAC operations per output sample.

### Step 2 — Compute Total MAC Operations Per Second

$$\text{MACS/sec} = N \cdot f_s = 64 \cdot 8000 = 512{,}000 \text{ MACs/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Clock Cycles Per Second

Each MAC takes $\tau = 4$ clock cycles, so:

$$\text{cycles/sec} = 512{,}000 \cdot 4 = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ cycles/s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second)

Assuming 1 instruction = 1 cycle in this idealized MAC architecture:

$$\text{MIPS} = 2{,}048{,}000 / 10^6 = 2.048 \text{ MIPS}$$

### Step 5 — Required Processor Speed

The processor must be clocked such that 2.048 million cycles occur per second:

$$f_{\text{clk,min}} = 2.048 \text{ MHz}$$

A real-world DSP would typically run at 50–100 MHz, providing a safety margin of 25×–50× for overhead, interrupts, and additional processing (e.g., gain control, sidechain processing).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Block-Processing Throughput for FFT

**Problem Statement:** A 1024-point radix-2 FFT must execute in real-time for a sonar system sampling at $f_s = 1 \text{ MHz}$. Compute the minimum MIPS needed assuming the FFT requires $\frac{N}{2} \log_2 N$ complex MACs.

### Step 1 — Compute Number of Complex MACs for FFT

$$\text{MACs}_{\text{FFT}} = \frac{N}{2} \log_2 N = \frac{1024}{2} \cdot \log_2(1024) = 512 \cdot 10 = 5120 \text{ complex MACs}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Block Deadline

The block contains $L = 1024$ samples, sampled at $f_s = 1 \text{ MHz}$:

$$T_{\text{DL}} = \frac{L}{f_s} = \frac{1024}{10^6} = 1.024 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Required MAC Rate

The processor must execute 5120 complex MACs in 1.024 ms:

$$\text{MAC rate} = \frac{5120}{1.024 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ MACs/s} = 5 \text{ MMACS}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to MIPS

Assuming 4 cycles per complex MAC (typical for fixed-point DSPs):

$$\text{MIPS} = 5 \times 10^6 \cdot 4 = 20 \times 10^6 = 20 \text{ MIPS}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Minimum Clock Frequency

$$f_{\text{clk,min}} = 20 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Modern DSPs (e.g., TMS320C6748) run at 300–456 MHz, giving 15×–22× margin. This margin is critical because it accommodates the data movement (memory accesses) which often dominates real-world DSP workloads.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Real-Time DSP Pipeline with Constraint Annotations

The following diagram maps the data flow through a canonical real-time DSP system, annotating each block with the timing constraint it contributes to the overall system budget.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AnalogInputSignal] --> B[AntiAliasingFilter]
    B --> C[ADCSampler]
    C --> D[InputBufferDMA]
    D --> E[DSPCoreProcessing]
    E --> F[OutputBufferDMA]
    F --> G[DACReconstructor]
    G --> H[ReconstructionFilter]
    H --> I[AnalogOutputSignal]
    E -.-> E1[AlgorithmicLoadMIPS]
    E -.-> E2[LatencyBudgetus]
    C -.-> C1[SamplingPeriodTs]
    G -.-> G1[DACConversionDelay]
    D -.-> D1[MemoryBandwidthBytes]
    F -.-> F1[MemoryBandwidthBytes]
    E2 -.-> J[RealTimeFeasibilityCheck]
    E1 -.-> J
    C1 -.-> J
    D1 -.-> J
    G1 -.-> J
    J --> K{ConstraintSatisfied}
    K -->|YES| L[SystemOperational]
    K -->|NO| M[BufferUnderflowOverrun]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The dotted lines from each block to the central `RealTimeFeasibilityCheck` node represent the *parameters* exported by each block into the feasibility equation. The diamond-shaped `ConstraintSatisfied` node is the decision gate that determines whether the system operates correctly or fails.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hierarchical View of Real-Time Constraint Categories

This diagram organizes the four categories of real-time constraints discussed in Section 2 into a hierarchical taxonomy, showing how each category decomposes into measurable sub-parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RTC[RealTimeConstraints] --> THR[ThroughputConstraint]
    RTC --> LAT[LatencyConstraint]
    RTC --> DDL[DeadlineConstraint]
    RTC --> DET[DeterminismConstraint]
    THR --> THR1[MSPSMetric]
    THR --> THR2[MIPSCapacityCheck]
    THR --> THR3[MemoryBandwidthCheck]
    LAT --> LAT1[ADCDelay]
    LAT --> LAT2[BufferDelay]
    LAT --> LAT3[AlgoExecutionTime]
    LAT --> LAT4[DACDelay]
    LAT --> LAT5[FilterGroupDelay]
    DDL --> DDL1[BlockWindowTDivfs]
    DDL --> DDL2[AbsoluteDeadlineTime]
    DDL --> DDL3[WCETBound]
    DET --> DET1[JitterMetric]
    DET --> DET2[WorstCaseExecutionTime]
    DET --> DET3[CacheStallAnalysis]
    DET --> DET4[DMAOverlapSchedule]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Real-Time Feasibility Analysis

This flowchart captures the procedural logic a KTU student should follow when assessing whether a proposed DSP design will meet its real-time constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[StartSpecifyAlgorithmAndSamplingRate] --> S2[ComputeAlgorithmicLoadMOPS]
    S2 --> S3[ComputeRequiredMIPS]
    S3 --> S4{ProcessorMIPSAvailable}
    S4 -->|Insufficient| F1[FeasibilityFailureChooseFasterDSP]
    S4 -->|Sufficient| S5[ComputeLatencyBudget]
    S5 --> S6{ApplicationLatencyThreshold}
    S6 -->|Exceeded| F2[FeasibilityFailureReduceAlgoComplexity]
    S6 -->|WithinBudget| S7[AnalyzeWCET]
    S7 --> S8{WCETLessThanDeadline}
    S8 -->|No| F3[FeasibilityFailureRefactorAlgorithm]
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[VerifyMemoryBandwidth]
    S9 --> S10{BusBandwidthSufficient}
    S10 -->|No| F4[FeasibilityFailureAddDMAChannels]
    S10 -->|Yes| P1[DesignIsRealTimeFeasible]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Use This Diagram in Exam Answers:** When a KTU question asks "Is this system feasible?", trace the path through this flowchart. Each diamond decision point is a potential 1–2 mark answer point. The "feasible" path requires 4 sequential YES answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Real-Time Constraint. Differentiate between Hard and Soft Real-Time Systems. Give one DSP example for each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Real-Time Constraint is a strict timing requirement imposed on a DSP system such that the correctness of the output depends on both the *computational result* and the *time* at which the result is delivered. The fundamental requirement is that processing of each input sample (or block) must complete within the inter-sample interval $T_s = 1/f_s$.

**Hard vs Soft Real-Time (2 marks):**

- **Hard Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline constitutes a complete system failure with potentially catastrophic consequences. The worst-case execution time (WCET) must be provably less than the deadline. *Example:* Missile guidance radar — a missed sample causes loss of target lock, leading to mission failure.
- **Soft Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline causes quality degradation but the system continues to operate. Late results may be discarded or used with reduced weight. *Example:* MP3 audio decoder — a missed deadline causes a brief audio dropout (click), but playback continues.

---

### Question 2: What is Throughput Constraint in a Real-Time DSP System? Write the formula relating sampling frequency, number of operations per sample, and MIPS. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Throughput Constraint Definition (1 mark):** Throughput Constraint specifies the minimum rate at which the DSP must process input samples to keep pace with the incoming data stream. It is measured in **MSPS** (Million Samples Per Second) and is directly related to the sampling frequency $f_s$.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$\text{MIPS}_{\text{required}} = \frac{N_{\text{ops/sample}} \cdot f_s}{10^6}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** The MIPS rating of the chosen processor must exceed $\text{MIPS}_{\text{required}}$ with a safety margin (typically 2×–5×) to accommodate I/O, interrupts, and other overhead. For example, a 64-tap FIR filter at 8 kHz sampling requires $64 \cdot 8000 / 10^6 = 0.512$ MIPS, easily met by any modern DSP.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Choice Based)

### Question A (14 Marks): Throughput and Latency Analysis of a Multi-Rate DSP System `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the minimum processor clock frequency required to implement a 128-tap FIR filter in real-time at a sampling rate of 44.1 kHz, assuming the processor requires 4 clock cycles per MAC operation. State the assumption made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital audio mixing console has the following latency components: A/D conversion = 30 μs, input buffering = 12 μs, algorithmic processing = 250 μs, D/A conversion = 30 μs, and reconstruction filter group delay = 18 μs. Calculate the total system latency. Comment on whether this is acceptable for a live performance scenario and justify your answer using the relevant perceptual threshold. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — FIR Filter Equation:**

The 128-tap FIR filter output is:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{127} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute Operations Per Sample:**

A 128-tap FIR filter requires 128 multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations per output sample.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Total Clock Cycles Per Sample:**

Each MAC operation takes $\tau = 4$ clock cycles, so:

$$C_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \tau = 128 \cdot 4 = 512 \text{ cycles per sample}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Real-Time Constraint:**

The execution time per sample must not exceed the inter-sample period:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{44{,}100} = 22.676 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Derive Minimum Clock Frequency:**

$$T_{\text{exec}} = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{f_{\text{clk}}} \le T_s$$

Rearranging:

$$f_{\text{clk}} \ge \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{T_s} = C_{\text{total}} \cdot f_s = 512 \cdot 44{,}100$$

$$f_{\text{clk,min}} = 22{,}579{,}200 \text{ Hz} = 22.58 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Stated Assumption:**

The derivation assumes a single-cycle architecture where each instruction completes in one clock cycle, and that no memory stalls, interrupts, or peripheral I/O operations consume processor bandwidth.

**Final Answer:** $f_{\text{clk,min}} = 22.58 \text{ MHz}$. A processor running at 50 MHz or higher would provide a comfortable safety margin.

#### Model Solution to Part A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Substitute Latency Components:**

$$T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{ADC}} + T_{\text{buf}} + T_{\text{algo}} + T_{\text{DAC}} + T_{\text{filter}}$$

$$T_{\text{latency}} = 30 + 12 + 250 + 30 + 18$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Calculate Total:**

$$T_{\text{latency}} = 340 \text{ \mu s} = 0.34 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Perceptual Threshold:**

The well-established perceptual threshold for audio latency in live performance is **10 ms** (10,000 μs). This is the maximum delay below which musicians do not perceive timing discrepancies between their action and the heard sound. Above 10 ms, musicians report playing "out of time" and may experience rhythmic desynchronization.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Comparison and Conclusion:**

Since $0.34 \text{ ms} = 340 \text{ \mu s} \ll 10{,}000 \text{ \mu s}$, the system latency is **acceptable for live performance**. The delay is approximately **29 times below** the perceptual threshold, meaning musicians will not detect any lag between striking a key and hearing the sound through the monitor speakers.

**Verdict:** ✓ **Real-time constraint satisfied.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Determinism and Worst-Case Execution Time in Real-Time DSP `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Determinism in real-time DSP systems. Why is determinism critical for hard real-time applications, and what architectural features of a DSP processor ensure it? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radar signal processing system must complete 2400 complex MAC operations within a 250 μs deadline (determined by the antenna rotation rate). Compute the minimum MIPS required. If the chosen DSP operates at 100 MHz and executes one complex MAC in 5 clock cycles, determine if the real-time constraint is satisfied. Show all calculation steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition of Determinism:**

Determinism is the property that the execution time of a given DSP algorithm is **bounded and predictable** across all invocations. In a deterministic system, the worst-case execution time (WCET) can be mathematically guaranteed to be below a known upper bound. The opposite — *non-determinism* — occurs when execution time varies unpredictably due to cache misses, pipeline stalls, interrupts, or memory contention.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Why Determinism is Critical for Hard Real-Time:**

For hard real-time applications (missile guidance, anti-collision radar, pacemakers), missing a deadline causes **catastrophic system failure**. Therefore, the system designer must be able to *prove* that every possible execution scenario will complete before the deadline. Without determinism, this proof is impossible — the system might be fast enough on average, but a rare cache miss could push execution beyond the deadline, causing a failure that may be unacceptable in safety-critical systems.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Architectural Features Ensuring Determinism in DSPs:**

DSP processors are specifically designed with the following features to ensure timing determinism:

1. **Single-Cycle MAC Unit:** A dedicated hardware multiplier-accumulator completes one MAC operation in a fixed number of clock cycles, independent of data values.
2. **No Data Cache or Locked Cache:** Many DSPs (e.g., older TMS320C5x) omit caches entirely, eliminating cache-miss variability. Modern DSPs use software-managed cache locking.
3. **No Dynamic Branch Prediction:** Branches are typically resolved in a known number of cycles; no speculative execution.
4. **DMA-Driven I/O with Circular Buffers:** Data movement occurs in parallel with computation via DMA controllers, with deterministic transfer times. The DSP core is not stalled for I/O.
5. **No Garbage Collection or Dynamic Memory Allocation:** Memory is statically allocated at compile time; no runtime overhead.
6. **Memory Architecture with Separate Program and Data Buses (Harvard Architecture):** Allows simultaneous instruction fetch and data access, eliminating the von Neumann bottleneck and its associated timing variability.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify Given Parameters:**

- Complex MACs per block: $N_{\text{CMAC}} = 2400$
- Deadline: $T_{\text{DL}} = 250 \text{ \mu s} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$
- DSP clock: $f_{\text{clk}} = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Cycles per complex MAC: $\tau = 5$ cycles

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute Total Required Clock Cycles:**

$$C_{\text{req}} = N_{\text{CMAC}} \cdot \tau = 2400 \cdot 5 = 12{,}000 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute Minimum MIPS Required:**

$$\text{MIPS}_{\text{required}} = \frac{C_{\text{req}}}{T_{\text{DL}} \cdot 10^6} = \frac{12{,}000}{250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^6}$$

$$\text{MIPS}_{\text{required}} = \frac{12{,}000}{250} = 48 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute Available MIPS of the Chosen DSP:**

$$\text{MIPS}_{\text{available}} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{cycles per instruction} \cdot 10^6} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1 \cdot 10^6} = 100 \text{ MIPS}$$

*(Note: Assuming 1 instruction = 1 cycle for this architecture; for a VLIW DSP executing multiple instructions per cycle, this could be higher.)*

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Feasibility Verdict:**

Since $\text{MIPS}_{\text{available}} = 100 \text{ MIPS} > \text{MIPS}_{\text{required}} = 48 \text{ MIPS}$, the **real-time constraint is satisfied** with a safety margin of:

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{100 - 48}{48} \times 100\% = 108.3\%$$

The system has more than double the required computational capacity, leaving comfortable room for additional processing (e.g., target detection, thresholding, communication overhead).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always carry units through the calculation. Writing "MIPS = 48" without showing $12{,}000 / (250 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6)$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing complex MACs with real MACs:** A complex MAC involves 4 real multiplies and 2 real adds. The 5 cycles/CMAC given in the problem accounts for this — students often incorrectly multiply by 4.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption about CPI:** The 1 instruction/cycle assumption must be explicitly stated.
> 4. **Skipping the comparison statement:** A feasibility problem requires a yes/no verdict *with justification*. Writing only the numbers without a concluding "The constraint is satisfied" loses the final 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up WCET and average execution time:** For hard real-time, *WCET* must be less than the deadline, not the average. Always use the worst case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Real-Time Constraints (Module 1)**

- [x] **Real-Time Definition:** Correctness depends on *both* result and *timing*.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $T_{\text{process}} \le T_s = 1/f_s$ is the most-tested formula.
- [x] **Four Categories:** Throughput, Latency, Deadline, Determinism.
- [x] **Hard vs. Soft:** Hard = catastrophic failure; Soft = degraded quality.
- [x] **FIR Throughput:** $f_{\text{clk,min}} = N \cdot \tau \cdot f_s$ — memorize and derive.
- [x] **Latency Formula:** $T_{\text{lat}} = T_{\text{ADC}} + T_{\text{buf}} + T_{\text{algo}} + T_{\text{DAC}} + T_{\text{filter}}$.
- [x] **Block Deadline:** $T_{\text{DL}} = L / f_s$ where $L$ is the block size.
- [x] **MIPS Calculation:** $\text{MIPS} = (N_{\text{ops}} \cdot f_s) / 10^6$ — always include the $10^6$ factor.
- [x] **Jitter:** $J = T_{\max} - T_{\min}$ measures determinism.
- [x] **Determinism Features:** Harvard architecture, no cache, DMA, single-cycle MAC.
- [x] **WCET vs Average:** Use *worst-case* for hard real-time proofs.
- [x] **Memory Bandwidth:** $B_{\text{mem}} = 2 \cdot N \cdot f_s \cdot W$ for FIR filter data movement.
- [x] **Anc Example Latency:** Must be < 100 μs to avoid positive feedback.
- [x] **Audio Perceptual Threshold:** 10 ms is the live performance limit.
- [x] **Exam Pitfall:** Always convert μs ↔ s ↔ ms carefully; $1 \text{ \mu s} = 10^{-6} \text{ s}$.
- [x] **Exam Pitfall:** State assumptions explicitly — single-cycle, no overhead, no I/O stalls.
- [x] **Mnemonic:** "**Hard = Harm; Soft = Slight**" for hard/soft real-time classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Likely KTU Exam Question Formats for This Topic:**
> 1. "Compute the minimum clock frequency for an N-tap FIR filter at sampling rate $f_s$." → Apply the throughput formula.
> 2. "Calculate the total system latency given a list of delay components." → Sum the components.
> 3. "Determine if the real-time constraint is satisfied given processor speed, sampling rate, and operations per sample." → MIPS comparison with explicit yes/no verdict.
> 4. "Explain hard vs soft real-time with examples." → Always pair the definition with a domain-specific DSP example.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DSP System Design - Algorithm Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DSP System Design - Algorithm Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Real-Time Digital Signal Processing (RT-DSP)**, **DSP System Design – Algorithm Development** refers to the systematic, mathematically rigorous procedure of translating a real-world signal processing requirement (e.g., filtering, spectral analysis, modulation) into a well-defined, finite-step computational procedure that can be executed on a programmable DSP processor within strict real-time timing constraints.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 framework, the DSP system design process follows a hierarchical top-down methodology consisting of **three fundamental stages**:

1. **System Level / Algorithm Development** – Defining *what* must be computed (mathematical operations, transforms, difference equations).
2. **Processor / Architecture Mapping** – Deciding *how* and *where* each computation will be performed (hardware selection, memory allocation, pipelining).
3. **Real-Time Implementation** – Embedding the algorithm into the target DSP hardware (assembly, C, or hardware description language) such that the output is produced before the next input sample arrives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> *Algorithm Development* is the bridge between pure mathematical theory (Z-transform, DFT, difference equations) and the physical silicon of a DSP processor. The KTU Module 1 outcomes (CO1) specifically test the student's ability to **derive, optimize, and represent DSP algorithms in formats suitable for real-time execution** (e.g., difference equations, signal flow graphs, block diagrams).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a high-speed commercial kitchen** preparing 500 meals per hour. Here is the analogy:

- **The Real-World Signal** = the *incoming stream of customer orders*.
- **The DSP Algorithm** = the *recipe* (a clear, step-by-step procedure: chop, boil, fry, plate).
- **The DSP Processor** = the *kitchen equipment* (ovens, stoves, blenders) with limited capacity.
- **Real-Time Constraint** = the *deadline*: each meal must leave the kitchen exactly when the customer expects it, not a second later.

A *bad* chef would try to cook all 500 meals one ingredient at a time (sample-by-sample inefficient processing). A *good* chef (algorithm developer) would **batch-process** similar meals, **pre-stage** commonly used ingredients (look-ahead/pipelining), and **reuse** the same cutting board efficiently (memory optimization). This is exactly what DSP algorithm development does: it takes the raw mathematical "recipe" of a signal processor and refines it into an efficient, scheduled procedure that fits the kitchen (processor) precisely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Algorithm development is **NOT** about inventing new math. It is about *structurally restructuring existing mathematical operations* (sums, products, delays) to meet the three pillars of real-time DSP: **speed, accuracy, and memory efficiency**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following standardized engineering metrics form the backbone of DSP algorithm design and are **frequently tested in KTU university exams**:

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** – the rate at which the input signal is digitized, measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **Samples per Second (SPS)**.
- **Nyquist Rate ($f_N = 2f_m$)** – the *minimum* sampling frequency required to perfectly reconstruct a band-limited analog signal of maximum frequency $f_m$.
- **Quantization Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** – **SQNR ≈ 6.02N + 1.76 dB**, where $N$ is the number of bits in the ADC/DAC.
- **Instruction Cycle Time ($T_{cyc}$)** – time taken by the DSP processor to execute one instruction, typically **5–20 ns** for modern fixed-point DSPs.
- **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) Time** – the time to perform one multiplication and one addition, the *fundamental* DSP atomic operation.
- **Throughput** – samples processed per second by the algorithm.
- **Latency** – the time delay (in samples or seconds) between input and corresponding output.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage DSP System Design Flow

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes DSP system design into a **top-down hierarchical flow**. A student must internalize this hierarchy because Part B (14-mark) questions frequently ask to *trace* a design requirement through all three stages.

#### Stage 1: Algorithm Development (Software / Mathematical Level)

- Convert the system specification into a **difference equation**, **transfer function**, or **convolution sum**.
- Choose the appropriate transform domain: **time-domain FIR/IIR**, **frequency-domain DFT/FFT**, or **z-domain pole-zero placement**.
- Optimize for computational cost: minimize the number of **multiplications, additions, memory accesses, and data transfers**.
- Represent the algorithm visually using **signal flow graphs (SFG)** or **block diagrams**.

#### Stage 2: Architecture Mapping (Hardware Abstraction Level)

- Select a target DSP architecture: **Von Neumann**, **Modified Harvard**, or **Super Harvard (SHARC)**.
- Partition the algorithm into tasks that map efficiently onto **ALU, MAC unit, shifter, and address generation unit (AGU)**.
- Decide data representation: **fixed-point Q15, Q31** vs **floating-point IEEE 754**.

#### Stage 3: Real-Time Implementation (Hardware Level)

- Write the algorithm in **C, assembly, or a hybrid** for the chosen DSP processor (e.g., TMS320C6x, SHARC, Blackfin).
- Verify that execution time per sample is less than the sampling period.
- Profile memory usage (program + data + coefficient memory) to fit on-chip resources.

### 2.2 Core Algorithmic Building Blocks

Every DSP algorithm (filter, FFT, modulator, demodulator) is built from the following primitive operations:

| Building Block | Mathematical Form | DSP Hardware Realization |
|---|---|---|
| Multiplication | $y = a \cdot x$ | Hardware Multiplier |
| Addition | $y = a + b$ | ALU |
| Delay (Shift) | $y[n] = x[n-1]$ | Memory Register / Circular Buffer |
| Accumulation | $y = \sum a_i x_i$ | MAC Unit (Multiply-Accumulate) |
| Scaling (Shift) | $y = a \cdot 2^k$ | Barrel Shifter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the MAC unit so important?**
> Because **every FIR filter, every IIR filter, and every butterfly in an FFT** can be reduced to a sequence of MAC operations. A DSP processor's speed is essentially defined by how fast its MAC unit can perform these operations. This is why a modern DSP boasts **Multi-MAC** units (e.g., the TMS320C67x has two MAC units running in parallel, doubling throughput).

### 2.3 Real-Time Constraints — The Heart of Algorithm Development

A DSP algorithm is said to be **real-time** if and only if the following condition is satisfied for *every* input sample:

$$T_{\text{process}} \leq T_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{process}}$ = time taken by the processor to compute one full output sample.
- $T_{\text{sample}}$ = time interval between two consecutive input samples.
- $f_s$ = sampling frequency.

If $T_{\text{process}} > T_{\text{sample}}$, the system **overflows** — output lags input, causing data loss (a real-time failure).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Determining minimum ADC rate |
| 2 | $T_{\text{sample}} = 1/f_s$ | Sampling period | Real-time feasibility check |
| 3 | $T_{\text{process}} \leq T_{\text{sample}}$ | Real-time condition | Pass/Fail of an algorithm |
| 4 | $\text{MACs per sample} = N$ | FIR filter order (length $N+1$ has N MACs) | Computational load estimation |
| 5 | $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | Quantization SNR | Bit-precision design |
| 6 | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | General LTI system TF | Algorithm specification |
| 7 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$ | Difference equation | Direct implementation |
| 8 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}} \times \text{cycles per sample}}$ | Samples per second | Speed benchmarking |
| 9 | $\text{CMFL} = 2^{B-1} - 1$ | Max fixed-point magnitude ($B$-bit signed) | Overflow analysis |
| 10 | $\text{Memory (bytes)} = 2 \times N \times B / 8$ | Circular buffer size ($N$ taps, $B$ bits) | Memory budgeting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoiding Markdown Errors:** In the formulas above, notice that absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` or contextually avoided inside table cells to prevent breaking the KTU 2024 markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` for absolute value in your answers.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Audio Engineering:** MP3/AAC decoders running on smartphone DSP chips (e.g., Qualcomm Hexagon) require algorithm development for real-time playback.
- **Telecommunications:** OFDM modulation in 4G/5G base stations uses FFT/IFFT algorithms optimized for real-time DSP (e.g., TI KeyStone).
- **Medical Imaging:** MRI and ultrasound machines use real-time DSP for beamforming and image reconstruction.
- **Radar and Sonar:** Adaptive beamforming and matched filtering require pipelined DSP algorithms.
- **Control Systems:** Digital motor controllers (Field-Oriented Control) implement real-time IIR filters on micro-DSP cores.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Real-Time Feasibility Equation

**Problem Statement:** Given a sampling frequency $f_s$ and a DSP processor with instruction cycle time $T_{\text{cyc}}$, derive the condition under which an $N$-tap FIR filter can be executed in real time.

**Step 1 — Express the sampling period.**
The sampling period is the reciprocal of the sampling frequency:

$$T_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

**Step 2 — Express the computational load of an FIR filter.**
An $N$-tap FIR filter (with $N$ coefficients) requires exactly **$N$ multiplications** and **$N-1$ additions** for every output sample. On a modern DSP with a single-cycle MAC unit, this can be reduced to **$N$ MAC cycles**:

$$C_{\text{FIR}} = N \quad \text{(MAC cycles per output sample)}$$

**Step 3 — Express the time taken to execute the algorithm.**
If each MAC cycle takes $T_{\text{MAC}}$ seconds, then the total time to process one sample is:

$$T_{\text{process}} = N \times T_{\text{MAC}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the real-time constraint.**
For the filter to operate in real time, the processor must finish its computation *before* the next sample arrives:

$$T_{\text{process}} \leq T_{\text{sample}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve.**
Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 3:

$$N \times T_{\text{MAC}} \leq \frac{1}{f_s}$$

Rearranging to find the maximum allowable tap length $N$:

$$N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{1}{f_s \cdot T_{\text{MAC}}} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
This is a *hard* engineering limit. If the required filter order (e.g., 100 taps) exceeds $N_{\max}$, the system **cannot** run in real time on that processor. The developer must either:
- Lower the sampling rate (often unacceptable in the application),
- Choose a faster processor (costly), or
- Use **algorithmic optimization** (pipelining, parallel MAC, block processing) to reduce effective $T_{\text{MAC}}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantization SNR for a Fixed-Point ADC

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) of a uniform $N$-bit ADC.

**Step 1 — Quantization step size.**
For a uniform $N$-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$ (peak-to-peak), the quantization step $\Delta$ is:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization noise power.**
Assuming the quantization error $e$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with zero mean, its variance (noise power) is:

$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Signal power for a full-scale sine wave.**
A full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $A = V_{FS}/2$ has RMS power:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the SNR ratio.**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{\Delta^2/12}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$.**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{V_{FS}^2 \cdot 2^{-2N}/12} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot 2^{2N} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels.**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 20 N \log_{10}(2)$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the constants.**
Since $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ and $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering Implication.**
Every additional bit of ADC resolution improves SNR by approximately **6 dB** (a factor of 2 in voltage, or 4 in power). For example, a 16-bit ADC (common in audio) theoretically provides:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

This is why CD-quality audio (16-bit, 44.1 kHz) is widely accepted as transparent.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Computational Complexity of Direct-Form FIR vs. FFT-Based Convolution

**Problem Statement:** Compare the number of multiplications required by (a) direct-form FIR convolution and (b) FFT-based fast convolution for an $L$-point filter and an $M$-point input block.

**Step 1 — Direct-form FIR convolution complexity.**
Each output sample requires $L$ multiplications. For $M$ output samples:

$$C_{\text{direct}} = L \times M$$

**Step 2 — FFT-based fast convolution complexity.**
Using the overlap-add or overlap-save method, the FFT must be of length $N \geq L + M - 1$ (next power of 2). The number of complex multiplications is:

$$C_{\text{FFT}} = 3 \times \frac{N}{2} \log_2 N + L$$

(Three FFTs: forward, frequency-domain multiply with $H(k)$, inverse FFT, plus the $L$ multiplies in frequency domain.)

**Step 3 — Crossover point.**
FFT-based convolution is more efficient when:

$$L \times M \gg 3 \times \frac{N}{2} \log_2 N + L$$

**Step 4 — Practical observation.**
For short filters ($L < 64$), direct-form is faster. For long filters with block processing ($L > 256$, $M$ large), FFT-based convolution wins. This is a key trade-off in algorithm development for real-time systems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Development Decision Tree

| Design Question | Decision Criterion | Likely KTU Answer |
|---|---|---|
| FIR or IIR? | Phase linearity required? | Yes → FIR; No → IIR |
| Direct Form I or II? | Memory-constrained? | Yes → DF-II (canonic) |
| Block or sample processing? | Latency vs. throughput? | Real-time audio → block |
| Fixed or floating point? | Cost vs. dynamic range? | Cost-sensitive → fixed |
| Pipelining? | High throughput required? | Yes → use MAC pipelining |
| Circular buffering? | Delay line needed? | Yes → circular buffer |
| Look-ahead transformation? | Need to break feedback loop? | Yes → look-ahead |
| FFT or direct DFT? | $N$ large (>32)? | Yes → FFT |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Down DSP System Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Real World Application e.g. Audio Filtering] --> B[Step2: Define Specifications fs, Attenuation, Phase]
    B --> C[Step3: Algorithm Development Difference Equation and Signal Flow Graph]
    C --> D[Step4: Computational Analysis Count MACs and Memory Accesses]
    D --> E{Step5: Real Time Feasible on Target DSP?}
    E -- No --> F[Step5a: Algorithm Optimization Pipelining and Look Ahead]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G[Step6: Architecture Mapping ALU MAC Shifter AGU]
    G --> H[Step7: Code Implementation C plus Assembly]
    H --> I[Step8: Profiling and Verification on Target Hardware]
    I --> J[Step9: Real Time DSP Product]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 1 Interpretation:** This is the canonical KTU-recommended DSP design methodology. Notice the *feedback loop* from Step 5 to Step 3 — this represents the iterative nature of algorithm optimization, where a real-time failure forces the developer back to the algorithm stage to apply techniques such as pipelining, look-ahead, or block processing.

### 4.2 Generic DSP Processor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PGM[Program Memory] -->|Instruction Fetch| CTL[Program Sequencer and Control Unit]
    CTL --> DEC[Instruction Decoder]
    DEC --> AGU[Address Generation Unit AGU]
    AGU -->|Addresses| DMEM[Data Memory]
    DMEM -->|Operands| REG[Register File]
    REG --> MUL[Hardware Multiplier]
    MUL --> ALU[ALU and Shifter]
    ALU --> ACC[Accumulator]
    ACC --> REG
    ACC -->|Output| OUT[Output Data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 2 Interpretation:** The Modified Harvard architecture shown here is the *backbone* of nearly all commercial DSP processors (TMS320, SHARC, Blackfin). The **multiplier → ALU → accumulator** chain is the dedicated data path that makes MAC operations execute in a single cycle — the single most important reason DSP processors outperform general-purpose microcontrollers for signal processing.

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Real-Time FIR Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Sample x of n] --> SHIFT[Shift Register Circular Buffer]
    SHIFT --> MAC1[MAC Stage Multiply by h0]
    MAC1 --> SUM[Accumulator]
    SUM --> DELAY1[Feedback Path for Next Coefficient]
    DELAY1 --> MAC2[MAC Stage Multiply by h1]
    MAC2 --> SUM
    SUM --> DELAY2[Feedback Path]
    DELAY2 --> MAC3[MAC Stage Multiply by hN minus 1]
    MAC3 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Output Sample y of n]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 3 Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates the *direct-form FIR* structure as a sequence of cascaded MAC operations on a tapped delay line. Each tap reads one delayed sample $x[n-k]$, multiplies it by the corresponding coefficient $h[k]$, and accumulates the result. This is the **most common algorithm developed** in KTU Module 1 laboratory and examination problems.

### 4.4 Algorithm Optimization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OPT[Algorithm Optimization Required] --> Q1{Deadline Missed by How Much?}
    Q1 -- Less than 20 percent --> PIPE[Apply Pipelining to MAC Unit]
    Q1 -- 20 to 50 percent --> LA[Apply Look Ahead Transformation]
    Q1 -- More than 50 percent --> BP[Switch to Block Processing]
    PIPE --> RE[Re Evaluate Timing]
    LA --> RE
    BP --> RE
    RE --> OK{Real Time Met?}
    OK -- Yes --> DONE[Algorithm Approved]
    OK -- No --> HW[Consider Hardware Upgrade]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define Real-Time Digital Signal Processing. List any two key features of a DSP processor that distinguish it from a general-purpose microprocessor.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Real-Time DSP refers to a class of signal processing in which the output samples are produced within a strict, bounded time interval such that the result is available *before* the next input sample arrives. The defining condition is:

$$T_{\text{process}} \leq T_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

**Two distinguishing features of a DSP processor:**
1. **Hardware Multiplier and MAC Unit:** A dedicated single-cycle multiply-accumulate unit enables efficient execution of the dot-product operations fundamental to all DSP algorithms (filters, FFTs). General-purpose microprocessors require many cycles for a single multiplication.
2. **Modified Harvard Architecture:** Separate program and data memory buses allow simultaneous instruction fetch and data access in a single cycle, doubling throughput compared to the single-bus Von Neumann architecture of conventional microprocessors.

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> What is meant by the term "algorithm development" in the context of DSP system design? Why is the signal flow graph (SFG) representation preferred over a difference equation in algorithm development?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Algorithm development in DSP is the stage of system design where the mathematical specification of a signal processing task (e.g., a difference equation, a transfer function, or a convolution sum) is converted into a structured, executable procedure suitable for implementation on a DSP processor. It includes specifying the order of operations, memory access patterns, and computational structure.

**SFG is preferred over a difference equation because:**
1. **Visual identification of parallelism:** SFG explicitly shows which operations are independent and can be executed in parallel, which is critical for exploiting multi-MAC DSP architectures.
2. **Hardware mapping:** Each node of the SFG maps directly to a DSP hardware element (multiplier, adder, delay), making architectural mapping straightforward.
3. **Canonical form analysis:** SFGs reveal redundancies (e.g., common subexpressions) that can be eliminated to reduce computational cost, which is not obvious from a textual difference equation.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, describe the top-down design methodology for a real-time DSP system. Explain each stage in detail. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An FIR filter of length $N = 64$ taps is to be implemented on a DSP processor with an instruction cycle time of $T_{\text{cyc}} = 10$ ns. Each MAC operation requires 1 cycle. The sampling frequency is $f_s = 1$ MHz. Determine whether the filter can be executed in real time. If not, suggest two algorithmic optimization techniques to make it feasible. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a)**

The top-down design methodology for a real-time DSP system consists of the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Application and Specification:**
The design begins with the real-world application (e.g., audio equalization, biomedical signal denoising). The developer defines quantitative specifications: sampling frequency $f_s$, required passband/stopband attenuation, group delay tolerance, and output SNR.

**Stage 2 — Algorithm Development:**
The specification is translated into a mathematical procedure — typically a difference equation derived from the desired transfer function $H(z)$. For an FIR filter of order $N$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

The algorithm is then represented as a **signal flow graph** that identifies the sequence of MAC operations and the delay-line (tapped) memory structure.

**Stage 3 — Computational Analysis:**
The number of multiplications, additions, and memory accesses per output sample is counted. For an $N$-tap FIR filter, the count is $N$ multiplications and $N-1$ additions per output sample.

**Stage 4 — Architecture Selection:**
A DSP processor is chosen that provides the required MAC throughput, memory bandwidth, and I/O capability. The decision between fixed-point (low cost, low power) and floating-point (high dynamic range, easier algorithm development) is made here.

**Stage 5 — Real-Time Feasibility Check:**
The execution time per sample is computed and compared against the sampling period $T_{\text{sample}} = 1/f_s$. If $T_{\text{process}} > T_{\text{sample}}$, the algorithm must be optimized.

**Stage 6 — Optimization and Implementation:**
Techniques such as pipelining, look-ahead, and block processing are applied. The algorithm is then coded in C, assembly, or a hybrid and downloaded to the target DSP.

**Stage 7 — Verification:**
The real-time system is tested with representative input signals, and metrics such as SNR, group delay, and throughput are measured.

*(Block diagram reference: See Figure 1 in Section 4.1.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b)**

**Given:**
- $N = 64$ taps
- $T_{\text{cyc}} = 10$ ns
- 1 MAC per cycle
- $f_s = 1$ MHz

**Step 1 — Compute the sampling period.**

$$T_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1 \, \mu\text{s} = 1000 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the processing time per sample.**
For an FIR filter of length $N = 64$ with 1 MAC per cycle, the number of MAC cycles required is 64. So:

$$T_{\text{process}} = 64 \times T_{\text{cyc}} = 64 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 640 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compare against real-time constraint.**

$$T_{\text{process}} = 640 \text{ ns} \leq T_{\text{sample}} = 1000 \text{ ns} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The filter **can** be executed in real time, with a timing margin of $1000 - 640 = 360$ ns (36% slack).

**Step 4 — Discussion of optimization (in case of failure):**
If the problem had yielded $T_{\text{process}} > T_{\text{sample}}$, two algorithmic optimization techniques would be:
1. **Pipelining the MAC unit** — overlap the multiplication of sample $i+1$ with the accumulation of sample $i$, effectively halving the per-sample cost.
2. **Block Processing** — compute $L$ output samples at once, exploiting the reduced complexity of FFT-based convolution (fast convolution) for large $L$, achieving a net reduction in per-sample computational cost.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the three primary stages of a DSP system design process with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) unit in this process. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize an audio signal with a full-scale sinusoidal amplitude of 2 V peak-to-peak. Calculate the theoretical signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) in dB. If the application requires an SQNR of at least 90 dB, determine the minimum number of additional bits required. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a)**

**The three primary stages of DSP system design:**

**1. Algorithm Development:** This stage defines *what* the system must compute. Example: For a 1 kHz digital low-pass filter with 40 dB stopband attenuation, the algorithm is specified as a 50-tap FIR filter with windowed-sinc coefficients, and is represented as a difference equation or signal flow graph.

**2. Processor / Architecture Mapping:** This stage decides *how* the algorithm is mapped onto a processor. Example: Choosing a TMS320C5509 fixed-point DSP for a hearing aid, with 16-bit Q15 data representation, a single-cycle MAC unit, and 128 KB on-chip RAM.

**3. Real-Time Implementation:** This stage embeds the algorithm into the target. Example: Writing the FIR filter in mixed C and assembly, with circular buffer addressing for the delay line, and verifying on hardware using a logic analyzer.

**Role of the MAC unit:**
The MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) unit is the *atomic computational engine* of a DSP processor. It performs a multiplication and an addition in a single instruction cycle:

$$\text{ACC} \leftarrow \text{ACC} + (A \times B)$$

Almost every signal processing kernel — FIR filtering, IIR filtering, matrix multiplication, FFT butterfly, convolution — can be decomposed into a sequence of MAC operations. The presence of a single-cycle MAC unit is the *defining* feature that distinguishes a DSP processor from a general-purpose microprocessor, and is the single largest contributor to meeting real-time computational deadlines.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b)**

**Given:**
- ADC resolution $N = 12$ bits
- Full-scale range $V_{FS} = 2$ V (peak-to-peak)

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical SQNR using the standard formula:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76$$

Substituting $N = 12$:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the requirement.**
The required SQNR is 90 dB. Currently we have 74 dB, which is **insufficient** (short by 16 dB).

**Step 3 — Calculate the additional bits needed.**

Let $N_{\text{new}} = 12 + \Delta N$ be the new resolution. Then:

$$6.02 \times N_{\text{new}} + 1.76 \geq 90$$

$$6.02 \times N_{\text{new}} \geq 88.24$$

$$N_{\text{new}} \geq \frac{88.24}{6.02} \approx 14.66$$

Since $N_{\text{new}}$ must be an integer:

$$N_{\text{new}} = 15 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Number of additional bits required:**

$$\Delta N = 15 - 12 = 3 \text{ additional bits}$$

**Verification:** With 15 bits, $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 15 + 1.76 = 90.3 + 1.76 = 92.06$ dB $\geq$ 90 dB. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid**
>
> 1. **Sign error in SQNR formula:** Many students write `6.02N − 1.76`. The correct form is **+1.76 dB** for a full-scale sine wave. Using the minus sign loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit confusion in timing:** $T_{\text{sample}}$ must be in the *same* unit as $T_{\text{cyc}}$. Mixing microseconds and nanoseconds is a frequent silent error; always convert to a common unit (e.g., both in ns) before comparing.
> 3. **Confusing FIR length with order:** An FIR filter of *length* $L$ has $L$ taps and $L-1$ delays, not $L-1$ taps. The number of MAC operations equals the number of taps, not the order.
> 4. **Forgetting the Nyquist factor of 2:** Sampling rate must be $\geq 2f_m$, not $\geq f_m$. Marks are routinely deducted for the missing factor of 2.
> 5. **Not stating the real-time condition explicitly:** KTU examiners expect a *box* or a clear inequality stating $T_{\text{process}} \leq T_{\text{sample}}$ before any calculation. Skipping this loses the first step's marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Summary — DSP System Design / Algorithm Development**

- ☐ **DSP Design Flow** = Application → Algorithm → Architecture → Implementation. Memorize the 7-stage top-down methodology.
- ☐ **Real-Time Condition:** $T_{\text{process}} \leq T_{\text{sample}} = 1/f_s$. Always state this as the first line of any real-time feasibility problem.
- ☐ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The factor of 2 is non-negotiable.
- ☐ **SQNR Formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76$ (for full-scale sinusoid).
- ☐ **MAC Unit** = single-cycle Multiply-Accumulate. The atomic engine of all DSP processors.
- ☐ **FIR Filter:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h[k] x[n-k]$. Requires $N$ MACs per output sample.
- ☐ **Modified Harvard Architecture** = separate program and data memory buses. Enables single-cycle instruction + data fetch.
- ☐ **Algorithm Optimization Tools:** Pipelining, Look-Ahead, Block Processing, Circular Buffering, Fast Convolution (FFT-based).
- ☐ **SFG (Signal Flow Graph):** Preferred over difference equation for algorithm development because it visualizes parallelism and hardware mapping.
- ☐ **Computational Complexity:** Direct FIR = $O(NM)$; FFT-based convolution = $O(N \log N)$ — crossover typically at $N > 64$.
- ☐ **Memory Budget:** For a circular buffer of $N$ taps at $B$ bits, memory = $2 \times N \times B/8$ bytes (account for ping-pong buffering).
- ☐ **Fixed-Point vs Floating-Point:** Fixed-point is faster and cheaper but has overflow/range issues; floating-point is easier to develop but uses more silicon and power.
- ☐ **Block Processing Advantage:** Allows FFT-based fast convolution for long filters, dramatically reducing per-sample cost.
- ☐ **Look-Ahead Transformation:** Breaks feedback loops in recursive algorithms to enable pipelining.
- ☐ **Causal vs Non-Causal:** A real-time DSP system *must* be causal (output depends only on present and past inputs).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of DSP Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of DSP Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DSP Hardware Selection** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating and choosing a Digital Signal Processor (or DSP system-on-chip) whose computational architecture, numerical precision, memory bandwidth, I/O capability, power budget, and economic cost best satisfy the real-time performance constraints of a target signal-processing application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"DSP hardware selection is a trade-off analysis between computational throughput (measured in **MIPS / MMACS / MFLOPS**), numerical precision (**fixed-point vs floating-point**), on-chip memory size, peripheral set, power dissipation, and unit cost — all bounded by the real-time deadline of the application."*

The primary candidates under evaluation are:
1. **General-Purpose Microprocessors (GPP)** — e.g., ARM Cortex, x86.
2. **Microcontrollers (MCU)** — e.g., ARM Cortex-M, PIC, MSP430.
3. **Dedicated DSP Processors** — e.g., TI TMS320C6x, SHARC ADSP-21xxx, Analog Devices Blackfin.
4. **FPGAs / ASICs** — for parallel hardware acceleration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "Choosing the Right Workhorse"**
> Imagine you need to transport 10,000 bricks across a city before sunset (your real-time deadline).
> - A **family hatchback** (GPP) can do it, but slowly, with many trips.
> - A **pickup truck** (Microcontroller) handles moderate loads efficiently.
> - A **dump truck** (DSP) is purpose-built for hauling — fast, rugged, but expensive and overkill for groceries.
> - A **conveyor-belt factory line** (FPGA/ASIC) is the fastest, but only economical if you are hauling bricks every single day for years.
>
> The "selection" is the engineering judgement that matches the **load (algorithm complexity)**, **distance (sample rate × latency)**, **deadline (real-time constraint)**, and **fuel budget (power)** to the most suitable vehicle.

## 1.3 The "Big Five" Selection Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU exam answer on this topic must mention these five pillars:
> 1. **Computational Throughput** (MIPS / MOPS)
> 2. **Numerical Precision & Dynamic Range** (Q-format vs IEEE-754)
> 3. **Memory Architecture** (Harvard, on-chip RAM/ROM, cache)
> 4. **I/O & Peripherals** (McBSP, I²S, DMA, EMIF)
> 5. **Power Dissipation & Cost** (mW per MIPS, USD per unit)

## 1.4 Fixed-Point vs Floating-Point — The Core Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Fixed-Point DSP:** Operates on integers in Q-format (e.g., Q.15). Faster, cheaper, lower power, but the programmer must scale every coefficient manually to avoid overflow.
>
> **Floating-Point DSP:** Operates on IEEE-754 single/double precision. Massive dynamic range (~1500 dB), no scaling headaches, but 2–4× larger silicon, more power, higher cost.
>
> **Rule of thumb:** Audio/video algorithms with high coefficient sensitivity → floating-point. High-volume, low-power embedded (hearing aids, earbuds) → fixed-point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Selection Logic — Step by Step

A rigorous KTU answer should follow this analytical chain:

**Step 1 — Quantify the Algorithm Load (Computational Complexity)**
- Count multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations per output sample.
- For an N-tap FIR filter, samples per output = **N MACs**.
- For a Biquad IIR section, samples per output = **5 MACs**.

**Step 2 — Determine the Sampling Rate Fs**
- Audio CD: **44.1 kHz**, Pro-audio: **48 kHz / 96 kHz**, Wideband radar: **> 100 MHz**.

**Step 3 — Calculate Required Throughput**
$$\text{Required MACs/sec} = (\text{MACs per output}) \times F_s$$

**Step 4 — Add Algorithmic & Architectural Overhead**
- Factor in I/O latency, DMA stalls, context switches, OS jitter (typically 30–50% headroom).

**Step 5 — Map to Available DSP Clock & IPC**
$$\text{MIPS}_{\text{required}} \leq \frac{f_{\text{clk}} \times \text{IPC}}{1 + \text{overhead}}$$

where IPC = Instructions Per Cycle (e.g., VLIW TMS320C6x can do up to 8 MACs/cycle).

**Step 6 — Verify Memory Bandwidth**
- The "Von Neumann bottleneck": one MAC needs **2 fetches (data) + 1 fetch (coefficient) + 1 write (output)**.
- Harvard architecture (separate program & data buses) breaks this bottleneck.

**Step 7 — Check Power Budget**
$$\text{Power} \approx \alpha \, C \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{\text{clk}}$$

A doubling of clock speed can **quadruple** dynamic power, so always run at the *minimum* sufficient clock.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Why it matters in real-time DSP |
|---|---|
| Harvard architecture | Allows simultaneous fetch of instruction + 2 data operands → enables single-cycle MAC |
| Hardware MAC unit | Avoids emulating MAC with shift-and-add → **10–100×** speed-up |
| Pipelining | Increases clock frequency; reduces critical path delay |
| On-chip RAM | Off-chip memory access takes 5–20 cycles; on-chip takes 1 cycle |
| DMA engine | Frees CPU from I/O bookkeeping → enables true parallelism |
| Zero-overhead looping | Avoids branch penalty in tight inner loops (FIR/FFT kernels) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — at least two formulas appear in every KTU Module 1 exam.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Required MAC rate | $\text{MAC}_{\text{req}} = N \cdot F_s$ | MACs / second | Filter throughput |
| 2 | Required clock | $f_{\text{clk}} \geq \dfrac{\text{MAC}_{\text{req}}}{\text{IPC}}$ | MHz | DSP clock selection |
| 3 | SNR of N-bit ADC / fixed-point DSP | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB | Quantisation analysis |
| 4 | Dynamic range of N-bit system | $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20\,N \log_{10} 2 \approx 6.02\,N$ | dB | Headroom for floating-point |
| 5 | Dynamic power dissipation | $P = \alpha \, C \, V^{2} f$ | Watts | Power budget analysis |
| 6 | Energy per MAC | $E_{\text{MAC}} = P / \text{MAC}_{\text{rate}}$ | nJ / MAC | Battery-life estimation |
| 7 | MIPS rating | $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk}} / \text{CPI}$ | Million Instr / s | General throughput |
| 8 | MMACS rating | $\text{MMACS} = \dfrac{\text{MACs per sec}}{10^{6}}$ | Million MACs / s | DSP-specific throughput |
| 9 | Q-format range | $\text{Range} = \pm 2^{\,m-1}$ to $2^{\,m-1} - 2^{-n}$ | numeric | Fixed-point scaling |
| 10 | Q-format resolution | $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ | unitless | LSB weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The Power formula $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ is the *single most important equation* in portable DSP — it explains why every modern mobile DSP (e.g., Qualcomm Hexagon, Apple Neural Engine) uses **dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)** and why hearing-aid DSPs run at sub-mW.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hearing Aids** (e.g., Starkey, Phonak) → ultra-low-power fixed-point DSPs from ON Semi (formerly AMI Semiconductor) running at < 1 mW.
- **5G Base Stations** → Massive-MIMO demands hundreds of MMACS → uses TI KeyStone II / C6678 multi-core DSPs.
- **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC)** → floating-point during R&D, fixed-point for production silicon.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI, Ultrasound)** → beamforming requires 10s–100s of GMACS → FPGAs or GPU acceleration.
- **Automotive Radar (77 GHz)** → Real-time FFT on IF signal → TI C6000 or NXP DSP + FPGA hybrid.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required MIPS for an N-tap FIR Filter

**Problem Statement:** An FIR filter with $N = 64$ taps is to operate on a sampled signal at $F_s = 48\text{ kHz}$. The target DSP executes one MAC per cycle (IPC = 1). Compute the minimum clock frequency and MIPS rating, assuming 30% architectural overhead.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of MACs per output sample.**
For an FIR filter, the difference equation is:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \, x[n-k]$$

This sum contains exactly $N$ multiplications and $N-1$ additions, i.e., one MAC per tap. Hence:
$$\text{MACs per output} = N = 64$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required MAC throughput.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MAC}_{\text{req}} &= N \times F_s \\
&= 64 \times 48{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}072{,}000 \ \text{MACs/sec} \\
&= 3.072 \ \text{MMACS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 30% architectural overhead factor.**
The effective requirement is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MAC}_{\text{eff}} &= \text{MAC}_{\text{req}} \times (1 + \text{OH}) \\
&= 3.072 \times 1.30 \\
&= 3.9936 \ \text{MMACS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Map to minimum clock frequency (since IPC = 1).**
$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{clk, min}} &= \text{MAC}_{\text{eff}} \\
&= 3.9936 \ \text{MHz} \\
&\approx 4 \ \text{MHz (rounded up to nearest standard value)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MIPS.**
Since CPI = 1 when IPC = 1 (one MAC = one instruction):
$$\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk, min}} = 4 \ \text{MIPS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 4-MIPS DSP is trivially cheap — even an ARM Cortex-M0 can do this. The KTU lesson is that for *audio-bandwidth* FIR filtering, the computational load is modest; the bottleneck shifts to **memory and power**, not raw MIPS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SNR & Dynamic Range for Fixed vs Floating Point

**Given:** A 16-bit fixed-point DSP and a 32-bit IEEE-754 single-precision floating-point DSP.

**Step 1 — Compute SNR of the 16-bit fixed-point system.**
Using the canonical quantisation formula (assuming uniform rounding quantisation of an ideal sinusoid):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{dB} &= 6.02\,N + 1.76 \\
&= 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 \\
&= 96.32 + 1.76 \\
&= 98.08 \ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dynamic range of the floating-point system.**
A 32-bit IEEE-754 float has 23 mantissa bits, giving effectively:
$$\text{DR}_{\text{float, dB}} \approx 6.02 \times 24 \approx 144.5 \ \text{dB}$$
(Equivalent to a 24-bit fixed-point system in mantissa precision, but with a 254-bit exponent range for scaling.)

**Step 3 — Convert dB SNR to linear amplitude ratio.**
$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{16-bit}} &= 10^{98.08/20} = 10^{4.904} \approx 80{,}170 \\
A_{\text{32-float}} &= 10^{144.5/20} = 10^{7.225} \approx 1{,}678{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 32-bit float offers ~21× more linear dynamic range than a 16-bit fixed-point. For applications with large dynamic range (e.g., audio mixing a 120 dB SPL kick drum with a -90 dB FS whisper), the floating-point DSP is essential unless careful Q-format scaling is done by the programmer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power-Budget Analysis Using $P = \alpha C V^2 f$

**Given:** A DSP running at $f_1 = 100\text{ MHz}$ at $V_1 = 1.2\text{ V}$ dissipates $P_1 = 50\text{ mW}$. Assume activity factor $\alpha C = K$ is constant. Find $P_2$ at $f_2 = 200\text{ MHz}$ and $V_2 = 1.0\text{ V}$ (DVFS profile).

**Step 1 — Solve for the constant $K$ from the first operating point.**
$$P_1 = K \, V_1^{\,2} \, f_1 \implies K = \frac{P_1}{V_1^{\,2} f_1}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{(1.2)^2 \times 100 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.44 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= 3.472 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{W·s/V}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_2$ at the new voltage and frequency.**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= K \, V_2^{\,2} \, f_2 \\
&= 3.472 \times 10^{-10} \times (1.0)^2 \times 200 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 3.472 \times 10^{-10} \times 2 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 6.944 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 69.44 \ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**
Although the frequency doubled (potentially doubling compute throughput), the **voltage drop from 1.2 V to 1.0 V** reduced the $V^2$ term by a factor of $(1.0/1.2)^2 = 0.694$. Net result: only **1.39× more power** for **2× the throughput** — a *33% energy-efficiency gain per MAC*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the foundational math behind **DVFS in mobile SoCs (Snapdragon, Apple A-series, Exynos)** — and is a frequent KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DSP Family Comparison

| Feature | TI TMS320C55x (Fixed) | TI TMS320C67x (Float) | SHARC ADSP-214xx | ARM Cortex-M4 (MCU w/ DSP ext.) | FPGA (e.g., Xilinx Zynq) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | VLIW / Harvard | VLIW / Harvard | SIMD / Harvard | Von Neumann + MAC ext. | Parallel fabric |
| Precision | 16-bit fixed | 32/64-bit float | 32-bit float + 16-bit fixed | 32-bit fixed (Q) | Arbitrary bit-width |
| Peak MMACS | 600 | 1000 | 400 | 50 | 10 000+ (per fabric slice) |
| Power (typ.) | 0.15 mW/MIPS | 0.5 mW/MIPS | 0.3 mW/MIPS | 0.01 mW/MHz | 1–10 W |
| On-chip RAM | 320 KB | 256 KB | 5 Mb | 256 KB | Block RAM (Mb) |
| Cost (low vol.) | \$3–8 | \$15–40 | \$10–25 | \$1–3 | \$20–200 |
| Best for | Low-power audio, voice | Research, prototyping | Pro-audio, military | IoT edge, motor control | High-throughput radar/5G |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — DSP Hardware Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Algorithm] --> B[Count MACs per Output Sample]
    B --> C[Multiply by Fs to get MACs per second]
    C --> D{Is MAC rate under 50 MMACS?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Need floating point?}
    D -- No --> F{Can algorithm be parallelised?}
    E -- Yes --> G[Use Floating Point DSP e.g. SHARC]
    E -- No --> H[Use Fixed Point DSP e.g. TMS320C55x]
    F -- Yes --> I[Use FPGA or GPU]
    F -- No --> J[Use Multi core DSP e.g. C6678]
    G --> K[Check Power Budget]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Power under 100 mW?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Final Selection]
    L -- No --> N[Use DVFS or offload to FPGA]
    N --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal DSP Architecture (Harvard + MAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PG[Program Memory Bus] --> CD[Control Decode Unit]
    DA[Data Memory Bus A] --> MAC[Hardware MAC Unit]
    DB[Data Memory Bus B] --> MAC
    CD --> MAC
    MAC --> ACC[Accumulator Register]
    ACC --> SH[Barrel Shifter]
    SH --> DA
    PG --> PL[Pipeline Registers]
    PL --> CD
    DMA[DMA Engine] --> DA
    DMA --> DB
    PER[Pheripherals McBSP I2S UART] --> DB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Trade-off Map — The Five Selection Axes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERF[Performance Axis]
        P1[MIPS MMACS]
        P2[Latency]
        P3[Real time deadline]
    end
    subgraph PREC[Precision Axis]
        Q1[Fixed point Q format]
        Q2[Floating point IEEE 754]
    end
    subgraph MEM[Memory Axis]
        M1[On chip RAM KB]
        M2[External memory interface]
        M3[Harvard bus]
    end
    subgraph IO[IO Axis]
        I1[DMA]
        I2[Serial ports]
        I3[Timers interrupts]
    end
    subgraph PWR[Power Cost Axis]
        W1[mW per MIPS]
        W2[Unit cost USD]
        W3[Package size]
    end
    PERF --- PREC
    PREC --- MEM
    MEM --- IO
    IO --- PWR
    PWR --- PERF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (Block-Level Functional Architecture):** The trade-off map illustrates that DSP selection is *not* a 1-D optimisation — it is a **closed-loop design problem** where every choice in the Performance axis feeds back into Power/Cost, which in turn restricts what level of Performance is economically achievable. The five axes (Performance, Precision, Memory, I/O, Power/Cost) form a **pentagonal dependency graph** that a KTU examiner expects you to be able to *describe* even without seeing a diagram.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Real-Time DSP System-on-Chip

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AIN[Analog Input] --> ADC[ADC]
    ADC --> DMA1[DMA Channel 0]
    DMA1 --> RAM1[On chip Data RAM]
    CPU[CPU with MAC and ALU] --> PMEM[Program ROM Flash]
    CPU --> RAM1
    CPU --> RAM2[On chip Coefficient RAM]
    CPU --> ACC[Accumulator]
    ACC --> DAC[DAC]
    DAC --> AOUT[Analog Output]
    DMA2[DMA Channel 1] --> RAM1
    SER[Serial Port McBSP] --> DMA2
    TIM[Timer] --> CPU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A frequent 14-mark question asks to *"draw and explain the block diagram of a typical DSP system."* You must show: **ADC → DMA → On-chip RAM → CPU/MAC → Accumulator → DAC**, and the role of Harvard buses separating program from data memory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any three key parameters that must be considered while selecting a DSP processor for a real-time application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Computational Throughput** — measured in MIPS or MMACS; must satisfy the worst-case real-time deadline of the algorithm. *(1 mark)*
2. **Numerical Precision** — choice between fixed-point (Q-format) and floating-point (IEEE-754) based on dynamic range and coefficient sensitivity. *(1 mark)*
3. **Power Dissipation & Cost** — quantified as mW/MIPS and USD/unit; critical for battery-powered and high-volume products. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> *Valid alternatives: Memory architecture (Harvard vs Von Neumann), I/O bandwidth (DMA, serial ports), and on-chip memory size also carry full marks.*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between fixed-point and floating-point DSP processors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed-Point DSP | Floating-Point DSP |
|---|---|---|
| Data format | Integer / Q-format (e.g., Q.15) | IEEE-754 single/double precision |
| Dynamic range | $\approx 6.02N$ dB | $\approx 1500$ dB (huge) |
| Cost & power | Low | High (2–4× silicon) |
| Programming | Manual scaling needed | Compiler handles scaling |
| Speed (per clock) | Faster MAC | Slightly slower MAC |

*(1 mark per correct point; minimum 3 distinct points required for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *(Module 1, Choice Option A)*

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **five major criteria** used for the selection of DSP hardware in real-time embedded systems. For each criterion, state its engineering significance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 128-tap FIR filter must operate on a signal sampled at $F_s = 8\text{ kHz}$. The DSP has IPC = 2 (can execute 2 MACs per cycle). Compute the minimum clock frequency required, assuming 25% architectural overhead. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The five major criteria for DSP hardware selection are:

**1. Computational Throughput (MIPS / MMACS)** *(1.5 marks)*
The DSP must execute all MAC operations within the sample period $T = 1/F_s$. Insufficient throughput causes buffer overruns and lost samples.

**2. Numerical Precision (Fixed vs Floating Point)** *(1.5 marks)*
Determines the signal-to-quantisation-noise ratio via $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. Floating-point offers higher dynamic range but consumes more power and silicon.

**3. Memory Architecture** *(1.5 marks)*
Harvard architecture (separate program/data buses) enables single-cycle MAC by fetching two operands simultaneously. On-chip RAM minimises the wait-state penalty of off-chip access (5–20 cycles).

**4. I/O Bandwidth & Peripherals** *(1.5 marks)*
DMA engines free the CPU from data-movement overhead. Serial ports (McBSP, I²S, SPI) must match the data rate of external ADCs/DACs.

**5. Power Dissipation & Cost** *(1 mark)*
Given by $P = \alpha C V^2 f$. Lower mW/MIPS and USD/MMACS are preferred for portable and high-volume products.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 128$, $F_s = 8\text{ kHz}$, IPC = 2, overhead = 25%.

**Step 1 — MACs per output sample:**
$$\text{MACs/sample} = N = 128$$

**Step 2 — Raw MAC throughput:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MAC}_{\text{raw}} &= N \times F_s \\
&= 128 \times 8000 \\
&= 1.024 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 1.024 \ \text{MMACS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply 25% overhead:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MAC}_{\text{eff}} &= 1.024 \times 1.25 \\
&= 1.28 \ \text{MMACS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum clock:**
$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{clk, min}} &= \frac{\text{MAC}_{\text{eff}}}{\text{IPC}} \\
&= \frac{1.28 \times 10^{6}}{2} \\
&= 0.64 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.64 \ \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer with engineering rounding:**
$$\boxed{f_{\text{clk, min}} \approx 1 \ \text{MHz (next standard value above 0.64 MHz)}}$$

**Verification:** At 1 MHz with IPC = 2, throughput = 2 MMACS, headroom = (2 − 1.28)/2 = 36% — acceptable. *(1 mark for engineering interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *(Module 1, Choice Option B)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a typical Harvard-architecture DSP processor**. Highlight the role of the **hardware MAC unit** and **DMA controller**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A DSP runs at $V_1 = 1.0\text{ V}$, $f_1 = 200\text{ MHz}$ and dissipates $P_1 = 80\text{ mW}$. Compute the new power dissipation $P_2$ when the voltage is scaled to $V_2 = 0.8\text{ V}$ and the frequency to $f_2 = 150\text{ MHz}$. Comment on the energy efficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Block diagram (described in words for the answer script):**

The typical Harvard DSP contains:
- **Program Memory Bus** connecting Program ROM/Flash to the Control Unit.
- **Two Data Memory Buses (A and B)** connecting Data RAM to the MAC and ALU.
- **Control Decode Unit** that fetches instructions from Program Memory and generates control signals.
- **Hardware Multiplier–Accumulator (MAC)** — the heart of the DSP; performs one $A \times B + C$ operation in a single clock cycle.
- **Barrel Shifter** — for variable left/right shifts in a single cycle (essential for Q-format scaling).
- **Accumulator Register** — wide (40-bit in TMS320C55x) to prevent intermediate overflow.
- **DMA Controller** — autonomous data mover that transfers ADC samples into Data RAM *without CPU intervention*, freeing the CPU for computation.
- **Peripherals** — McBSP, timers, EMIF, GPIO.

**Role of Hardware MAC (2 marks):**
Without a hardware MAC, an FIR tap would take 10–20 instructions (load, multiply, accumulate, store). The dedicated MAC unit reduces this to **1 cycle per tap**, yielding a 10–100× speed-up. It also allows two operands to be fetched simultaneously via the dual data buses — the essence of the Harvard advantage.

**Role of DMA Controller (2 marks):**
In real-time DSP, the CPU cannot afford to spend cycles moving samples between ADC and memory. The DMA engine uses a dedicated bus to copy data autonomously, allowing **overlapped I/O and compute** — the key to sustained real-time throughput.

*(1 mark for labelled block diagram, 2 marks for MAC explanation, 2 marks for DMA explanation, 2 marks for Harvard-bus justification.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_1 = 1.0\text{ V}$, $f_1 = 200\text{ MHz}$, $P_1 = 80\text{ mW}$, $V_2 = 0.8\text{ V}$, $f_2 = 150\text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Extract the constant $K = \alpha C$ from the first operating point.**
$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{P_1}{V_1^{\,2} f_1} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 10^{-3}}{(1.0)^2 \times 200 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{W·s/V}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_2$ at the new operating point.**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= K \, V_2^{\,2} \, f_2 \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{-10} \times (0.8)^2 \times 150 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.64 \times 1.5 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 9.6 \times 10^{7} \\
&= 3.84 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 38.4 \ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Energy efficiency comparison.**

Power ratio:
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{38.4}{80} = 0.48 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 52\% \text{ power saving}$$

Throughput ratio (assumed proportional to frequency):
$$\frac{f_2}{f_1} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 25\% \text{ throughput loss}$$

Energy per MAC ratio:
$$\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{P_2 / f_2}{P_1 / f_1} = \frac{0.48}{0.75} = 0.64$$

$$\boxed{36\% \text{ energy saving per MAC at the cost of 25\% lower throughput.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** This is the classic DVFS trade-off. For a battery-powered DSP, running at 0.8 V / 150 MHz is significantly more energy-efficient *per computation*, but the algorithm must still meet its real-time deadline at 150 MHz. If it does, this scaling is the correct design choice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise (–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting architectural overhead** — students compute $f_{\text{clk}} = N \times F_s$ and stop, ignoring 25–50% real-world overhead. Always state and apply the overhead factor explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing MIPS and MMACS** — MIPS = million instructions per second (generic CPU metric); MMACS = million MAC operations per second (DSP-specific). They differ by a factor equal to the average MACs-per-instruction ratio.
> 3. **Using $|\cdot|$ (vertical pipe) in formula tables** — KTU digital submissions break markdown tables if you write $\vert x \vert$ as `|x|`. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — "$f_{\text{clk}} = 1$" loses a mark; write "$f_{\text{clk}} = 1\text{ MHz}$" to secure full credit.
> 5. **Stating "DSP is faster than microcontroller" without justification** — must reference the **hardware MAC unit** and **Harvard architecture** specifically.
> 6. **Confusing Harvard with Von Neumann** — Von Neumann has *one* shared bus; Harvard has *separate* program and data buses (often two data buses). Drawing one bus loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DSP Hardware Selection**
> - [ ] DSP selection = trade-off across **5 axes**: Throughput, Precision, Memory, I/O, Power/Cost.
> - [ ] **Fixed-point** = Q-format, fast, low-power, requires manual scaling → SNR $= 6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
> - [ ] **Floating-point** = IEEE-754, huge dynamic range (~1500 dB), 2–4× costlier, easier programming.
> - [ ] **Harvard architecture** = separate program & data buses → enables single-cycle MAC.
> - [ ] **Hardware MAC unit** = 10–100× speed-up over emulated multiply-accumulate.
> - [ ] **DMA controller** = offloads I/O from CPU → enables overlapped I/O and compute.
> - [ ] **Power formula:** $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ → voltage scaling is the most effective lever.
> - [ ] **Clock formula:** $f_{\text{clk, min}} = (\text{MACs per sample} \times F_s \times (1+\text{OH})) / \text{IPC}$.
> - [ ] **mW/MIPS** is the standard energy-efficiency metric for embedded DSPs.
> - [ ] **On-chip RAM** eliminates 5–20 cycle off-chip wait-states.
> - [ ] **Pipelining** increases throughput at the cost of latency and branch-penalty sensitivity.
> - [ ] **Zero-overhead looping** and **hardware circular buffers** are DSP-specific features absent in GPPs.
> - [ ] **DVFS** (Dynamic Voltage & Frequency Scaling) is the primary mobile-DSP power-saving technique.
> - [ ] **Common families:** TI C55x (fixed), TI C67x/C66x (float), SHARC (float+fixed), Blackfin (hybrid), ARM Cortex-M4 (MCU+DSP extensions), FPGA (parallelism).
> - [ ] Always include **architectural overhead (25–50%)** in any MIPS/MMACS calculation.
> - [ ] Always state **units** (MHz, mW, MMACS, USD) explicitly in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Development and Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Development and Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
