# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A = 3 marks (short answer); Part B = 14 marks (full descriptive, with **internal choice** between two questions).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
*With a neat block diagram, explain the Von Neumann architecture. State its major drawback for real-time DSP applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Block diagram: single memory, single address bus, single data bus connecting CPU, memory, and I/O.
- **(1 mark)** Operation: instructions and data are fetched *sequentially* over the same bus.
- **(1 mark)** Drawback → **Von Neumann Bottleneck**: bus contention between instruction fetch and data fetch prevents the single-cycle, deterministic throughput required for real-time DSP (e.g., MAC per sample).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
*List any three DSP-specific addressing modes and give one real-time DSP use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Circular Addressing** → maintains delay-line / FIFO pointer for FIR/IIR filters (1 mark).
2. **Bit-reversed Addressing** → reorders butterfly outputs/inputs for in-place radix-2 FFT (1 mark).
3. **Auto-increment Indirect** → streams sample-by-sample through DM with zero overhead (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Harvard & Pipelining Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare Von Neumann and Harvard architectures with diagrams. Explain **why Modified Harvard is preferred** in modern real-time DSPs such as the TMS320C6x family. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage pipeline DSP runs at $f_{\text{clk}} = 200$ MHz. For $K = 500$ instructions, compute the (i) non-pipelined execution time, (ii) pipelined execution time, and (iii) actual speedup. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

- **(2 marks)** Von Neumann: single memory, single bus, sequential access. *(Mention Von Neumann Bottleneck.)*
- **(2 marks)** Harvard: separate PM/DM, separate buses, parallel fetch — supports single-cycle MAC.
- **(2 marks)** Modified Harvard: retains PM/DM separation but adds a **cross-path** so data can be read from PM (e.g., coefficients). Also includes instruction cache that unifies program flow logically.
- **(1 mark)** Conclusion: TMS320C6x uses Modified Harvard with VLIW — 8 functional units (`.L1, .L2, .S1, .S2, .M1, .M2, .D1, .D2`), two register files, and cross-paths to read constants from PM. This achieves high IPC while still allowing efficient coefficient access.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** $N = 5$ stages, $f_{\text{clk}} = 200$ MHz, $K = 500$ instructions.

**Step 1 — Clock period**

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 5\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 2 — Non-pipelined time (each instruction uses 5 stages sequentially)**

$$T_{\text{non-pipe}} = K \cdot N \cdot T_{\text{clk}} = 500 \times 5 \times 5\,\text{ns} = 12{,}500\,\text{ns} = 12.5\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Pipelined time (fill + K + drain)**

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (K + 2N - 1) \times T_{\text{clk}} = (500 + 2(5) - 1) \times 5\,\text{ns} = 509 \times 5\,\text{ns} = 2545\,\text{ns} = 2.545\,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Actual speedup**

$$S = \frac{12{,}500}{2545} \approx 4.912$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**
The actual speedup (≈ 4.91) is slightly less than the ideal 5× because of the **fill + drain overhead** of $2N - 1 = 9$ cycles, amortized over $K = 500$. As $K \to \infty$, the speedup approaches the ideal $N = 5$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — MAC & Circular Buffer Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a **single-cycle MAC unit** in a real-time DSP. Show how it computes $Y = A \cdot B + C$ in one cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time DSP runs an FIR filter of order $N = 128$ at a sampling rate $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (CD-quality audio). Assuming the DSP has a single MAC unit with $\text{CPI}_{\text{MAC}} = 1$:
    (i) Derive the minimum clock frequency needed to meet the real-time deadline.
    (ii) If the actual DSP clock is $f_{\text{clk}} = 150$ MHz, compute the **CPU utilization** for the FIR computation.
    (iii) If a new requirement demands a *fifth-order* polyphonic processing (5 parallel audio channels), determine whether the same DSP can handle it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

- **(2 marks)** Block diagram: hardware multiplier (e.g., $16 \times 16$-bit), wide adder (e.g., 40-bit), and accumulator register (e.g., A or B) with overflow flag.
- **(2 marks)** Data path: register file → multiplier inputs A & B; multiplier output + previous accumulator value → adder → accumulator.
- **(2 marks)** Single-cycle operation: the multiplier and adder are *combinational*, registered only at the accumulator → result $Y = A \cdot B + C$ is stable by the next clock edge.
- **(1 mark)** Wider accumulator (40-bit in TMS320) prevents overflow during long MAC chains (e.g., 256-tap FIR at 16-bit samples needs 8 headroom bits).

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Minimum clock frequency derivation**

$$f_{\text{clk,min}} = N \cdot \text{CPI}_{\text{MAC}} \cdot f_s = 128 \times 1 \times 44.1 \times 10^3$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{\text{clk,min}} = 5.6448\,\text{MHz}\,}$$

**(ii) CPU utilization at $f_{\text{clk}} = 150$ MHz**

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{f_{\text{clk,min}}}{f_{\text{clk,actual}}} = \frac{5.6448}{150} = 0.0376 \approx 3.76\%$$

**(iii) Polyphonic scaling for 5 channels**

Required MACs per second (5 channels, each 128-tap at 44.1 kHz):

$$\text{MACs/s} = 5 \times 128 \times 44.1 \times 10^3 = 2.8224 \times 10^7\,\text{MACs/s}$$

Available MACs/s at 150 MHz:

$$\text{MACs/s}_{\text{avail}} = 150 \times 10^6 \times 1 = 1.5 \times 10^8$$

Utilization for 5 channels:

$$U_{5\text{ch}} = \frac{2.8224 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^8} \approx 0.1882 \approx 18.82\%$$

**Conclusion:** Yes — the same DSP comfortably handles 5 channels with ~19% utilization, leaving > 80% headroom for codec, I/O, and system tasks. ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in CPU Architecture Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting CPI in the FIR clock equation.** The correct form is $f_{\text{clk,min}} = N \cdot \text{CPI} \cdot f_s$, not $N \cdot f_s$ alone. (−1 to −2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing latency with throughput.** Pipeline *latency* = $N \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ (one instruction takes N cycles to finish). Pipeline *throughput* = $1/T_{\text{clk}}$ (one instruction completes per cycle *after* fill). Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Drawing Von Neumann with two buses.** If the diagram shows a separate program and data bus, it is *Harvard*, not Von Neumann. Examiners auto-deduct for mislabelling.
> 4. **Ignoring the cross-path in Modified Harvard.** A block diagram with no path from PM to data units loses the "Modified" distinction.
> 5. **Omitting the word *deterministic*.** Real-time DSP is not just *fast* — it is **bounded-latency**. Phrases like "real-time needs speed" alone are incomplete. Mention **determinism / bounded worst-case execution time (WCET)**.
> 6. **Circular buffer wrap formula written as `mod L` only.** Examiners expect explicit handling: $\text{AR}_n = (\text{AR}_n + 1)$; if $\text{AR}_n = L$ then $\text{AR}_n \leftarrow 0$. One-line answer is fine, but the logic must be visible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Three architectures:** Von Neumann (1 bus, bottleneck) → Harvard (2 buses, parallel) → Modified Harvard (2 buses + cross-path for constants).
- [x] **Why DSP ≠ GPP:** DSPs are *deterministic*, *single-cycle MAC*, *Harvard-native*, *with DSP-specific addressing*.
- [x] **Pipeline speedup (ideal):** $S = N$ (number of stages); (realistic) $\dfrac{K \cdot N}{K + 2N - 1}$; (with bottlenecks) Amdahl: $\dfrac{1}{(1-P) + P/N}$.
- [x] **MAC unit** computes $Y = A \cdot B + C$ in **one cycle** with a wide accumulator (40-bit typical) to prevent overflow.
- [x] **FIR real-time constraint:** $f_{\text{clk,min}} = N \cdot \text{CPI}_{\text{MAC}} \cdot f_s$.
- [x] **Circular addressing:** $\text{AR}_n \leftarrow (\text{AR}_n + 1) \bmod L$ — zero overhead, used for delay lines.
- [x] **Bit-reversed addressing:** for in-place radix-2 FFT; e.g., $\text{rev}(011_2) = 110_2 = 6$.
- [x] **Register file partitioning:** accumulators (A, B), auxiliary registers (AR0–AR7), index/status, page registers.
- [x] **On-chip vs off-chip memory:** on-chip SRAM = single-cycle (latency-critical); off-chip = multi-cycle (bulk storage).
- [x] **DMA + zero-overhead loops:** keep the CPU free of sample-movement overhead — the *real-time* contract.
- [x] **VLIW (TMS320C6x):** 8 functional units, instruction-level parallelism, static scheduling → Modified Harvard under the hood.
- [x] **Power equation (bonus):** $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V^2 f$ — drives low-power DSP selection for portable/edge devices.
- [x] **Examiner mantras to memorise:** *"deterministic," "Von Neumann Bottleneck," "single-cycle MAC," "zero-overhead loop," "Modified Harvard cross-path."*

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "V-H-M-P-M-A-R":**
> **V**on Neumann, **H**arvard, **M**odified Harvard → **P**ipelining → **M**AC unit → **A**ddressing modes (circular, bit-rev) → **R**egisters & on-chip memory.
> Recite this in the exam hall before writing the answer to a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPU Data Paths and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CPU Data Paths and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a CPU Data Path?

The **CPU Data Path** is the collection of functional hardware units within the Central Processing Unit that collectively move, transform, and store binary data during instruction execution. It comprises the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), multiplier-accumulators (MAC), register files, multiplexers, demultiplexers, and the internal bus structures that interconnect them.

In the context of **Real-Time DSP**, the data path is not a general-purpose integer pipeline. It is a **specialised heterogeneous processing fabric** engineered to sustain the high computational throughput demanded by kernels such as FIR filters, FFTs, and matrix multiplications within deterministic time budgets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The Data Path is the portion of the CPU that performs arithmetic, logic, and data-movement operations. It is steered by externally generated control signals originating from the Control Unit. Together, the Data Path and Control Unit constitute the CPU datapath-controller pair.

## 1.2 What is the Control Unit?

The **Control Unit (CU)** is the "conductor" of the CPU. It receives the binary machine instruction from memory, decodes its opcode, and emits a precisely timed sequence of **control signals** that activate registers, gate bus drivers, select ALU operands, and steer the flow of data through the data path.

Two principal implementations exist:

1. **Hardwired Control** – Realised using a Finite State Machine (FSM), Programmable Logic Array (PLA), or random combinational logic. Each control signal is a Boolean function of the current state and the opcode bits.
2. **Microprogrammed Control** – Realised using a dedicated **Control Store** (ROM or RAM) that holds microinstructions. Each machine instruction triggers a micro-routine whose micro-words drive the data path.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern DSP processors such as the **TMS320C6x** family predominantly employ a **microprogrammed / firmware-driven control** philosophy because it allows the micro-architect to patch bugs post-silicon and to add custom instructions for proprietary kernels.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Factory Assembly Line

Imagine a **highly automated bottling plant**.

- The **Data Path** is the physical line: the conveyor belts (buses), the filling nozzles (ALU), the capping machines (shifter/rotator), the inspection cameras (comparators), and the storage crates (register file).
- The **Control Unit** is the plant supervisor standing at the control panel. The supervisor reads a *work order* (the fetched instruction), interprets it ("Fill 500 ml bottle with orange juice"), and then presses a pre-defined sequence of buttons (control signals) that activate the corresponding machinery.
- The **Clock** is the factory whistle that synchronises every station so that each operation begins and ends within a fixed time slot.

If the work order says *"Multiply, then accumulate"*, the supervisor does **not** need to invent a new button sequence — the sequence is hard-coded in the supervisor's training manual (microprogram) or wired into the panel circuitry (hardwired logic). The faster the whistle blows (higher clock frequency), the more bottles the line can produce per minute.

## 1.4 DSP-Specific Architectural Philosophy

Unlike a generic microcontroller, a DSP-grade data path is **algorithm-aware**:

| DSP Kernel Required | Data Path Feature |
|---|---|
| FIR convolution | Single-cycle MAC unit |
| FFT butterflies | Bit-reversed addressing hardware |
| Adaptive filters | Barrel shifter for scaling |
| Circular buffers | Modulo arithmetic unit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hallmark of a DSP data path is the **hardware Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) unit** — a two-input multiplier feeding directly into an accumulator — that completes a `y = y + a × b` operation in **one clock cycle**. General-purpose CPUs emulate this with many cycles.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pipeline depth for modern VLIW DSPs:** $N_p = 8$ stages (typical for TMS320C6x)
- **MAC latency:** $\tau_{MAC} = 1$ clock cycle (single-cycle MAC)
- **Control Store width (microprogrammed):** typically $W_{cs} = 32$ to $128$ bits
- **Instruction fetch bandwidth (Harvard DSP):** up to **two fetches per cycle** (one instruction word, one data word)
- **Standard clock for canonical examples:** $f_{clk} = 100\,\text{MHz}$ ⇒ $T_{clk} = 10\,\text{ns}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Canonical Data Path

A canonical CPU data path contains the following cooperating blocks:

### 2.1.1 Storage Elements
- **Program Counter (PC):** Holds the address of the *next* instruction to fetch. It is a *synchronous* up-counter with parallel-load capability.
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Latches the fetched opcode so it remains stable during decode and execute.
- **Memory Address Register (MAR):** Drives the external address bus during a memory access.
- **Memory Data Register (MDR):** Acts as the gateway for data moving between CPU and external memory.
- **Register File:** A small, multi-ported SRAM array (typically $N = 16$ to $32$ registers) supplying operands to the ALU.
- **Accumulator (ACC):** A special high-bandwidth register that captures the output of the ALU/MAC and feeds it back for chained operations.

### 2.1.2 Processing Elements
- **ALU:** Performs integer add, subtract, AND, OR, XOR, shift, compare.
- **Multiplier (MUL):** Fixed-point hardware multiplier — typically $16 \times 16 \to 32$-bit or $32 \times 32 \to 64$-bit.
- **MAC unit:** Multiplier followed by an adder that always adds the product into ACC.
- **Barrel Shifter:** Variable multi-bit shifter for scaling, alignment, or bit-reversal.

### 2.1.3 Steering Elements
- **Multiplexers (MUX):** Select one of $2^n$ data sources onto a shared bus.
- **Tri-state Bus Drivers:** Allow multiple sources to share a bus, time-multiplexed.
- **Demultiplexers:** Route the single output to one of many destinations.

## 2.2 Bus Organisations

The number of internal buses is a primary design trade-off.

### 2.2.1 Single-Bus Data Path
- One internal bus is time-shared.
- Every operand must be sequentially staged through temporary registers.
- **Cost:** Minimal silicon area.
- **Penalty:** An operation such as `A + B → C` consumes three bus cycles (fetch A, fetch B, write C).

### 2.2.2 Two-Bus Data Path
- Two independent internal buses feed the ALU.
- Operation `A + B → C` finishes in two cycles (fetch A and B in parallel, write C).

### 2.2.3 Three-Bus Data Path
- Two source buses feed the ALU, one destination bus writes back.
- Operation `A + B → C` completes in **one cycle**.
- Used in modern RISC and DSP cores.

## 2.3 Harvard vs Von Neumann Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Real-Time DSP:** Real-time kernels demand deterministic memory bandwidth. Harvard's parallel fetch of instruction and data eliminates the *Von Neumann bottleneck*.

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard | Modified Harvard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | Unified I + D | Separate I and D | Separate I/D caches, unified main memory |
| Simultaneous I + D fetch | **No** | **Yes** | Yes (via cache) |
| Bandwidth | $1 \times BW$ | $2 \times BW$ | $\approx 2 \times BW$ at L1 |
| Used in | General CPUs | DSP, MCUs | Modern DSPs (TMS320, SHARC) |
| Programming complexity | Low | High (separate address spaces) | Medium |

## 2.4 Pipeline Fundamentals

A pipeline overlaps the execution of multiple instructions, much like an assembly line overlaps cars.

For a pipeline of depth $K$ executing $I$ instructions, the theoretical speed-up is:

$$S_{\text{ideal}} = \lim_{I \to \infty} \frac{I \cdot K}{I + K - 1} \to K$$

The *ideal* throughput approaches one instruction per clock cycle (IPC = 1) once the pipe is full.

### 2.4.1 Hazards
- **Structural:** Two stages need the same hardware. *Solution:* duplicate resources.
- **Data:** A later instruction reads a value an earlier one has not yet written. *Solutions:* forwarding, stalling, reordering.
- **Control:** A branch is taken but the next instruction was already fetched. *Solutions:* delay slot, branch prediction, predicated execution.

In DSP code, which is dominated by tight loops, branches are highly predictable, and DSP compilers exploit **delayed branches** and **zero-overhead looping** to neutralise control hazards.

## 2.5 Hardwired Control Unit

A hardwired CU synthesises each control signal as a Boolean function:

$$c_k = f_k(\text{Opcode bits}, \text{State bits}, \text{Condition flags})$$

Each $f_k$ is realised as an AND-OR network feeding into a PLA, a PAL, or a gate-array implementation. The next-state logic follows:

$$S_{t+1} = g(\text{Opcode bits}, S_t, \text{Condition flags})$$

### Strengths
- **Very fast** — purely combinational, zero microinstruction-fetch overhead.
- **Compact for small instruction sets.**

### Weaknesses
- **Inflexible** — adding an instruction requires redesigning the PLA.
- **Bug-prone** — no easy mechanism for post-fabrication patching.

## 2.6 Microprogrammed Control Unit

A microprogrammed CU uses a **Control Store** (CS) holding microinstructions (also called *control words*). Each machine instruction points to a starting micro-address; a micro-sequencer walks through the routine.

The width of each microinstruction equals the number of control signals in the data path:

$$W_{\text{CS}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i$$

where $M$ is the total number of distinct control signals.

The microsequencer computes the next address via:

$$CAR_{t+1} = h(\text{CAR}_t, \text{Condition flags}, \text{External branch input})$$

where $CAR$ is the **Control Address Register**.

### Strengths
- **Highly flexible** — microcode can be patched in ROM/RAM.
- **Systematic** — easier to design for complex instruction sets.
- **Microdiagnostics** — bit-level visibility into CPU state.

### Weaknesses
- **Slower** — microinstruction fetch adds latency.
- **More silicon** — wide CS ROM consumes significant area.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a student must internalise for the ESE and internal assessments. Pay close attention to units.

| Formula ID | Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F-2.1 | $T_{\text{exe}} = I \cdot CPI \cdot T_{clk}$ | CPU execution time | seconds (s) |
| F-2.2 | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{I_i}{I} \cdot CPI_i$ | Weighted average cycles per instruction | cycles/instruction |
| F-2.3 | $S_{\text{ideal}} = K$ | Speed-up of a $K$-stage pipeline (limit) | dimensionless |
| F-2.4 | $S_{\text{practical}} = \dfrac{I \cdot K}{I + K - 1}$ | Realistic pipeline speed-up | dimensionless |
| F-2.5 | $\text{Throughput}_{\text{pipe}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Pipelined throughput (saturated) | instr/s |
| F-2.6 | $\text{Throughput}_{\text{non-pipe}} = \dfrac{1}{K \cdot T_{clk}}$ | Non-pipelined throughput | instr/s |
| F-2.7 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | Amdahl's Law speed-up | dimensionless |
| F-2.8 | $W_{\text{CS}} = \sum w_i$ | Control store word width | bits |
| F-2.9 | $D_{\text{CS}} = N_{\mu\text{instr}} \times W_{\text{CS}}$ | Total control store size | bits |
| F-2.10 | $t_{\text{MAC}} = T_{clk}$ | Single-cycle MAC latency | seconds |
| F-2.11 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| F-2.12 | $\text{MOPS} = \dfrac{\text{MAC ops}}{10^6 \cdot T_{\text{exe}}}$ | Million MAC Operations Per Second | MOPS |
| F-2.13 | $\eta_{\text{pipe}} = \dfrac{K \cdot I}{K \cdot I + K(K-1)}$ | Pipeline efficiency | dimensionless |

> **Boundary condition notes:**
> - $f$ in Amdahl's law is the *parallelisable fraction*; $n$ is the number of parallel units.
> - $CPI$ defaults to $1$ for an ideal RISC/DSP pipeline.
> - $W_{\text{CS}}$ for a 64-signal data path ⇒ $W_{\text{CS}} = 64$ bits minimum.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The data-path / control paradigm is the cornerstone of:

- **Mobile baseband SoCs** — VLIW DSPs (TI TMS320C6x, Qualcomm Hexagon) execute convolution and FFT kernels using deeply pipelined, microprogrammed data paths.
- **Audio codecs** — Real-time MP3 / AAC decoding requires deterministic MAC throughput, achievable only with hardwired MAC units.
- **Radar / SDR systems** — Adaptive beamforming needs vector MAC chains whose timing is verified by static analysis of the data path's worst-case latency.
- **Automotive ECU signal conditioning** — Hardwired control units guarantee deterministic response in safety-critical loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pipeline Speed-Up

**Problem statement:** Prove that a $K$-stage pipeline executing $I$ instructions yields a real speed-up of $S = \dfrac{I \cdot K}{I + K - 1}$ (this is the canonical derivation examiners expect).

**Logic narrative:**
A non-pipelined processor executes each instruction in $K$ sequential cycles, so $I$ instructions consume $K \cdot I$ cycles. A pipelined processor incurs a *fill* penalty and a *drain* penalty: the first instruction takes $K$ cycles to traverse, and the last instruction also requires $K$ cycles, but during the steady-state between them, one instruction completes per cycle. So the total cycles are $K + (I - 1)$.

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{non-pipe}} &= I \cdot K \cdot T_{clk} \\
T_{\text{pipe}} &= \bigl(K + (I - 1)\bigr) \cdot T_{clk} \\
S &= \frac{T_{\text{non-pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{I \cdot K \cdot T_{clk}}{\bigl(K + (I - 1)\bigr) \cdot T_{clk}} \\
S &= \frac{I \cdot K}{K + I - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** When $I \gg K$ (a very long instruction stream), the $K - 1$ term is negligible and $S \to K$. For short kernels, the overhead is significant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Cycles for an Instruction Mix

**Problem statement:** A DSP executes four classes of instructions with the following profile. Compute the average CPI.

| Class | Count $I_i$ | CPI per instruction |
|---|---|---|
| ALU | $300$ | $1$ |
| MAC | $200$ | $1$ |
| Load | $150$ | $2$ |
| Branch | $50$ | $3$ |

**Logic narrative:** Apply F-2.2 directly.

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{tot} &= 300 + 200 + 150 + 50 = 700 \\
\sum I_i \cdot CPI_i &= 300(1) + 200(1) + 150(2) + 50(3) \\
&= 300 + 200 + 300 + 150 \\
&= 950 \\
CPI_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{950}{700} = 1.357\,\text{cycles/instr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A CPI close to 1 indicates that the pipeline is well-utilised (most instructions complete in a single cycle).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: MAC Throughput in a Real-Time FIR Filter

**Problem statement:** A 64-tap FIR filter runs at a sampling rate of $f_s = 48\,\text{kHz}$. The DSP has a single-cycle MAC with $f_{clk} = 200\,\text{MHz}$. What is the minimum number of MAC units needed to keep up?

**Logic narrative:** Each output sample needs $N = 64$ MACs, plus one extra store. So total MAC operations per second is $N \cdot f_s$. The available MAC rate per unit is $f_{clk}$. Number of units $M$ must satisfy $M \cdot f_{clk} \ge N \cdot f_s$.

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MACs required per second} &= N \cdot f_s = 64 \times 48{,}000 = 3{,}072{,}000\,\text{MACs/s} \\
\text{MACs available per unit per second} &= f_{clk} = 200 \times 10^6 = 2 \times 10^8\,\text{MACs/s} \\
M &= \left\lceil \frac{3.072 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.01536 \rceil = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even a *single* MAC unit at $200\,\text{MHz}$ vastly over-provisions for this filter. The DSP is operating at less than $\approx 1.5\%$ utilisation — meaning the processor can comfortably handle many parallel audio channels.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Control Word Synthesis for a Single Instruction

**Problem statement:** For a load instruction `LDA addr` (load accumulator from memory), synthesise the Boolean expression for the control signal that enables the MDR → ACC transfer.

**Logic narrative:** The signal `MDR_to_ACC` must be asserted only when (a) the opcode is `LDA`, (b) the CPU is in the EXECUTE state, AND (c) the memory read has completed (RDY flag asserted).

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{\text{MDR}\to\text{ACC}} &= (\text{opcode} = \text{LDA}) \cdot (\text{state} = \text{EXEC}) \cdot (\text{RDY} = 1) \\
\text{Using bits:} \quad & I_7 I_6 I_5 I_4 = 0\,0\,0\,1 \\
c_k &= \bar{I_7} \cdot \bar{I_6} \cdot \bar{I_5} \cdot I_4 \cdot S_{\text{EXEC}} \cdot \text{RDY}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This single Boolean product term is one row of the PLA. Hardwired control is essentially a huge bank of such AND-OR equations, one per control signal per state.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Control Store Sizing for a Microprogrammed CU

**Problem statement:** A data path has $M = 48$ distinct control signals. The instruction set requires an average of $\bar{n}_\mu = 6$ microinstructions per macroinstruction. There are $N_{\text{macro}} = 64$ macroinstructions. Compute the total control store size.

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{CS}} &= M = 48\,\text{bits per micro-word} \\
N_{\mu\text{-total}} &= N_{\text{macro}} \cdot \bar{n}_\mu = 64 \times 6 = 384\,\text{micro-words} \\
D_{\text{CS}} &= 384 \times 48 = 18{,}432\,\text{bits} \\
D_{\text{CS}} &= \frac{18{,}432}{1024} \approx 18\,\text{Kbits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is trivial by modern standards — a 64-tap FIR with microprogrammed control needs only an 18-Kbit control store.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Vector MAC

**Problem statement:** Suppose $f = 0.9$ of a DSP kernel can be parallelised across $n = 4$ MAC units. What is the maximum achievable speed-up?

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.1 + \frac{0.9}{4}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.225} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.325} \\
  &\approx 3.077
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even quadrupling the hardware yields only $\approx 3 \times$ speed-up, because the remaining $10\%$ of serial code dominates. This is precisely why real-time DSP designers keep the serial fraction $1 - f$ as low as possible.

---

## 3.7 Derivation: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Trade-off

**Problem statement:** A CU handles $N_{\text{mac}} = 256$ macroinstructions.

- Hardwired: each instruction needs $\approx 5$ AND-OR terms on average.
- Microprogrammed: average $\bar{n}_\mu = 4$ microinstructions per macroinstruction; CS width $W = 64$ bits.

Compute the equivalent gate count for both approaches (1 micro-bit $\equiv$ 1 memory cell $\equiv$ 6 transistors; 1 AND-OR term $\equiv$ 10 transistors in PLA).

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{hard}} &= N_{\text{mac}} \times 5 \times 10 = 256 \times 50 = 12{,}800\,\text{transistors} \\
G_{\text{micro}} &= N_{\text{mac}} \times \bar{n}_\mu \times W \times 6 \\
                &= 256 \times 4 \times 64 \times 6 \\
                &= 393{,}216\,\text{transistors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Hardwired control is roughly **30× smaller** in transistor count. This is why low-power DSPs and microcontrollers prefer hardwired control, while complex CPUs (x86) accept the area overhead in exchange for flexibility.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Decision Cheat-Sheet

| Design Choice | When to pick | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Single bus | Cost-critical MCUs | Slow, sequential |
| Three-bus | Modern DSP/CPU | Larger silicon |
| Hardwired control | Small ISAs, high speed | Inflexible |
| Microprogrammed control | Complex ISAs, patches | Area, micro-fetch latency |
| Harvard | Real-time DSP | Programming complexity |
| Von Neumann | General-purpose CPUs | Bandwidth bottleneck |
| Pipeline depth $K=3$ to $K=5$ | Embedded DSP | Diminishing returns |
| Pipeline depth $K \ge 8$ | VLIW DSP | Hazard mitigation cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Single-Cycle Data Path

The following diagram maps the block-level functional architecture of a single-cycle CPU data path suitable for DSP extensions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEM[MEMORY SUBSYSTEM]
        IM[INSTRUCTION MEMORY]
        DM[DATA MEMORY]
    end

    subgraph FETCH[FETCH STAGE]
        PC[PROGRAM COUNTER PC]
        MAR[MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER MAR]
        MDR[MEMORY DATA REGISTER MDR]
        IR[INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR]
    end

    subgraph EXEC[EXECUTE STAGE]
        RF[REGISTER FILE 16x16]
        MUXA[MUX A]
        MUXB[MUX B]
        ALU[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU]
        MUL[MULTIPLIER]
        MAC[MULTIPLY ACCUMULATOR MAC]
        ACC[ACCUMULATOR ACC]
        SHF[BARREL SHIFTER]
        MUXDST[MUX DESTINATION]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL UNIT]
        DEC[INSTRUCTION DECODER]
        TC[TIMING AND CONTROL SIGNAL GENERATOR]
        CS[CONTROL STORE MICRO ROM]
    end

    PC --> MAR
    MAR --> IM
    IM --> MDR
    MDR --> IR
    IR --> DEC
    DEC --> TC
    CS --> TC
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| RF
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| ALU
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| MUL
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| MAC
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| SHF
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| MUXDST
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| PC
    TC -->|CTRL SIGNALS| MAR

    RF --> MUXA
    RF --> MUXB
    MUXA --> ALU
    MUXB --> ALU
    ALU --> MAC
    MUL --> MAC
    MAC --> ACC
    ACC --> SHF
    SHF --> MUXDST
    MUXDST --> RF

    RF --> MAR
    DM --> MDR
    MDR --> MUXA
```

## 4.2 Control Unit — Hardwired Implementation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[OPCODE BITS I7 I6 I5 I4 I3 I2 I1 I0] --> DEC[INSTRUCTION DECODER]
    DEC --> EQ1[IS LDA]
    DEC --> EQ2[IS STA]
    DEC --> EQ3[IS ADD]
    DEC --> EQ4[IS MAC]
    DEC --> EQ5[IS BRANCH]

    SREG[STATE REGISTER S2 S1 S0] --> DEC
    FLAG[CONDITION FLAGS Z N C V] --> DEC

    EQ1 --> AND1[AND GATE]
    EQ2 --> AND2[AND GATE]
    EQ3 --> AND3[AND GATE]
    EQ4 --> AND4[AND GATE]
    EQ5 --> AND5[AND GATE]

    SREG --> AND1
    SREG --> AND2
    SREG --> AND3
    SREG --> AND4
    SREG --> AND5

    AND1 --> OR[OR PLANE]
    AND2 --> OR
    AND3 --> OR
    AND4 --> OR
    AND5 --> OR

    OR --> CSIG[CONTROL SIGNAL C0 C1 ... CN MINUS 1]
    CSIG --> DATAPATH[DATA PATH BLOCKS]
```

## 4.3 Microprogrammed Control Unit Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MIR[MACHINE INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR] --> MAP[OPCODE TO MICROADDRESS MAPPER]
    MAP --> CAR[CONTROL ADDRESS REGISTER CAR]
    CAR --> CSROM[CONTROL STORE ROM]
    CSROM -->|MICRO WORD| MIR2[MICROINSTRUCTION REGISTER MIR]

    MIR2 --> CSIG[CONTROL SIGNALS TO DATA PATH]
    MIR2 --> SEQC[SEQUENCER NEXT ADDRESS LOGIC]

    FLAG[CONDITION FLAGS Z N C V] --> SEQC
    MIR2 -->|BRANCH FIELD| SEQC
    SEQC -->|NEXT ADDRESS| CAR

    MIR2 --> EXTMUX[EXTERNAL OR INTERNAL BRANCH MUX]
    EXTMUX --> CAR
```

## 4.4 Pipeline Timing Diagram (5-Stage)

```mermaid
gantt
    title 5 Stage Pipeline Timing for 4 Instructions
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s

    section IF
    I1_FETCH    :a1, 0, 1
    I2_FETCH    :a2, 1, 1
    I3_FETCH    :a3, 2, 1
    I4_FETCH    :a4, 3, 1

    section ID
    I1_DECODE   :b1, 1, 1
    I2_DECODE   :b2, 2, 1
    I3_DECODE   :b3, 3, 1
    I4_DECODE   :b4, 4, 1

    section EX
    I1_EXECUTE  :c1, 2, 1
    I2_EXECUTE  :c2, 3, 1
    I3_EXECUTE  :c3, 4, 1
    I4_EXECUTE  :c4, 5, 1

    section MA
    I1_MEM      :d1, 3, 1
    I2_MEM      :d2, 4, 1
    I3_MEM      :d3, 5, 1
    I4_MEM      :d4, 6, 1

    section WB
    I1_WRITEBACK:e1, 4, 1
    I2_WRITEBACK:e2, 5, 1
    I3_WRITEBACK:e3, 6, 1
    I4_WRITEBACK:e4, 7, 1
```

## 4.5 Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[INSTRUCTION EXECUTION REQUEST] --> D{COMPLEXITY OF ISA}
    D -->|SMALL LESS THAN 50 INSTRUCTIONS| HW[HARDWIRED CONTROL FSM PLA]
    D -->|LARGE OR EVOLVING| MP[MICROPROGRAMMED CONTROL STORE ROM]

    HW --> H1[FAST ZERO MICRO FETCH]
    HW --> H2[AREA EFFICIENT]
    HW --> H3[INFLEXIBLE HARD TO PATCH]

    MP --> M1[FLEXIBLE ROM PATCHABLE]
    MP --> M2[EASY TO DESIGN FOR CISC]
    MP --> M3[HIGHER LATENCY DUE TO MICRO FETCH]

    H1 --> DATAPATH[HARDWIRED DATA PATH]
    H2 --> DATAPATH
    H3 --> DATAPATH
    M1 --> DATAPATH2[MICROPROGRAMMED DATA PATH]
    M2 --> DATAPATH2
    M3 --> DATAPATH2
```

## 4.6 DSP-Specific Data Path (MAC-Centric)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP[OPERAND FETCH]
        AX[ADDRESS GEN UNIT 1 AGU1]
        AY[ADDRESS GEN UNIT 2 AGU2]
        RX[X MEMORY BANK]
        RY[Y MEMORY BANK]
    end

    subgraph CORE[MAC PIPELINE]
        MX[MULTIPLIER X TIMES Y]
        AD[ADD PIPELINE REGISTER]
        AC[ACCUMULATOR 40 BIT]
    end

    subgraph OUT[POST]
        SH[BARREL SHIFTER]
        ST[SATURATION AND STORE UNIT]
    end

    AX --> RX
    AY --> RY
    RX --> MX
    RY --> MX
    MX --> AD
    AC --> AD
    AD --> AC
    AC --> SH
    SH --> ST
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
Differentiate clearly between the **Von Neumann** and **Harvard** architectures. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each in the context of Real-Time DSP.

**Model Answer:**

**Von Neumann Architecture:**
- Uses a *single* memory and *single* bus for both instructions and data.
- *Advantage:* Simpler hardware design, easier programming model with a unified address space.
- *Disadvantage:* The *Von Neumann bottleneck* — instructions and data compete for the same memory port, leading to limited bandwidth unsuitable for high-throughput DSP kernels.

**Harvard Architecture:**
- Uses *physically separate* instruction and data memories with independent buses.
- *Advantage:* Allows *simultaneous* fetch of an instruction and a data operand, doubling effective bandwidth — ideal for FIR filters and FFTs.
- *Disadvantage:* More complex hardware, two distinct address spaces complicate the programming model, and total memory utilisation may be inefficient.

**Conclusion:** For Real-Time DSP, the **Harvard** (or **Modified Harvard**) architecture is overwhelmingly preferred due to its deterministic, high-bandwidth dual-fetch capability.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
List the **three classical hazards** of a pipelined CPU data path. For each, give a one-line mitigation.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Structural Hazard** — Two pipeline stages demand the same hardware resource in the same cycle.
   *Mitigation:* Duplicate the contended resource (e.g., dual-port register file, separate I-cache and D-cache).

2. **Data Hazard** — An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline.
   *Mitigation:* Use *data forwarding* (bypassing) paths from later stages back to the ALU input, or insert *pipeline stalls* (bubbles).

3. **Control Hazard** — The next instruction to execute depends on a branch outcome that is not yet known.
   *Mitigation:* Use *delayed branching*, *static/dynamic branch prediction*, or *predicated execution*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — Data Path Architecture (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **block-level architecture of a single-cycle CPU data path** containing PC, MAR, MDR, IR, register file, ALU, accumulator, and a memory subsystem. Clearly indicate the control signals flowing from the Control Unit to each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss **single-bus, two-bus, and three-bus** organisations of the data path. Compute the number of bus cycles required to execute `R3 ← R1 + R2` in each case. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The block diagram should show (as already illustrated in Section 4.1):

| Block | Function | Control Signal Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PC | Holds address of next instruction | PC_INC, PC_LOAD |
| MAR | Drives external address bus | MAR_IN |
| IR | Latches fetched instruction | IR_IN |
| MDR | Gateway between CPU and memory | MDR_IN, MDR_OUT |
| Register File | Supplies ALU operands | RF_READ_A, RF_READ_B, RF_WRITE, RF_DEST_SEL |
| ALU | Performs arithmetic and logic | ALU_OP_SELECT, ALU_OUT_ENABLE |
| Accumulator | Stores ALU/MAC output | ACC_IN, ACC_OUT |
| Control Unit | Generates all the above | State machine driven by opcode + clock |

**Marking guide for part (a):**
- Correct identification of all 8 blocks: **3 marks**
- Correct data-flow arrows and bus connections: **2 marks**
- Correct labeling of at least 6 control signals: **2 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Single-bus data path:**
1. Place `R1` onto the bus, latch into `A` (temporary) → **1 cycle**.
2. Place `R2` onto the bus, drive ALU to ADD, latch into `B` → **1 cycle**.
3. ALU computes and writes to `R3` → **1 cycle**.
4. **Total = 3 cycles.**

**Two-bus data path:**
1. Place `R1` on bus1 and `R2` on bus2 simultaneously; ALU computes `R1+R2`; result placed on bus → **1 cycle**.
2. Write result to `R3` → **1 cycle**.
3. **Total = 2 cycles.**

**Three-bus data path:**
1. `R1` on bus1, `R2` on bus2, ALU computes, result on bus3 written to `R3` **in one cycle**.
2. **Total = 1 cycle.**

| Organisation | Cycles | Cost | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single bus | $3$ | Lowest | Slowest |
| Two bus | $2$ | Medium | Medium |
| Three bus | $1$ | Highest | Fastest |

**Marking guide for part (b):**
- Description of all three organisations: **3 marks**
- Correct cycle count for each: **3 marks**
- Comparison table: **1 mark**

---

### QUESTION B — Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control Unit (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **hardwired control unit** based on a Finite State Machine and a Programmable Logic Array. Synthesise the Boolean expression for a typical control signal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **microprogrammed control unit** with a Control Store ROM and a microsequencer. Derive an expression for the total control store size. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A hardwired control unit consists of:
- An **instruction decoder** that produces a one-hot signal from the opcode.
- A **state register** holding the current FSM state (typically FETCH, DECODE, EXECUTE, MEMORY, WRITEBACK).
- A **PLA** with an AND-plane and an OR-plane generating control signals as Boolean products of opcode bits, state bits, and condition flags.

The Boolean expression for a generic control signal $c_k$ is:

$$
c_k = \bigvee_{j} \big( I_{\text{opcode}=j} \cdot S_{\text{state}=q} \cdot F_{\text{cond}} \big)
$$

**Worked synthesis example:** For the `MDR_to_ACC` signal of an `LDA` instruction (as derived in Section 3.4):

$$
c_{\text{MDR}\to\text{ACC}} = \bar{I_7} \cdot \bar{I_6} \cdot \bar{I_5} \cdot I_4 \cdot S_{\text{EXEC}} \cdot \text{RDY}
$$

**Marking guide for part (a):**
- Block diagram with decoder, state reg, PLA: **3 marks**
- Boolean synthesis of one control signal: **2 marks**
- Mention of FSM states: **2 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

The microprogrammed control unit consists of:
- A **Control Address Register (CAR)** holding the address of the next microinstruction.
- A **Control Store (CS)** ROM containing the microinstruction words.
- A **Microinstruction Register (MIR)** latching the current micro-word.
- A **Sequencer / Next-Address Logic** that computes $CAR_{t+1}$.
- A **Mapper** that translates the macroinstruction opcode into the starting micro-address.

**Derivation of control store size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{CS}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \\
N_{\mu\text{-total}} &= N_{\text{macro}} \cdot \bar{n}_\mu \\
D_{\text{CS}} &= N_{\mu\text{-total}} \cdot W_{\text{CS}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked numerical example:** For $M = 48$ control signals, $N_{\text{macro}} = 64$, $\bar{n}_\mu = 6$ (as in Section 3.5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{CS}} &= 48\,\text{bits} \\
N_{\mu\text{-total}} &= 64 \times 6 = 384\,\text{micro-words} \\
D_{\text{CS}} &= 384 \times 48 = 18{,}432\,\text{bits} \approx 18\,\text{Kbits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marking guide for part (b):**
- Block diagram of microsequencer and control store: **3 marks**
- Correct expressions for $W$ and $D$: **2 marks**
- Numerical example: **2 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not omit the arrow labels** in data-path diagrams. Marks are reserved for explicitly naming control signals on every connecting wire.
> 2. **Always carry units** in numerical answers. Writing "18" without "Kbits" loses a mark.
> 3. In cycle-count derivations, **show each micro-step** (e.g., "fetch operand → drive ALU → write result"). A single-sentence answer without stepwise enumeration will be capped at 50% of the available marks.
> 4. When synthesising Boolean expressions, **use the *exact* opcode bits** (e.g., $I_7 \ldots I_0$) and not generic letters — KTU examiners look for this rigour.
> 5. In pipelining questions, always state the **fill** and **drain** cycle penalties explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Data Path = ALU + Registers + Buses + MUXes.** Control Unit = Decoder + State Machine + Signal Generator.
- **Von Neumann** has *one* memory; **Harvard** has *two* (I and D). **Modified Harvard** is the practical sweet spot used in modern DSPs.
- **Three-bus data path** allows single-cycle ALU ops; single-bus needs three cycles for `A + B → C`.
- **Hardwired control** = combinational PLA, fast but inflexible.
- **Microprogrammed control** = Control Store ROM, flexible but slower due to micro-fetch.
- **MAC unit** is the single most important DSP data-path block — completes `y += a*b` in 1 cycle.
- **Pipeline speed-up limit** is $S \to K$ for very long instruction streams; exact formula $S = \dfrac{I \cdot K}{I + K - 1}$.
- **Amdahl's law** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + f/n}$ bounds parallel speed-up; serial fraction $1 - f$ is the killer.
- **CPI** = weighted average cycles per instruction; ideal pipeline CPI = 1.
- **CPU time** $T_{\text{exe}} = I \cdot CPI \cdot T_{clk}$.
- **Pipeline hazards** = Structural, Data, Control — each has a specific mitigation.
- **Control store sizing** = (instructions) × (avg micro-words per macro) × (CS width in bits).
- **DSP-specific addressing** includes circular (modulo) and bit-reversed addressing for FFTs.
- **TMS320C6x** style VLIW uses an $8$-stage pipeline with microprogrammed control.
- **Modified Harvard** = separate L1 caches for I and D, but unified L2 / main memory.
- **Single-cycle MAC** is the throughput benchmark; $\text{MOPS} = \text{MIPS} \times \text{MACs per instr}$.
- **Branch hazards** are minimised by **delayed branching** and **zero-overhead looping** in DSP code.
- **Control signal** = Boolean function of `(opcode, state, flags)` — explicit AND-OR expression is expected in answers.
- **PLA** AND-plane generates minterms; OR-plane consolidates them onto control lines.
- **Microsequencer** may use increment, conditional branch, or subroutine-call mechanisms.

> **Final Mantra:** *Real-Time DSP is won or lost in the data path — not in the control unit. The control unit exists to keep the data path fed every single cycle.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Real-Time Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**, a **Timer** is a dedicated **hardware peripheral** integrated within a DSP processor (such as the *Texas Instruments TMS320C6x* family) that generates **precisely-timed events** by counting clock pulses derived from the CPU clock or an external source. The timer produces a periodic pulse, an output signal transition, or a **maskable interrupt** when its internal down-counter reaches zero, after which it is automatically reloaded with a user-defined value from the **Period Register (PRD)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, timers fall under the study of **on-chip peripherals of programmable DSP processors**, and they are essential for:
- **Sample-Rate Generation (Fs)** for ADC and DAC operations.
- **Periodic Interrupt Generation** for Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) tick scheduling.
- **EDMA (Enhanced Direct Memory Access) Synchronization** to trigger data transfers without CPU intervention.
- **General-Purpose Event Timing** for control applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A timer in a DSP is NOT a software construct — it is a **32-bit hardware counter** that operates independently of the CPU pipeline, ensuring deterministic, jitter-free timing critical for real-time DSP.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a busy restaurant kitchen** preparing a dish that requires checking the oven **exactly every 2 minutes**. Instead of staring at a wall clock and doing the math each time, you set a **kitchen timer**. The moment you press "Start," the timer runs silently in the background. After exactly 2 minutes, it **rings loudly**, pulling your attention back to the task. While it runs, you are free to chop vegetables, stir sauces, and do other things.

A **DSP Timer is exactly that kitchen timer**, but for the processor:

| Kitchen Timer | DSP Timer |
|---------------|-----------|
| Battery / mains power | **CPU Clock** (e.g., 200 MHz) |
| Time you set (2 min) | **Period Register (PRD)** value |
| Countdown mechanism | **32-bit Down-Counter (CNT)** |
| Buzzer / ringing sound | **Timer Interrupt → CPU** |
| Reset to original setting | **Auto-reload from PRD** |
| Stop / Start button | **Control Register (CTL)** bits |

So, when we say *"the DSP samples audio at 48 kHz,"* it is the **timer firing an interrupt 48,000 times per second**, telling the CPU "an audio sample is ready, fetch it now!" — just like the chef's timer ringing 48,000 times per second to demand attention (which would be absurd in a kitchen, but perfect for a DSP).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following numerical values and metrics are highlighted in **bold** because they appear repeatedly in KTU board examination problems:

- **CPU Master Clock Frequency ($F_{CPU}$):** Typically **200 MHz, 225 MHz, 300 MHz, or 1 GHz** depending on the specific TMS320C6x device variant (C6713 → 225 MHz; C6748 → 456 MHz).
- **Timer Resolution:** **1 CPU clock cycle** (no prescaling needed for high-frequency applications).
- **Timer Counter Width:** **32 bits** (range: **0 to 4,294,967,295** = $2^{32} - 1$).
- **Standard Audio Sample Rates (Fs):** **8 kHz, 16 kHz, 44.1 kHz, 48 kHz, 96 kHz, 192 kHz**.
- **Standard Timer Tick:** 1 / $F_{CPU}$ seconds (e.g., at 200 MHz, one tick = **5 ns**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept Box:** A timer in a real-time DSP converts a **frequency-domain requirement** (e.g., 48 kHz sample rate) into a **discrete integer count value** stored in a register. This translation is the heart of sample-rate generation.

---

## 1.4 Why Timers Are Indispensable in Real-Time DSP

Real-time DSP demands **deterministic execution** — a speech codec must produce 8,000 samples per second **every second**, with **minimal jitter** (deviation from the ideal sampling instant). Software loops using `for` or `while` constructs to generate delays are non-deterministic because:
1. Cache misses cause variable latency.
2. Pipeline hazards stall execution unpredictably.
3. Compiler optimizations reorder instructions.

**Hardware timers solve this** by counting clock cycles in silicon, decoupled from the CPU pipeline, and triggering events with **single-cycle precision**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Block-Level Components of a DSP Timer

Every TMS320C6x timer consists of **three principal registers** and a **clock selection multiplexer**:

1. **Counter Register (CNT):** A **32-bit down-counter** that decrements by 1 on every active clock edge.
2. **Period Register (PRD):** A **32-bit reload register** that defines the period of the timer event.
3. **Control Register (CTL):** A configuration register that determines clock source, mode (continuous vs. one-shot), output polarity, and enable/disable state.

Additional signals:
- **TINP (Timer Input Pin):** External clock input.
- **TOUT (Timer Output Pin):** External pulse output to ADC/DAC or external ICs.
- **Timer Interrupt Output:** Routed to the CPU's interrupt controller.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The timer operates in a **repetitive decrement-and-compare cycle**:

- **Step 1 — Load:** When the timer is enabled (via the **HLD = 0** mode in CTL), the value in **PRD** is loaded into **CNT**.
- **Step 2 — Decrement:** On every rising edge of the selected clock (internal CPU clock or external TINP), **CNT** decrements by 1.
- **Step 3 — Compare:** The hardware continuously compares **CNT** with zero.
- **Step 4 — Event Generation:** When **CNT = 0**:
  - The timer generates an **interrupt** (if enabled in the Interrupt Enable Register (IER)).
  - The **TOUT** pin is pulsed (configured by the **CPOL** and **STAT** bits of CTL).
  - An **EDMA synchronization event** is optionally generated.
  - **CNT is automatically reloaded from PRD** (in **continuous mode**).
- **Step 5 — Repeat:** The cycle continues indefinitely until the CPU clears the timer enable bit in CTL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why (CNT counts down to 0, not up to PRD)?** Counting down to zero allows the same comparator hardware to detect "end of period" as a single equality check, simplifying silicon design and minimizing critical-path delay in the processor.

---

## 2.3 Timer Operating Modes

The TMS320C6x timer supports two primary operating modes selected by the **MODE bit** in the CTL register:

| Mode | Behavior | Typical Use Case |
|------|----------|------------------|
| **Continuous Mode (MODE = 1)** | Timer auto-reloads from PRD after reaching zero. Runs forever once started. | **Audio sample generation**, periodic control loops |
| **One-Shot / Single Mode (MODE = 0)** | Timer stops after one full period. Must be re-enabled by software. | **One-time delays**, event timestamping |

The **HLD (Hold) bit** in CTL:
- **HLD = 1:** Counter is **frozen** (HOLD mode) — useful for debugging.
- **HLD = 0:** Counter **runs**.

The **PHS (Phase) bit** in CTL: Synchronizes multiple timers to a common clock edge.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THESE EQUATIONS — they are guaranteed to appear in calculations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{TOUT Frequency} = \dfrac{F_{CPU}}{\text{PRD} + 1}$ | Output frequency of the timer pulse | Hz |
| 2 | $\text{TOUT Period} = \dfrac{(\text{PRD} + 1)}{F_{CPU}}$ | Time between two consecutive pulses | seconds |
| 3 | $\text{PRD} = \dfrac{F_{CPU}}{F_s} - 1$ | Period register value for desired sample rate $F_s$ | dimensionless integer |
| 4 | $\text{Max TOUT Frequency} = \dfrac{F_{CPU}}{1} = F_{CPU}$ | When PRD = 0 (one-cycle period) | Hz |
| 5 | $\text{Min TOUT Frequency} = \dfrac{F_{CPU}}{2^{32}}$ | When PRD = $2^{32} - 1$ (full 32-bit count) | Hz |
| 6 | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{F_{CPU}}$ | Duration of one timer clock tick | seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{F_{CPU}}$ | Smallest time increment the timer can measure | seconds |
| 8 | $N_{overflows} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{period}} \right\rceil$ | Number of timer interrupts to achieve a software delay | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** The "+1" in formulas 1, 2, and 3 arises because the counter starts at **PRD**, decrements through (PRD + 1) distinct values (PRD, PRD-1, ..., 1, 0), and only **then** generates the event. Forgetting the "+1" is the **#1 most common error** in KTU exam scripts.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Timers in DSP processors are used in:

1. **Audio Codecs (PCM, MP3, AAC):** Generating Fs = 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz interrupt to fetch a sample from the ADC buffer.
2. **Motor Control (FOC, PWM):** Generating PWM carrier frequencies (e.g., 20 kHz for ultra-sonic motor drives).
3. **Telecommunications:** Generating symbol-rate clocks for QAM, OFDM, FSK modems.
4. **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG sampling at 256 Hz / 512 Hz.
5. **Radar / SONAR:** Precisely timed pulse transmission and echo reception.

The **determinism** of timers is what makes a DSP "real-time" — without hardware timers, real-time guarantees would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Period Register Value for a Given Sample Rate

**Given:**
- CPU Clock Frequency: $F_{CPU}$
- Desired Sample Rate: $F_s$

**Goal:** Find the value to be programmed into the PRD register.

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** The time between two consecutive timer events (one period) must equal the sampling period $T_s$.

$$\text{Period} = T_s = \frac{1}{F_s}$$

**Step 2:** Each timer period is composed of (PRD + 1) clock ticks, each of duration $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{F_{CPU}}$.

$$\text{Period} = (\text{PRD} + 1) \times T_{tick} = (\text{PRD} + 1) \times \frac{1}{F_{CPU}}$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for the period.

$$(\text{PRD} + 1) \times \frac{1}{F_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{F_s}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $F_{CPU}$ to isolate (PRD + 1).

$$\text{PRD} + 1 = \frac{F_{CPU}}{F_s}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract 1 to solve for PRD.

$$\boxed{\text{PRD} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{F_s} - 1}$$

**Verification of Units:** $F_{CPU}$ (Hz) ÷ $F_s$ (Hz) yields a **dimensionless ratio**, then we subtract 1 — units are consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A TMS320C6713 DSP operates at $F_{CPU} = 225$ MHz. The audio codec requires a sample rate of $F_s = 48$ kHz for stereo audio. Calculate the value to be loaded into the PRD register.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Write the formula.

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{F_s} - 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{225 \times 10^6}{48 \times 10^3} - 1$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio.

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{225{,}000{,}000}{48{,}000} - 1$$

$$\text{PRD} = 4687.5 - 1$$

**Step 4:** Round to the nearest integer (PRD must be an unsigned 32-bit integer).

$$\text{PRD} = 4686$$

**Step 5:** Verify the actual achieved sample rate.

$$F_{s,actual} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{\text{PRD} + 1} = \frac{225 \times 10^6}{4687} \approx 48{,}005.97 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the **sample-rate error** (jitter).

$$\text{Error} = F_{s,actual} - F_s = 48{,}005.97 - 48{,}000 = +5.97 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{5.97}{48{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 0.0124\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The error of 0.0124% is **well below** the 50 ppm (0.005%) tolerance of most consumer audio DACs, so this PRD value is acceptable. However, for **high-precision instrumentation**, the fractional part (0.5) could be compensated using a **fractional-N divider** or by adjusting the PLL multiplier.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum and Minimum Achievable Frequencies

**Given:** 32-bit counter, $F_{CPU} = 200$ MHz.

### Maximum Frequency (PRD = 0):

$$F_{max} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{0 + 1} = 200 \text{ MHz}$$

The timer fires on **every single clock cycle** — useful for benchmarking the highest possible interrupt rate.

### Minimum Frequency (PRD = $2^{32} - 1$):

$$F_{min} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{2^{32}} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{4{,}294{,}967{,}296}$$

$$F_{min} \approx 0.04657 \text{ Hz} \approx 21.47 \text{ seconds per pulse}$$

This corresponds to a **time-out of ~21.5 seconds** — useful for long-duration watchdog timers or sleep-mode wake-up.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Timer Behavior Under Different PRD Values

| PRD Value | Timer Count Steps | Resulting Period @ 200 MHz | Resulting Frequency | Typical Application |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 0 | 1 | 5 ns | 200 MHz | CPU benchmarking |
| 1 | 2 | 10 ns | 100 MHz | High-speed test equipment |
| 99 | 100 | 500 ns | 2 MHz | Bit-banging GPIO |
| 1,999 | 2,000 | 10 μs | 100 kHz | DC motor PWM |
| 4,166 | 4,167 | ~20.83 μs | 48 kHz | **Audio DAC** |
| 24,999 | 25,000 | 125 μs | 8 kHz | **Voice / Telephony** |
| 199,999 | 200,000 | 1 ms | 1 kHz | System tick / RTOS |
| 1,999,999 | 2,000,000 | 10 ms | 100 Hz | LED dimming / UI refresh |
| 199,999,999 | 200,000,000 | 1 s | 1 Hz | Clock second pulse |
| 4,294,967,295 | 4,294,967,296 | ~21.47 s | ~0.0466 Hz | Watchdog / Sleep wakeup |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design a timer for Fs," always show the **substitution of numerical values** explicitly. A common 2-mark loss occurs when students write only the formula but skip the numerical substitution step.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Software Delay Using Timer Overflow Count

If a software task requires a delay of $T_{delay} = 500$ ms using a timer ticking at $F_s = 1$ kHz, how many timer overflows (interrupts) must be counted?

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Find the timer period.

$$T_{period} = \frac{1}{F_s} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the number of overflows.

$$N_{overflows} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{period}} = \frac{500 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 500$$

**Step 3:** The ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) must increment a software counter and only act when the count reaches **500**.

This technique is the foundation of **software timing stacks** built atop a single hardware tick.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLK_SRC[Clock Source Mux: CPUCLK or TINP pin] --> PREE[Prescaler Divider 1, 2, 4, 8]
    PREE --> CNTR[32-bit Down Counter Register CNT]
    PRD_REG[Period Register PRD 32-bit] --> CNTR
    CNTR --> COMP[Zero Comparator Logic]
    COMP --> EVT_GEN[Event Generator]
    CTL_REG[Control Register CTL] --> CLK_SRC
    CTL_REG --> PREE
    CTL_REG --> CNTR
    CTL_REG --> EVT_GEN
    EVT_GEN --> TOUT_PIN[TOUT Pin to External ADC or DAC]
    EVT_GEN --> INT_REQ[Interrupt Request to CPU]
    EVT_GEN --> EDMA_EVT[EDMA Synchronization Event]
    INT_REQ --> ISR[Interrupt Service Routine in CPU]
    ISR --> SAMPLE[Fetch or Store Audio Sample from Buffer]
    EVT_GEN --> RELOAD[Auto Reload CNT from PRD]
    RELOAD --> CNTR
```

---

## 4.2 Timer Programming Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Power On or Reset] --> B[Step 2: Configure PLL for desired CPU clock]
    B --> C[Step 3: Disable Timer in CTL register]
    C --> D[Step 4: Load PRD register with computed value]
    D --> E[Step 5: Select clock source in CTL: internal or external]
    E --> F[Step 6: Select mode in CTL: continuous or one-shot]
    F --> G[Step 7: Configure TOUT pin polarity in CTL]
    G --> H[Step 8: Enable timer interrupt in IER if required]
    H --> I[Step 9: Write control word to CTL to start timer]
    I --> J[Step 10: Timer runs autonomously until stopped]
    J --> K[On CNT equals zero: generate interrupt, pulse TOUT, reload CNT]
    K --> J
```

---

## 4.3 Real-Time DSP Sampling Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ANALOG[Analog Signal Input] --> ADC[ADC Hardware]
    ADC --> BUF[Buffer in Memory]
    TIMER[Timer fires every 1 over Fs seconds] --> DMA[EDMA Trigger]
    DMA --> BUF
    BUF --> CPU[CPU runs DSP algorithm]
    CPU --> OUTBUF[Output Buffer]
    OUTBUF --> DMA2[EDMA Trigger]
    DMA2 --> DAC[DAC Hardware]
    DAC --> ANALOG_OUT[Analog Signal Output]
    TIMER --> DMA2
    TIMER --> INT[CPU Interrupt for Algorithm Execution]
    INT --> CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Timer** is the *heartbeat* of the entire real-time DSP pipeline. It synchronizes the ADC sampling, the EDMA data transfer, the CPU processing, and the DAC output — all to a single, deterministic, hardware-generated clock edge. This is the essence of **Real-Time Signal Processing**.

---

## 4.4 Pin-Level Signal Timing Diagram (ASCII Schematic)

```
              ┌─────┐                                  ┌─────┐
TOUT ─────────┘     └────────── ... ──────────────────┘     └──────
              │                                  │
              │  T_period = (PRD+1) * T_tick     │
              │                                  │
              │←──────── N clock ticks ─────────→│

CNT  ──────► (PRD) ──► (PRD-1) ──► ... ──► (1) ──► (0) ──► (PRD) ──► ...
                                       (Event fires here)
```

The TOUT pin transitions to its active state (logic HIGH or LOW depending on **CPOL** bit in CTL) at the instant CNT transitions from 1 to 0, and holds for one CPU clock cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q: Define a hardware timer in a DSP processor. Why is a hardware timer preferred over software delay loops for real-time DSP applications?**

**Model Answer:**

A **hardware timer** is a dedicated on-chip peripheral in a DSP processor (such as the TMS320C6x) that consists of a 32-bit down-counter, a period register (PRD), and a control register (CTL). It generates precisely timed events — interrupts, output pulses, or EDMA triggers — by counting clock cycles in hardware.

A hardware timer is preferred over software delay loops in real-time DSP for the following reasons:

1. **Determinism:** Software loops suffer variable latency due to cache misses, pipeline stalls, and branch mispredictions. Hardware timers count in silicon with **single-cycle precision** and **zero jitter**.
2. **CPU Offload:** The timer runs independently of the CPU, freeing the processor to perform DSP computations while the timer handles scheduling.
3. **EDMA Synchronization:** Hardware timers can directly trigger EDMA transfers, enabling **ping-pong buffering** without CPU intervention — essential for streaming audio.
4. **Long-Duration Timing:** A 32-bit timer can measure up to **~21 seconds at 200 MHz**, far exceeding practical software loop counts.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q: Differentiate between Continuous Mode and One-Shot Mode of operation in a DSP timer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Continuous Mode (MODE = 1) | One-Shot Mode (MODE = 0) |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Behavior** | Timer auto-reloads CNT from PRD after reaching zero and continues indefinitely. | Timer stops after the first period expires; CNT remains at zero until software re-enables it. |
| **Re-start** | Automatic reload — no CPU intervention required. | CPU must re-write the CTL register to re-start. |
| **Use Case** | Sample-rate generation for ADC/DAC, periodic control loops. | One-time delays, time-stamping of external events. |
| **CPU Overhead** | Minimal — set up once, runs forever. | High — must re-enable after each period. |
| **EDMA Use** | Ideal for continuous streaming applications. | Useful for single-trigger EDMA transfers. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the on-chip timer in the TMS320C6x DSP processor. List all its registers and their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The TMS320C6x DSP processor contains **two or three identical 32-bit general-purpose timers** (Timer0, Timer1, optionally Timer2). Each timer consists of the following blocks:

**1. Three Main Registers:**
- **Counter Register (CNT) — 32 bits:** Holds the current count value. It decrements by 1 on every active clock edge. When CNT reaches 0, the timer generates an event.
- **Period Register (PRD) — 32 bits:** Defines the period of the timer. When CNT underflows to zero, it is automatically reloaded from PRD (in continuous mode).
- **Control Register (CTL) — 32 bits:** Configures the timer. Key bits include:
  - **HLD** (bit 0): Hold — freezes the counter when set.
  - **MODE** (bit 1): Selects continuous (1) or one-shot (0) mode.
  - **PHS** (bit 2): Phase synchronization with external pulse.
  - **TSTAT** (bit 3): TOUT pin status (read-only).
  - **INVIN** (bit 4): Invert TINP input.
  - **INVOUT** (bit 5): Invert TOUT output.
  - **CP** (bit 6): Clock source prescaler (÷2).
  - **CLKSRC** (bit 7): Clock source (0 = internal CPU clock, 1 = external TINP pin).
  - **ENAMODE** (bits 9–8): Enable mode (00 = disabled, 01 = one-shot, 10 = continuous, 11 = reserved).

**2. Supporting Hardware:**
- **Clock Multiplexer:** Selects between the CPU clock (CLKIN) and the external TINP pin.
- **Prescaler:** Optionally divides the clock by 2 for slower applications.
- **Zero Comparator:** Detects when CNT = 0.
- **Event Generator:** Produces a TOUT pulse, an interrupt to the CPU, and an EDMA synchronization event.
- **TOUT Pin:** Output pin routed to external hardware (ADC, DAC, FPGA, etc.).
- **TINP Pin:** Optional external clock input for synchronization with external systems.

*(Insert the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 here for full credit.)*

**3. Functional Operation:**
When enabled, PRD is loaded into CNT. On every clock edge, CNT decrements. When CNT = 0, an event is generated, and CNT is reloaded from PRD. The cycle repeats indefinitely in continuous mode.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A TMS320C6713 DSP operates at a CPU clock of 225 MHz. An audio codec requires a sample rate of 44.1 kHz. Calculate the value to be loaded into the Period Register (PRD). Also compute the actual sample rate achieved and the percentage error.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{F_s} - 1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$F_{CPU} = 225 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad F_s = 44.1 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{225 \times 10^6}{44.1 \times 10^3} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{225{,}000{,}000}{44{,}100} - 1 = 5102.04... - 1 = 5101.04$$

**Step 4 — Round to nearest integer (PRD must be unsigned 32-bit):**

$$\boxed{\text{PRD} = 5101}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the actual achieved sample rate:**

$$F_{s,actual} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{\text{PRD} + 1} = \frac{225 \times 10^6}{5102}$$

$$F_{s,actual} \approx 44{,}100.35 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the absolute error:**

$$\Delta F_s = F_{s,actual} - F_s = 44{,}100.35 - 44{,}100 = +0.35 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the percentage error:**

$$\text{Error \%} = \frac{0.35}{44{,}100} \times 100\% \approx 0.000794\% \approx 7.94 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 8 — Conclude:**

The achieved sample rate of 44,100.35 Hz deviates from the target of 44,100 Hz by only **0.35 Hz (7.94 ppm)**, which is well within the **±50 ppm tolerance** of typical consumer audio equipment. Therefore, the PRD value of **5101** is acceptable for high-fidelity audio applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the Timer Control Register (CTL) of the TMS320C6x DSP in detail. List all important control bits and their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Control Register (CTL)** is a 32-bit register that governs the entire behavior of the timer. Its bit fields are as follows:

| Bit Position | Name | Function |
|:------------:|:----:|----------|
| 0 | **HLD** | **Hold** — When set, freezes the counter; useful for debugging. When cleared, counter runs. |
| 1 | **MODE** | Selects **Continuous Mode (1)** or **One-Shot Mode (0)**. |
| 2 | **PHS** | **Phase** — When set, the counter is reset and synchronized on the rising edge of TINP. |
| 3 | **TSTAT** | **TOUT Status** (read-only) — Current logic level of the TOUT pin. |
| 4 | **INVIN** | **Invert Input** — Inverts the TINP pin before clocking the counter. |
| 5 | **INVOUT** | **Invert Output** — Inverts the TOUT pin polarity. |
| 6 | **CP** | **Clock Prescaler** — When set, the input clock is divided by 2. |
| 7 | **CLKSRC** | **Clock Source** — 0 = internal CPU clock, 1 = external TINP pin. |
| 9–8 | **ENAMODE** | **Enable Mode** — 00 = disabled, 01 = one-shot, 10 = continuous, 11 = reserved. |
| 31–10 | **Reserved** | Always write 0. |

**Key programming sequence:**

1. Write **0** to ENAMODE bits to disable the timer (safe initialization).
2. Load the PRD register.
3. Configure CLKSRC, MODE, CP, INVIN, INVOUT.
4. Set ENAMODE = 10 for continuous mode (or 01 for one-shot) to start the timer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Never modify the CTL bits other than ENAMODE while the timer is running. This can cause a glitch on TOUT or a spurious interrupt. Always disable, configure, then re-enable.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a suitable example, explain how the DSP timer is used to generate periodic interrupts for real-time audio sampling. Draw the interrupt timing diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

**Application:** A real-time audio recorder samples a microphone signal at **Fs = 8 kHz** on a DSP with **F_CPU = 100 MHz**. Each sample must be processed (e.g., filtered, encoded) within **125 μs**.

**Step 1 — Calculate PRD:**

$$\text{PRD} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{F_s} - 1 = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^3} - 1 = 12{,}500 - 1 = 12{,}499$$

**Step 2 — Initialize the timer:**

```
CTL  = 0x00000140  (or as per bit field)  // CLKSRC = internal, MODE = continuous
PRD  = 12499
CTL  = 0x00000340                         // Set ENAMODE = 10 (continuous, enabled)
IER  |= 0x00000020                        // Enable timer interrupt in IER
```

**Step 3 — Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) behavior:**

On every timer interrupt (every 125 μs):
1. Read ADC sample register.
2. Store in the next position of a circular buffer.
3. Increment buffer index (mod buffer size).
4. If buffer half-full, set a flag for the main loop to process the half-buffer.

**Step 4 — Timing diagram:**

```
       t=0          t=125us        t=250us       t=375us       t=500us
        │              │              │              │              │
ISR  ───┐─────────────┐─────────────┐─────────────┐─────────────┐───
        │   125 μs    │   125 μs    │   125 μs    │   125 μs    │
TOUT  ──┐─────────────┐─────────────┐─────────────┐─────────────┐───
        └─ one tick ──┘─ one tick ──┘─ one tick ──┘─ one tick ──┘
```

Each interrupt fires exactly 125 μs after the previous one, with **jitter of less than 5 ns** (one CPU clock cycle at 100 MHz). This is the **deterministic, real-time guarantee** that software loops cannot provide.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The hardware timer, programmed with PRD = 12,499, generates a **deterministic 8 kHz interrupt cadence**, freeing the CPU to focus on DSP computations while the peripheral handles timing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully to Avoid Losing Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in PRD formula:** The #1 most common error. Students write PRD = F_CPU / Fs, missing the "-1" suffix. Cost: **2 marks lost per instance**.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion:** Mixing up kHz with Hz. Always write $F_s = 48 \times 10^3$ Hz explicitly. Cost: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not specifying 32-bit width:** Examiners expect students to mention that PRD and CNT are **32-bit registers**. Forgetting this costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing CNT (Counter) with CTL (Control):** These are different registers. CTL configures the timer; CNT holds the current count.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step:** After computing PRD, students should compute the actual $F_s$ achieved and show the percentage error. Skipping this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram** in architecture questions: Examiners award up to **3–4 marks** for a clear, labeled block diagram of the timer. A textual-only answer is penalized.
> 7. **Mixing up clock sources:** The CLKSRC bit selects between CPU clock and TINP. Stating "the timer uses the system clock" without specifying is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- ☐ A **timer** is a 32-bit hardware peripheral that generates precisely timed events.
- ☐ The three key registers are **CNT (Counter)**, **PRD (Period)**, and **CTL (Control)**.
- ☐ The **golden formula**: $\text{PRD} = \dfrac{F_{CPU}}{F_s} - 1$ — **never forget the +1 in the denominator of the period relation**.
- ☐ **Continuous Mode** = auto-reload, runs forever. **One-Shot Mode** = single period, must be re-enabled.
- ☐ **Clock Source** (CLKSRC bit): internal CPU clock OR external TINP pin.
- ☐ **TOUT pin** can drive external hardware (ADC, DAC, FPGAs).
- ☐ Timer can generate **CPU interrupts** AND **EDMA synchronization events** — both simultaneously.
- ☐ **Maximum frequency** = $F_{CPU}$ (PRD = 0). **Minimum frequency** = $F_{CPU} / 2^{32}$.
- ☐ Always **disable the timer first** (ENAMODE = 00), then **configure PRD and CTL**, then **enable** (ENAMODE = 10 for continuous).
- ☐ Hardware timers are **deterministic** — single-cycle precision, no jitter, unlike software delays.
- ☐ Standard sample rates to memorize: **8 kHz (voice), 44.1 kHz (CD audio), 48 kHz (pro audio)**.
- ☐ Always **show the verification** (compute actual $F_s$ and percentage error) to earn full marks.
- ☐ Timers are the **heartbeat of real-time DSP** — they synchronize ADC, DAC, EDMA, and CPU processing.
- ☐ The "+1" in formulas arises because the counter traverses **(PRD + 1) distinct values** before the event fires.

---

*End of Module 2 — Timers Notes*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus & Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Module 2: Interrupts in Real-Time DSP Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt (KTU Standard Definition):** An interrupt is an **asynchronous event** (hardware or software) that suspends the currently executing program in a DSP processor, forcing the CPU to switch execution to a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** to handle a time-critical event. After the ISR completes, the processor resumes the original task exactly where it left off, preserving the program context.

In the context of **Real-Time DSP**, interrupts are the *primary mechanism* that allows a processor to respond to external real-world signals (e.g., a new audio sample arriving at an ADC) within a deterministic time bound — a property essential for embedded DSP applications like audio codecs, motor control, and telecommunications.

### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a chef **baking a cake** (the main program). Suddenly, the **doorbell rings** (an external event/interrupt). You:

1. **Pause** the current step of the recipe (saving the current state — e.g., "currently mixing eggs").
2. **Walk to the door** and greet the guest (this is the ISR — Interrupt Service Routine).
3. **Return to the kitchen** and **resume** from exactly where you stopped (context restoration).
4. **Continue baking** the cake (return to main program).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Without interrupts, the chef would have to keep checking the door every 30 seconds (a technique called **polling**) — wasting enormous CPU time. Interrupts allow the CPU to focus on the main task and *only* respond when truly needed.

### 🔑 Types of Interrupts (KTU Classification)

| Type | Trigger Source | Example in DSP |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| **Hardware Interrupt** | External pin or on-chip peripheral | McBSP receives new sample, External INT pin |
| **Software Interrupt** | Executed instruction (TRAP/SWI) | `TRAP #n` instruction in C6x |
| **Maskable Interrupt** | Can be disabled/enabled by software | Most DSP interrupts (via IER register) |
| **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** | Cannot be disabled; highest priority | Power failure, hardware reset |

### 🎯 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **TMS320C67x Family:** Supports up to **16** interrupt sources (INT4 – INT15).
- **Interrupt Vector Table Size:** 16 vectors × 4 bytes = **64 bytes**, located at address **0x0000 0000**.
- **Pipeline Drain Time (C6x):** **5 cycles** fixed latency before ISR fetch begins.
- **Typical Real-Time Latency Bound:** **1 µs – 10 µs** for audio DSP applications.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the focus is on the **TMS320C6x DSP family** interrupt architecture. Make sure to memorize the 5-step interrupt processing sequence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. Theoretical Framework of Interrupt Processing

### 🏗️ The 5-Step Interrupt Processing Sequence (TMS320C6x)
