The C6x DSP handles interrupts via a strict, deterministic sequence — crucial for real-time guarantees:

1. **CPU completes the current instruction** in the pipeline (no instruction preemption mid-execution).
2. **CPU acknowledges the interrupt** by setting the corresponding bit in the **Interrupt Flag Register (IFR)**.
3. **Pipeline is drained** (flush = **5 clock cycles** for C6x). This is a *fixed* hardware latency.
4. **Program Counter (PC) is loaded** with the address of the ISR fetched from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**.
5. **ISR executes**, then issues a `B IRP` (Branch using Interrupt Return Pointer) instruction to resume the main program.

### 🧩 Interrupt-Related Registers in TMS320C6x

| Register | Full Name | Function | KTU Significance |
|----------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **CSR** | Control Status Register | Contains `GIE` (Global Interrupt Enable) bit | Master switch for all maskable interrupts |
| **IER** | Interrupt Enable Register | Enables/disables specific interrupts (1=enable) | Per-interrupt mask |
| **IFR** | Interrupt Flag Register | Latched status of pending interrupts | Read to identify source |
| **ISR** | Interrupt Set Register | Manually sets (triggers) an interrupt | Software interrupt generation |
| **ICR** | Interrupt Clear Register | Clears pending interrupt flags | Edge-triggered acknowledgment |
| **ISTP** | Interrupt Service Table Pointer | Base address of the IVT | Points to vector table |
| **IRP** | Interrupt Return Pointer | Saves PC for return from ISR | Used by `B IRP` instruction |
| **NMIE** | Non-Maskable Interrupt Enable | Special bit in CSR for NMI | Must be set to 1 to receive NMI |

### ⚙️ Interrupt Priority Scheme (Fixed Priority in C6x)

The C6x uses **fixed hardware priority** — interrupt **INT4 has lowest priority**, **INT15 (or NMI) has highest**:

$$\text{Priority: } \text{RESET} > \text{NMI} > \text{INT15} > \text{INT14} > \cdots > \text{INT4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-time DSP, **priority determines preemption**. A higher-priority interrupt can interrupt a lower-priority ISR (this is called **nested interrupts**). To enable nesting, the ISR must explicitly set `GIE = 1` early in its execution.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{latency} = T_{pipeline\_drain} + T_{ISR\_fetch} + T_{context\_save}$ | CPU cycles | C6x: 5 + 1 + N cycles |
| **Pipeline Drain Time** | $T_{drain} = 5$ cycles (fixed) | CPU cycles | Cannot be reduced |
| **Total Interrupt Response Time** | $T_{resp} = 5 + 1 + (N_{save} \times C_{save}) + T_{ISR}$ | CPU cycles | Includes ISR execution |
| **Max Interrupt Rate (No Miss)** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{resp}}$ | interrupts/sec | Hard real-time bound |
| **CPU Utilization by ISR** | $U_{ISR} = \dfrac{T_{ISR}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | Must be $\leq 70\%$ typically |
| **Worst-Case Execution Time** | $T_{WCET} = T_{main} + \sum T_{ISR_{i}}$ | CPU cycles | Sum of all ISRs |
| **Time per Cycle (C67x @ 225 MHz)** | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{225 \times 10^{6}} \approx 4.44$ ns | nanoseconds | Standard KTU value |
| **Interrupt Vector Address** | $Addr_{vec} = ISTP + (n \times 4)$ | bytes | $n$ = interrupt number (4–15) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in real-time DSP systems (audio codecs, radar processors, motor controllers) to mathematically **guarantee** that no interrupt is missed. If $T_{resp} > T_{period}$, the system is **overloaded** and samples will be dropped — a catastrophic failure in real-time DSP.

### 🔄 Context Saving Strategies

Two methods exist for saving CPU registers during an ISR:

1. **Automatic (Hardware) Saving:** Only the PC, CSR, and a few control registers are saved. Fast, but limited.
2. **Software (Manual) Saving:** Programmer writes ISR prologue/epilogue to save all modified registers. Slower, but flexible.

$$\text{Prologue: } \text{STW } R_n, *SP--[N]$$

$$\text{Epilogue: } \text{LDW } *++SP[N], R_n$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3. Exhaustive Mathematical Derivations

### 🧮 Derivation 1: Interrupt Latency Bound for TMS320C67x

**Given:** A TMS320C67x DSP running at $f_{CPU} = 225$ MHz. An external ADC generates an interrupt every $T_{period} = 10$ µs. The ISR requires $N_{ISR} = 200$ CPU cycles. The context save takes $N_{save} = 20$ cycles.

**Find:** Maximum interrupt latency in nanoseconds, and verify the system can handle the real-time load.

**Step 1: Identify the fixed pipeline drain latency.**

The C6x architecture has a deterministic **5-cycle pipeline drain** before the ISR fetch begins. This is a hardware constant.

$$T_{drain} = 5 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2: Identify the ISR fetch time.**

After the pipeline drain, exactly **1 additional cycle** is required to fetch the first instruction of the ISR from the vector table.

$$T_{fetch} = 1 \text{ cycle}$$

**Step 3: Identify the context save time.**

The software prologue saves the required registers. Given in the problem:

$$T_{save} = N_{save} \times 1 \text{ cycle} = 20 \times 1 = 20 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4: Identify the ISR execution time.**

Given in the problem as:

$$T_{ISR} = 200 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 5: Sum all latency components to get total interrupt response time.**

$$T_{resp} = T_{drain} + T_{fetch} + T_{save} + T_{ISR}$$

$$T_{resp} = 5 + 1 + 20 + 200 = 226 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 6: Convert cycles to absolute time using the CPU clock period.**

The CPU clock period is:

$$T_{cyc} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{225 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{cyc} = 4.444 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 4.444 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 7: Compute absolute response time.**

$$T_{resp}^{abs} = 226 \times 4.444 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{resp}^{abs} \approx 1004 \text{ ns} \approx 1.004 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 8: Verify the real-time constraint.**

The interrupt period is $T_{period} = 10$ µs. The system meets the real-time bound if:

$$T_{resp} \leq T_{period}$$

$$1.004 \text{ µs} \leq 10 \text{ µs} \quad \checkmark \text{(SATISFIED)}$$

**Step 9: Compute CPU utilization by the ISR.**

$$U_{ISR} = \frac{T_{resp}^{abs}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$

$$U_{ISR} = \frac{1.004}{10} \times 100\% = 10.04\%$$

> [!TIP]
> A utilization of **~10%** is healthy — the CPU has **~90% headroom** for the main DSP algorithm (e.g., FFT, filtering). Anything above **70%** is risky for real-time systems.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Rate

**Given:** Same C67x processor (225 MHz), with $T_{resp} = 226$ cycles total.

**Find:** The maximum rate at which interrupts can be serviced without missing any.

**Step 1: State the real-time constraint.**

To avoid missing interrupts:

$$T_{period} \geq T_{resp}$$

**Step 2: Express the maximum interrupt frequency.**

The maximum rate is the inverse of the minimum period:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{period}^{min}} = \frac{1}{T_{resp}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute cycle-based time.**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{resp}^{abs}} = \frac{1}{1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate.**

$$f_{max} \approx 996{,}016 \text{ interrupts/sec} \approx 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**Conclusion:** The system can service up to **~1 million interrupts per second** reliably.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Interrupt Vector Table Address Calculation

**Given:** `ISTP` register contains base address `0x0000 0200`. Find the vector address for `INT8`.

**Step 1: Use the vector address formula.**

$$Addr_{vec} = ISTP + (n \times 4)$$

where $n$ is the interrupt number.

**Step 2: Substitute values (n = 8).**

$$Addr_{vec} = 0x0000 0200 + (8 \times 4)$$

**Step 3: Compute in hex.**

$$Addr_{vec} = 0x0000 0200 + 0x0000 0020$$

$$Addr_{vec} = 0x0000 0220$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**

The ISR for INT8 is located at memory address `0x0000 0220` and is exactly 4 bytes (one instruction word for a branch).

---

### 📋 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Polling vs. Interrupts

| Parameter | Polling | Interrupts |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| **CPU Utilization** | High (wastes cycles) | Low (event-driven) |
| **Response Time** | Variable (worst-case = full loop) | Deterministic (bounded latency) |
| **Code Complexity** | Simple | Complex (ISR + vector table) |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | Difficult to prove | Easy via $T_{latency} \leq T_{period}$ |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor (CPU always active) | Good (CPU can idle) |
| **Suitability for DSP** | Rarely used | **Standard practice** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4. Mermaid Block Diagrams for Interrupt Architecture

### 🔄 Diagram 1: Complete Interrupt Processing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Executing] --> B{Interrupt Pending Check}
    B -->|No Pending| A
    B -->|Yes - IFR Bit Set| C[Complete Current Instruction]
    C --> D[Pipeline Drain - 5 Cycles]
    D --> E[Fetch Vector Address from IVT]
    E --> F[Load PC with ISR Address]
    F --> G[Save Context - CSR, AMR, etc.]
    G --> H[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    H --> I{More Pending Interrupts}
    I -->|Yes Higher Priority| H
    I -->|No| J[Restore Context]
    J --> K[B IRP - Branch to Return]
    K --> A
```

### 🏛️ Diagram 2: Interrupt Vector Table Architecture (C6x)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0x0000 0000 - RESET Vector] --> B[0x0000 0004 - NMI Vector]
    B --> C[0x0000 0008 - Reserved]
    C --> D[0x0000 000C - Reserved]
    D --> E[0x0000 0010 - INT4 Vector]
    E --> F[0x0000 0014 - INT5 Vector]
    F --> G[0x0000 0018 - INT6 Vector]
    G --> H[0x0000 001C - INT7 Vector]
    H --> I[0x0000 0020 - INT8 Vector]
    I --> J[0x0000 0024 - INT9 Vector]
    J --> K[0x0000 0028 - INT10 Vector]
    K --> L[0x0000 002C - INT11 Vector]
    L --> M[0x0000 0030 - INT12 Vector]
    M --> N[0x0000 0034 - INT13 Vector]
    N --> O[0x0000 0038 - INT14 Vector]
    O --> P[0x0000 003C - INT15 Vector]
```

### 🧩 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Interrupt Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExternalSources[External Interrupt Sources]
        S1[ADC - Audio Sample Ready]
        S2[McBSP - Serial Port]
        S3[GPIO - User Button]
        S4[Timer Overflow]
    end
    subgraph InterruptController[Interrupt Controller Block]
        MUX[Interrupt Selector MUX]
        IFR[Interrupt Flag Register IFR]
        IER[Interrupt Enable Register IER]
        AND[AND Gate - Mask Logic]
    end
    subgraph CPUCore[CPU Core]
        CSR[CSR - GIE Bit]
        PC[Program Counter]
        EXE[Execution Unit]
    end
    subgraph MemoryMap[Memory]
        IVT[Interrupt Vector Table IVT]
        ISR[Interrupt Service Routine Code]
    end
    S1 --> MUX
    S2 --> MUX
    S3 --> MUX
    S4 --> MUX
    MUX --> IFR
    IER --> AND
    IFR --> AND
    AND --> CSR
    CSR --> PC
    IVT --> PC
    PC --> EXE
    EXE --> ISR
    ISR --> EXE
```

### 🛡️ Diagram 4: Priority-Based Nested Interrupt Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Main Program] -->|INT4 Arrives| I1[ISR for INT4 - Low Priority]
    I1 -->|INT12 Arrives| I2[ISR for INT12 - High Priority]
    I2 -->|INT12 Complete| I1
    I1 -->|INT4 Complete| P1
    P1 -->|INT15 Arrives| I3[ISR for INT15 - Highest Priority]
    I3 -->|INT15 Complete| P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams:** All flow from top to bottom or left to right. The interrupt controller combines `IFR` (which interrupt is pending) with `IER` (which interrupts are enabled) and `CSR.GIE` (global enable) — all three must be **active** for the CPU to take the interrupt.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5. KTU Practice Question Bank

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Interrupt** as applied to a real-time DSP system. Differentiate between **maskable** and **non-maskable** interrupts with one example for each in the TMS320C6x processor.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An interrupt is an asynchronous event (hardware or software generated) that temporarily halts the normal execution of the main program, causing the CPU to execute a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Once the ISR completes, the processor resumes the main program from the exact point of suspension, with all register states preserved.

**Maskable Interrupt (1 Mark):** A maskable interrupt can be enabled or disabled by the programmer using the **Interrupt Enable Register (IER)** and the **Global Interrupt Enable (GIE)** bit in the **Control Status Register (CSR)**. *Example:* `INT4` through `INT15` in TMS320C67x — used for McBSP, timers, and other peripherals.

**Non-Maskable Interrupt (1 Mark):** A non-maskable interrupt (NMI) **cannot be disabled** by software and has the highest priority in the system. It is used for catastrophic events requiring immediate CPU attention. *Example:* The **NMI** and **RESET** signals in TMS320C67x, used for power failure or fatal hardware errors.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the role of the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** in the TMS320C6x DSP? State its starting address and total size.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role (2 Marks):** The Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) is a pre-defined block of memory that stores the **branch addresses (vectors)** of all Interrupt Service Routines supported by the processor. When an interrupt is acknowledged, the CPU fetches the appropriate vector from this table and loads it into the **Program Counter (PC)**, transferring control to the corresponding ISR. It is the bridge between the hardware interrupt signal and the software service routine.

**Location & Size (1 Mark):** The IVT in TMS320C6x starts at address **`0x0000 0000`** (or at the address pointed to by the `ISTP` register) and contains **16 vectors × 4 bytes = 64 bytes** total. The 16 entries include RESET, NMI, Reserved × 2, and INT4 through INT15.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete interrupt processing sequence** in the TMS320C6x DSP processor with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the following registers: `CSR`, `IER`, `IFR`, and `IRP`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A TMS320C67x DSP is clocked at $f_{CPU} = 200$ MHz. An interrupt arrives every $T_{period} = 20$ µs. Given the following ISR details: pipeline drain = 5 cycles, ISR fetch = 1 cycle, context save = 15 cycles, ISR body = 250 cycles. Calculate: (i) total response time in cycles, (ii) absolute response time in µs, (iii) maximum sustainable interrupt rate, (iv) CPU utilization. Comment on whether the system meets the real-time deadline. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Interrupt Processing Sequence (7 Marks):**

The TMS320C6x DSP processes interrupts in a **5-stage deterministic sequence** that guarantees bounded latency — a critical property for real-time DSP systems.

**Stage 1 — Interrupt Detection (1 Mark):**
When a hardware interrupt is asserted (e.g., a new audio sample arrives at the McBSP), the corresponding bit in the **Interrupt Flag Register (IFR)** is automatically set to 1 by hardware. This latches the interrupt request so it is not lost.

**Stage 2 — Masking Check (1 Mark):**
The CPU checks three conditions before taking the interrupt:
- The interrupt bit in `IER` must be 1 (per-interrupt enable).
- The `GIE` bit in the `CSR` register must be 1 (global enable).
- For NMI only, the `NMIE` bit must also be 1.
If any condition fails, the interrupt remains pending in `IFR` but is not serviced.

**Stage 3 — Pipeline Drain (1 Mark):**
The CPU completes the **current instruction** in the pipeline. The C6x architecture has a variable-length pipeline, so a **fixed 5-cycle drain** is enforced. This is a hardware constant and cannot be optimized by software.

**Stage 4 — Vector Fetch & Context Save (2 Marks):**
- The CPU fetches the ISR's branch address from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** at address `ISTP + n*4`.
- The current **Program Counter (PC)** value is saved into the special register **IRP (Interrupt Return Pointer)** for later use by the `B IRP` instruction.
- The PC is loaded with the ISR's vector address, and execution jumps to the ISR.
- If the ISR uses additional registers, the programmer's prologue manually saves them to the stack.

**Stage 5 — ISR Execution & Return (2 Marks):**
- The ISR executes its body (reading the ADC, computing the filter output, writing to the DAC, etc.).
- During execution, **higher-priority interrupts** can preempt (interrupt) the current ISR if `GIE = 1` is re-enabled — this is called **nested interrupts**.
- Once the ISR completes, it executes `B IRP` (Branch using Interrupt Return Pointer), which restores the PC and returns to the main program.

**Role of Registers (Summarized for marks):**

| Register | Role |
|----------|------|
| `CSR` (Control Status Register) | Contains `GIE` (global interrupt enable) and processor status flags |
| `IER` (Interrupt Enable Register) | Per-interrupt mask — must be 1 to allow specific interrupts |
| `IFR` (Interrupt Flag Register) | Latches pending interrupts — read to identify the source |
| `IRP` (Interrupt Return Pointer) | Saves PC of interrupted instruction; used by `B IRP` for return |

---

**Part (b) — Latency & Real-Time Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f_{CPU} = 200$ MHz, $T_{period} = 20$ µs, $T_{drain} = 5$ cycles, $T_{fetch} = 1$ cycle, $T_{save} = 15$ cycles, $T_{ISR} = 250$ cycles.

**(i) Total Response Time in Cycles (2 Marks):**

$$T_{resp} = T_{drain} + T_{fetch} + T_{save} + T_{ISR}$$

$$T_{resp} = 5 + 1 + 15 + 250 = 271 \text{ cycles}$$

**(ii) Absolute Response Time in Microseconds (2 Marks):**

The CPU clock period is:

$$T_{cyc} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \text{ ns}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{resp}^{abs} = 271 \times 5 \text{ ns} = 1355 \text{ ns} = 1.355 \text{ µs}$$

**(iii) Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Rate (1.5 Marks):**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{resp}^{abs}} = \frac{1}{1.355 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 7.38 \times 10^{5} \text{ interrupts/sec}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 738{,}007 \text{ Hz} \approx 738 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) CPU Utilization (1.5 Marks):**

$$U_{ISR} = \frac{T_{resp}^{abs}}{T_{period}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.355}{20} \times 100\% = 6.775\%$$

**Comment on Real-Time Compliance:**

Since $T_{resp}^{abs} = 1.355$ µs $\leq T_{period} = 20$ µs, the system **meets the real-time deadline** with a healthy margin. The CPU spends only ~6.78% of its time servicing the interrupt, leaving **~93.22% headroom** for the main DSP algorithm (e.g., FIR filter, FFT).

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **polling** and **interrupt-driven I/O** mechanisms in real-time DSP systems. Which mechanism is preferred for audio sampling at 48 kHz, and why? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the TMS320C6x interrupt architecture, explain the concept of **nested interrupts**. How is priority determined? What is the role of the `NMIE` bit? An interrupt `INT8` is currently being serviced. During the ISR, `INT12` and `INT14` arrive simultaneously. Which one is serviced next, and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Polling vs. Interrupts (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Polling | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|-----------|---------|----------------------|
| **Mechanism** | CPU repeatedly checks a flag in a loop | CPU is notified by hardware only when event occurs |
| **CPU Utilization** | High — CPU is busy looping even when no event | Low — CPU executes other tasks |
| **Response Latency** | Variable — depends on where CPU is in the polling loop | Deterministic — bounded by $T_{drain} + T_{fetch}$ |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Simple — just a `while` loop | Complex — requires ISR, vector table, context save |
| **Power Consumption** | High (CPU never sleeps) | Low (CPU can use IDLE instruction) |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | Difficult to mathematically prove | Easy: $T_{latency} \leq T_{period}$ must hold |
| **Throughput at High Rates** | Poor (CPU saturated by polling overhead) | Excellent (handles up to ~1 MHz rates) |

**Preferred Mechanism for 48 kHz Audio Sampling (2 Marks):**

The **interrupt-driven** mechanism is preferred. Justification:

- **Sample Period at 48 kHz:** $T_{period} = \frac{1}{48{,}000} \approx 20.83$ µs.
- A polling loop running at 200 MHz would need to check the ADC flag every cycle, wasting **~5 MIPS** of CPU time just for polling overhead.
- With interrupts, the CPU can spend **>99% of its time** executing the DSP algorithm (e.g., biquad filter, echo cancellation) and only briefly diverts to the ISR every 20.83 µs to read the new sample.
- This achieves **deterministic low jitter**, which is critical for high-fidelity audio — polling introduces timing jitter that causes audible distortion.

---

**Part (b) — Nested Interrupts (7 Marks):**

**Definition of Nested Interrupts (2 Marks):**
Nested interrupts occur when a **higher-priority interrupt preempts (interrupts) an already executing lower-priority ISR**. This is essential in real-time DSP to handle urgent events (e.g., emergency shutdown) without waiting for the current ISR to finish.

**Priority Determination (2 Marks):**
In the TMS320C6x, priority is **fixed by hardware**:
- **Highest:** `RESET` and `NMI`
- **Then:** `INT15` > `INT14` > `INT13` > ... > `INT4` (lowest)
The interrupt with the **lowest numerical value is serviced last** in this convention. The CPU checks priority at every cycle.

**Role of `NMIE` Bit (1 Mark):**
The `NMIE` (Non-Maskable Interrupt Enable) bit is a special bit in the CSR register. It **must be explicitly set to 1** by the ISR to allow subsequent NMIs to be recognized. After a RESET, `NMIE = 0` and must be enabled by software.

**Specific Scenario: INT8 vs. INT12 vs. INT14 (2 Marks):**
- Currently servicing: `INT8` (mid-ISR)
- New arrivals: `INT12` and `INT14` arrive simultaneously
- `INT14` has a **higher priority** than `INT12` (lower number = higher priority in C6x convention).
- Therefore, **`INT14` is serviced first**, preempting the `INT8` ISR.
- After `INT14` completes, `INT12` is serviced.
- Finally, execution returns to complete the `INT8` ISR, and then back to the main program.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently make these errors and lose 2–3 marks per question:
> 1. **Confusing the priority direction:** In TMS320C6x, **INT4 has the lowest** priority and **INT15 has the highest**. Some students assume INT4 is highest because it has the smallest number — this is **WRONG** for C6x.
> 2. **Forgetting to save CSR:** When modifying `GIE` inside an ISR for nesting, the original `GIE` value must be saved and restored — otherwise, the main program may return with interrupts disabled.
> 3. **Ignoring pipeline drain:** The 5-cycle pipeline drain is a **hardware constant** and cannot be reduced by software optimization. Students who omit it in latency calculations lose full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up `IFR` and `IER`:** `IFR` is **status** (read-only mostly), `IER` is **control** (read-write). Writing to `IFR` does not enable an interrupt.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interrupt** = asynchronous event that suspends the main program to execute an ISR.
- **TMS320C6x has 16 interrupts** (RESET, NMI, 2 reserved, INT4–INT15).
- **Fixed priority:** INT15 > INT14 > ... > INT4 > RESET > NMI (lowest number = lowest priority).
- **5-cycle pipeline drain** is a hardware constant in C6x interrupt processing.
- **Vector Table** is 64 bytes (16 × 4) starting at address `0x0000 0000` (or `ISTP` base).
- **Registers to memorize:** `CSR` (GIE), `IER` (enable mask), `IFR` (flag status), `IRP` (return pointer), `ISTP` (vector table base), `ISR`/`ICR` (set/clear flags).
- **Latency formula:** $T_{resp} = 5 + 1 + T_{save} + T_{ISR}$ (in cycles).
- **Real-time condition:** $T_{resp} \leq T_{period}$ (must hold for hard real-time).
- **CPU utilization should stay below ~70%** for safety margin.
- **Nested interrupts** require `GIE = 1` inside the ISR to allow preemption.
- **Polling is rarely used** in real-time DSP — interrupts are the standard.
- **NMI** cannot be masked and is used for catastrophic events only.
- **Maximum interrupt rate** = $1 / T_{resp}^{abs}$ (samples per second).
- **Two context-saving methods:** automatic (limited) and software/manual (flexible).
- **Time per cycle @ 225 MHz = 4.44 ns**; @ 200 MHz = 5 ns; @ 100 MHz = 10 ns.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** If a numerical problem is given, always show: (1) identification of all 4 latency components, (2) substitution into $T_{resp}$ formula, (3) cycle-to-time conversion, (4) comparison with $T_{period}$ for real-time compliance. This 4-step structure is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Data/Program Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Data/Program Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Internal Data/Program Memory** in a Real-Time Digital Signal Processor (DSP) refers to the **on-chip memory subsystem** that resides within the DSP integrated circuit itself, providing ultra-high-speed storage for both the executable instruction stream (Program Memory) and the operands/intermediate results (Data Memory) required by the signal processing algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** Internal memory is the high-speed, low-latency storage embedded within the DSP silicon die. It is architecturally segregated into **Program Memory** (holding opcodes, coefficients, and lookup tables) and **Data Memory** (holding variables, samples, and state information), enabling simultaneous fetch of instruction and operand — a feature impossible in classical von Neumann machines.

In KTU-referenced architectures (such as the **TMS320C5x** family which is the syllabus benchmark), the internal memory is divided as follows:

| Memory Type | Technology | Typical Capacity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Memory | ROM / EPROM | 2K – 32K words | Stores algorithms, coefficients, boot code |
| Program/Data RAM | Dual-Access RAM (DARAM) | 1K – 8K words | Configurable as P or D memory |
| Data Memory | Single-Access RAM (SARAM) | 4K – 32K words | Runtime samples, delay lines, buffers |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 The "Chef's Two Hands" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a master chef in a busy restaurant (the CPU) cooking dishes (instructions). He has:
> - **Left hand holding the recipe book** (Program Memory) — he must glance at it *constantly* to know the next step.
> - **Right hand picking ingredients from a cutting board** (Data Memory) — he chops, mixes, and processes them.
>
> If both hands had to reach into the *same drawer* for everything, he would constantly collide and slow down. So the chef uses **two separate, dedicated workstations**: one for the recipe (read-mostly cookbook) and one for ingredients (constantly changing). This is **Harvard Architecture** — the foundation of DSP internal memory design.
>
> The "cutting board" is kept **right next to the chef** (on-chip, internal memory) for instant access. The "pantry" (external memory) is far away in the storeroom — slower, used only when the cutting board overflows.

---

### 1.3 Why Internal Memory Matters in Real-Time DSP

A real-time DSP system (e.g., audio codec at **48 kHz sample rate**, video processing at **100 MHz pixel clock**) must complete all computations *within one sample period* without any latency drift. Internal memory provides three critical advantages:

1. **Single-cycle access** — no wait states (typically **0 wait states** at full clock).
2. **Parallel fetch** — separate buses for program and data (Harvard), so the CPU is never starved.
3. **Deterministic timing** — guaranteed access time, critical for hard real-time constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** External memory accesses on typical DSPs cost **1–7 wait states** per fetch, while internal memory accesses cost **0 wait states** — a **7× throughput penalty** if algorithms are not mapped internally.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Parameters (KTU Board Reference)

- **Internal Memory Access Time:** $t_{acc} \leq 1$ clock cycle (typically **15–20 ns** at 50 MHz for TMS320C5x).
- **External Memory Access Time:** $t_{acc} \geq 1 + N_{WS}$ cycles, where $N_{WS}$ = wait states (1–7).
- **Word Size:** 16 bits (TMS320C5x), 32 bits (TMS320C6x).
- **Memory Space Separation:** Program and Data spaces are **logically and physically distinct** with separate address buses ($PAB$, $DAB$) and data buses ($PBDB$, $DBDB$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Foundation — Harvard vs. Von Neumann

The DSP's internal memory layout is the *physical embodiment* of the **Modified Harvard Architecture**. Let us dissect why this matters:

- **Von Neumann bottleneck:** A single bus serves both instruction and data fetches → throughput halved.
- **Harvard architecture:** Two physically separate memory spaces (P and D) with two independent bus sets → simultaneous fetch + operand read/write.
- **Modified Harvard:** Allows data to be read from program memory (coefficients stored as constants in P-space) and allows instructions to be fetched from data memory in special cases (e.g., on-chip bootloader).

---

### 2.2 Program Memory — Detailed Anatomy

#### 2.2.1 Function
Program memory holds the **executable opcodes** of the DSP algorithm. In a real-time DSP application, it is common to also store:
- **Filter coefficients** (e.g., $h[k]$ of an FIR filter)
- **Sine/cosine lookup tables** (for Goertzel, DFT, NCOs)
- **Waveform tables** (for synthesizers)

#### 2.2.2 Address Space
For the TMS320C5x (KTU benchmark):
- **Total program address space:** $64\text{K} \times 16\text{-bit words}$ (16 address lines → $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations).
- A portion of the high address space is mapped to **on-chip ROM** (e.g., 4K words for the bootloader and standard routines).
- The rest is either **on-chip DARAM** (reconfigurable) or **external memory** (off-chip EPROM/SRAM).

#### 2.2.3 Program Counter (PC) and Address Generation
- The **Program Counter (PC)** is a 16-bit register that holds the address of the next instruction to fetch.
- Pipeline stages (4-stage in C5x) prefetch instructions; this requires the program memory bus to deliver one opcode per cycle without stalls.

---

### 2.3 Data Memory — Detailed Anatomy

#### 2.3.1 Function
Data memory stores:
- **Live input samples** $x[n]$, $x[n-1]$, … (circular buffer).
- **Accumulator results**, intermediate sums.
- **State variables** (e.g., integrator memory $y[n-1]$ in IIR filters).
- **Peripheral registers** (memory-mapped I/O).

#### 2.3.2 Memory-Mapped Registers (MMRs)
Special on-chip registers occupy the **top 128 words** of data memory (addresses 0x0000 to 0x007F on TMS320C5x). These include:
- **AR0–AR7:** Auxiliary registers for indirect addressing.
- **SP:** Stack pointer.
- **TREG0, TREG1, TREG2:** Temporary registers for multiply-accumulate.
- **PREG:** Product register (holds output of the multiplier).
- **ACC:** 32-bit accumulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** When a KTU question asks "Where are auxiliary registers located?" — the answer is **"in the data memory address space, occupying the first 128 locations (0x0000–0x007F), making them memory-mapped."**

#### 2.3.3 DARAM vs. SARAM
| Feature | DARAM (Dual-Access RAM) | SARAM (Single-Access RAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Ports | 2 simultaneous accesses per cycle | 1 access per cycle |
| Speed | 1 op/cycle (full speed) | 1 op/cycle (with care) |
| Use case | Critical data (delay lines) | Code, large buffers |
| Latency | 0 wait states | 0–1 wait states |

---

### 2.4 Addressing Modes for Internal Memory

The DSP supports three primary addressing modes (KTU-emphasized):

1. **Direct Addressing:** Instruction embeds a 7-bit page pointer + 7-bit offset → accesses a 128-word page.
2. **Indirect Addressing:** Auxiliary register (ARn) holds the effective address; supports auto-increment, decrement, bit-reversal (for FFT), and circular buffering.
3. **Immediate Addressing:** Operand is embedded in the instruction word itself (used for constants).

> [!NOTE]
> **Circular Buffering** is a KTU-favorite topic. Implemented using the BK (Block Size) register and the CDP (Circular Data Pointer) logic. When an indirect address wraps around the buffer, the hardware auto-rolls without software overhead — essential for FIR filter delay-line tap updates.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Program Memory Addressable | $2^{N_{addr}} \text{ words}$ | $N_{addr}=16$ for C5x → 64K words |
| 2 | Internal Access Time | $t_{int} = T_{clk}$ | seconds, $T_{clk}$ = clock period |
| 3 | External Access Time | $t_{ext} = (1 + N_{WS}) \cdot T_{clk}$ | $N_{WS}$ = wait states (1–7) |
| 4 | Throughput Penalty | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{ext}}{t_{int}} = 1 + N_{WS}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Real-Time Deadline (sample period) | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Max instructions per sample | $N_{inst}^{max} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{f_s}$ | integer instructions |
| 7 | Circular buffer length | $L = 2^{BK}$, where BK = block-size field | words, must be power of 2 |
| 8 | MAC execution time | $t_{MAC} = 1$ cycle (single-cycle MAC) | C5x: 1-cycle MAC = key DSP feature |
| 9 | Multiplication-Accumulate per sample (FIR of order N) | $T_{FIR} = (N+5) \cdot T_{clk}$ | N taps + overhead |

---

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Audio Equalizers & Codecs (e.g., MP3, AAC players):** Delay lines and coefficient arrays must reside in **on-chip DARAM** to meet sample deadlines at 44.1 kHz.
- **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** FIR/IIR channel filters and FFT buffers live in on-chip memory; off-chip SDRAM is only for the wideband sample stream.
- **Motor Control DSPs:** Inner current-loop algorithms (sub-µs loop times) demand single-cycle memory access — impossible with external SRAM.
- **Biomedical DSP (hearing aids, ECG monitors):** Ultra-low-power DSPs (e.g., TMS320C5504) keep entire algorithm in **< 64 KB on-chip RAM** to avoid power-hungry external bus toggling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Filter Order Achievable in Real-Time

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A DSP system uses a clock of $f_{CLK} = 50 \text{ MHz}$ and samples at $f_s = 48 \text{ kHz}$. The processor executes one MAC per cycle using data stored in **internal memory** (0 wait states). What is the maximum order $N$ of an FIR filter that can run in real time, assuming $N+5$ cycles are needed per tap (5-cycle overhead for loop control, I/O, and coefficient fetch)?

---

#### Step 1 — Define the Real-Time Constraint
For real-time processing, the algorithm must complete within **one sample period**:

$$T_{algo} \leq T_s$$

The sample period is the reciprocal of the sampling frequency:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{48{,}000} \approx 20.833 \ \mu s$$

#### Step 2 — Total Available Clock Cycles per Sample
Each sample period contains a certain number of clock cycles:

$$N_{cycles}^{avail} = \frac{T_s}{T_{clk}} = f_{CLK} \cdot T_s = \frac{f_{CLK}}{f_s}$$

Substituting values:

$$N_{cycles}^{avail} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{48 \times 10^3} = \frac{50{,}000{,}000}{48{,}000} \approx 1041.67 \text{ cycles/sample}$$

Rounding down (we cannot use a fractional cycle):

$$N_{cycles}^{avail} = 1041 \text{ cycles/sample}$$

#### Step 3 — Relate Cycles to Filter Order
For an FIR filter of order $N$ (which has $N+1$ taps), with 5-cycle overhead per output sample:

$$T_{algo} = (N + 5) \cdot T_{clk}$$

Expressed in cycles:

$$N_{cycles}^{used} = N + 5$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Real-Time Deadline
The algorithm's cycle count must not exceed the available cycles:

$$N + 5 \leq 1041$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for the Maximum Order N
$$N \leq 1041 - 5 = 1036$$

$$\boxed{N_{max} = 1036}$$

---

#### Step 6 — Counter-Example: What if We Used External Memory?
Suppose all data were in **external memory** with $N_{WS} = 2$ wait states. Then each MAC (which we earlier assumed took 1 cycle) now takes $1 + 2 = 3$ cycles.

$$N_{cycles}^{used} = 3(N + 5)$$

Setting $3(N+5) \leq 1041$:

$$N + 5 \leq 347$$

$$N_{max}^{ext} = 342$$

This is a **3× drop in filter order**, demonstrating why **internal memory mapping is critical**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wait-State Effect on Throughput

**Given:** External memory with 2 wait states, $f_{CLK} = 40$ MHz, $f_s = 8$ kHz (speech codec). 

**Find:** Real-time feasibility of a 200-tap FIR filter.

#### Step 1 — Compute available cycles per sample
$$N_{cycles}^{avail} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^3} = 5000 \text{ cycles}$$

#### Step 2 — Cycles used (with 2 wait states)
Each MAC → 1 + 2 = 3 cycles
$$N_{cycles}^{used} = 3 \times 200 + 5 = 605 \text{ cycles}$$

#### Step 3 — Slack time per sample
$$N_{slack} = 5000 - 605 = 4395 \text{ cycles}$$

The algorithm fits, with **87.9% slack** — confirming the design is feasible even with external memory in this lower-throughput case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** Internal memory is *mandatory* for high-throughput DSP (audio at 48 kHz with long filters, video); for low-sample-rate applications (slow sensor monitoring at 1 kHz), external memory may suffice.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Memory Configuration Trade-offs

| Configuration | Internal P | Internal D | External | Throughput | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| All-internal | ✓ Full | ✓ Full | ✗ | Maximum | High-end DSP (audio, comm) |
| Mixed-1 | ✓ (coeff) | ✗ | ✓ (samples) | Moderate | Cost-sensitive, moderate DSP |
| Mixed-2 | ✗ | ✓ (delay line) | ✓ (code) | Lower | Boot-from-Flash, large code |
| All-external | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Slowest | General-purpose MCU emulation (NOT recommended) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Memory Architecture of a Harvard DSP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUCore["DSP CPU Core\nALU + Multiplier + Shifter + ACC"]
    PAB["Program Address Bus\nPAB 16-bit"]
    DAB["Data Address Bus\nDAB 16-bit"]
    PBDB["Program Data Bus\nPBDB 16-bit"]
    DBDB["Data Data Bus\nDBDB 16-bit"]
    PMEM["Program Memory\nROM/DARAM\n64K words\n0x0000 to 0xFFFF"]
    DMEM["Data Memory\nDARAM + SARAM\n64K words\n0x0000 to 0xFFFF"]
    MMR["Memory Mapped Registers\nAR0-AR7, ACC, PREG\n0x0000 to 0x007F"]
    COEFF["Coefficient Storage\nFilter taps h[0]..h[N]"]
    BUFRAM["Delay Line Buffer\nx[n], x[n-1]..x[n-N]"]
    EXT["External Memory Bus\nAddress Latch + Wait State Logic"]
    PCREG["Program Counter PC\n16-bit register"]
    ARREGS["Auxiliary Registers\nAR0 to AR7"]

    CPUCore -- Fetch Instruction --> PAB
    CPUCore -- Read/Write Operand --> DAB
    PAB --> PMEM
    DAB --> DMEM
    PMEM -- Opcode Out --> PBDB
    DMEM -- Data Out --> DBDB
    PBDB --> CPUCore
    DBDB --> CPUCore
    PMEM --- COEFF
    DMEM --- BUFRAM
    DMEM --- MMR
    CPUCore --- PCREG
    CPUCore --- ARREGS
    DMEM -.->|overflow| EXT
    PMEM -.->|overflow| EXT
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The CPU core is the *chef's two hands*. Solid arrows show the two parallel data paths (Harvard), while dashed arrows show overflow to external memory (the "pantry").

---

### 4.2 Data Memory Map (TMS320C5x — KTU Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAP["Data Memory Address Space 0x0000 to 0xFFFF"]
    MMR["Memory Mapped Registers\n0x0000 to 0x007F\n128 words"]
    SCRATCH["Scratch Pad RAM\n0x0080 to 0x01FF\n384 words"]
    DARAM["On-Chip DARAM\n0x0200 to 0x07FF\n1.5K words"]
    SARAM["On-Chip SARAM\n0x0800 to 0x17FF\n4K words"]
    EXT["External Memory Mapped\n0x1800 to 0xFFFF\n59K words"]

    MAP --> MMR
    MAP --> SCRATCH
    MAP --> DARAM
    MAP --> SARAM
    MAP --> EXT

    MMRREG["AR0 AR1 AR2 AR3\nAR4 AR5 AR6 AR7\nSP TREG0 TREG1 TREG2\nPREG ACC"]
    MMR --- MMRREG

    DLINE["Delay Line\nx[n]..x[n-N]\nCircular Buffer"]
    DARAM --- DLINE
```

---

### 4.3 Pipeline–Memory Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Stage 1\nFetch\nProgram Memory Read"]
    F2["Stage 2\nDecode\nInstruction Register"]
    F3["Stage 3\nRead Operands\nData Memory Read"]
    F4["Stage 4\nExecute\nALU/MAC"]

    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    F1 -.->|reads from| PM[(Program\nMemory)]
    F3 -.->|reads from| DM[(Data\nMemory)]
    F4 -.->|writes to| DM
```

> **Key Insight:** In every clock cycle, **Stage 1 reads from Program Memory** *and* **Stage 3 reads from Data Memory** *simultaneously*. This is why two physically separate memories are mandatory.

---

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Memory Access with Wait States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["CPU Issues Address"]
    DEC["Address Decode Logic"]
    WS["Wait State Counter\n0 to 7 cycles"]
    READY["Memory READY signal"]
    ACK["Data Latched into CPU"]

    REQ --> DEC --> WS --> READY --> ACK
    WS -.->|increment| WS
    READY -.->|not ready| WS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Memory Mapped Registers" in the context of a DSP processor. Give two examples.*

**Model Answer:**

Memory-mapped registers (MMRs) are special-purpose on-chip CPU registers (such as the accumulator, auxiliary registers, and stack pointer) that are assigned fixed addresses in the **data memory address space** of the DSP. They are accessed using the same data-memory read/write instructions as ordinary RAM locations, but they control the internal operation of the CPU rather than storing user data.

**Two examples:**
1. **ACC (Accumulator):** Holds the 32-bit result of arithmetic/MAC operations. Address: 0x0005 (C5x).
2. **AR0–AR7 (Auxiliary Registers):** Eight 16-bit registers used for indirect addressing, auto-increment, and bit-reversed addressing. Address: 0x0010–0x0017.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Marking Insight:** A 3-mark answer should ideally contain: (i) the concept, (ii) a one-line purpose, (iii) two named examples with addresses. Missing addresses = –½ mark per missing item.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
*List any three differences between internal and external memory in a DSP system.*

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Internal Memory | External Memory |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Located on the same silicon die as the CPU. | Located on a separate chip; connected via off-chip bus. |
| 2 | Access time ≈ 1 clock cycle (0 wait states). | Access time = 1 + N_WS cycles (with wait states). |
| 3 | Limited capacity (a few KB to tens of KB). | Large capacity (MB to GB). |
| 4 | High bandwidth, deterministic latency. | Lower effective bandwidth due to bus arbitration. |
| 5 | Higher cost per bit. | Lower cost per bit. |

Any three valid contrasting pairs earn full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Harvard architecture** of a DSP processor. How does it improve instruction throughput compared to the von Neumann architecture? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** An FIR filter of order 256 is to be implemented on a DSP with $f_{CLK} = 100 \text{ MHz}$ and $f_s = 44.1 \text{ kHz}$. Each tap requires one MAC cycle, but coefficient fetches from external memory incur **3 wait states**, while sample fetches from internal memory incur **0 wait states**. Determine whether the filter can run in real time. If not, by what factor must the clock be increased, or how many wait states must be eliminated? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

> **Harvard Architecture** has **two physically separate memory spaces**:
> - **Program Memory** (with its own address bus PAB and data bus PBDB) for storing instructions and constants.
> - **Data Memory** (with its own address bus DAB and data bus DBDB) for storing operands.
> - Both connect to the **CPU** (ALU, MAC unit, registers) in parallel.
> - The CPU can fetch one instruction **and** read/write one operand in the **same cycle**.

**Why throughput improves:**

- In **von Neumann architecture**, the single shared bus is time-multiplexed. If an instruction fetch occupies the bus, the operand access must *wait*, doubling the effective cycle count for any instruction that needs both. Theoretically, the **von Neumann throughput is ≤ 0.5 instructions per clock**.
- In **Harvard architecture**, the two buses operate in parallel. The CPU can fetch and access data every cycle → **up to 1 instruction per clock**, i.e., a **2× throughput improvement**.

**Additional benefit in DSPs (Modified Harvard):**
- Data can also be read from program memory (e.g., coefficients stored as constants in P-space), and in some architectures instructions can be fetched from D-space. This adds flexibility without sacrificing the dual-bus speed.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Compute available cycles per sample.**
$$N_{cycles}^{avail} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{f_s} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{44.1 \times 10^3} \approx 2267.57$$

Rounded down: $N_{cycles}^{avail} = 2267$ cycles.

**Step 2 — Compute cycles used per filter output sample.**

The filter has 256 taps. Each tap needs:
- 1 cycle to read sample $x[n-k]$ (internal, 0 wait states) → 1 cycle
- 1 cycle to read coefficient $h[k]$ (external, 3 wait states) → 1 + 3 = 4 cycles
- 1 cycle to perform the MAC → 1 cycle

Total per tap: $1 + 4 + 1 = 6$ cycles.

For 256 taps + 5 overhead cycles:
$$N_{cycles}^{used} = 256 \times 6 + 5 = 1536 + 5 = 1541 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Check feasibility.**
$$1541 \leq 2267 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FEASIBLE ✓}$$

**Step 4 — Compute slack and margin.**
$$N_{slack} = 2267 - 1541 = 726 \text{ cycles (≈ 32% margin)}$$

**Conclusion:** The filter can run in real time with a healthy **32% timing margin**, even with external memory for coefficients, because the fast internal data access compensates for the slow coefficient fetch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to add wait states only to the *external* memory access. Internal access is always 0 wait states.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Rounding $N_{cycles}^{avail}$ *up* instead of *down*. We must floor it because a fractional cycle cannot be used.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting the 5-cycle loop overhead. The exact value may differ by processor, but some accounting must be shown.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Writing the answer without explicit comparison "$1541 \leq 2267$, hence feasible." Always state the inequality conclusion.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Circular Buffering** as implemented in the on-chip data memory of a DSP. How is the **BK (Block Size) register** used? Show a small example. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the on-chip memory organization of a typical fixed-point DSP (e.g., TMS320C5x). Distinguish between **DARAM** and **SARAM**, and state where the auxiliary registers are mapped. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Definition:**
Circular buffering is a hardware-assisted addressing technique where a contiguous region of data memory is treated as a **wrap-around ring**. The DSP automatically rolls the pointer back to the start of the buffer when it exceeds the end, eliminating the software overhead of explicit modulo arithmetic.

**Role of the BK register:**
- The **BK (Block Size)** register holds a 16-bit value that defines the size of the circular buffer in words.
- The buffer length must be a **power of 2** ($L = 2^{m}$, where $m$ is encoded in BK).
- The DSP's addressing logic compares the current auxiliary register value against BK on every indirect access. If the pointer would go out of range, it is automatically wrapped.

**Example (C5x):**
Suppose we need a 256-word circular buffer for an FIR filter's delay line $x[n], x[n-1], \ldots, x[n-255]$.

- Set $\text{BK} = 256$ (note: actual encoding may use the field $\vert \log_2(256) \vert = 8$).
- Load AR4 with the **start address** of the buffer, e.g., 0x0200.
- On each sample, the address in AR4 is post-incremented; when it would exceed 0x02FF, the DSP automatically wraps to 0x0200.

**Pseudo-code demonstration (purely descriptive, not Python):**
```
Initialize:  AR4 = 0x0200,  BK = 256
Loop:
   FIRS   ; MAC using *AR4++%, reads sample, advances AR4 with circular wrap
   ; After 256 iterations, AR4 has wrapped back to 0x0200
   ; No explicit modulo needed in software
```

**Advantage:** The MAC instruction `FIRS` (Finite Impulse Response, Symmetric) on the C5x uses this circular buffer in a single cycle — making real-time FIR filtering feasible.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**On-chip memory organization of TMS320C5x:**

| Region | Type | Size (typical C5x) | Address Range (data space) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Memory-Mapped Registers | Special | 128 words | 0x0000 – 0x007F | AR0–AR7, ACC, PREG, SP, TREG, IMR, IFR, … |
| Scratch-pad RAM | DARAM | 256 words | 0x0080 – 0x017F | Temporary variables, small buffers |
| On-chip DARAM Block 0 | Dual-Access | 2K words | 0x0200 – 0x09FF | Delay lines, primary data (can be split as P/D) |
| On-chip SARAM Block 0 | Single-Access | 4K words | 0x0A00 – 0x19FF | Larger data/code, moderate speed |
| External Memory | Off-chip | Up to 64K | 0x1A00 – 0xFFFF | Mass storage, boot from EPROM |

**DARAM vs. SARAM:**

- **DARAM (Dual-Access RAM):** Has **two independent access ports**. In one clock cycle, the CPU can *read from one address and write to another* within the same DARAM block. This enables the **single-cycle MAC** (Multiply-Accumulate) without stalling the pipeline.
- **SARAM (Single-Access RAM):** Has **only one access port**. Two accesses in the same cycle would conflict and require pipeline stalls. SARAM is used for less time-critical data or for storing program code.

**Where are auxiliary registers mapped?**
- The auxiliary registers **AR0 through AR7** are mapped to the **data memory address space** at addresses **0x0010 through 0x0017** (8 contiguous 16-bit words) within the **Memory-Mapped Registers region (0x0000 – 0x007F)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B**
>
> 1. **Confusing DARAM/SARAM (–2 marks):** Students often write "DARAM is faster" without explaining *why* (dual-port = no conflict). Always mention the *simultaneous dual access* capability.
> 2. **Forgetting the power-of-2 constraint on circular buffer size (–1 mark):** $L$ must be $2^m$. Non-power-of-2 sizes need software modulo and lose hardware acceleration.
> 3. **Writing BK = 256 instead of BK = 8 (encoding field) (–1 mark):** The exact encoding varies by processor; show both interpretations to be safe.
> 4. **Not stating the address range of AR0–AR7 explicitly (–½ mark):** KTU loves specific address references in MMR questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - ✅ **Internal Memory = on-chip, single-cycle (0 wait states) access.**
> - ✅ **Program Memory** holds opcodes and constants; **Data Memory** holds operands and state.
> - ✅ **Harvard / Modified Harvard** = two separate buses for P and D → 1 instruction + 1 data access per cycle.
> - ✅ **Memory-Mapped Registers (MMRs)** occupy the **first 128 words (0x0000–0x007F)** of data memory.
> - ✅ **Auxiliary registers AR0–AR7** = 0x0010–0x0017; used for indirect, circular, and bit-reversed addressing.
> - ✅ **DARAM** = 2 accesses/cycle, used for delay lines; **SARAM** = 1 access/cycle, used for code/bulk data.
> - ✅ **Circular Buffering** uses the **BK register**; size $L = 2^m$ (power of 2).
> - ✅ **External memory penalty:** $t_{ext} = (1 + N_{WS}) \cdot T_{clk}$ — every wait state halves the effective throughput.
> - ✅ **Real-time deadline:** $f_{CLK} / f_s \geq$ cycles/sample needed → determine feasibility.
> - ✅ **Single-cycle MAC** is the hallmark of DSP — relies on dual-bus Harvard + on-chip DARAM.
> - ✅ **For KTU 14-mark problems:** Always draw the block diagram, show all numbers, end with a clear feasibility inequality and a numeric margin.
> - ✅ **Frequent KTU keywords to use in answers:** *Harvard architecture, dual-bus, 0 wait state, on-chip DARAM, memory-mapped, circular buffer, BK register, MAC, real-time deadline, sample period.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Memory Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Memory Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **External Memory Interface (EMIF)** is a dedicated on-chip peripheral in modern Digital Signal Processors (such as the TMS320C6000 series) that provides a high-speed, configurable gateway between the DSP core and **off-chip memory devices** (SRAM, SDRAM, Flash, ROM). The EMIF manages address generation, data transfer, timing synchronization, and bus arbitration, allowing the DSP to access memory mapped beyond its on-chip L1/L2 cache and RAM.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCPT504 - Module 2):**
> The EMIF is critical in real-time DSP because on-chip memory alone (typically **64 KB to 256 KB**) is insufficient for storing large data buffers, look-up tables (LUTs), and program code in applications like audio/video processing, radar, and communications.

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **EMIF** | External Memory Interface — hardware block that connects DSP to external memory |
| **CE Space** | Chip-Enable Space — a memory-mapped region controlled by one /CE signal |
| **ECLKOUT** | EMIF Clock Output — drives external memory timing reference |
| **Wait States** | Idle ECLKOUT cycles inserted by EMIF to match slower memory access time |
| **SBSRAM** | Synchronous Burst SRAM — high-speed pipelined memory |
| **SDRAM** | Synchronous Dynamic RAM — dense, refresh-based volatile memory |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Airport" Model

Imagine the **DSP core** as a busy **business traveller** and the **external memory** as **foreign destinations**. The **EMIF** is the **airport authority** that makes the journey possible:

- **Check-in counter (/CE — Chip Enable):** Activates the right "airline" (memory chip) for the destination.
- **Boarding gate (Data Bus ED[31:0]):** The corridor through which passengers (data bytes) actually travel.
- **Flight schedule (ECLKOUT):** Defines exact departure times — how fast transfers occur.
- **Lounge waiting time (Wait States):** When the destination's runway isn't ready, the EMIF holds the traveller (pauses the DSP) until memory responds.
- **Customs officer (Control Signals /OE, /WE, /BE):** Decides whether you are reading (immigration check) or writing (export declaration), and verifies identity of each byte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without the EMIF "airport authority," the DSP cannot "fly" data to large, slow, or non-volatile external memory chips — making real-time processing of large data sets impossible.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **EMIF Data Bus Width:** **32 bits** (C6713) or **64 bits** (C6416)
- **Addressable Memory Space:** Up to **512 MB** per CE space
- **EMIF Clock (ECLKOUT):** Typically **CPU Clock / 2 to CPU Clock / 4** (e.g., **100 MHz to 200 MHz** for C6713 at 300 MHz CPU)
- **EMIF Voltage Level:** **3.3 V** LVCMOS (C6713)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is the EMIF Required?

Modern DSP algorithms (FIR/IIR filters, FFT, convolution) demand:
1. **Large data buffers** — e.g., a 1024-point FFT on 16-bit data needs **2 KB** of samples alone; longer windows need megabytes.
2. **High-speed access** — to sustain real-time throughput (e.g., 48 kHz audio, 100 MHz IF signals).
3. **Multiple memory types** — volatile fast memory (SDRAM) for buffers + non-volatile memory (Flash) for boot code.

The on-chip L1/L2 memory (typically **64–256 KB**) is too small. The EMIF solves this by **expanding the addressable memory** through external chips.

## 2.2 EMIF Architecture — Component Breakdown

The EMIF is logically divided into:

### 2.2.1 Signal Groups

| Signal Group | Signals (C6713 example) | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Bus** | ED[31:0] | Bidirectional 32-bit data path |
| **Address Bus** | EA[21:2] | Word-aligned address (byte address shifted by 2) |
| **Byte Enables** | /BE[3:0] | Selects which byte lane (8-bit slice) is active |
| **Chip Enables** | /CE2, /CE3 | Activates one of two external CE memory regions |
| **Output Enable** | /OE | Enables memory to drive data bus during a read |
| **Write Enable** | /WE | Commands memory to latch data during a write |
| **Clock** | ECLKOUT, ECLKIN | Synchronization reference for EMIF |
| **Ready/Hold** | /ARDY, /HOLD | Asynchronous memory "I'm done" handshake |

### 2.2.2 CE Space Configuration

The C6713 EMIF provides **two external CE spaces** (/CE2 and /CE3), each independently programmable for memory type. /CE0 is reserved for on-chip memory, and /CE1 is reserved for the on-chip peripheral configuration bus.

| CE Space | Address Range (C6713) | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CE0** | 0x0000 0000–0x000F FFFF | Internal Memory (L2/Cache) |
| **CE1** | 0x0180 0000–0x01BF FFFF | Internal Peripherals |
| **/CE2** | 0x0200 0000–0x02FF FFFF | External — usually **SDRAM** |
| **/CE3** | 0x0300 0000–0x03FF FFFF | External — usually **Flash/Async** |

## 2.3 Memory Types Supported by EMIF

### 2.3.1 Asynchronous Memory Interface (AMI)
Used for **ROM, Flash, Asynchronous SRAM**.
- EMIF must insert **wait states** to accommodate slow memory access.
- Controlled by **read setup, read strobe, read hold, write setup, write strobe, write hold** timing parameters.

### 2.3.2 Synchronous Memory Interface (SMI)
Used for **SBSRAM, ZBT SRAM, DDR SDRAM, SDR SDRAM**.
- Transfers are pipelined, one data word per ECLKOUT cycle.
- For SDRAM, the EMIF also generates **refresh commands** and **row/column address multiplexing**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — EMIF Timing Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for KTU exam problems. Memorize these — they form 80% of the calculation-based questions.

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ECLKOUT Period** | $T_{ECLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{ECLKOUT}}$ | seconds | EMIF clock period |
| **Read/Write Setup** | $S = \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{setup}}{T_{ECLK}} \right\rceil$ | ECLKOUT cycles | Address stable before /OE or /WE |
| **Read/Write Strobe** | $R = \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{access}}{T_{ECLK}} \right\rceil$ | ECLKOUT cycles | Active strobe width (memory access time) |
| **Read/Write Hold** | $H = \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{hold}}{T_{ECLK}} \right\rceil$ | ECLKOUT cycles | Data hold after strobe de-assertion |
| **Total Read/Write Time** | $T_{total} = (S + R + H + 1) \cdot T_{ECLK}$ | seconds | Full memory access cycle |
| **Wait States** | $W = (S + R + H) - 1$ | ECLKOUT cycles | Extra cycles beyond 1-cycle minimum |
| **Memory Region Size** | $\vert Size \vert = 2^{(n+1)}$ | bytes | For a memory chip with n address lines |
| **SDRAM Refresh Interval** | $t_{REF} = 64 \text{ ms (typical)}$ | milliseconds | All rows must be refreshed within this |
| **Number of Refresh Cycles** | $N_{REF} = \dfrac{t_{REF} \cdot f_{ECLKOUT}}{R_{CYCLE}}$ | cycles | Calculated for SDRAM controller |

> **Where:**
> - $t_{setup}$, $t_{access}$, $t_{hold}$ are memory chip datasheet timings (in seconds)
> - $R_{CYCLE}$ is the SDRAM row cycle time
> - $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling function (round up to next integer)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | External Memory Used | EMIF Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Audio Codec Buffering** | SDRAM (16–64 MB) | Stores 10–30 s of 48 kHz stereo PCM |
| **Boot System** | Flash (4–16 MB) | Holds boot code loaded at power-on |
| **OFDM Communication** | SDRAM (256 MB) | Buffers time-domain symbols for FFT/IFFT |
| **Radar Pulse Compression** | SRAM (1–4 MB) | Stores matched filter coefficients |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wait State Calculation from Memory Datasheet

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A **TMS320C6713** DSP runs with **CPUCLK = 200 MHz** and **EMIF configured at CPU/2 = 100 MHz** ECLKOUT. An external **asynchronous SRAM** has the following timing requirements from its datasheet:

- $t_{setup} = 5$ ns (address setup time)
- $t_{access} = 20$ ns (data access from /OE falling edge)
- $t_{hold} = 2$ ns (data hold time after /OE rising)

Calculate the **number of wait states** that must be programmed into the EMIF.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine ECLKOUT frequency and period.**

$$
f_{ECLKOUT} = \frac{f_{CPU}}{2} = \frac{200 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 100 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
T_{ECLK} = \frac{1}{f_{ECLKOUT}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Convert each timing parameter into ECLKOUT cycles using the ceiling function.**

Setup cycles:
$$
S = \left\lceil \frac{t_{setup}}{T_{ECLK}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 0.5 \right\rceil = 1 \text{ cycle}
$$

Strobe (access) cycles:
$$
R = \left\lceil \frac{t_{access}}{T_{ECLK}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{20 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.0 \right\rceil = 2 \text{ cycles}
$$

Hold cycles:
$$
H = \left\lceil \frac{t_{hold}}{T_{ECLK}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 0.2 \right\rceil = 1 \text{ cycle}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate total access time in ECLKOUT cycles.**

$$
T_{total\_cycles} = S + R + H + 1 = 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 5 \text{ cycles}
$$

The "+1" accounts for the cycle in which the setup phase begins.

**Step 4: Calculate wait states.**

$$
W = (S + R + H) - 1 = (1 + 2 + 1) - 1 = 3 \text{ wait states}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the actual memory access time in nanoseconds.**

$$
T_{total} = T_{total\_cycles} \times T_{ECLK} = 5 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 50 \text{ ns}
$$

**Final Answer:** The EMIF must be programmed with **1 setup, 2 strobe, 1 hold, and 3 wait states** for this SRAM. The total read cycle occupies **50 ns**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Region Size from Address Lines

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A Flash memory chip has **20 address lines (A[19:0])** and a **16-bit data bus (D[15:0])**. Determine the **total memory capacity** of the chip in **MB**.

### 3.2.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Number of addressable locations.**

Each unique address refers to one data word. With 20 address lines, the number of unique locations is:

$$
N_{locations} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ words}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total memory in bits.**

Each location stores 16 bits = 2 bytes:

$$
\text{Size} = N_{locations} \times \text{Data Width} = 2^{20} \times 2^4 \text{ bits} = 2^{24} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to bytes.**

$$
\text{Size (bytes)} = 2^{24} \div 2^{3} = 2^{21} \text{ bytes} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to MB (using 1 MB = 2²⁰ bytes).**

$$
\text{Size (MB)} = 2^{21} \div 2^{20} = 2^{1} = 2 \text{ MB}
$$

**Final Answer:** The Flash chip has a capacity of **2 MB**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EMIF Clock Divider Selection

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A TMS320C6713 has CPUCLK = 225 MHz. The user wants ECLKOUT = 75 MHz to interface with a memory device whose maximum clock input is 80 MHz. Determine the **EMIF clock divider (ECLKOUTDIV) ratio** and verify it satisfies the constraint.

### 3.3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Express the desired ECLKOUT as a ratio of CPUCLK.**

$$
\text{Required Ratio} = \frac{f_{CPU}}{f_{ECLKOUT}} = \frac{225 \text{ MHz}}{75 \text{ MHz}} = 3
$$

**Step 2: Match to the C6713's allowed dividers.**

The C6713 EMIF supports divider ratios of **1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8** (encoded in the **ECLKOUTDIV** field of the ECLKCFG register). A ratio of **3** is valid.

**Step 3: Verify the timing constraint.**

$$
f_{ECLKOUT} = \frac{225}{3} = 75 \text{ MHz} \le 80 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Compute ECLKOUT period for downstream calculations.**

$$
T_{ECLK} = \frac{1}{75 \times 10^{6}} = 13.33 \text{ ns}
$$

**Final Answer:** Set **ECLKOUTDIV = 3**, giving ECLKOUT = **75 MHz** (period 13.33 ns), safely below the 80 MHz memory limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EMIF Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **internal organization of the EMIF** and its connections to the DSP core and external memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DSPCore["DSP CORE CPU"] -->|Read Write Request| EMIFCtrl["EMIF CONTROLLER LOGIC"]
    EMIFCtrl -->|Address Bus EA21 to EA2| AddMux["ADDRESS MUX AND LATCH"]
    EMIFCtrl -->|Data Bus ED31 to ED0| DataBus["32 BIT BIDIRECTIONAL DATA BUS"]
    EMIFCtrl -->|Control Signals| CtlGen["CONTROL SIGNAL GENERATOR"]
    AddMux -->|Row Col Address| SDRAMchip["EXTERNAL SDRAM CHIP"]
    AddMux -->|Word Address| SRAMchip["EXTERNAL SRAM OR FLASH CHIP"]
    DataBus --> SDRAMchip
    DataBus --> SRAMchip
    CtlGen -->|CE2 CE3 OE WE BE| SDRAMchip
    CtlGen -->|CE2 CE3 OE WE BE| SRAMchip
    ECLKsrc["ECLKOUT GENERATOR DIVIDER"] --> EMIFCtrl
    ECLKsrc --> SDRAMchip
    ECLKsrc --> SRAMchip
    SDRAMchip -->|Refresh ACK| EMIFCtrl
    SRAMchip -->|ARDY Ready Signal| EMIFCtrl
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The DSP Core issues read/write requests to the **EMIF Controller**, which generates an address (latched by the Address Mux) and drives the data bus. Control signals (/CE, /OE, /WE, /BE) are produced by the Control Signal Generator. The ECLKOUT generator supplies a synchronized clock to both the EMIF logic and the external memories. Ready/refresh acknowledgments return to the controller for handshaking.

---

## 4.2 Asynchronous Memory Read Cycle Timing

This flowchart shows the **state machine** of an asynchronous read transaction in EMIF.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Setup: Address Valid and CE Asserted
    Setup --> Strobe: After S ECLKOUT Cycles
    Strobe --> Strobe: Wait for Memory Access Time
    Strobe --> Hold: Memory Drives Data Bus
    Hold --> Capture: Latch Data on ECLKOUT Edge
    Capture --> Idle: Deassert OE and CE
    Strobe --> Extend: ARDY Low Insert Wait States
    Extend --> Strobe: ARDY High Resume Access
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The transaction begins in **Idle**. The EMIF asserts the address and /CE for the **Setup** phase (S cycles). It then activates /OE for the **Strobe** phase (R cycles) during which the memory prepares data. If /ARDY is low, **wait states are inserted** (Extend loop). Data is captured during **Hold**, then the cycle returns to **Idle**.

---

## 4.3 EMIF-to-External-Memory Physical Connection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DSP_CHIP["TMS320C6713 DSP"]
        EMO[EMIF MODULE]
    end
    subgraph MEM_REGION_1["CE2 SPACE SDRAM"]
        SDA[SDRAM ADDRESS A12 to A0]
        SDD[SDRAM DATA DQ15 to DQ0]
        SDC[SDRAM CONTROL RAS CAS WE CS]
    end
    subgraph MEM_REGION_2["CE3 SPACE FLASH"]
        FLA[FLASH ADDRESS A19 to A0]
        FLD[FLASH DATA D15 to D0]
        FLC[FLASH CONTROL CE OE WE]
    end
    EMO -->|EA21 to EA2| SDA
    EMO -->|ED31 to ED0| SDD
    EMO -->|SDRAM CTRL| SDC
    EMO -->|EA21 to EA2| FLA
    EMO -->|ED31 to ED0| FLD
    EMO -->|ASYNC CTRL| FLC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The same EMIF module serves **two distinct external memory regions**. /CE2 activates the SDRAM region (synchronous, with RAS/CAS/WE control). /CE3 activates the Flash region (asynchronous, with /OE and /WE control). Both share the address and data buses but use different control logic.

---

## 4.4 SDRAM Bank/Row/Column Address Multiplexing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EA21 to EA2 DSP Address] --> MUX[ADDRESS MULTIPLEXER]
    MUX -->|RAS Low| R[ROW ADDRESS A12 to A0]
    MUX -->|CAS Low| C[COLUMN ADDRESS A8 to A0]
    R --> SDRAM[SDRAM CHIP]
    C --> SDRAM
    SDRAM -->|Refresh Every 64 ms| REF[AUTO REFRESH CONTROLLER]
    REF --> MUX
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The DSP's word address bits are split: during **RAS low**, bits become the row address; during **CAS low**, the same lines carry the column address. The EMIF's **Auto Refresh Controller** periodically issues refresh commands to preserve data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the External Memory Interface (EMIF). List the two main types of memory it supports.**

**Model Answer:**

The **External Memory Interface (EMIF)** is an on-chip peripheral of a DSP that provides a configurable interface between the DSP core and off-chip memory devices. It manages address generation, data transfer, control signaling, and timing synchronization.

EMIF supports two main memory types:
1. **Asynchronous memories** — ROM, Flash, Asynchronous SRAM (uses wait-state based timing)
2. **Synchronous memories** — SDRAM, SBSRAM, ZBT SRAM (uses pipelined cycle-by-cycle timing)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the function of the /ARDY (Asynchronous Ready) signal in the EMIF?**

**Model Answer:**

The **/ARDY (Asynchronous Ready) signal** is an input to the EMIF from external asynchronous memory. It indicates whether the memory has completed the current access:
- When **/ARDY is HIGH**, the EMIF proceeds with the programmed timing.
- When **/ARDY is LOW**, the EMIF **inserts wait states** (extends the strobe phase) until the memory drives /ARDY HIGH.

This allows interfacing with memories of varying access times without reprogramming the EMIF.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently lose marks by (1) forgetting to **convert nanoseconds to ECLKOUT cycles** before applying the ceiling function, (2) omitting the **+1 cycle** in the total access time formula, and (3) confusing **synchronous (no wait states) vs. asynchronous (wait states required)** timing. Always show unit conversions explicitly.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the EMIF in the TMS320C6713 DSP**. Label the major signal groups (data bus, address bus, control signals, clock).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TMS320C6713 DSP runs at CPUCLK = **200 MHz** with ECLKOUT = CPU/2 = **100 MHz**. An external asynchronous SRAM requires $t_{setup}$ = 4 ns, $t_{access}$ = 12 ns, $t_{hold}$ = 2 ns. Calculate the **number of wait states** required and the **total access time** in nanoseconds.

#### Model Solution to (a)

The EMIF architecture consists of:

1. **EMIF Controller Logic** — Central state machine that processes DSP read/write requests and orchestrates the bus.
2. **Address Bus Interface (EA[21:2])** — 20-bit word-aligned address output.
3. **Data Bus Interface (ED[31:0])** — 32-bit bidirectional data path.
4. **Byte Enable Logic (/BE[3:0])** — Selects which of the four byte lanes is active.
5. **Chip Enable Generator (/CE2, /CE3)** — Selects the target external CE space.
6. **Control Signal Generator (/OE, /WE)** — Generates output-enable and write-enable pulses.
7. **ECLKOUT Generator** — Produces the EMIF clock from the CPU clock via a programmable divider.
8. **SDRAM Controller** — Manages row/column multiplexing, refresh, and bank activation.
9. **Asynchronous Ready Handler (/ARDY)** — Extends timing when slow memory signals "not ready."

(Refer to **Section 4.1 Block Diagram** for the full schematic.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Compute ECLKOUT period.**

$$
T_{ECLK} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Convert timing parameters to ECLKOUT cycles.**

Setup:
$$
S = \left\lceil \frac{4 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.4 \rceil = 1 \text{ cycle}
$$

Strobe (access):
$$
R = \left\lceil \frac{12 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.2 \rceil = 2 \text{ cycles}
$$

Hold:
$$
H = \left\lceil \frac{2 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.2 \rceil = 1 \text{ cycle}
$$

**Step 3: Total access cycles and wait states.**

$$
T_{total\_cycles} = S + R + H + 1 = 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 5 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
W = (S + R + H) - 1 = (1 + 2 + 1) - 1 = 3 \text{ wait states}
$$

**Step 4: Total access time in nanoseconds.**

$$
T_{total} = 5 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 50 \text{ ns}
$$

**Final Answer:** Program **1 setup, 2 strobe, 1 hold, 3 wait states**. Total read access time = **50 ns**.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **synchronous and asynchronous memory interfaces** in the EMIF. Give one example of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Flash memory has **22 address lines** and a **16-bit data bus**. Calculate its **total capacity in MB**. If it is mapped to /CE3 space of C6713 starting at address **0x0300 0000** and the next chip /CE2 is enabled at **0x0200 0000**, determine the **size of the /CE3 region** in MB and verify whether the Flash fits.

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Feature | Asynchronous Memory Interface | Synchronous Memory Interface |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Clock dependence** | No external clock; uses wait states | Uses ECLKOUT for all timing |
| **Speed** | Slower; limited by strobe period | Faster; one word per ECLKOUT cycle |
| **Handshaking** | /ARDY signal possible | No /ARDY; pipelined |
| **Data bus usage** | Latched at end of strobe | Latched on ECLKOUT edge |
| **Example** | Flash, Async SRAM | SDR SDRAM, SBSRAM |
| **Pin count** | Fewer control pins | More (RAS, CAS, CKE) |
| **Refresh needed?** | No (for SRAM) | Yes (for DRAM) |

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Flash capacity in bytes.**

Number of locations:
$$
N = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ words}
$$

Total bits:
$$
2^{22} \times 16 = 2^{22} \times 2^{4} = 2^{26} \text{ bits}
$$

Bytes:
$$
2^{26} \div 2^{3} = 2^{23} \text{ bytes} = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2: Size of /CE3 region in C6713.**

In C6713, /CE3 covers the address range **0x0300 0000 to 0x03FF FFFF**. The size is:

$$
\text{Region size} = 0x03FF\,FFFF - 0x0300\,0000 + 1 = 0x0100\,0000 = 16 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3: Verify Flash fits.**

Flash size = 8 MB. Region size = 16 MB. Since 8 MB ≤ 16 MB, the Flash fits with 8 MB of unused space remaining in the /CE3 region.

**Final Answer:** Flash = **8 MB**; /CE3 region = **16 MB**; Flash **fits** with 8 MB spare.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for EMIF (KTU 2024):**

- ⭐ **EMIF = "Airport Authority"** — bridges DSP core to external memory; manages address, data, control, and timing.
- ⭐ **Two CE Spaces externally:** /CE2 and /CE3 on C6713. /CE0 and /CE1 are internal.
- ⭐ **Wait state formula:** $W = S + R + H - 1$, where each parameter is the ceiling of (datasheet time / $T_{ECLK}$).
- ⭐ **Total access time:** $T_{total} = (S + R + H + 1) \times T_{ECLK}$.
- ⭐ **Memory size:** $\vert Size \vert = 2^{n} \times \text{data width}$, where n = number of address lines.
- ⭐ **/ARDY LOW = insert wait states; /ARDY HIGH = normal timing.**
- ⭐ **Synchronous memories (SDRAM, SBSRAM) need refresh and row/column address multiplexing.**
- ⭐ **Asynchronous memories (Flash, Async SRAM) need setup, strobe, hold, and wait states.**
- ⭐ **ECLKOUT divider is programmable** in the ECLKCFG register; typical ratio = CPUCLK / 2 to /4.
- ⭐ **Voltage level on C6713 EMIF is 3.3 V LVCMOS**; not 5 V tolerant.
- ⭐ **Byte enables /BE[3:0] allow byte, halfword, and word accesses** in a 32-bit bus.
- ⭐ **Memory map addresses for C6713:** /CE2 = 0x0200 0000, /CE3 = 0x0300 0000.
- ⭐ **DRAM requires refresh every ~64 ms**; EMIF handles this automatically when configured.
- ⭐ **Always use the ceiling function** when converting nanoseconds to clock cycles — rounding down will under-size wait states and cause data corruption.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pipelining # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** where multiple instructions are overlapped in their execution. The instruction cycle is broken down into a series of independent sequential stages (such as Fetch, Decode, Read, Execute, and Write), and each stage operates concurrently on a different instruction. In the context of real-time DSP, pipelining is the architectural mechanism that allows a processor to begin processing a new instruction *before* the previous instruction has finished executing. This is achieved by subdividing complex operations (like a Multiply-Accumulate, or MAC) into smaller, time-balanced sub-operations, each handled by a dedicated hardware stage. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Terminology:** The two primary performance metrics derived from pipelining are: > - **Throughput**: The number of instructions completed per unit time (measured in **MIPS** — Million Instructions Per Second, or **MOPS** — Million Operations Per Second). > - **Latency**: The total time delay from the start of an instruction's execution to its completion. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Assembly Line Imagine a **laundry process with 4 stages**: 1. **Washing** (30 minutes) 2. **Drying** (30 minutes) 3. **Folding** (30 minutes) 4. **Storing** (30 minutes) **Without Pipelining (Sequential):** If you have 4 loads of laundry, you must wash load 1, dry it, fold it, and store it (taking **2 hours**) before even touching load 2. Total time for 4 loads = **8 hours**. **With Pipelining (Parallel Overlap):** - At time 0: Load 1 is being washed. - At time 30 min: Load 1 is drying, while **simultaneously** Load 2 starts washing. - At time 60 min: Load 1 is folding, Load 2 is drying, **Load 3 starts washing**. - At time 90 min: Load 1 is stored, Load 2 folding, Load 3 drying, **Load 4 starts washing**.
