# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master IIR Filter Design Flow (Bilinear Transform Method)

This diagram captures the canonical KTU Module 3 workflow for IIR filter design using MATLAB.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Specification: Fp1, Fp2, Fs1, Fs2, Rp, As, Fs] --> B[Normalize Frequencies to 0 to 1]
    B --> C{Choose Approximation}
    C -->|Max flat passband| D[Butterworth buttord + butter]
    C -->|Equiripple passband| E[Chebyshev I cheb1ord + cheby1]
    C -->|Equiripple stopband| F[Chebyshev II cheb2ord + cheby2]
    C -->|Equiripple both| G[Elliptic ellipord + ellip]
    D --> H[Order N and Cutoff Wn returned]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Coefficients b and a]
    I --> J{Stability Check}
    J -->|Pass inside unit circle| K[Visualize freqz grpdelay fvtool]
    J -->|Fail outside unit circle| L[Reduce N or change method]
    L --> I
    K --> M[Quantize to Fixed Point Q15]
    M --> N[Export via fdatool to C header]
    N --> O[Deploy on DSP Hardware TMS320 ARM]
```

## 4.2 FIR Design Flow — Two Competing Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Fp, Fs, Rp, As, Fs] --> Q{Linear Phase Required?}
    Q -->|Yes, sharp transition| R[Parks-McClellan firpmord plus firpm]
    Q -->|Yes, simple shape| S[Window Method fir1 with Kaiser or Hamming]
    Q -->|No, arbitrary response| T[Frequency Sampling fir2]
    R --> U[Optimal Equiripple Coefficients]
    S --> V[Truncated Windowed Coefficients]
    T --> W[Sampled Frequency Response]
    U --> X[fvtool Verify]
    V --> X
    W --> X
    X --> Y[Group Delay and Linear Phase Check]
    Y --> Z[Ready for Real Time Implementation]
```

## 4.3 MATLAB Internal Pipeline for `bilinear`

This block diagram reveals *what happens inside MATLAB* when you call `[bz, az] = bilinear(b, a, Fs)`. Understanding this is a frequent 14-mark question in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input: Analog Coefficients b s and a s] --> B1[Compute Poles and Zeros using roots]
    B1 --> C1[For each Pole compute D = 1 minus p times T over 2]
    C1 --> D1[For each Zero compute D = 1 minus z times T over 2]
    D1 --> E1[Map each root using z new = 1 plus r T over 2 divided by 1 minus r T over 2]
    E1 --> F1[Gain K adjusted by product factor]
    F1 --> G1[Convert back to polynomial form using poly]
    G1 --> H1[Output: Digital Coefficients b z and a z]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: MATLAB Function Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specification f and a] --> B1[fir1]
    A --> B2[fir2]
    A --> B3[firpm]
    A --> B4[fircls]
    B1 --> C1[Windowed sinc]
    B2 --> C2[Sampled frequency]
    B3 --> C3[Equiripple optimal]
    B4 --> C4[Constrained least squares]
    C1 --> D1[Always linear phase]
    C2 --> D2[Approximate linear phase]
    C3 --> D3[Linear phase in pass and stop]
    C4 --> D4[Linear phase with min squared error]
```

## 4.5 Real-Time Deployment Chain (Beyond MATLAB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MATLAB Design] --> B[Coefficients b and a]
    B --> C[fdatool or filterDesigner Quantization]
    C --> D[Q15 or Q31 Fixed Point Format]
    D --> E[Generate C Header File]
    E --> F[TMS320C6x Library or ARM CMSIS DSP]
    F --> G[Real Time Execution on Hardware]
    G --> H[Verify using Spectrum Analyzer or Oscilloscope]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]`**
*Explain the role of the `buttord` function in MATLAB. What are its inputs and outputs?*

**Model Answer:**

The `buttord` function is the **Butterworth order estimator** in MATLAB's Signal Processing Toolbox. It computes the minimum filter order $N$ and the cutoff frequency $W_n$ required for a Butterworth filter to meet a given magnitude specification.

**Syntax:**
```matlab
[N, Wn] = buttord(Wp, Ws, Rp, Rs)
```

**Inputs:**
- `Wp` — normalized passband edge(s), $0 \le W_p \le 1$ (scalar for lowpass/highpass, two-element vector for bandpass/bandstop).
- `Ws` — normalized stopband edge(s).
- `Rp` — maximum passband ripple in **dB** (e.g., 1 dB).
- `Rs` — minimum stopband attenuation in **dB** (e.g., 40 dB).

**Outputs:**
- `N` — the minimum integer filter order.
- `Wn` — the normalized cutoff frequency (or band edges).

For example, `buttord(0.3, 0.5, 1, 40)` returns the minimum order needed for a lowpass filter with passband edge 0.3, stopband edge 0.5, 1 dB passband ripple, and 40 dB stopband attenuation. The output `N` is then used as input to the `butter` function to compute the actual coefficients `[b, a]`.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar]`**
*Compare the `fir1` and `firpm` functions in MATLAB. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `fir1` | `firpm` |
|---|---|---|
| Method | **Window-based** | **Parks-McClellan (equiripple)** |
| Algorithm | Truncates ideal impulse response with a window | Iterative Chebyshev approximation |
| Optimality | Not optimal — side-lobe level fixed by window | Optimal — minimum order for given spec |
| Typical use | Quick designs, simple shapes | High-performance, sharp filters |
| Phase | Linear (with symmetric window) | Linear in passband and stopband |

**Advantage of `fir1`:** Simple, predictable, only one parameter (order) plus cutoff.

**Advantage of `firpm`:** Achieves the **minimum filter order** for a given specification because it uses the Chebyshev equiripple algorithm that directly minimizes the maximum deviation from the ideal response in both bands. This is a KTU favorite.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. **Always normalize frequencies** to $[0, 1]$ before calling any `*ord` function. Forgetting this silently gives wrong orders.
> 2. **Always show the pre-warp step** explicitly in bilinear-transform problems. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if it is skipped.
> 3. **For `firpm`**, the magnitude vector `a` must have one more element than the frequency vector `f` for stopband → passband transitions. Students commonly miss this.
> 4. **For `bilinear`**, the input is analog coefficients $(b, a)$, *not* digital. Mixing these up is the most common error.
> 5. **Always use `ceil()` to round up** the order from `*ord` functions to ensure the spec is met; rounding down fails the spec.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — FIR Design Focus

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Design a **linear-phase FIR lowpass filter** using the window method with the following specifications:
- Passband edge: $F_p = 1500$ Hz
- Stopband edge: $F_s = 2000$ Hz
- Passband ripple: $R_p = 0.1$ dB
- Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 50$ dB
- Sampling frequency: $F_s = 8000$ Hz

Use the **Hamming window**. Show the MATLAB code and compute the order using the Kaiser formula.

**(b)** Compare the above window-method design with a **Parks-McClellan design** for the same specification. State the orders obtained and discuss the trade-off in terms of computational complexity and passband ripple distribution.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Window Method (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute transition width.**

$$\Delta F = F_s - F_p = 2000 - 1500 = 500 \text{ Hz}, \quad \Delta\omega = 2\pi \frac{500}{8000} = 0.3927 \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Compute required order using Kaiser formula:**

$$N \approx \frac{A_s - 8}{2.285 \cdot \Delta\omega} = \frac{50 - 8}{2.285 \times 0.3927} = \frac{42}{0.8973} \approx 46.8$$

So $N = 47$ (round up using `ceil`).

> [!NOTE]
> For a Hamming window, the achievable stopband attenuation is fixed at approximately **53 dB**. Since we need 50 dB, the Hamming window is just sufficient. For higher attenuation, we must use a Blackman (74 dB) or Kaiser window.

**Step 3 — MATLAB code:**

```matlab
Fs  = 8000;
Fp  = 1500;  Fs_edge = 2000;
Wp  = Fp / (Fs/2);             % Normalized: 1500/4000 = 0.375
N   = 47;
b   = fir1(N, Wp, hamming(N+1));

% Visualize
fvtool(b, 1);
[H, fHz] = freqz(b, 1, 1024, Fs);
plot(fHz, 20*log10(abs(H))); grid on;
xlabel('Frequency (Hz)'); ylabel('Magnitude (dB)');
```

**Result:** A 48-tap FIR lowpass filter with linear phase, group delay $\tau_g = N/2 = 23.5$ samples.

**(b) Parks-McClellan Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Use `firpmord` to estimate the order.**

```matlab
f    = [Fp Fs_edge] / (Fs/2);          % [0.375  0.500]
a    = [1  0];                          % Ideal lowpass amplitudes
dev  = [(10^(0.1/20)-1)/(10^(0.1/20)+1),  10^(-50/20)];  % [0.0058, 0.0032]
[Npm, fo, ao, w] = firpmord(f, a, dev);
Npm = ceil(Npm);                        % Round up
bpm = firpm(Npm, fo, ao, w);
```

**Step 2 — Expected result.** `firpmord` will return an order of approximately **$N_{\text{pm}} \approx 38$** to **$42$**, which is **slightly lower** than the window method's $N = 47$.

**Step 3 — Trade-off discussion.**

| Aspect | Window Method (Hamming) | Parks-McClellan |
|---|---|---|
| Order $N$ | 47 | ~40 |
| Passband ripple | Monotonically decreasing toward $\omega_p$ | **Equiripple** (uniform) |
| Stopband ripple | Monotonically decreasing | Equiripple |
| Group delay | $N/2 = 23.5$ (constant) | $N/2$ (constant) |
| Design complexity | Very simple | Iterative (Remez exchange) |
| Best feature | Predictable, robust to quantization | Optimal minimum order |

**Conclusion:** The Parks-McClellan design achieves the **minimum order** because it directly minimizes the maximum error (Chebyshev norm), distributing the ripple uniformly. The window method's order is higher because the ripple decays away from the band edge, wasting margin. Both are linear-phase FIR filters, suitable for real-time applications where phase linearity is critical.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — IIR Design Focus

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain the **frequency warping phenomenon** in the bilinear transform. Derive the relationship between analog frequency $\Omega$ and digital frequency $\omega$.

**(b)** Design a **digital Chebyshev Type-I bandpass filter** with these specifications:
- Lower stopband edge: $F_{s1} = 600$ Hz
- Lower passband edge: $F_{p1} = 800$ Hz
- Upper passband edge: $F_{p2} = 1600$ Hz
- Upper stopband edge: $F_{s2} = 1800$ Hz
- Passband ripple: $R_p = 1$ dB
- Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 50$ dB
- Sampling frequency: $F_s = 6000$ Hz

Provide the complete MATLAB code and the resulting filter order.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Frequency Warping (7 Marks)**

The bilinear transform is defined as:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

To find how the analog frequency $\Omega$ maps to the digital frequency $\omega$, substitute $z = e^{j\omega T}$ on the unit circle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{1 + e^{-j\omega T}} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2}}{e^{j\omega T/2} + e^{-j\omega T/2}} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{2j\sin(\omega T/2)}{2\cos(\omega T/2)} \\[4pt]
  &= j \cdot \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $s = j\Omega$ for sinusoidal steady state:

$$\boxed{\;\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)\;}$$

**Physical meaning:** As $\omega$ ranges over $[0, \pi]$ (the full digital frequency range), $\tan(\omega T/2)$ ranges over $[0, \infty)$. Hence the entire analog frequency axis is **compressed** into a finite digital range. This causes **non-linear warping** — equal analog frequency intervals do **not** map to equal digital frequency intervals.

**Consequence for design:** To design a digital filter with a specific digital cutoff $\omega_d$, we must first **pre-warp** it to the analog domain:

$$\Omega_a = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_d T}{2}\right)$$

Design the analog prototype at $\Omega_a$, then apply the bilinear transform. The digital cutoff will then land exactly at $\omega_d$.

**(b) Chebyshev Type-I Bandpass Design (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Normalize frequencies** (Nyquist is $F_s/2 = 3000$ Hz).

$$W_{s1} = 600/3000 = 0.200, \quad W_{p1} = 800/3000 = 0.267$$
$$W_{p2} = 1600/3000 = 0.533, \quad W_{s2} = 1800/3000 = 0.600$$

**Step 2 — MATLAB code.**

```matlab
Fs = 6000;
Wp = [800  1600] / (Fs/2);     % [0.2667  0.5333]
Ws = [600  1800] / (Fs/2);     % [0.2000  0.6000]
Rp = 1;     Rs = 50;

% Estimate order
[N, Wn] = cheb1ord(Wp, Ws, Rp, Rs);
% Typical output: N = 8

% Design the filter
[b, a] = cheby1(N, Rp, Wn);

% Verify
fvtool(b, a);
[H, fHz] = freqz(b, a, 1024, Fs);
plot(fHz, 20*log10(abs(H))); grid on;

% Stability check
[z, p, k] = tf2zp(b, a);
fprintf('All poles inside unit circle: %d\n', all(abs(p) < 1));
```

**Step 3 — Expected result.** `cheb1ord` returns an order **$N \approx 8$** with cutoff `Wn = [0.2667  0.5333]`. The filter has 9 zeros and 8 poles. The passband shows equiripple behavior (1 dB peak-to-peak), and the stopband is maximally flat.

**Step 4 — Real-time cost.** Number of multiplications per output sample = $9 + 8 = 17$ MACs. Group delay varies with frequency (non-linear phase), peaking near the passband edges.

**Step 5 — Comparison with FIR.** A linear-phase FIR achieving the same spec would require $N \approx 80$–$100$, giving ~$180$ MACs — an order of magnitude more computation. The trade-off is the loss of linear phase in the IIR design.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MATLAB is the standard KTU design environment** for both FIR and IIR digital filters, providing `fir1`, `firpm`, `butter`, `cheby1`, `cheby2`, `ellip`, and `bilinear` functions.
- **Frequency normalization is mandatory** — every MATLAB frequency argument must be in $[0, 1]$ where $1.0$ corresponds to $F_s/2$.
- **Order-estimation functions** (`buttord`, `cheb1ord`, `cheb2ord`, `ellipord`, `firpmord`) compute the **minimum order** satisfying the specification; always round up using `ceil()`.
- **Window-method FIR** is simple but not optimal; **Parks-McClellan (`firpm`)** achieves the minimum order by equiripple optimization.
- **Bilinear transform** maps the LHP of the $s$-plane to the inside of the unit circle, but causes **non-linear frequency warping**: $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega T/2)$.
- **Pre-warping is non-negotiable** when using the bilinear transform — design the analog prototype at the warped frequency, then transform.
- **Impulse invariance** (`impinvar`) preserves the time-domain shape but suffers aliasing; use only for low-pass filters with $\omega_c \ll \pi$.
- **IIR filter stability** requires **all poles inside the unit circle** — verify with `zplane(b, a)` or `abs(p) < 1`.
- **FIR filters are always stable** (no feedback) and can achieve **exact linear phase** with symmetric coefficients.
- **Visualization tools** — `freqz` (magnitude/phase), `grpdelay` (group delay), `fvtool` (interactive), `zplane` (pole-zero plot) — are essential for verifying the design.
- **Real-time deployment** requires coefficient quantization (typically Q15), which can degrade IIR stability margins; FIR is more robust.
- **Group delay** for a length-$(N+1)$ linear-phase FIR is exactly $\tau_g = N/2$ samples, constant for all frequencies.
- **Elliptic IIR** gives the **smallest order** for a given specification but has equiripple in both pass and stop bands.
- **Butterworth IIR** has the **flattest passband** but the slowest roll-off; useful when amplitude distortion must be minimized.
- **For KTU Part-B 14-mark questions**, always show: (1) normalized frequencies, (2) the chosen MATLAB function and its inputs, (3) the resulting order $N$, (4) the verification step (`freqz` plot), and (5) a comment on stability and real-time feasibility.
- **Common MATLAB command sequence** to remember for IIR: `buttord → butter → freqz → fvtool → zplane`. For FIR: `firpmord → firpm → freqz → grpdelay`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Transform: DFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fourier Transform: DFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is the sampled Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of a finite-duration discrete-time signal. It converts a finite-length time-domain sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$ into a finite-length frequency-domain sequence $X(k)$ of length $N$, where both sequences are periodic and discrete.

The **DFT pair** is mathematically defined as:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j \frac{2\pi}{N} kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j \frac{2\pi}{N} kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The complex exponential kernel is compactly represented using the **Twiddle Factor**:

$$W_N = e^{-j \frac{2\pi}{N}}$$

Using this shorthand, the DFT pair reduces to:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Musical Chord Analyzer":** Imagine a musician plays a complex musical chord on a piano. Your ear cannot easily tell which individual notes (frequencies) are present. Now imagine a special device — a **DFT analyzer** — that takes that single chord and tells you *exactly* which keys were struck and how hard. The chord is the time-domain signal $x(n)$, and the list of individual notes with their intensities is the frequency-domain signal $X(k)$. The DFT is the "frequency microscope" for digital signals.

A simpler real-world analogy: think of **white light passing through a prism**. White light is the time-domain signal (a mixture of all colors). The prism separates it into a rainbow (the frequency-domain representation). DFT performs exactly this role for digital signals — it decomposes a discrete signal into its constituent sinusoids (sines and cosines) of different frequencies.

## 1.3 Why DFT Matters in Real-Time DSP

- **Computability:** Unlike DTFT, which is continuous in frequency and requires infinite memory, the DFT produces a **finite set of $N$ complex numbers** that can be directly stored and processed.
- **Real-Time Implementation:** The DFT is the bridge between mathematical theory and practical hardware (DSP chips, FPGAs). Fast algorithms like the **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)** compute the DFT in $O(N \log N)$ instead of $O(N^2)$.
- **Standard Sampling Frequency:** The frequency resolution is $\Delta f = f_s / N$ where **$f_s$ is the sampling frequency** (in Hz) and $N$ is the DFT length.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favorite Line:** "DFT is the *discretized, periodic, finite-length* version of the DTFT, sampled at $N$ equally spaced frequencies in $[0, 2\pi)$."

## 1.4 Relationship of DFT with Other Transforms

The DFT sits at the bottom of the Fourier transform hierarchy in DSP:

| Transform | Time | Frequency | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fourier Transform (FT)** | Continuous, Aperiodic | Continuous, Aperiodic | Theoretical |
| **Fourier Series (FS)** | Continuous, Periodic | Discrete, Aperiodic | Theoretical |
| **DTFT** | Discrete, Aperiodic | Continuous, Periodic | Theoretical |
| **DFT** | Discrete, Periodic | Discrete, Periodic | Computational |

> [!NOTE]
> The DFT is the *only* Fourier transform that is **discretized in BOTH** time and frequency and is computationally tractable on digital hardware. This is the fundamental reason it is indispensable in real-time DSP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How the DFT Works

The DFT works by computing the **correlation** between the input signal $x(n)$ and $N$ complex exponentials (basis functions) at $N$ uniformly spaced frequencies. Each $X(k)$ value tells you "how much" of frequency $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$ is present in $x(n)$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Fix the DFT length $N$:** The number of frequency samples to be computed.
2. **Compute the Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$. This is a complex number on the unit circle.
3. **Generate Basis Vectors:** For each $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$, construct the basis sequence $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$.
4. **Inner Product:** Compute $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{kn}$ — this is the discrete inner product of $x(n)$ with the $k$-th basis vector.
5. **Result:** $X(k)$ is a complex number encoding both **magnitude** $\vert X(k) \vert$ and **phase** $\angle X(k)$ at frequency bin $k$.

## 2.2 Key Properties of the DFT (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Statement |
|---|---|
| **Linearity** | $\text{DFT}\{a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)\} = a X_1(k) + b X_2(k)$ |
| **Periodicity** | $X(k+N) = X(k)$ and $x(n+N) = x(n)$ |
| **Circular Time Shift** | $x((n-m))_N \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} W_N^{km} X(k)$ |
| **Circular Frequency Shift** | $W_N^{-kn} x(n) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X((k-m))_N$ |
| **Conjugate Symmetry (real $x$)** | $X(N-k) = X^*(k)$ |
| **Circular Convolution** | $x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X_1(k) X_2(k)$ |
| **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^2$ |
| **Duality** | $X(n) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} N x((-k))_N$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU examinations on DFT.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ | Definition of Twiddle Factor |
| 2 | $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$ | Periodicity of Twiddle Factor (period $N$) |
| 3 | $W_N^{N/2} = e^{-j \pi} = -1$ | Special value (for even $N$) |
| 4 | $W_N^{(N-k)} = W_N^{-k} = (W_N^k)^*$ | Conjugate symmetry of Twiddle |
| 5 | $W_N^{k + N/2} = -W_N^k$ | Half-period shift property |
| 6 | $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{kn}$ | Forward DFT (analysis) |
| 7 | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-kn}$ | Inverse DFT (synthesis) |
| 8 | $X(0) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$ | DC component (sum of all samples) |
| 9 | $X(N/2) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (-1)^n x(n)$ | Nyquist component (for even $N$) |
| 10 | $\Delta \omega = 2\pi / N$ | Frequency resolution in rad/sample |
| 11 | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | Frequency resolution in Hz |
| 12 | $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{km} = N$ if $m=0 \pmod N$, else $0$ | Orthogonality of exponentials |
| 13 | DFT matrix: $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{x}$ | Matrix form, $\mathbf{W}[k,n] = W_N^{kn}$ |
| 14 | $\text{DFT}\{x^*(((-n)))_N\} = X^*(k)$ | Conjugate property |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spectrum Analyzers:** Real-time display of frequency content of audio/radio signals.
- **OFDM in 4G/5G/Wi-Fi:** Modulation/demodulation uses IDFT/DFT.
- **Audio Coding (MP3, AAC):** DFT/FFT applied to time-windowed audio frames.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG, EEG analysis in time-frequency domain.
- **Image Processing (2D-DFT):** JPEG compression, image filtering.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Predictive maintenance in mechanical systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-Time DSP Context:** In real-time applications, we compute the DFT of streaming data by buffering $N$ samples, applying a window function, computing FFT, then shifting the buffer and repeating. This is the essence of **block processing** in real-time DSP.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the IDFT from the Forward DFT

**Goal:** Start from the forward DFT equation and derive the inverse transform $x(n)$.

**Step 1:** Write the forward DFT definition.

$$
X(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) W_N^{km}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $W_N^{-kn}$ and sum over $k = 0$ to $N-1$.

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-kn} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) W_N^{km} \right] W_N^{-kn}
$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation on the right-hand side.

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-kn} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the inner sum using the **orthogonality principle** of complex exponentials.

For any integers $p$ and $q$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k p} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } p \equiv 0 \pmod N \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Setting $p = m - n$:

- The inner sum equals $N$ when $m = n$ (i.e., $m - n = 0$).
- The inner sum equals $0$ for all $m \neq n$ (within one period).

**Step 5:** Substitute back. Only the $m = n$ term survives:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-kn} = x(n) \cdot N
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $x(n)$:

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-kn}
$$

This is the **IDFT** (Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform). $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of $1/N$ is what makes the DFT pair "energy-preserving." The forward DFT has no scaling factor; the inverse DFT carries it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DFT in Matrix Form

**Goal:** Express the DFT as a matrix-vector product.

For an $N$-point DFT, define the **DFT matrix** $\mathbf{W}$ of size $N \times N$:

$$
\mathbf{W} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
W_N^0 & W_N^0 & W_N^0 & \cdots & W_N^0 \\
W_N^0 & W_N^1 & W_N^2 & \cdots & W_N^{N-1} \\
W_N^0 & W_N^2 & W_N^4 & \cdots & W_N^{2(N-1)} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
W_N^0 & W_N^{N-1} & W_N^{2(N-1)} & \cdots & W_N^{(N-1)(N-1)}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Write the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x(0), x(1), \dots, x(N-1)]^T$ and output vector $\mathbf{X} = [X(0), X(1), \dots, X(N-1)]^T$.

**Step 2:** The DFT equation $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{kn}$ is exactly the $k$-th row of $\mathbf{W}$ dotted with $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 3:** Thus the matrix form is:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W} \, \mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 4:** The IDFT matrix form is:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}^* \, \mathbf{X}
$$

where $\mathbf{W}^*$ is the conjugate transpose (Hermitian) of $\mathbf{W}$.

**Step 5:** **Verification that $\mathbf{W}$ is unitary up to scaling:** Compute $\mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^*$:

$$
[\mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^*]_{k,l} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{kn} W_N^{-ln} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{(k-l)n} = N \cdot \delta_{kl}
$$

Hence $\mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^* = N \mathbf{I}$, confirming $\mathbf{W}^{-1} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}^*$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 4-Point DFT

**Problem:** Compute the 4-point DFT of $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (i.e., $x(0)=1, x(1)=2, x(2)=3, x(3)=4$).

**Step 1:** Compute the Twiddle Factor $W_4 = e^{-j 2\pi/4} = e^{-j \pi/2} = -j$.

**Step 2:** Compute all powers of $W_4$:

$$
W_4^0 = 1, \quad W_4^1 = -j, \quad W_4^2 = -1, \quad W_4^3 = j
$$

**Step 3:** Build the DFT matrix:

$$
\mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_4 \mathbf{x}$ where $\mathbf{x} = [1, 2, 3, 4]^T$:

**For $X(0)$** (DC component):
$$
X(0) = 1(1) + 1(2) + 1(3) + 1(4) = 10
$$

**For $X(1)$**:
$$
X(1) = 1(1) + (-j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (j)(4) = (1 - 3) + j(4 - 2) = -2 + j2
$$

**For $X(2)$** (Nyquist component):
$$
X(2) = 1(1) + (-1)(2) + 1(3) + (-1)(4) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2
$$

**For $X(3)$**:
$$
X(3) = 1(1) + (j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (-j)(4) = (1 - 3) + j(2 - 4) = -2 - j2
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
X(k) = \{10, \ -2+j2, \ -2, \ -2-j2\}
$$

**Verification using conjugate symmetry:** $X(4-1) = X(3) = -2 - j2 = (-2 + j2)^* = X^*(1)$. ✓

**Verification using Parseval's theorem:** $\sum \vert x(n) \vert^2 = 1+4+9+16 = 30$. $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert X(k) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 8 + 4 + 8) = \frac{120}{4} = 30$. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DFT vs DTFT vs FFT

| Feature | DTFT | DFT | FFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Infinite/aperiodic | Finite length $N$ | Finite length $N$ (power of 2) |
| **Output** | Continuous, periodic | Discrete, periodic (N points) | Discrete, periodic (N points) |
| **Frequency Range** | $0$ to $2\pi$ (continuous) | $0$ to $2\pi$ (sampled at $N$ points) | $0$ to $2\pi$ (sampled at $N$ points) |
| **Computational Cost** | Not computable | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N \log_2 N)$ |
| **Implementation** | Theoretical | Direct | Algorithm (Cooley-Tukey) |
| **Real-time Use** | No | Yes (block-by-block) | Yes (most common) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFT Computation Flow — Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level functional architecture** of how an $N$-point DFT is computed in a real-time DSP system, including input buffering, twiddle factor generation, multiplication-accumulation, and output formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal x of n] --> B[Sampling at f sub s]
    B --> C[Buffer N Samples]
    C --> D[Apply Window Function]
    D --> E[Initialize k = 0 to N minus 1]
    E --> F[Generate Twiddle Factor W sub N]
    F --> G[Complex Multiply x n times W sub N super kn]
    G --> H[Accumulator Sum over n]
    H --> I[Output X sub k]
    I --> J{All k bins done}
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[Store X sub k Spectrum]
    K --> L[Compute Magnitude and Phase]
    L --> M[Spectrum Display or Further DSP]
```

## 4.2 DFT Matrix Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector x with N samples] --> B[DFT Matrix W sub N]
    B --> C[Matrix Vector Product]
    C --> D[Output Vector X with N frequency bins]
    D --> E[Bin 0 DC Component]
    D --> F[Bin 1 Fundamental]
    D --> G[Bin N by 2 Nyquist]
    D --> H[Bin N minus 1 Highest Frequency]
```

## 4.3 Relationship of DFT to Other Transforms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Continuous Time Signal x sub a of t] -->|Sampling| Q[Discrete Signal x of n]
    Q -->|N-point Window| R[Finite Sequence x sub N of n]
    R -->|Apply DFT| S[DFT X sub k of k]
    Q -->|Z Transform on unit circle| T[DTFT X of e sup j omega]
    T -->|Sample at omega = 2 pi k over N| S
    S -->|IDFT| R
```

## 4.4 Real-Time Block Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ADC Samples] --> B[Shift Register Buffer]
    B --> C[Window Multiplication]
    C --> D[FFT Engine]
    D --> E[Frequency Bins]
    E --> F[Magnitude Phase]
    F --> G[Spectrum Output]
    B -->|Shift by 1 sample| H[Next Block Ready]
    H --> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the "DFT computation block diagram" in KTU exams, the most marks are awarded for correctly labeling: (1) the **buffer**, (2) the **twiddle factor generator**, (3) the **complex multiplier**, (4) the **accumulator**, and (5) the **N output bins**.
