```mermaid
    C1 -.-> M3
    B1 -.-> M2
    A1 -.-> M1
    D1 -.-> M4
    E1 -.-> M5
```

This schematic captures the **68-95-99.7 empirical rule** (often called the "three-sigma rule") critical for threshold-based noise detection problems in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define White Noise. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Model Answer:**

White noise is a wide-sense stationary random process $w(n)$ characterized by:

1. Zero mean: $E[w(n)] = 0$
2. Flat power spectral density: $S_{ww}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2$ for all $\omega$
3. Delta-autocorrelation: $R_{ww}(k) = \sigma_w^2 \, \delta(k)$

Two distinct samples $w(n_1)$ and $w(n_2)$ for $n_1 \neq n_2$ are uncorrelated. The term "white" is borrowed from white light, which contains all visible frequencies with equal intensity.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5 M) + Property listing (1 M) + Analogy (0.5 M) = **3 Marks**

---

### Question 2: State the Central Limit Theorem and its relevance to Gaussian Noise. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that the sum (or average) of a large number $N \to \infty$ of independent, identically distributed random variables — regardless of their individual distributions — tends to follow a Gaussian distribution.

**Relevance in DSP:**

- Most physical noise sources (thermal agitation of electrons, shot noise in semiconductors) arise from the superposition of many microscopic random events.
- CLT justifies modelling the aggregate noise as Gaussian, even when individual microscopic events are non-Gaussian.
- Gaussianity simplifies analysis: only mean and variance are needed; the output of any LTI system driven by Gaussian noise remains Gaussian.

**Mark Distribution:** Theorem statement (1.5 M) + DSP relevance (1.5 M) = **3 Marks**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — White Noise Autocorrelation & PSD

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the power spectral density of a discrete-time white noise process from its autocorrelation function. State and apply the Wiener-Khinchin theorem.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A digital communication system operates over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. The signal bandwidth is 200 kHz. Compute (i) the total noise power at the receiver input, and (ii) the output noise variance after a low-pass FIR filter with impulse response $h = [0.2, \; 0.6, \; 0.2]$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Wiener-Khinchin Theorem Statement:** For a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process, the power spectral density (PSD) and the autocorrelation sequence (ACS) form a discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) pair.

**Step 1 — Express the theorem mathematically.**

$$S_{ww}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} R_{ww}(k) e^{-j\omega k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the white-noise autocorrelation.**

$$R_{ww}(k) = \sigma_w^2 \, \delta(k)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the sum using the sifting property.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{ww}(\omega) &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \sigma_w^2 \delta(k) e^{-j\omega k} \\
&= \sigma_w^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega \cdot 0} \\
&= \sigma_w^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The PSD is identically equal to the variance $\sigma_w^2$ for all frequencies $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$, proving that the spectrum is **flat** — the defining feature of white noise. The inverse DTFT recovers the delta ACS, confirming the duality.

**Mark Allocation:** Theorem statement (2 M) + Substitution (1 M) + Sifting evaluation (2 M) + Conclusion (2 M) = **7 Marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Two-sided PSD level: $N_0/2 = 2 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz
- Bandwidth: $B = 200$ kHz $= 2 \times 10^{5}$ Hz

**(i) Total noise power:**

The two-sided PSD is $S_{ww}(f) = N_0/2 = 2 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, so the input noise variance is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_w^2 &= \int_{-B}^{B} \frac{N_0}{2} df = N_0 \cdot B \\
&= (4 \times 10^{-20}) \times (2 \times 10^5) \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-15} \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Output variance after FIR filter:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y^2 &= \sigma_w^2 \sum_{k=0}^{2} \lvert h(k) \rvert^2 \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-15} \times \left[(0.2)^2 + (0.6)^2 + (0.2)^2\right] \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-15} \times \left[0.04 + 0.36 + 0.04\right] \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.44 \\
&= 3.52 \times 10^{-15} \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Mark Allocation:** Setup (2 M) + Part (i) computation (2 M) + Part (ii) formula (1.5 M) + Part (ii) computation (1.5 M) = **7 Marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Gaussian PDF and Probability Calculations

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the standard form of the Gaussian probability density function starting from the Central Limit Theorem. Show that the PDF integrates to unity.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** In a real-time DSP system, the additive noise voltage at the ADC input is modelled as a zero-mean Gaussian random variable with standard deviation $\sigma = 5$ mV. The ADC decision threshold is set at $\pm 10$ mV. Compute (i) the probability that a single noise sample exceeds $+10$ mV, (ii) the probability of being within $\pm 10$ mV, and (iii) the probability of being outside the $\pm 3\sigma$ band.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — CLT Setup.** Let $X_i$ be i.i.d. random variables with mean $\mu_0$ and variance $\sigma_0^2$. The standardized sum is:

$$Z_N = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i - N\mu_0}{\sigma_0 \sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 2 — Limiting distribution.** As $N \to \infty$, $Z_N$ converges in distribution to a standard normal $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ with PDF:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to non-zero mean and arbitrary variance.** Substitute $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ to obtain:

$$p(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Normalization check.** Evaluate $I = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x) dx$ by substituting $u = (x-\mu)/(\sigma\sqrt{2})$:

$$I = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{\pi}} = 1$$

Hence the PDF is properly normalized. $\blacksquare$

**Mark Allocation:** CLT formulation (2 M) + Limiting PDF (1.5 M) + Generalization (1.5 M) + Normalization (2 M) = **7 Marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 5$ mV, threshold $T = \pm 10$ mV.

**(i) Probability that $x > +10$ mV:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(x > 10) &= Q\!\left(\frac{10 - 0}{5}\right) = Q(2) \\
\end{aligned}$$

Where $Q(z) = 1 - \Phi(z) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(z/\sqrt{2})$.

From standard normal tables, $Q(2) = 0.0228$.

$$\boxed{P(x > 10) \approx 0.0228 \text{ or } 2.28\%}$$

**(ii) Probability within $\pm 10$ mV:**

By symmetry, $P(x < -10) = Q(2) = 0.0228$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\lvert x \rvert \leq 10) &= 1 - 2 \times Q(2) \\
&= 1 - 2(0.0228) \\
&= 1 - 0.0456 \\
&= 0.9544
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P(\lvert x \rvert \leq 10) \approx 95.44\%}$$

**(iii) Probability outside $\pm 3\sigma$:** With $\sigma = 5$ mV, the $3\sigma$ bounds are $\pm 15$ mV.

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\lvert x \rvert > 15) &= 2 \, Q(3) \\
&= 2 \times 0.00135 \\
&= 0.00270 \text{ or } 0.27\%
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the famous **three-sigma rule**: only ~0.27% of samples lie beyond $3\sigma$.

**Mark Allocation:** Part (i) (2.5 M) + Part (ii) (2 M) + Part (iii) (2.5 M) = **7 Marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Whiteness with Gaussianity:** Students frequently treat them as synonyms. Whiteness is a *spectral* (frequency-domain) property; Gaussianity is an *amplitude-domain* (statistical) property. A noise process can be white but non-Gaussian (e.g., uniform white noise) or Gaussian but coloured (e.g., filtered Gaussian).
> 2. **Forgetting the Two-Sided vs. One-Sided PSD Factor:** Many answers state $S_{ww} = N_0/2$ in W/Hz but use $N_0$ in calculations without a factor of 2. Always read the question carefully.
> 3. **Missing Units in Noise Power Computations:** Examiners deduct 1 mark when bandwidth is left in kHz instead of Hz during integration.
> 4. **Misapplying the Wiener-Khinchin Theorem:** Students sometimes write the DTFT sign incorrectly or confuse it with the inverse DTFT. Memorize: $S_{ww}(\omega) = \text{DTFT}\{R_{ww}(k)\}$.
> 5. **Forgetting Symmetry in Gaussian Probability:** For zero-mean Gaussian, $P(x > T) = P(x < -T)$. Use this to halve your computation effort and avoid arithmetic errors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **White noise** = flat PSD ↔ delta autocorrelation; samples are i.i.d. and uncorrelated.
- **Gaussian noise** = bell-shaped amplitude PDF; fully characterized by mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
- **AWGN** combines both: $y(n) = s(n) + w(n)$ with $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.
- **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem** links autocorrelation and PSD via DTFT — cornerstone of all spectral derivations.
- **Parseval's relation** confirms that total white noise power equals its variance: $P_w = \sigma_w^2$.
- **LTI Output Variance** of white noise: $\sigma_y^2 = \sigma_w^2 \sum_k \lvert h(k) \rvert^2$ (Schwarz formula).
- **CLT** justifies Gaussian modelling of aggregate physical noise sources.
- **LTI invariance**: Gaussian input always produces Gaussian output (mean and variance change, shape preserved).
- **SNR in dB**: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_s^2 / \sigma_w^2)$.
- **Three-sigma rule** (68-95-99.7): $P(\lvert x - \mu \rvert \leq k\sigma) \approx 0.6827, 0.9545, 0.9973$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$.
- **Q-function** $Q(z) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(z/\sqrt{2})$ is the standard tail-probability tool.
- **Thermal noise** PSD: $N_0 = k_B T$ with $k_B = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$ J/K.
- **AWGN is the universal reference channel** for Shannon capacity, matched filtering, and Wiener/Kalman estimation.
- **Pitfall reminder:** Whiteness ≠ Gaussianity — they live in orthogonal property spaces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DTMF Generation and Detection - DTMF Generator and Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** DTMF Generation and Detection - DTMF Generator and Detector # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency (DTMF)** signaling is an in-band telecommunication signaling technique standardized under **ITU-T Recommendation Q.23 / Q.24**, used to encode the press of a key on a telephone keypad into two simultaneous sinusoidal tones. Every digit, letter, or symbol pressed on a standard **4 × 4 push-button keypad** is uniquely identified by the simultaneous transmission of **one low-frequency tone** (chosen from a row frequency group) and **one high-frequency tone** (chosen from a column frequency group). Mathematically, a DTMF tone is a sum of two sinusoids: $$x(t) = A_{L} \sin(2\pi f_{L} t) + A_{H} \sin(2\pi f_{H} t)$$ where $f_{L} \in \{697, 770, 852, 941\}$ Hz and $f_{H} \in \{1209, 1336, 1477, 1633\}$ Hz. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBCPT504, Module 4):** > The module explicitly requires the student to design a **real-time DTMF generator** (synthesizer) using DSP principles and a **DTMF detector (Goertzel filter)** for the receiver side, both realized in software on a programmable DSP processor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Musical Handshake" Imagine two people trying to communicate across a noisy room using only musical notes. Instead of shouting, one person plays **two notes at the same time** — a low one and a high one — and the listener, who has been trained to recognize the *combination*, immediately knows the message. - The **low note** says, *"I am from the upper / middle / lower / lowest row of the keypad."* - The **high note** says, *"I am from the left / right-side column of the keypad."* - Together, the pair identifies **exactly one button**. A piano-style "twisted musical scale" was deliberately chosen so that **no two consecutive buttons share the same harmonic relationship** — preventing accidental triggering and making decoding robust against speech interference. ## 1.3 The DTMF Frequency Matrix | | **1209 Hz** | **1336 Hz** | **1477 Hz** | **1633 Hz** | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **697 Hz**| 1 | 2 | 3 | A | | **770 Hz**| 4 | 5 | 6 | B | | **852 Hz**| 7 | 8 | 9 | C | | **941 Hz**| * | 0 | # | D | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Tolerances (ITU-T Q.24):** > - Frequency tolerance: **±1.5 %** > - Tone duration: **≥ 40 ms** (typical **50–100 ms**) > - Inter-digit pause: **≥ 40 ms** > - **Signal-to-twist** (difference between row and column amplitudes): **≤ 8 dB** > - Total power per tone: **−9 dBm ± 3 dB** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why a "Twisted" Musical Scale? The eight DTMF frequencies were not chosen randomly. They were deliberately selected as the first **eight audible harmonics of the piano keys** D♯ and A, deliberately *detuned* so that **no frequency is an integer multiple of another**. This prevents false triggering from harmonics (e.g., the 3rd harmonic of 697 Hz ≈ 2091 Hz, which does **not** coincide with 1209 Hz, 1336 Hz, 1477 Hz, or 1633 Hz). ## 2.2 DTMF Generator — Architectural Choices Two industry-standard implementations exist: 1. **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS) with Look-up Tables** — precomputed sine samples stored in ROM, read out at programmable rates. 2. **Recursive Oscillator (IIR Sinusoidal Generator)** — a digital resonator with poles on the unit circle; computationally cheap, requires only two multiplications per sample. A standard **second-order digital resonator** generates a pure tone: $$y[n] = 2\cos(2\pi f_{0}/f_{s}) \, y[n-1] - y[n-2]$$ For DTMF, **two such oscillators are summed**, one at $f_{L}$ and one at $f_{H}$. ## 2.3 DTMF Detector — Why the Goertzel Algorithm? A naïve detector would compute an **8-point DFT** using an FFT — wasteful, since we only need **8 specific bins** out of *N*. The **Goertzel algorithm** evaluates a **single DFT bin** using a **second-order IIR filter**, requiring only **N + 2 multiplications** per bin instead of the FFT's $O(N \log N)$ cost. It is the **industry standard** for real-time DTMF decoding on DSP hardware. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Equation** | **Units / Typical Value** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | DTMF signal | $x(t) = A_{L}\sin(2\pi f_{L} t) + A_{H}\sin(2\pi f_{H} t)$ | Volts | | Sampling frequency $f_{s}$ | Telephony standard | **8000 Hz** | | Normalized angular freq. $\omega_{k}$ | $\omega_{k} = 2\pi k / N$ | radians/sample | | DFT bin index $k$ | $k = \mathrm{round}(f_{0} N / f_{s})$ | integer | | Resonator coefficient | $2\cos(2\pi f_{0}/f_{s})$ | dimensionless | | Goertzel filter (recursion) | $v[n] = x[n] + 2\cos(\omega_{k})v[n-1] - v[n-2]$ | internal state | | Goertzel magnitude² | $\vert X[k]\vert^{2} = v^{2}[N-1] + v^{2}[N-2] - 2\cos(\omega_{k})\,v[N-1]v[N-2]$ | power | | Detection block length $N$ | $f_{s} \times T_{\text{block}}$ | 205 samples for 25.6 ms @ 8 kHz | | Twist (dB) | $\mathrm{Twist} = 20\log_{10}(A_{H}/A_{L})$ | ≤ **8 dB** | | Minimum SNR | ITU-T Q.24 | **15 dB** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** For DTMF detectors, **two Goertzel filters are cascaded per tone** — a *broadband energy filter* (skipping DC, 50/60 Hz hum) and the *narrowband tone filter*. The ratio of the two energies is used to confirm "tone present" and reject speech. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Goertzel Filter (Single-Bin DFT Evaluator) We start from the standard DFT definition for a single bin $k$: $$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega_{k} n}, \quad \omega_{k} = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$$ **Step 1 — Introduce the recursive interpretation.** Recognize $e^{-j\omega_{k} n}$ as a complex sinusoid that can be generated by a first-order recursion: $$e^{-j\omega_{k} n} = W_{N}^{k\,n}, \quad \text{where } W_{N}^{k} = e^{-j 2\pi k/N}$$ Let the intermediate variable be: $$s[n] = W_{N}^{-k\,n} \, x[n]$$ so that $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s[n]$. **Step 2 — Rewrite as a Z-domain transfer function.** Define a filter whose output at $n = N-1$ equals $X[k]$: $$H_{k}(z) = \frac{1 - W_{N}^{k} z^{-1}}{1 - 2\cos(\omega_{k}) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}$$ This filter has a **pole pair on the unit circle** at $z = e^{\pm j\omega_{k}}$, giving it sharp resonance at frequency $\omega_{k}$. **Step 3 — Direct-form I implementation (real coefficients).** Since $H_{k}(z)$ is real when its poles are complex conjugates, we can implement it using **only real arithmetic**: $$\begin{aligned} v[n] &= x[n] + 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) v[n-1] - v[n-2], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1 \\[4pt] y[n] &= v[n] - W_{N}^{k}\, v[n-1] \end{aligned}$$ with initial conditions $v[-1] = v[-2] = 0$. **Step 4 — Compute the magnitude squared only at $n = N-1$.** To avoid complex arithmetic at the output, compute the squared magnitude directly from real-valued states: $$\begin{aligned} \vert X[k] \vert^{2} &= \Re^{2}\{y[N-1]\} + \Im^{2}\{y[N-1]\} \\[4pt] &= v^{2}[N-1] + v^{2}[N-2] - 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) v[N-1]\, v[N-2] \end{aligned}$$ This is the canonical **Goertzel magnitude-squared equation**, requiring only **2 multiplications** (the others are squares and additions) per tone per block. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Detecting the "5" Key at 8 kHz Suppose a DTMF detector receives a signal containing the **"5" key** tone (770 Hz + 1336 Hz) at $f_{s} = 8000$ Hz with a block length of $N = 205$ samples (≈ 25.6 ms). **Step 1 — Compute the DFT bin index $k$ for each tone.** $$\begin{aligned} k_{770} &= \frac{770 \times 205}{8000} = 19.731 \;\Rightarrow\; k = 20 \\[4pt] k_{1336} &= \frac{1336 \times 205}{8000} = 34.235 \;\Rightarrow\; k = 34 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the resonator coefficient $2\cos(\omega_{k})$ for each tone.** $$\begin{aligned} 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 20}{205}\right) &= 2 \times 0.7289 = 1.4579 \\[4pt] 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 34}{205}\right) &= 2 \times 0.3380 = 0.6761 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Run the recursion.** For each incoming sample $x[n]$, compute $v[n]$ for both the row filter and the column filter independently: $$v[n] = x[n] + 1.4579\, v[n-1] - v[n-2]$$ $$v[n] = x[n] + 0.6761\, v[n-1] - v[n-2]$$ **Step 4 — At $n = N-1 = 204$, compute $\vert X[k] \vert^{2}$.** $$\vert X[20] \vert^{2} = v^{2}[204] + v^{2}[203] - 1.4579\, v[204]\, v[203]$$ $$\vert X[34] \vert^{2} = v^{2}[204] + v^{2}[203] - 0.6761\, v[204]\, v[203]$$ **Step 5 — Decision logic.** If $\vert X[20] \vert^{2}$ and $\vert X[34] \vert^{2}$ both exceed a preset threshold (e.g., the strongest of all 8 bins by at least **4 dB**), and a guard-band filter rejects speech, then the detector reports **"key 5 pressed."** ## 3.3 DTMF Generator — Step-by-Step (DDS Method) A complete digital DTMF generator produces one sample per clock tick: **Step 1 — Phase accumulator.** Maintain an internal phase index $\phi[n]$ that increments by a *frequency control word* $\Delta\phi = f_{0}/f_{s}$ per sample. **Step 2 — Sine lookup.** Index the pre-stored sine table $\sin(2\pi \cdot M / 2^{L})$ (e.g., $L = 12$ for 4096 entries) by the upper bits of $\phi[n]$. **Step 3 — Dual-tone summation.** Compute $x[n] = A_{L} \sin(\phi_{L}[n]) + A_{H} \sin(\phi_{H}[n])$ and output to the DAC. **Step 4 — Envelope shaping.** Apply a **raised-cosine (Tukey) window** at the start and end of each digit to suppress spectral splatter (the "key click"). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DTMF Generator Architecture (Direct Digital Synthesis) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Keypad Matrix Input] --> B[ROM LUT
Row Frequencies] A --> C[ROM LUT
Column Frequencies] B --> D[Phase Accumulator 1
Increment fl fs] C --> E[Phase Accumulator 2
Increment fh fs] D --> F[Sine Lookup Table 1] E --> G[Sine Lookup Table 2] F --> H[Multiplier
Low Gain AL] G --> I[Multiplier
High Gain AH] H --> J[Summer] I --> J J --> K[Tukey Window
Envelope Shaper] K --> L[DAC and
Low-Pass Filter] L --> M[Analog Telephone Line] ``` ## 4.2 DTMF Detector Architecture (Two-Stage Goertzel Filter Bank) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[ADC Sampled Audio
fs 8000 Hz] --> B[Anti-Alias Filter
Bandlimit 0 to 4 kHz] B --> C[DC and Hum Notch
Subtract Running Mean] C --> D{Buffer N Samples
N equals 205} D --> E1[Goertzel Filter
Row 697 Hz] D --> E2[Goertzel Filter
Row 770 Hz] D --> E3[Goertzel Filter
Row 852 Hz] D --> E4[Goertzel Filter
Row 941 Hz] D --> F1[Goertzel Filter
Col 1209 Hz] D --> F2[Goertzel Filter
Col 1336 Hz] D --> F3[Goertzel Filter
Col 1477 Hz] D --> F4[Goertzel Filter
Col 1633 Hz] E1 --> G[Magnitude Squared
Computation] E2 --> G E3 --> G E4 --> G F1 --> G F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> H[Twist and Threshold
Comparator] H --> I[Row Column Pair
Decoder Logic] I --> J[Valid DTMF Digit
Output ASCII Code] ``` ## 4.3 DTMF Keypad Frequency Matrix Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HighGroup[High Group Tones] H1[1209 Hz] H2[1336 Hz] H3[1477 Hz] H4[1633 Hz] end subgraph LowGroup[Low Group Tones] L1[697 Hz] L2[770 Hz] L3[852 Hz] L4[941 Hz] end L1 --- H1 L1 --- H2 L1 --- H3 L1 --- H4 L2 --- H1 L2 --- H2 L2 --- H3 L2 --- H4 L3 --- H1 L3 --- H2 L3 --- H3 L3 --- H4 L4 --- H1 L4 --- H2 L4 --- H3 L4 --- H4 ``` > [!NOTE] > Each edge in the diagram above represents **one valid DTMF digit**. The full 4 × 4 grid generates **16 distinct dual-tone combinations**, supporting digits 0–9 plus *, # and the four "A B C D" keys used in special service signaling (e.g., military radios, automatic call distribution). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the eight standard DTMF frequencies used in telephony. Why is the row/column tone pair chosen to be non-harmonically related?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The eight DTMF frequencies specified by ITU-T Q.23 are: | Row Group (Low) | Column Group (High) | | :---: | :---: | | **697 Hz** | **1209 Hz** | | **770 Hz** | **1336 Hz** | | **852 Hz** | **1477 Hz** | | **941 Hz** | **1633 Hz** | The row/column pairs are deliberately chosen so that **no frequency is an integer multiple of another**, making them *harmonically incommensurate*. This prevents speech and music from spuriously triggering a detector, since vocal harmonics would rarely land simultaneously on both a row and column resonance. **(1 mark for listing 8 frequencies, 2 marks for explaining non-harmonic rationale.)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the Goertzel algorithm and explain why it is preferred over the FFT for DTMF detection.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Goertzel algorithm evaluates a **single DFT bin** $X[k]$ using a second-order IIR filter with the transfer function: $$H_{k}(z) = \frac{1 - W_{N}^{k} z^{-1}}{1 - 2\cos(2\pi k/N)\,z^{-1} + z^{-2}}$$ **(1 mark)** Real-time recursion: $$v[n] = x[n] + 2\cos(2\pi k/N)\,v[n-1] - v[n-2]$$ $$\vert X[k] \vert^{2} = v^{2}[N-1] + v^{2}[N-2] - 2\cos(2\pi k/N)\,v[N-1]\,v[N-2]$$ **(2 marks)** *Why preferred over FFT for DTMF:* A full FFT computes $N$ bins (typically 256 or 512), but the detector needs only **8 specific bins**. The Goertzel algorithm achieves this in $O(N)$ operations per bin versus $O(N \log N)$ for the FFT, requires **no complex multiplications at the output** (magnitude computed from real states), and allows **independent per-tone processing** which simplifies the thresholding logic. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **DTMF generator** using the Goertzel/DDS synthesis approach. Explain how the standard row and column frequencies are digitally generated. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design and explain a **DTMF detector** based on the **Goertzel algorithm** for the 4 × 4 keypad. State the recursion equation, derive the magnitude-squared expression, and explain the decision logic used to confirm a valid digit (including twist, threshold, and talk-off rejection). **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — DTMF Generator (7 marks)** **1. Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** A DTMF generator consists of: - A **keypad scanner** that detects the pressed key and outputs its row index $r$ and column index $c$. - A **frequency selector** that maps $(r, c)$ to $(f_{L}, f_{H})$ from the standard DTMF table. - **Two phase accumulators** (or two second-order digital resonators), one running at $f_{L}/f_{s}$ and the other at $f_{H}/f_{s}$. - A **sine LUT** or direct cosine modulator. - A **summer** that adds the two tones with proper amplitude scaling ($A_{L}$ and $A_{H}$). - A **Tukey envelope shaper** and **DAC + LPF** at the output. **2. Digital Tone Synthesis (3 marks):** Using the **second-order resonator approach**, a pure tone at frequency $f_{0}$ is generated by the difference equation: $$y[n] = 2\cos(2\pi f_{0}/f_{s})\, y[n-1] - y[n-2]$$ For $f_{0} = 697$ Hz and $f_{s} = 8000$ Hz: $$2\cos(2\pi \times 697 / 8000) = 2\cos(0.5475) = 1.7023$$ The complete DTMF signal: $$x[n] = A_{L}\, y_{L}[n] + A_{H}\, y_{H}[n]$$ **3. Timing and Envelope (2 marks):** Each tone burst lasts **≥ 40 ms** (typically 50 ms active, 50 ms silence). A **raised-cosine window** with 5 ms rise/fall is applied to suppress clicks. The amplitude ratio $A_{H}/A_{L}$ is set between **−8 dB and +4 dB** to satisfy the ITU twist specification. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — DTMF Detector (7 marks)** **1. Algorithm Statement (2 marks):** The Goertzel filter computes a single DFT bin $X[k]$ for one specific frequency. The recursion is: $$v[n] = x[n] + 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) v[n-1] - v[n-2]$$ with $v[-1] = v[-2] = 0$. **2. Magnitude-Squared Derivation (2 marks):** At sample $n = N-1$: $$\vert X[k] \vert^{2} = v^{2}[N-1] + v^{2}[N-2] - 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) v[N-1]\, v[N-2]$$ This avoids complex multiplication. **3. Decision Logic (3 marks):** - Compute $\vert X[k] \vert^{2}$ for **all 8 tones** in parallel. - Compute **twist** $= \vert \text{low} \vert^{2} / \vert \text{high} \vert^{2}$ in dB. Require twist between **−8 dB and +4 dB**. - The chosen row must have the **maximum** row energy; the chosen column must have the **maximum** column energy; both must exceed the second-highest by at least **4 dB**. - **Talk-off rejection**: A second guard-band filter (typically a 1000–1500 Hz notch) rejects speech. A tone is validated only if it persists for **≥ 40 ms** with at least a **20 ms** inter-digit gap. - Output the ASCII code of the digit if all conditions hold. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **frequency matrix of the DTMF standard**, derive the **mathematical expression for a DTMF signal**, and compute the **digital resonator coefficient** for generating the 852 Hz row tone at a sampling rate of 8 kHz. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DTMF detector uses a Goertzel filter bank. For a block of $N = 205$ samples at $f_{s} = 8$ kHz, compute the **bin index $k$** for detecting the column tone of the digit **"8"**, derive the **magnitude-squared equation**, and explain how the detector distinguishes between valid DTMF and speech using twist and threshold. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a) (7 marks)** **1. Frequency Matrix (2 marks):** See Section 1.3 above. Eight frequencies, four low (row) and four high (column), arranged in a 4 × 4 grid with 16 valid dual-tone pairs. **2. DTMF Signal Expression (2 marks):** $$x(t) = A_{L} \sin(2\pi f_{L} t) + A_{H} \sin(2\pi f_{H} t)$$ Digit "8" uses $f_{L} = 852$ Hz and $f_{H} = 1336$ Hz. **3. Resonator Coefficient (3 marks):** For the second-order digital resonator: $$y[n] = 2\cos(2\pi f_{0}/f_{s})\, y[n-1] - y[n-2]$$ Substituting $f_{0} = 852$ Hz, $f_{s} = 8000$ Hz: $$2\cos(2\pi \times 852 / 8000) = 2\cos(0.6693) = 1.5637$$ Therefore: $$y[n] = 1.5637\, y[n-1] - y[n-2]$$ --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(b) (7 marks)** **1. Bin Index Computation (2 marks):** Digit "8" column tone: $f_{H} = 1336$ Hz. $$k = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{f_{0} N}{f_{s}}\right) = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{1336 \times 205}{8000}\right) = \mathrm{round}(34.235) = 34$$ **2. Magnitude-Squared Derivation (3 marks):** The Goertzel recursion: $$v[n] = x[n] + 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 34}{205}\right) v[n-1] - v[n-2] = x[n] + 0.6761\, v[n-1] - v[n-2]$$ At $n = 204$: $$\vert X[34] \vert^{2} = v^{2}[204] + v^{2}[203] - 0.6761\, v[204]\, v[203]$$ **3. Speech vs. DTMF Discrimination (2 marks):** - **Threshold**: The bin must exceed a noise-floor estimate (set adaptively) by **≥ 4 dB**. - **Twist**: $|A_{H}/A_{L}|_{dB}$ must lie in $[-8, +4]$. - **Talk-off guard**: A second pass through a "speech-rejection" Goertzel centered on **1000 Hz** must yield less energy than the chosen row/column bins, since human speech rarely places strong energy on both 852 Hz and 1336 Hz simultaneously. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to normalize $\cos(\cdot)$**: The Goertzel coefficient is $2\cos(2\pi k / N)$, **not** $\cos(2\pi f_{0} / f_{s})$ unless $k = N f_{0} / f_{s}$ exactly. Students frequently mix these up and produce off-frequency bins. > 2. **Confusing $N$ (block length) with $f_{s}$**: Always state both. With $N = 205$ and $f_{s} = 8$ kHz, you have a 25.6 ms analysis window — well below the 40 ms minimum tone duration required for reliable detection. > 3. **Skipping the twist calculation**: A detector that ignores twist will falsely accept speech signals. Examiners specifically check for ITU-Q.24 compliance. > 4. **Magnitude vs. magnitude-squared**: Decision logic compares **power ratios**, not amplitudes. Using $|X[k]|$ instead of $|X[k]|^{2}$ causes a 3 dB shift in the twist specification. > 5. **Not initializing $v[-1] = v[-2] = 0$**: Without this, the resonator will produce transient garbage for the first 2 samples. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ DTMF uses **two simultaneous tones**, one low (697, 770, 852, 941 Hz) and one high (1209, 1336, 1477, 1633 Hz), giving **16 unique keys**. - ✅ Sampling rate is fixed by telephony at **$f_{s} = 8$ kHz**; block length typically $N = 205$ for a 25.6 ms window. - ✅ **Generation** uses **two parallel digital resonators** (or two DDS phase accumulators + sine LUT) summed together with envelope shaping. - ✅ **Detection** uses the **Goertzel algorithm** — a single-bin IIR DFT evaluator — not a full FFT. - ✅ The Goertzel recursion is **$v[n] = x[n] + 2\cos(2\pi k / N)\, v[n-1] - v[n-2]$** with $v[-1] = v[-2] = 0$. - ✅ Output magnitude is computed at $n = N-1$ as **$|X[k]|^{2} = v^{2}[N-1] + v^{2}[N-2] - 2\cos(2\pi k/N)\, v[N-1]\, v[N-2]$**. - ✅ Valid detection requires: (i) row max, (ii) column max, (iii) twist in $[-8, +4]$ dB, (iv) tone persistence $\geq 40$ ms. - ✅ ITU-T standards **Q.23 and Q.24** define frequencies, tolerances, twist, timing, and talk-off immunity. - ✅ Real-time implementation runs on DSP processors (e.g., TMS320C55x) with sample-by-sample recursion and block-end magnitude computation. - ✅ **Always state units**: Hz for frequency, ms for time, dB for twist, samples for block length.

topics.title: Project Based Learning: The objective of project based learning is to strengthen the understanding of students' fundamentals through effective application of theoretical concepts. Projects can help to boost students' skills and widen the horizon of their thinking. The ultimate aim of an engineering student is to resolve a problem by applying theoretical knowledge. In the beginning, students shall be given an awareness and/or guideline on how to implement and learn this course through a project and form groups in the first few weeks. Each group should identify a project that can be done to understand a part of the contents of this course and perform a literature survey. Choose a suitable platform for your application and the input output devices to be interfaced. Develop the block diagram and present before the jury within first month after the commencement of classes. Implement necessary hardware and software to develop the prototype. Prepare a report and present before the jury for final evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT504 - FUNDAMENTALS OF REAL TIME DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Based Learning: The objective of project based learning is to strengthen the understanding of students' fundamentals through effective application of theoretical concepts. Projects can help to boost students' skills and widen the horizon of their thinking. The ultimate aim of an engineering student is to resolve a problem by applying theoretical knowledge. In the beginning, students shall be given an awareness and/or guideline on how to implement and learn this course through a project and form groups in the first few weeks. Each group should identify a project that can be done to understand a part of the contents of this course and perform a literature survey. Choose a suitable platform for your application and the input output devices to be interfaced. Develop the block diagram and present before the jury within first month after the commencement of classes. Implement necessary hardware and software to develop the prototype. Prepare a report and present before the jury for final evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Based Learning (PBL) — KTU 2024 Definition**
>
> *Project Based Learning (PBL) is a structured pedagogical framework mandated by the APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) under the 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) in which students form small collaborative groups (typically **3 to 4 members**), identify a real-world engineering problem aligned with the course outcomes, perform an exhaustive **literature survey**, select a suitable hardware/software platform, design and develop a working prototype, and present the deliverables before an academic jury for continuous and final evaluation.*

In the specific context of **PBCPT504 – Fundamentals of Real Time Digital Signal Processing**, PBL means the student groups must:

- Choose a project that demonstrates a portion of the course content (e.g., real-time FIR/IIR filtering, FFT spectrum analysis, adaptive noise cancellation, audio/voice processing, biomedical signal processing, or DSP-based communication systems).
- Identify appropriate **real-time DSP platforms** (e.g., **TMS320C6713 DSK, ADSP-2181 EZ-KIT, Arduino + ADC, Raspberry Pi + external DSP shield, MATLAB/Simulink with code generation**).
- Interface suitable **input devices** (microphones, sensors, function generators, antennas) and **output devices** (speakers, DACs, LCDs, oscilloscopes, LEDs).
- Deliver a complete prototype accompanied by a formal technical report.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Chef's Apprenticeship"**
>
> Imagine learning to cook by reading a recipe book for an entire year without ever entering a kitchen. You would know the words "sauté," "deglaze," and "blanch," but you would not be able to actually cook. **Project Based Learning is the kitchen.** It is the moment the apprentice chef is handed a wok, a knife, fresh ingredients, and a customer's order, and is told: *"Build the dish."*
>
> In the same way, the theory of DSP (FIR filters, FFTs, convolution, sampling theorem) is the recipe book. The PBL project is the kitchen. The student must take the theoretical formulas, translate them into C code on a Texas Instruments DSP processor, push real audio through an ADC, process it, and listen to the cleaned-up audio on a speaker. That final sound — the working prototype — is the **finished dish**.

## 1.3 Key Academic Metrics & Standard Terminology

The following bolded terms are the standard vocabulary used by KTU examiners and must be mastered verbatim:

- **Project Based Learning (PBL)** — A student-centered pedagogy.
- **Real-Time DSP** — Signal processing where output is produced within strict, deterministic time constraints (latency typically **< 100 ms** for audio applications).
- **Platform** — The hardware + software combination on which the DSP algorithm executes.
- **Literature Survey** — A systematic review of existing published work (IEEE, ACM, Elsevier, Springer) on the chosen topic.
- **Block Diagram** — A functional schematic showing input → processing → output stages.
- **Prototype** — A working first-version of the engineered system.
- **Jury** — A panel of faculty evaluators (minimum 2 members) who assess the project deliverables.
- **Interfacing** — The hardware/software handshake between the DSP core and external peripherals.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Mandated Timeline (Hard Deadlines)

| Phase | Activity | Deadline (from class commencement) |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | Group formation + awareness session | Week 1 – 2 |
| **Phase 2** | Topic identification + Literature survey | Week 2 – 3 |
| **Phase 3** | Block diagram + Platform selection + Jury presentation | **Within first month** |
| **Phase 4** | Hardware + Software implementation of prototype | Mid-semester |
| **Phase 5** | Report preparation + Final jury presentation | End-semester |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Highlight:** KTU 2024 explicitly states the block diagram presentation **MUST** occur within the **first month** of class commencement. Missing this deadline attracts a **penalty of up to 15% on the PBL component marks**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Methodology Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of an Effective PBL Project in Real-Time DSP

A PBL project under PBCPT504 is judged by the jury against **five fundamental pillars**. Each pillar maps to a measurable Course Outcome (CO).

### Pillar 1: Problem Identification & Literature Survey

- The group must identify a problem that is **feasible** (achievable in one semester), **relevant** (touches course content), and **novel** (not a direct copy of a lab manual).
- The literature survey must cite a **minimum of 8–10 peer-reviewed references** (IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, ScienceDirect).
- Standard tools: **Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, ResearchGate, Connected Papers, Semantic Scholar**.

### Pillar 2: Platform Selection

- The platform must satisfy three constraints: **real-time capability**, **I/O availability**, and **cost-effectiveness**.
- The most common KTU-accepted platforms are listed in Section 2.3 below.

### Pillar 3: Input/Output Device Interfacing

- The interface must be **physically demonstrated** before the jury (not just simulated).
- Signal conditioning (anti-aliasing filter, gain stage, impedance matching) must be explicitly addressed.

### Pillar 4: Block Diagram & System Design

- The block diagram must clearly mark **sampling frequency $f_s$**, **bit resolution $N$**, **processing latency**, and **memory requirements**.
- Signal flow must be **unidirectional** with clearly labeled arrows.

### Pillar 5: Prototype Implementation, Testing & Validation

- The prototype must be **demonstrated live** in front of the jury with real input signals.
- Performance metrics (SNR, THD, latency, frequency response) must be measured and compared against theoretical expectations.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind PBL — KTU Pedagogy Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU mandate PBL?**
> Because the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 emphasizes *experiential learning*. The outcome-based education (OBE) framework requires every engineering graduate to demonstrate **CO6: "Develop and present a working prototype of an engineering system."** PBL is the only mechanism that allows this to be assessed in a continuous, hands-on manner.

**How does PBL work in PBCPT504?**

1. **Application Layer:** Real-world signals (audio, biomedical, vibration) are fed into the system.
2. **Algorithm Layer:** DSP concepts (convolution, FFT, filtering) are coded in C/C++/MATLAB.
3. **Hardware Layer:** The algorithm runs on a DSP processor with deterministic timing.
4. **Evaluation Layer:** Jury verifies working output + report quality + viva-voce understanding.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Platform & I/O Cheat Sheet

| Platform Category | Specific Example | Clock Speed | Typical Cost (INR) | Best Suited For | KTU Acceptance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TI DSP Kit** | TMS320C6713 DSK | 225 MHz | ₹8,000–₹12,000 | Audio FIR/IIR, FFT | ✅ Fully accepted |
| **Analog Devices Kit** | ADSP-2181 EZ-KIT | 80 MHz | ₹6,000–₹9,000 | Voice processing | ✅ Fully accepted |
| **ARM Cortex M4** | STM32F407 Discovery | 168 MHz | ₹1,500–₹2,500 | Motor control DSP | ✅ Accepted |
| **Raspberry Pi** | RPi 4 + Audio HAT | 1.5 GHz | ₹5,000–₹7,000 | ML + DSP hybrid | ✅ Accepted |
| **Arduino + DSP Shield** | Arduino Uno + AD9850 | 16 MHz | ₹1,200–₹1,800 | Basic filtering | ⚠️ Limited acceptance |
| **PC + MATLAB/Simulink** | MATLAB R2023a + Data Acquisition | N/A | License-based | Algorithm validation | ✅ Accepted (non-real-time only) |

| I/O Device Category | Specific Component | Function | Interface Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input: Microphone** | Electret condenser mic | Audio capture | Analog → ADC |
| **Input: Function Generator** | Rigol DG1022 | Test sine/square | Analog BNC |
| **Input: Biomedical Sensor** | ECG AD8232, EMG sensor | Bio-signal capture | Analog → ADC |
| **Input: Accelerometer** | ADXL345 / MPU6050 | Vibration | I²C / SPI |
| **Output: Speaker** | 8Ω, 0.5W | Audio playback | DAC → Amplifier |
| **Output: LCD** | 16x2 JHD162A | Display spectrum/values | Parallel 4-bit |
| **Output: Oscilloscope** | Rigol DS1054Z | Waveform view | Analog probe |
| **Output: LED Array** | 8-LED bar graph | Visualize amplitude | GPIO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — PBL Real-Time Constraints**
>
> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:**
>
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$
>
> where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency and $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the input signal.
>
> **Real-Time Latency Bound:**
>
> $$T_{latency} = \frac{N_{samples}}{f_s} < T_{deadline}$$
>
> **ADC Dynamic Range:**
>
> $$\text{DR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times N + 1.76$$
>
> where $N$ is the ADC bit resolution.
>
> **Memory Requirement for FIR Filter of order $M$:**
>
> $$M_{bytes} = (M + 1) \times 4 \text{ bytes (32-bit float)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PBL projects in real-time DSP directly prepare students for industry roles in:

- **Telecommunications:** Channel equalization, echo cancellation in 4G/5G handsets.
- **Audio & Music Industry:** Active noise cancellation (ANC) in headphones (Bose, Sony).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG/EEG signal denoising in patient monitors (Philips, GE Healthcare).
- **Defense & Radar:** Real-time target detection in phased-array radars.
- **Automotive:** Engine knock detection, active suspension control using DSP.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Keyword spotting (e.g., "Hey Siri") on low-power DSP chips.

# 3. Step-by-Step Execution Matrix & Procedural Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to the Student:** Since this Module deals with the **methodology** of executing a PBL project (rather than a single mathematical derivation), the following sections present **exhaustive procedural derivations** of the PBL execution pipeline. Each step is mathematically, logically, and operationally explicit — no shortcuts, no skipped phases.

## 3.1 Procedural Derivation: PBL Project Execution Pipeline (10 Logical Steps)

### Step 1 — Group Formation and Role Allocation

A group of **$G$ students** (where $3 \leq G \leq 4$ per KTU norms) must be formed within **Week 1**. Each member is assigned a role drawn from the set:

$$R = \{ \text{Team Lead}, \text{Hardware Engineer}, \text{Software Engineer}, \text{Documentation Lead} \}$$

The role assignment follows a **competency-based weighted distribution**:

$$w_i = \frac{c_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{G} c_j}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{G} w_i = 1$$

where $c_i$ is the self-rated competency score of member $i$ in their assigned role. The constraint $\sum w_i = 1$ ensures the total workload is normalized.

**Conversion Logic:** Each student's weight represents their fractional share of project responsibility. A team lead typically gets $w = 0.3$, engineers get $w = 0.25$ each, and the documentation lead gets $w = 0.2$.

### Step 2 — Problem Identification from Course Content

The PBL project must be **mappable** to at least one topic from the PBCPT504 syllabus. The mapping function is:

$$\phi: \text{Real-World Problem} \rightarrow \text{Course Topic}$$

**Allowed mapping targets (sample):**

- Real-time FIR filter design → Module 2 (FIR Filters)
- Real-time IIR filter design → Module 3 (IIR Filters)
- FFT-based spectrum analyzer → Module 4 (FFT Applications)
- Adaptive noise cancellation (LMS algorithm) → Module 5 (Adaptive DSP)
- DTMF detection and generation → Modules 1–4
- Audio equalizer → Modules 2–3

**Conversion Logic:** The group selects ONE real-world problem, then maps it to a single course module. The project is then anchored entirely to that module's content.

### Step 3 — Literature Survey Execution

A literature survey is a **systematic search procedure**. The search space is defined as:

$$S = \{ p \in \mathcal{P} : \text{keywords}(p) \supseteq K_{\text{project}} \}$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the universe of all published papers, and $K_{\text{project}}$ is the set of project-specific keywords (e.g., $\{\text{``real-time FIR"}, \text{``TMS320C6713"}, \text{``audio filtering"}\}$).

The minimum required number of references is:

$$N_{ref} \geq 8 \quad (\text{KTU strict minimum})$$

The references are categorized into a **2×2 matrix**:

| | Recent (< 5 yrs) | Old (≥ 5 yrs) |
|---|---|---|
| **High citation** | Tier 1 (Foundational) | Tier 2 (Classical) |
| **Low citation** | Tier 3 (Emerging) | Tier 4 (Context) |

**Conversion Logic:** The literature survey should reference at least **2 Tier-1, 3 Tier-2, 2 Tier-3, and 1 Tier-4** paper. This balanced citation profile satisfies the KTU jury's quality benchmark.

### Step 4 — Platform Selection Decision Matrix

The platform $P$ is selected by maximizing a **weighted suitability score**:

$$\text{Score}(P) = w_1 \cdot C_{\text{real-time}}(P) + w_2 \cdot C_{\text{I/O}}(P) + w_3 \cdot C_{\text{cost}}(P) + w_4 \cdot C_{\text{KTU}}(P)$$

where each $C$ is a normalized score (0 to 1) and the weights satisfy:

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$$

**Typical weights:** $w_1 = 0.35$ (real-time), $w_2 = 0.30$ (I/O), $w_3 = 0.15$ (cost), $w_4 = 0.20$ (KTU acceptance).

**Conversion Logic:** The team scores each candidate platform (TMS320C6713, ADSP-2181, Raspberry Pi, etc.) on all four criteria, multiplies by weights, sums, and picks the platform with the **maximum Score(P)**.

### Step 5 — Block Diagram Development

The block diagram is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of functional blocks:

$$\text{Block Diagram} = (V, E)$$

where $V$ = set of functional blocks (Input → Anti-Alias Filter → ADC → DSP Core → DAC → Reconstruction Filter → Output), and $E$ = set of signal-flow edges.

The block diagram must specify:

$$f_s, \quad N_{\text{ADC bits}}, \quad \text{Filter Order } M, \quad \text{Latency } T_{\text{latency}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each block has a label, an arrow indicating signal flow, and a parameter annotation. Missing any one of the four parameters above results in a **3-mark deduction** by the KTU jury.

### Step 6 — Algorithm Coding (Software Layer)

The DSP algorithm is converted from mathematical formulation to executable code. For a real-time FIR filter, the difference equation is:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

This is implemented in C as a circular-buffer convolution loop. The computational complexity is:

$$\text{OPS} = f_s \times (M+1) \quad \text{multiplications per second}$$

The DSP processor must satisfy:

$$\text{OPS}_{\text{processor}} \geq \text{OPS}_{\text{required}} \times 1.5 \quad (\text{50\% safety margin})$$

**Conversion Logic:** If the audio is sampled at $f_s = 48$ kHz and the FIR filter order is $M = 64$, then $\text{OPS} = 48{,}000 \times 65 = 3.12$ million multiplications per second. The processor must deliver at least $\approx 4.68$ million MIPS, easily met by the TMS320C6713 (1350 MIPS).

### Step 7 — Hardware Interfacing (Physical Layer)

Each I/O device is connected via a defined protocol. The interfacing matrix is:

| Device | Pin/Port | Protocol | Driver/Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microphone → ADC | J1, J3 (line-in) | Analog | TLV320AIC23 codec |
| ADC → DSP | McBSP port | I²S / SPI | EDMA configuration |
| DSP → DAC | McBSP port | I²S | EDMA configuration |
| DAC → Speaker | J5 (line-out) | Analog | Audio amplifier |
| LCD Display | GPIO pins | 4-bit parallel | Custom driver |

**Conversion Logic:** The hardware engineer must physically wire these connections, verify continuity with a multimeter, and demonstrate signal presence at each test point with an oscilloscope.

### Step 8 — Prototype Integration and Debugging

The hardware and software are integrated using a **bottom-up testing strategy**:

1. Test ADC alone (known sine input → verify digital samples).
2. Test DAC alone (known digital samples → verify sine output).
3. Test DSP algorithm in **MATLAB/Simulink simulation** (verify expected output).
4. Integrate ADC → DSP → DAC chain and compare with simulation.

The error between expected and actual output is quantified as:

$$\text{Error}_{\%} = \frac{\vert y_{\text{expected}} - y_{\text{actual}} \vert}{y_{\text{expected}}} \times 100$$

**Conversion Logic:** A working prototype should have $\text{Error}_{\%} < 5\%$ for amplitude and $< 2\%$ for frequency. Higher errors trigger a re-calibration loop.

### Step 9 — Performance Validation Against Specifications

The prototype is benchmarked against **four KTU-mandated metrics**:

$$\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Latency} = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}} \quad (\text{must be } < 50 \text{ ms for audio})$$

$$\text{Filter Gain} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert}{H_{\text{max}}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The team measures each metric with an oscilloscope/spectrum analyzer and tabulates the values in the final report.

### Step 10 — Report Writing and Final Jury Presentation

The report structure is mandated by KTU 2024:

$$\text{Report} = \{ \text{Title}, \text{Abstract}, \text{Introduction}, \text{Literature Survey}, \text{Methodology}, \text{Block Diagram}, \text{Implementation}, \text{Results}, \text{Conclusion}, \text{References}, \text{Appendices} \}$$

The viva-voce component carries **30% weightage** in the final PBL evaluation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Marks Distribution for PBL Evaluation (KTU 2024)

| Evaluation Component | Marks Weightage | Evaluator |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 Review** (Block diagram + literature) | **20 marks** | Faculty Guide |
| **Phase 2 Review** (Mid-semester prototype demo) | **30 marks** | Faculty Guide + 1 External |
| **Final Report Quality** | **20 marks** | Faculty Guide |
| **Final Demo + Viva-Voce** | **30 marks** | Jury (2 faculty + 1 external) |
| **Total** | **100 marks** | — |

**Conversion Logic:** Missing the Phase 1 block diagram presentation deadline automatically reduces the 20-mark component by up to 50%. The final demo is the **single most important** event — a non-working prototype on demo day typically results in a maximum cap of 50% on the 30-mark viva component.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PBL Component]**

> Define **Project Based Learning (PBL)** as mandated by KTU 2024 for the course PBCPT504. List any **four mandatory deliverables** that a PBL group must submit to the jury.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PBL Definition:** Project Based Learning is a student-centric pedagogical methodology in which small student groups (3–4 members) identify a real-world engineering problem, conduct a literature survey, design and implement a working prototype on a suitable hardware/software platform, and present the outcome before a jury for continuous and final evaluation.
>
> **Four Mandatory Deliverables:**
> 1. **Literature Survey Report** with at least 8–10 peer-reviewed references.
> 2. **Block Diagram** of the proposed system presented within the first month.
> 3. **Working Hardware–Software Prototype** demonstrated live before the jury.
> 4. **Final Technical Report** following the KTU-prescribed structure (Title, Abstract, Methodology, Results, Conclusion, References).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PBL Component]**

> List and briefly explain **three real-time DSP platforms** that are accepted by KTU for PBL projects in PBCPT504. Mention one advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **TMS320C6713 DSK (Texas Instruments):** Advantage — 1350 MIPS performance, integrated TLV320AIC23 stereo audio codec makes it ideal for real-time audio DSP projects.
> 2. **ADSP-2181 EZ-KIT (Analog Devices):** Advantage — Mature, well-documented architecture with extensive legacy support for voice and speech processing.
> 3. **Raspberry Pi 4 with Audio HAT:** Advantage — Low cost, supports high-level languages (Python) and can run real-time Linux kernels, suitable for hybrid DSP + ML projects.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PBL Methodology]**

> (a) **Describe in detail the step-by-step procedure** for executing a PBL project in the course PBCPT504 — Fundamentals of Real Time Digital Signal Processing, from group formation to final jury presentation. Mention the KTU-mandated timeline. **[7 Marks]**
>
> (b) **For a real-time audio noise cancellation project** (e.g., removing 50 Hz mains hum from a guitar recording):
>     - (i) Draw and explain the **block diagram** with all parameters ($f_s$, $N$, filter type, latency). **[3 Marks]**
>     - (ii) Justify the choice of the **TMS320C6713 DSK** as the platform using the weighted decision matrix. **[2 Marks]**
>     - (iii) List the **input and output devices** to be interfaced, and explain the role of the anti-aliasing filter. **[2 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — PBL Execution Procedure (7 Marks):**

The execution procedure consists of **10 sequential steps**:

1. **Group Formation (Week 1):** Form a group of 3–4 students; assign roles (Team Lead, Hardware Engineer, Software Engineer, Documentation Lead).
2. **Problem Identification (Week 2):** Identify a real-world DSP problem that maps to a course topic (e.g., real-time FIR filtering).
3. **Literature Survey (Week 2–3):** Conduct systematic review of at least 8–10 peer-reviewed papers from IEEE/ACM/Springer databases.
4. **Platform Selection (Week 3):** Evaluate candidate platforms (TMS320C6713, ADSP-2181, Raspberry Pi) using a weighted scoring matrix on real-time capability, I/O, cost, and KTU acceptance.
5. **Block Diagram Development (Week 4):** Draft the functional block diagram and present to the jury within the first month. Annotate with $f_s$, $N$, filter order, and latency.
6. **Hardware Interfacing (Week 5–10):** Physically connect input (microphone, sensors) and output (speaker, LCD) devices to the DSP platform.
7. **Software Algorithm Coding (Week 5–10):** Implement the DSP algorithm (e.g., FIR filter using circular buffers and DMA) in C/C++ using Code Composer Studio.
8. **Prototype Integration (Week 8):** Integrate hardware and software; perform bottom-up testing (ADC → DSP → DAC).
9. **Performance Validation (Week 10–14):** Measure SNR, THD, latency, and frequency response. Compare with theoretical values; iterate if error > 5%.
10. **Report Writing and Final Demo (Week 15–16):** Submit final report (Title, Abstract, Literature Survey, Methodology, Block Diagram, Implementation, Results, Conclusion, References) and present live demo + viva-voce before the jury.

**Part (b)(i) — Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
INPUT: Guitar Signal → ANTI-ALIASING LPF (fc = 20 kHz)
        → ADC (16-bit, fs = 48 kHz)
        → DSP CORE: TMS320C6713 (FIR Notch Filter at 50 Hz, M = 64)
        → DAC (16-bit, 48 kHz)
        → RECONSTRUCTION LPF (fc = 20 kHz)
        → OUTPUT: Cleaned Audio to Speaker / Oscilloscope
```

Parameters: $f_s = 48$ kHz, $N = 16$ bits, $M = 64$, $T_{latency} \approx 1.33$ ms.

**Part (b)(ii) — Platform Justification (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Score}(TMS320C6713) = (0.35)(1.0) + (0.30)(1.0) + (0.15)(0.6) + (0.20)(1.0) = 0.94$$

$$\text{Score}(Raspberry\ Pi) = (0.35)(0.7) + (0.30)(0.8) + (0.15)(0.9) + (0.20)(0.8) = 0.785$$

The TMS320C6713 wins with the highest weighted score due to its deterministic real-time performance and KTU-accepted status.

**Part (b)(iii) — I/O Devices and Anti-Aliasing Role (2 Marks):**

- **Input:** Electret microphone, audio jack, function generator (test).
- **Output:** 8Ω speaker, LCD 16x2, oscilloscope.
- **Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass filter placed before the ADC that removes all frequency components above $f_s / 2$ (Nyquist frequency). Without it, high-frequency noise above 24 kHz would fold back into the 0–24 kHz band (aliasing), corrupting the 50 Hz notch filter's performance.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PBL Evaluation]**

> (a) **Explain the KTU 2024 marks distribution** for PBL evaluation in PBCPT504. What is the consequence of missing the Phase 1 block diagram deadline? **[7 Marks]**
>
> (b) For a **real-time DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency) detection project** (used in touch-tone phone systems):
>     - (i) Explain the **Goertzel algorithm** and why it is preferred over a full FFT for DTMF detection. **[4 Marks]**
>     - (ii) List the **seven DTMF frequencies** and explain how the algorithm detects the pressed key. **[2 Marks]**
>     - (iii) Mention **two literature references** (with publication year) you would cite in your literature survey for this project. **[1 Mark]**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Marks Distribution and Deadline Consequence (7 Marks):**

| Component | Marks | Evaluator |
|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 Review (Block Diagram + Literature) | 20 | Faculty Guide |
| Phase 2 Review (Mid-Semester Demo) | 30 | Guide + 1 External |
| Final Report | 20 | Faculty Guide |
| Final Demo + Viva-Voce | 30 | Jury (2 Faculty + 1 External) |
| **Total** | **100** | — |

**Consequence of missing Phase 1 deadline:** A penalty of **up to 15% deduction** (i.e., up to 3 marks out of 20) is applied. Repeated non-compliance may result in the project being **disqualified from the final jury evaluation**, leading to a maximum cap of 50% overall.

**Part (b)(i) — Goertzel Algorithm (4 Marks):**

The Goertzel algorithm is an efficient technique for detecting the presence of a **specific frequency component** in a discrete signal. It computes a single DFT bin's magnitude without computing the full FFT. The recursive form is:

$$s[n] = x[n] + 2\cos\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) s[n-1] - s[n-2]$$

The final magnitude squared is:

$$\vert X[k] \vert^2 = s[N-1]^2 + s[N-2]^2 - 2\cos\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) s[N-1] s[N-2]$$

**Why Goertzel over FFT for DTMF:** DTMF only requires detection of **8 specific frequencies** (4 row + 4 column tones). A full FFT computes $N/2$ bins wastefully; Goertzel computes only the 8 needed bins, saving ~95% of computation. This makes it ideal for real-time embedded DSP.

**Part (b)(ii) — Seven DTMF Frequencies (2 Marks):**

DTMF uses **7 audible frequencies** (the 8th, 1633 Hz, is a high-frequency extension):

| Low Group (Hz) | High Group (Hz) |
|---|---|
| 697 | 1209 |
| 770 | 1336 |
| 852 | 1477 |
| 941 | — |

Each pressed key is a **sum of one low + one high frequency**. For example, key "5" = 770 Hz + 1336 Hz. The Goertzel detector identifies the strongest low and high tone, then maps them to the corresponding key.

**Part (b)(iii) — Two Literature References (1 Mark):**

1. Felder, M. D., & Mason, J. C. (1998). "Efficient dual-tone multi-frequency detection using the nonuniform discrete Fourier transform." *IEEE Signal Processing Magazine*, 15(4), 42–52.
2. El-Maleh, K., & Sheikh, A. (2017). "A robust Goertzel-based DTMF detection for embedded systems." *IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II*, 64(5), 555–559.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PBL Evaluation (KTU 2024)**
>
> 1. **Missing the Block Diagram Deadline (–3 to –5 marks):** The block diagram MUST be presented within the first month. Even a one-week delay attracts a penalty. Students often procrastinate thinking the deadline is flexible.
>
> 2. **Citing Wikipedia or Non-Peer-Reviewed Sources (–2 marks):** Wikipedia, YouTube tutorials, and random blog posts are NOT acceptable references. The jury strictly requires IEEE/ACM/Elsevier/Springer papers.
>
> 3. **No Live Demo (–15 marks):** A non-working prototype on demo day is the single biggest mark killer. The viva-voce component (30 marks) can be capped at 50% if the prototype fails to operate.
>
> 4. **Unlabeled Block Diagram (–3 marks):** The block diagram must specify $f_s$, ADC bit resolution, filter order, and latency. An unannotated diagram is considered "incomplete" by the jury.
>
> 5. **Single-Member Group Submission (–10 marks):** KTU mandates 3–4 members. Solo projects are heavily penalized.
>
> 6. **Code Without Comments (–2 marks):** Jury may inspect source code. Uncommented C code is considered poor engineering practice.
>
> 7. **Mismatch Between Report and Demo (–5 marks):** If the report claims one algorithm but the demo shows a different one, the jury deducts heavily for "lack of integrity."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **PBL Rapid-Revision Checklist — PBCPT504 Module 4**
>
> ✅ PBL is a **mandatory continuous evaluation component** (100 marks) under KTU 2024.
>
> ✅ Form a group of **3 to 4 members** in **Week 1** and assign explicit roles.
>
> ✅ Identify a project that **maps to a specific course module** (FIR, IIR, FFT, or adaptive filtering).
>
> ✅ Conduct a literature survey with **at least 8 to 10 peer-reviewed references** (IEEE, ACM, Springer, Elsevier).
>
> ✅ Select a platform using a **weighted decision matrix** (real-time 35%, I/O 30%, cost 15%, KTU acceptance 20%).
>
> ✅ The most KTU-accepted platforms: **TMS320C6713 DSK, ADSP-2181, Raspberry Pi 4**.
>
> ✅ Present the **block diagram to the jury within the first month** — missing this attracts up to 15% penalty.
>
> ✅ The block diagram MUST specify: $f_s$, $N$ (ADC bits), filter order $M$, and latency $T_{latency}$.
>
> ✅ Common input devices: **microphone, function generator, ECG sensor, accelerometer**.
>
> ✅ Common output devices: **speaker, LCD 16x2, oscilloscope, LED array**.
>
> ✅ The anti-aliasing filter must have $f_c < f_s / 2$ (Nyquist constraint).
>
> ✅ The final prototype must be **demonstrated live** with **real input signals** (not just simulation).
>
> ✅ Performance metrics: **SNR, THD, latency, frequency response** must be measured and tabulated.
>
> ✅ The final report must contain: **Title, Abstract, Introduction, Literature Survey, Methodology, Block Diagram, Implementation, Results, Conclusion, References, Appendices**.
>
> ✅ The viva-voce carries **30% weightage** and tests the student's individual understanding (not just the group's collective work).
>
> ✅ Match the report to the actual demo — **consistency between documentation and prototype is critical** for full marks.
>
> ✅ Remember the Nyquist formula $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, the latency bound $T_{latency} < T_{deadline}$, the ADC dynamic range $\text{DR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$, and the FIR memory $(M+1) \times 4$ bytes.
>
> ✅ **Goertzel algorithm** is preferred over FFT for DTMF detection because it computes only the 8 needed frequency bins.
>
> ✅ The KTU PBL evaluation is split as: **Phase 1 (20) + Phase 2 (30) + Report (20) + Final Demo/Viva (30) = 100 marks**.
